Unlike most Monster Hunter games this one starts off pretty easy, so the beginning ends up being bit slow and boring, but it does pickup as you progress especially after HR 4. Combat is very fun, all the weapons are here and story is maybe one of the best of any Monster Hunter game so far. My only gripe is how confusing multiplayer is, the fact the I had to watch a 10 minute YouTube tutorial explaining how multiplayer works is baffling, but once you understand its fun times with friends. Performance is ok, but overall requires a decent PC to even run at comfortable state. Nonetheless its an amazing game I had a lot of fun with it!

This game runs terribly and has very poor texture quality with random spots of incredibly low resolution textures. On the other hand, it does look very good on average even at lower settings.

The actual gameplay though is definitely quite fun and is reminiscent of a faster-paced, easier to play version of Monster Hunter: World. It's got fun monsters like Arkveld and a lot to enjoy, but there is a big grinding issue with how much RNG there is. You can't just look at a build online and replicate it like in World unless you've gotten pretty lucky. Excessive RNG doesn't make a game difficult, it just makes it grindy and tiresome.

I personally just avoid the ridiculous grind and play for fun getting what I can.

For as easy as the game is, a lot of that can be explained by the improvement of the player's movement. As a lance main, this is easily the biggest updates to your move set, and the addition of focus strikes takes it from a counterpoking beast into a surgical monster-skewering behemoth with the biggest drawback of the weapon removed- the inability to aim pokes after initiating.

The monsters are a bit of a mixed bag, frontloading all of the baby-easy monsters into low rank. This is fine, but the early game monsters all have a feeling of being designed for earlier gen games and didn't get any new tools themselves to compete with the hunters' overall improved movesets and mobility. The late game is where the Wilds shines because it remembers that it has mechanics that they can take advantage of in fights, with Seregios and Arkveld being standouts for being incredibly quick and having tricky movesets as well as opportunities for quick players to take advantage of, and it makes me excited for the DLC.
The story does suck though. I get wanting to have the story be more of a focus, but I feel something closer to MH4/U or World would have been better than the on-rails sections that are quite prevalent throughout the low rank questline, and forcing me to listen to Nata and Alma yap away while we make our way towards the hunt target or next village at the blistering speed of 4MPH. Being held hostage by them only made me end up disliking them.
As for performance, I never experienced an issue, but my PC is also kind of overkill. I did however hit a few moments where the cutscenes would play at normal speed, but the audio would desync heavily during a few of the more loading-intensive ones that just snap you to a new area or need to load in all of something like Gogmazios. I would also get LOD models when the game would hard change areas mid-cutscene, but as far as actual gameplay is concerned, I experienced no issues.
Overall, it feels great to play once you escape the story and get to the challenging fights, but the early game is kind of braindead easy and babysitting Nata and Alma in the story feels tedious.

I am indifferent on my experience with this game. On one hand, I've had a fair amount of fun with it for about 58 hours. On another, I've been frustrated by the performance and just overall jank.

I am an ultra casual MH player. I don't play these games to get HR100+ and do all of the crazy G rank hunt stuff like previous games. I kill some monsters for 40-50 hours and the grind causes me to throw in the towel. I'm pretty sure I'm already in that phase with the game now.

I guess my biggest issue is, I don't recall there being such a massive control response issue in MH Rise or World, which were the last two MH games I played on Steam. There is some absolutely insane level of input buffering or animation locking going on where when you press buttons things just DO NOT HAPPEN some times. Biggest example is me trying to put my gun away to drink a potion. I have to hit the "E" button twice at least to holster my bowgun, but I'm running from the monster and my character might have stepped on a small incline so the animation of that happening basically cancels my holstering, so I have to hit the button again to make that happen. During that, I am mashing "E" to drink my Mega Potion but my character is yet again in some sort of small animation that is preventing that from happening.

I know MH games have a commitment to every single thing you do but it was NEVER THIS BAD in the last two games. I have tested this with and without Frame Generation and it doesn't matter. It's not input delay, it's just straight up animation locking and buffering.

The performance of this game is not good at all. I have a fairly good computer and his game's optimization is in the trash. I have to lower settings down to fairly mediocre levels, run DLSS Balanced and turn off all the nice bells and whistles like Ray tracing in order to get an acceptable framerate at any time. I shouldn't have to compromise the game's image quality so much just to get 60+ FPS but I do. 

I have settings dialed in right now that look "acceptable" but honestly the game has this really washed out, fuzzy look to it and I think World, a game from 2017 actually looks better. I don't really understand that but it's definitely something. I think MH Wilds has better animations and environment detail, but as far as graphical fidelity and art direction are concerned, World was better by a lot, and Rise had more color and pop to it.

Despite all of that, I still think I enjoyed the game enough to recommend it. I am glad that I bought it on sale, and that is where I'd recommend anyone to do as well is wait for a sale like 40% or something. It's definitely worthy enough for that. Hell, they might even make the game better with future updates.

I might come back to this one eventually but I think I'm just already tired of the HR grind (Hit HR63 as of review) and I'm not having as much fun anymore because I have to grind so much for materials and fix my build and I'm just not motived at all to do that.

Preface: I have beaten every monster and most events that are/have been active. Performance has been spotty the entire duration of playing this game with some updates resulting in my game crashing frequently. This game has also encouraged me to revisit some previous titles and try MHS2: Wings of Ruin.

Visually: I love what this game could be through use of RE Framework, manual editing of the configuration files located in the local installation of the game. Unfortunately, this game is visually boring, oversaturated, and most visual graphic settings have little to no impact on the appearance of the game that mods can allow with the benefit of better performance while having more visually distinct biomes.

Gameplay: It's fun. The gameplay is much better than previous titles while having the main mechanics be fun without being a requirement to fully understand them. Most of the monsters are fun to hunt, while most story hunts feel short compared to World, or extremely easy compared to Rise.

Overall the game can be fun, but I have played a lot less of this game compared to World (even pre-Iceborne). The environments are less visually interesting. They feel less impactful considering the lack of verticality and truly original ideas that aren't ankle deep. The story is weak, no true impact of the environment that isn't noticeable through tracking or shown beyond slightly more aggressive weather that retroactively explains the fauna that exists in a region.

[h2] Cons [/h2]
- The Price, get it on sale (any sale the game is worth a lot in my eyes but just not quite the base price)

- Gogma and Artian weapons (not a bad system but late game grinding revolving around these is a personal gripe)
- Focus Strikes are fun to use but overpowered and sometimes breaks some immersion on how Monsters just sit there taking it, hopefully Capcom will hear us and fix it in expansion

[h1] Worded thoughts [/h1]
So I want to start out by saying that I am a lifelong monster hunter fan, I am a fair bit younger than most but my first experience with the series was 4u on the 3ds and later with Tri on my Wii and have played every game in the series though only a few to absolute completion, if you are a fan of specifically old gen monster hunter you likely won't like this game it basically does away with a lot of pre 5th gen stuff making things like aiming your weapon and prepping for hunts is minimized and made easier here. However this is not to the games weakness especially as a monster hunter game, there are 7 (not including G rank expansions and including Frontier) old gen games with hundreds of hours of content Monster Hunter as a series should not be tried to be held to the standards that it hasn't kept itself since World and especially as a newer game the Gameplay is a meaningful step forward from 5th gen. Keep in mind this is only talking about gameplay, I personally abhor Capcom as a company and the microtransactions are VERY bad though i'd say a bit better than Rise. Ultimately MH is a series dominated by which one you like the most but this game is one i personally will reccomend.

This is a tenative review. As of writing this review, it is Post-Title Update 4, but before any news of the inevitable expansion.

For the short version, I'd recommend waiting until the game is on sale. While it's in the best state since launch, I still can't in good faith recommend the game at the full $70, especially when all the other games also go on amazing 90% sales. 

For the long version, I will divide this review into 3 sections: The Problems, The Solutions, and The Successes.

The Problems.
- There are a ton of really cool mechanics, but most of them feel half-implemented. Monster corpses rot, there are Pack Turf Wars, environmental destruction leading to new ways to stun and damage monsters. Yet most of these mechanics fall under the radar in favor of the other problems.
- Seikret. I both love and hate these things. Cool, I get MH chocobos. Not cool, they auto path, making most of the beautiful traversal pointless. It often feels like the game was designed around ignoring most of the cool stuff it does, and I really dislike it. 
- Slinger. I don't think this gets talked enough, but the Slinger is forced. It feels like it was added to the game just for the sake of mirroring World, and it's hard not to notice it. You get so many different damage types, yet most of them are useless except for lure pods, flash pods, and dung pods, which all could have been handheld items on their own. I also feel like it ruins the feel of natural gathering that previous games had, as you just spam one button on your Seikret as you race by. It also really annoys me that so many environmental hazards are so reliant on using the slinger, particularly against Jin Dahaad who has the most egregious fight for this. Unless you play SnS, the slinger is just really clunky to use in the scope of actual battle.
- I know this is a common gripe, but I think my issue with the balance is that the difficulty of the game didn't increase in proportion to the tools each weapon gained. Especially with offsets, perfect dodge, perfect guard, immunity frames, etc., there's too many ways to avoid damage, which the monsters, which feel the same in difficulty as World and Rise, don't really burst through. The Arch-Tempereds especially showcase this issue, where even with faster attacks, their attacks are clear to read, which when you're given a free immunity button on most weapons makes them really easy to counter. You're not in a 1v1 with monsters, like in past games, you're bullying them. For better or for worse. 

The Solutions.

With the expansion on the horizon, I have hope that the continued passion they're showing will let them fix some of these things, so here are my thoughts from my personal experience. 

- Give us more options regarding Seikrets. I want to be able to turn off my mini map, the scoutflies, and the auto pathing. I want missions where I'm not allowed to use my seikret or palico, forcing me to change my strategies and learn new techs. I know some people actually prefer this style or just want to go straight to the fight, and I get that alot of MH Wilds's design is around giving newer players to the series a sense of awe. But at the same time, I do want options to play the game in my own way, and experience the overall design of the game. 
- Some map changes would be in order. I think what we have is great, but at the same time there's more I'd like to see. I would love to see more of an underground layer in the plains, lava tunnels in the basin that burn you, and an overall increase in the map size for the cliffs and ruins. I think for a game about "wilderness" and expansiveness, the camps themselves feel one dimensional, and the biomes themselves feel claustrophobic. I'm sure people would complain about "a whole lot of nothing" but I want that nothing if it's scenic, especially since travel is so easy with camps and seikret. 
- Make terrains breakable by weapons. Have land and aerial obstacles to fight monsters with, but you should be able to break them with a bow or smash them with a lance to cause an effect. It's fine if there's a potential for accidents too! I want an excuse to need to be more aware of my surroundings and use my knowhow to beat up a monster. You can then add an additional way of creating obstacles by using slinger ammo. Would be cool if I need heavy piercing and fire ammo to fight Jin Dahaad in cliffs for example, and would make fighting him less annoying. It's like I have Breath of the Wild's TP and travel system with none of the map size to justify it.
- The logbook should note the times a monster spawns. The changing weather is amazing and one of the best features in the game, but it can be annoying to look up info that should be in the game in a wiki.
- On that note, the Weather should affect you in some way. It feels weird that I can see through a sandstorm, drink pepper juice to survive a blizzard, have my footing during a rainstorm, etc. I want the game to FORCE me to live with nature. That way then the Apex fights would also just that little bit more difficult, since you're not fighting in the same conditions as always. 
- More vertical combat? I don't know, it's weird to add in the scope of wilds, but like wedge beatles are useless and a lot of weapons have fallen short solely due to the loss of verticality from Rise (looks at switchaxe and insect glaive). In general though, I think some reanalysis needs to be on weapons other than Bow, Greatsword, or Lance. The rest have some real problems that while they do insane amounts of damage just feel worse to use. 
- Artian RNG. You know what I'll say. There needs to be a better way to reroll an rng mechanic. There's already plenty of grinding in the game, there doesn't need to be more. 
- A more fluent way of connecting between regions. The game would feel much more immersive if it was one gigantic map, rather than a bunch of separate maps. Would make the invasion of dosahguma into and out of the forest much cooler.
- More packs. Add more packs. You have a cool system, and legitimately there's only 2-3 packs in the entire game, with Hirabami being the only "real" one since Yian Kut-Ku scatter and Doshaguma aren't real packs. Facing more "alpha" monsters would also just be cool, which you can imagine my disappointment when Doshaguma was the only one with this concept. 
- Make rotting corpses more essential? Like, I almost find no reasons to gather the items on the corpses, and to be honest, don't even notice them because of the aforementioned problems. Would help if the game was all one map, like noted above. You don't have to load it all at once, but it would be a lot more immersive if racing across the map meant that I also saw my previous kills, and maybe even smaller monsters eating the corpses I leave behind. 

The Successes
- The Apexes holy s**t they're cool. All 4 (+ Arkveld) are incredibly sick. 
- I actually really appreciate the lack of Elder Dragons. I know there'll be more added in DLC, but I appreciate that they don't drown out the already small ecosystems of the base game. 
- The music is insanely good. So much love and passion went into it and I appreciate it for that.
- The armor and weapons look insanely good. I wish there were more goofy ones, but that'll come with time and events. The character creation being one of the best I've ever played with is also a nice touch. 
- Perfect Dodge and Perfect Guards are insanely fun. So are offsets. Just being able to dodge a monster's fire breath WHILE IN THE BREATH gives so many cool points, I can't express this enough. 
- I actually really enjoy the scripted boss fights. While they make the fights less difficult, it does make them really fun. Gogmazios and Zoh Shia are just fun to fight, and to be honest I don't get to fight them enough. 

That's all I have for now. I love Monster Hunter and the game is in a good state, but it absolutely could be better.

Once again, I have dusted off my hunting gear and ventured into the Forbidden Lands. And while the scale is impressive, I find myself looking back at my time in World with a certain aristocratic nostalgia. You see, being a Lord of my stature means I have a refined taste for things that actually work.

Don't get me wrong, Wilds has its charms. The monsters are fierce and the world is vast, but it lacks the "mature" elegance of its predecessor. Monster Hunter: World felt like a masterfully tailored velvet suit, it fit perfectly, it moved with grace, and it ran smoother than a greased pig at a country fair.

The developers seem to have forgotten that a Lord does not appreciate his vision being interrupted by technical stutters and unoptimized performance. World was a testament to what happens when devs truly polish their craft until it shines like the gold in my baroque chambers. Wilds, on the other hand, feels like a rough diamond that someone forgot to buff. It’s a bit clunky, a bit shaky, and frankly, my noble eyes deserve better frame rates than this.

I expected a revolution, but instead, I find myself missing the stability and the sheer "finished" feel of my previous hunting grounds. It is a good game, certainly, but it lacks that final touch of excellence that made World a true masterpiece of the genre.

And Capcom, I shall say it once more: The lack of a certain pale, electric gentleman is an oversight that even my Longwood cannot ignore.

Final Verdict: 7/10 – Entertaining, but I prefer my hunts as smooth as my silk sheets.

To preface this, I have been playing MH since Tri. The original Tri.
I'm surprised by this game in a positive way. The way people talked about it made it seem like Satan himself, but it does have some redeeming factors. Rise being available on PC also makes this game irrelevant however. If you want MH, buy Rise/Sunbreak, and thank me later. It's an okay game, and a mediocre MH in the end.

Some weapons, like the Gunlance, got a nice make over. Really fun to use now. Overall the combat feels pretty good. The entirety of low rank and most of high rank is way too easy however. 
Some of the new Monster design are also quite nice, including the introduction of a brand new group of them, the squidies, is welcomed as well.
It's also a very pretty game...if your rig is a monster. Mine is, buuuut...going to the bad points...

It doesn't look much better than World and has no excuse running this badly on most systems. My old Laptop was able to run Iceborne on very high and now struggles with Wilds. The steam deck just dies, while World, again, runs fine. It's pathetic. And this is after the performance patch.

Another thing I am baffled at is how they cut so many armor designs compared to the previous titles. Usually there are two design per gender (4 in total), and now there are only 2 in total. Fashion Hunters on the brink.

The open maps themselves are a mixed bag as well. The desert is fine, and the scarlet forest is pretty, but the rest is completely forgettable. The Oilwell Basin is a good idea, but completely underused and Wyveria? It's the ruins of a ancient civilization with building blocking out the sun...so its a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ circle without much Colour to it. Boring.
The story is forgettable , and both Low Rank and High Rank have almost the same structure, with the same monsters being fought in the same order. High rank does have a random MH4 plotline thrown in for like a quest and then it was forgotten about. Friendly reminder that frenzy monsters are in this game, I don't blame you if you forgot.
There are also not that many monsters to hunt, even after the updates. Some of them also seem like a copy of an already existing monster. Rey Dau is just TEMU Astalos. I even exclaimed "Is that a f-ing Astalos?" when it showed up in a cutscene.
 Guardian Monsters are just C/P so having Rathalos and Guarding Rathalos etc. is pointless.
The Flagship, Arkveld, has to be the most forgettable monster in this series. Especially after the two Rise Flagships being amazing

There's more but I save my breath here. Get it if you have nothing better to do, but if you want MH, get Rise/Sunbreak.

After an entire year, the game *finally* runs at more than 15 FPS and has a Final Boss Of The Story with a craftable armor and weapons : an okay game, a bad Monster Hunter - and sadly, I expected a Monster Hunter;

The gameplay is fun and all but *way* too easy, resulting in a BIG problem in term of vibe and setting imo.

Monster Hunter was always about how monsters were forces of nature, dangerous, and how humans lived in settlements while the world felt wild (lmao) and perilous.
Here, you can find one of the dozen of spots in the forest, shoot a flair up and have a UberCamp delivered immediately with no reaction from the monsters - they MIGHT destroy the camp if they walk by later but just pay some points and it automatically pops back up.
Sorry but the sense of danger is kind of out the window when I can literally cook a meal during a fight and not have any drawback.

With World & Wilds, Capcom decided to have a waaaay bigger focus on the story - and by that I mean having the story actively being in the way of playing the game.
I'm not going to talk about the story itself too much because, honestly, it didn't have that much of an impact on me - but the fact that the CORE GAMEPLAY feels almost secondary during low rank is weird.

Honestly, I think one of the core problem I have with the game can be explained with one quote from Our Hunter : [spoiler]By my own order, I'll slay Zoh Shia[/spoiler]
Cool quote. *Really* not the licence or even game it should be in.
We spend the ENTIRE game having Alma give the authorisation to start the quest - which, in setting, is accurate and understandable because she IS our handler. 
But because Capcom wanted A Cool Moment™, they decided to go against that core concept of the Guild/game world.

Writing this review also made me realise the "human power-creep" present since World, and how it changes the setting and the vibe
- In MH4U, you were part of a caravan and helped villages with their problems while searching for The Plot - you're a Hunter, one of the category of people capable of being safe where monsters walk free ;
- In World, you're part of an expedition to discover The New World and one of the FIRST CUTSCENE is how there are monsters LITERALLY AT THE GATE of the base but somehow, they never bother attacking it ? The Handler walking around unarmed and getting "attacked" (she never actually get hit tho) during multiple cutscene doesn't help the "is there really any danger ?" vibe. Also, the starts of the DLC makes it feels like you just build an entire basecamp in barely a few days without any issues in, again, the middle of an unknown continent.
- In Rise, you're basically a Naruto character - I'm certainly not the first one to make the parallel but it feels accurate : both series, at their beginning, were about strategy and trying to out-manoeuvre your opponent but ended up with "The Protag Is So Cool And Awesome And Will Explode Everything"
- And finally, Wilds. You can literally walk out of the main camp of the local and see a Large Monster 5s after. Gemma and Wagner, 2 of the engineers, can just show up in the middle of the map and say "HEEEEEEY, YE WE SET UP OUR CONTRAPTION IN THE MIDDLE OF THE ECOSYSTEM WITH A LOT OF MONSTERS - RIGHT HERE, HEY, YOU HEAR ME". You can fire a UberCamp without any problem. Cooking in the middle of nature without attracting any unwanted attention. For a game that was supposed to be focused on the ecosystem, the STORY is but the monsters themselves almost don't feel like "Large Weird Animals" anymore but just "Boss walking around and waiting to get attacked"

Performance is still a massive issue. The team are doing great working through it, but if you like testing your skills in speedrunning the monsters, ~40 fps gives a 20% damage boost for bows due to how logic is tied to FPS, and basically if you want a free 20% damage bonus you can just play really really choppy and it feels just terrible. Framegen doesn't make up for the fact those frames don't exist and make the game feel clunkier and less intuitive. Dunno if this affects the other weapons, but anything that uses piercing or multiple hits like LS Helmsplitter are all affected and get more ticks at 38 fps. 

The early story is quite railroaded, but it acts as a tutorial for all the systems as you gradually unlock them, and properly integrates most of the systems like our auto-farm is actually explained through a quest which helps the world feel alive. 

Artian and Gogmazios weapons are universal pieces of gear across all weapons and are the best weapons you will find for every purpoise, which massively narrows your grind and for me undoes the feeling of building my own gear as it's collecting tokens to exchange from a  magic cauldron as opposed to feeling like I'm commissioning a weapon from the blacksmith with parts I've hunted.

Depending on how you like, the artian weapon system is less of a grind than decos in World/IB, but remove a lot of the variation in the grind and for me I would prefer the monster weapons being the best and artian being a patch-up as they also look REALLY bad for the most part, and layering weapons is kinda awkward so it's a lot of faff just to not wield toilet bowl dual blades or a bow with 5 strings for no reason...

The game itself is really really fun. Weapons feel good, they are generally very fun, and monsters don't feel cheap with lots of variation and amazing dance-style fights to enjoy. It's just a shame that the design philosophy of going for universal weapon upgrades that discourage fighting different monsters, and the general performance issues make the endgame a bit of a chore. It's still MUCH better than World endgame, but that's not really saying much...

As a long time Monster Hunter fan I couldn't be more happy with Monster Hunter Wilds since launch. It was certainly a rough start with optimization however I've been playing on a cheap laptop with medium to high graphics with little to no issues outside of the first few days after each major title update. The replayability feels so much more fluid than both World and Rise with the jump in difficulty spiking quite high post HR 100. Whether you're new or not this title does feel far more easy and the story isn't as long since it basically concludes in low rank. Nothing too bad as this kind of 'downside' feels redundant to complain about as previous titles have been similar. The borderline seamless multiplayer sessions are very nice however I do find link parties to crash more so than just having a hunter friend lobby so do keep that in mind. The large monster roster is possibly the 3rd most diverse in the entire series as well and breathing new life into older monsters with improved fights, looking at you Gypceros. For $70 you're going to get so many hours worth of your money and we haven't even received the G-Rank/Master Rank expansion. As someone whose played since 3U I can confidently say Monster Hunter Wilds earns its spot in the top 3 titles among 4U+GU and Rise. The weapons and focus system can indefinitely make learning combos far easier than previous games and the wound system is extremely busted I do have hopes that the expansion will make these systems more impactful in a difficulty matter. In the end whether you're looking for a game to play with a small group of friends or prefer a solo experience I highly recommend Monster Hunter Wilds or even any of the other games that are available for purchase World, Rise, the Stories Trilogy. They're all fantastic games that offer drastically different experiences around the same precedent; hunt, forge, learn, repeat. And a final message to clear any outstanding questions, yes Monster Hunter will feature monsters in it.

Maybe adjust the weapon you're using or different tactics, gear, or consumables. Then it comes to the poorly tuned enemies. [b]TLDR:[/b]
- Recommendable, but just barely.
- Nowhere near as engaging and immersive or epic-feeling as World.
- Easier to get into than Rise in terms of mechanics and monster difficulty due to some very noticeable streamlining for better or worse.
- To this title's credit, the linear progression early on is extremely frustrating because the core gameplay itself is actually enjoyable.

[b]The Bad:[/b]
The story (if that's what it can be called) is garbage. The dialogue in said "story" is garbage. The NPCs (except for the merchants) and their poorly cobbled roles in the "story" are all garbage. Even some of the side quests are nonsensical garbage. Being constantly roped around by those very same garbage NPCs into a train of seikret riding tours and walking simulators with said garbage story and having to listen to their garbage dialogue is maddening as hell! It's annoyingly linear, literally and figuratively, for hours and hours on end unless you manage to get a break and just explore on your own. But then of course your HR is capped, so you have to deal with that along with other limitations until the "story" is almost complete. Even having to constantly deal with Alma pushed my patience to the limit. You have to get through at least 4-5 chapters of this ridiculous garbage before the "story" finally starts wrapping things up, stops leading you around by the nose, and actually hands you the bloody reins so you can really play the damn game without babysitting a bunch of pointless NPCs.

Yes, I realize how many times I repeated the word "garbage", because I found myself constantly cursing at how aggravating these production and UX elements were done, so anything else would be bound to be censored. And honestly, due to the manner in which they put the player through such an exceedngly exasperating user experience, it deserves that degree of severe criticism.

[b]The Good:[/b]
Now, finally. With that out of the way, if you have the patience to get through all of that mind-numbing, cliche, campy, nonsensical garbage (and it's going to take A LOT of patience), the game itself is actually good. The size of the world, the map designs, the environments, the visuals, the variety of monsters, as well as the monster fights themselves and the mechanics therein, are all great!

There's the occasional crash, especially when trying to move the camera around during panoramic shots or while riding and harvesting at the same time, but so far it's been less than ten times in all the hours I've played at the time of this writing. In terms of performance and stability, at least on my machine, it's been pretty good.

This game has a ton of potential to be fun but right now its just not...

So first the good: The fighting can be very fun at times, every weapon has its own fighting style and the potential for crazy combos and explosive specials will have you experimenting for hours. The wound system is clever. The armor and weapon forging can get very addictive, theres tons of different options and they are all genuinely unique. 

Now the bad: By far the worst part of this game (and also the easiest thing to fix) is how tanky the monsters are. You can easily spend 15-30 minutes (no exaggeration) just wailing on one monster. Secondly, the monsters will all run from you 4-5 times per fight and you have to chase them down. Every hit knocks you flat on your ass and it takes 5-10 sec to get back up, so some fight can feel like your just getting ragdolled and stumbling around more than actually fighting. So after all that when the thing doesnt drop what you need you want to put a hole though the monitor. Theres lots of other smaller things: The game doesnt run great, it doesnt particularly look good either. The story and characters are flat and one dimensional. combat feels sloppy, sometimes you need to hit the button 4-5 times before the game registers the action. theres no lock on, your hunter will just swing in whatever direction they are pointing with no regard for the monsters position. you have to eat constantly or your max health and stamina are lowered. youll need to sharpen your weapon 3-5 times per fight. it all gets very tedious. the UI/hud is confusing as hell and the controls for a lot of stuff will have you on your mount, running from the monster while you fumble around in your item menu.

All in all, once the addictiveness or the armor and weapon forging wore off, my desire to play the game completely evaporated.

Don’t play this if you're expecting engaging combat that stays fun. While the animations and attack concepts are good, the execution is incredibly mediocre just like the Clutch Claw in Iceborne

From Low Rank to most of endgame, monsters are extremely easy to crowd control. Wounds and Focus Strikes are overabundant, making combat repetitive and braindead for about 90% of the game. Endgame "fixes" this by inflating monster stats, which does let monsters express themselves more but it still feels like you're a demi god slaughtering helpless bunnies because even with all these adjustments, hunters still feel overpowered with too many tools. It does feel less braindead... atleast.

Whether it's worth the high price is up to you. The game does some things well and some not:

Fashion / Character Creation: 10/10 (I LOVE this)
Soundtrack: 5/10 (I just rarely heard good tracks in this game but there are gems in it)
Locales / Areas: 6/10 
Characters: 3/10
Story: 3/10
Traversal Mechanics: 2/10 (Atleast in previous games I had a more intimate connection with the areas 
                                             around me thanks to this, not just about knowing where to go)

Quests (Outside Main Story / Events): 1/10
Sidequests: 10/10

After all this the game still has a crapton of microtransactions... sigh

For those who crashed with AMD card, try lowering the Max Frequency using AMD Software. The card was automatically overclocked causing crashes in manay games. The more demanding the game is, the more frequent the crash will be. I have it crashed on different games, even some indie ones. But yes, since MHWilds is a quite demanding game, it did crash more.

You can visit the offical site of your card manufactor to check what should the max frequency be. If there are multiple frequencies and you don't know which one should be used, just pick the highest one. For example, my 7900XTX has the max frequency set to 2965MHz automatically while in the website it is 2525 MHz instead.

I've had Monster Hunter Wilds since its release. I own Monster Hunter World and Rise, as well as their respective DLCs. I put 450 hours into World, and about 100 into Rise. When I tell you this game has had many ups and downs, it's 100% in truth.
On release it was a hot mess to the point where I gave up on it half way through the story just due to the sheer unplayability. The monsters would completely jank out, the textures were broken everywhere, and the bugs were mountains.
As of where it stands now. Capcom has done a lot of work to fix Wilds. The monsters have much less "laggy" actions, and in most cases are very readable on what they're going to do. The environment no longer randomly turns into a pile of nintendo 64 textures. 
Beyond that however, Monster Hunter Wilds has issues in comparison to the previous two titles. When you compare Wilds to World, it has completely lost its identity as a "monster hunting" game. There is no "hunting", the autopathing takes you directly to your target, and the scoutflies know where the monster will go before it has even arrived there. When you compare Wilds to Rise, it lost the unique mechanics that were introduced that made every weapon feel customizable and unique to each player. Wilds ended up a strange middle ground where the combat overall is spammy. I have been an Insect Glaive main since it came out in Monster Hunter World, and Wilds out of the three titles to include it so far has a significantly spammy playstyle. Adding in the occasional wound pop like any other weapon, and you've got a pretty repetitive combat. I've also noticed in my recent time of hunting Gogmagzios and Arch Tempered Arkveld that the monsters themselves have extremely predictable move sets, most combats even following the same exact patterns including when they move to what locations. This itself is significantly different from the previous titles, monster fights always felt "fresh" where with the current highest tier fights I find myself feeling exhausted and frustrated when I have to fight them over and over instead of feeling like a proper "challenge" due to the level of repetition involved. 
Final note is, the story. Monster Hunter has never been the best story driven game out there, far more about the whole hunting the monster than learning about the characters. So I understand that this was a new thing for the franchise. However, the story is painful in the, sitting there waiting for the gameplay to happen kind of way. There's too many scenes where you sit there twiddling your thumbs waiting for dialogue to end, to do a 10-ish minute hunt, to go through another story segment longer than the hunt you just did. Capcom tried to make an impressive story, and honestly I think it did the exact opposite of its intended goal. It was too much of a slog, and too little gameplay or interactivity during it to make it memorable.
Overall, it's not a bad game, I would still recommend it to someone who has interest in the Monster Hunter series cautiously and likely to someone who has enjoyed previous titles, but I do genuinely have high hopes that the upcoming DLC will do rather significant changes to the game to make it feel as exciting as the previous two titles.

It's gut-wrenching to watch your favorite franchise die in real time. 
Bought it day 1 on PS5 to avoid the optimization issues and came back for round two here once all the updates were dropped and it was on sale. Every mechanical and design decision this team has made since the launch of Monster Hunter: World has continued to strip this franchise's identity for parts. This game is where these decisions finally come home to roost, where every mechanical inconvenience that once served to build tension and weight to actions has been abandoned for Capcom's typical brand of feather-weight stylish action slop. The world of Monster Hunter, once an interesting and breathing world of thoughtful ecological design, has been completely undermined by strange pseudo-science fantasy nonsense. The worst part? Knowing that this was always bound to happen when the series found western mass appeal and only being able to watch helplessly.
I love Monster Hunter: World. I like Monster Hunter Rise. I don't dislike playing Monster Hunter Wilds. But this series has lost everything I initially fell in love with and this game is the culmination of that. I feel numb having to come to terms with this, but it's over, and I'll likely never love again the way I loved Monster Hunter.

I really have a blast, great game from my personal perspective, I like it better than MH World.

Now, I get the criticism from previous entries MH fans: the game is easier, has a lot of QOL additions, and the combat has evolved with new mechanics. But I think that the game received a lot of undeserved hate. From my own perspective, all the changes and additions are good. I like the new formula better, it is more rewarding to me, it fits my more casual way of gaming:

- The graphics are beautiful: nice character looks, beautiful world with lots of gorgeous locations
- The combat is great, more dynamic and rewarding than in World in my opinion, it feels better, and it is less frustrating.
- The story is not bad, has some length to it and gets cheesy at times, but they put a lot of work on it, and I’m less enclined to skip dialogues than I was in World.
- hunts are faster (I’d say 15-20 min)
- the game is less grindy (can get an armour set and a weapon from a monster in about 3 to 4 hunt)
- the mount system is really nice: you get to travel fast from A to B looking at the world and your character, it’s like cruise control, I really enjoy that, it fits with the big world, and makes for beautiful scenic rides.
- there are a lot of cool QOL features
- if you prefer the old MH gameplay, you can always do it: you don’t have to use focus mode in combat, you don’t have to use the mount or the autopilot all the time, etc. But as I said, I like these additions.

Some things are not there anymore which one might miss compared to World, but it is not an issue for me: there is’nt the prehunt ritual of looking for monster footprints, monsters are always visible on the map. The levels are less intricate and vertical, but it doesn’t really change the gameplay loop: you still go from A to B when the monster changes location, only this time you don’t have to struggle for 20 minutes in a maze, instead you enjoy a dynamic poursuit ride in gorgeous locations, so in my mind big improvement for little loss.

Performance is average: have a beefy computer - RTX 3090, game is stable enough at 60fps with high graphics in 4k, so it performs similar to many other games.

I’d say, go for it !!!

I loved World and Rise considerably. I did everything there was to do in both those games, postgame and all. Now that the base game content cycle is out I can safely say this is a really poor entry in the series. Disregarding the genuinely horrendous performance, (same engine as RE9 and RE4R and RE8 and Rise and like 20 of Capcom's most recent games by the way), the new systems introduced are just boring. The game does not take advantage of them in any capacity. The wound mechanic in the base game is so poorly balanced until you hit 9 and 10 star monsters. The fix? Reduce their frequency. I could have told you that in the playtesting phase. The small monster herds roaming the landscape are cool to look at but don't do anything different than having like 3 Aptonoths in the background. The Ceratonoths can get struck by lightning during the Sandtide! Ok. Really neat detail. This means nothing otherwise since it has no gameplay mechanic attached to it and actively makes my game drop half the frames when they're on screen. The environmental changes could be really interesting but basically do nothing. The Apex Monster comes out and there's a really mild debuff (or in the case of the Ruins of Wyveria literally nothing happens), it looks cool but runs poorly in every instance. The large monster groups are both extremely annoying and not taken advantage of from a hunting perspective. When this was announced I thought it would mean quests where you need to take out a whole herd of individually weak monsters, but no, there's an alpha target that you just dung pod to make the rest of them go away. What's the point? The only time the game felt like a true Monster Hunter game in terms of actually having to react to and learn a monster's moves and mechanics was during the Arch-Tempered and Gogmazios (whose Alatreon-esque mechanic isn't even that crazy you can just brute force Dragon element the whole time) fights. My favorite fight in the game was the Arch-Tempered Arkveld, followed by 9/10 Star Gore Magala, and to a lesser extent Gogmazios because you need to actually respect the moveset and triumphing over them felt rewarding. I actually DIED to them, so I actually wanted to fight them. Introducing like four or five different forms of gacha mechanics for making builds by the final title update is also honestly concerning. It feels like a band-aid solution to the previous ease with which to make builds, so they decided to do gem AND talisman AND weapon AND armor gacha in the base game (previously a Master Rank level mechanic outside of like the Kulve Taroth raid in World which as such only had weapon and gem gacha), the fact they're pulling out all the stops in the base game before the expansion makes me worried they'll either run out of ideas and recycle the mechanics exactly or come up with some even more player unfriendly mechanic to compensate in Master Rank. The monster designs and the idea of the Constructs are really cool and allow basically any monster from any environment or game to be added for no reason. This is really good! Give me good fights, it doesn't need to explicitly make sense from a story perspective. The fact they're still able to throw cool decisions in gives me hope, but we lost so much in terms of mechanics and gained essentially nothing. There are only two elder dragons (by technicality of all things, originally one on launch WHICH YOU COULD ONLY FIGHT ONCE AND HAD NO DROPS BY THE WAY, I'm astounded none were stealth dropped like in Rise's title updates), there is no player home to customize, there is (after an update) a really REALLY inferior version of the endemic life pet system from World, and we didn't have a Gathering Hub and by extension a real home base until a month after launch. Any good change was only made in a post-game update. The original shipped game lacked genuinely so much stuff that I don't know what they were expecting people to think, or if Capcom just believed they could pass this off as a whole experience, and was overall just boring. They made Monster Hunter boring. That's the worst indictment for this type of game and its developers I can think of. Never have I had to endure so much just to find a game okay, Powered through clunky controls, unintuitive mount pathing (invisible walls everywhere), and the poorest excuses for boss phases where the monster just straight up runs away to a completely different part of the map, sometimes by phasing through the walls or ground, only to come back to the same area later, with little to no differences in its attack patterns or movements. The "cutscenes" take the worst of both worlds, refusing to commit to free roam exploration or cinematic camera angles, and instead electing to trap you on your mount walking at a snail's pace, unable to control your character despite all the game's regular hud being on screen. The only interaction is looking around and pressing A to look at the scenery. Button prompts regularly lie straight to your face, and the game overall just seems built upon minor inconveniences for the player. 

One of the few positives is the interesting variety of armor/weapons/builds that you can forge with the loot of the monsters you defeat. Learning the best weapon combos is rewarding, although the boss fights are so washed out both in regards to the colors and animations that there's not much precision to be had. If you're familiar with souls-like games you'll quickly notice all the ways this game fails to imitate the quality those games bring to their enemies and combat. 

The customization is cool. and the ability to take on monsters with your friends is also fun, although the actual systems surrounding multiplayer lobbies and environments is another spot of unintuitive mess. I've been forcing myself to get through this game to play with friends, and there are ways to enjoy it, but you have to the world's biggest optimist to look past all the flaws in the core mechanics of the game that don't really seem to go away.

Monster Hunter Wilds is a game that was unfortunately rushed before it was ready at its initial release ultimately launching with a slew of technical and performance issues, however many of these issues have since been fixed and/or improved (not all of them mind you) which isn't an excuse but is welcomed. With technical things out of the way the actual gameplay as a complete package is very good, there are areas I personally think could be improved a lot however.

Starting with the story, it felt very "anti-Monster Hunter" in its pacing meaning there was little reason to ever engage with the main gameplay loop of Monster Hunter until the story was over as you could typically craft a weapon and armor after one hunt which was more than enough to carry you forward. This left the story feeling more like a linear railroad then a Monster Hunter game, the game does go back to a more traditional gameplay loop after the story though so it wasn't a large issue for me ultimately I just wish the story was more harmonious to how Monster Hunter is typically. The actual story itself was enjoyable and I thoroughly liked the exploration into the worlds ancient history I have no notes on the writing.

My next issue with Wilds is how it's leaning into the trend of completely removing friction from anything that isn't directly hunting a monster, this was also an issue I had with Rise but it was leaned into a bit differently in that game. This lack of friction effectively removes an entire quarter if not more of the franchise that I have personally come to actually look forward to quite a lot. Without the friction I no longer have the quiet downtime moments in between hunts of peacefully running around a locale admiring the amazing amount of detail that was thoughtfully put into it while gathering ore, bones, or other materials, I haven't had to do this more than probably 10 times in my nearly 200 hours of gameplay in Wilds compared to once every few hours in World, GU, 4U or 3U. Gathering isn't the only place friction has been removed though, Wilds showers you in monster parts to the point that when Gogmazios was added I was able to make his entire set of armor after 2 hunts with parts to spare. Additionally Wilds also completely removed the joy of getting a rare gem or plate from a monster as you will very regularly get investigations that give you a guaranteed rare item. This all leads to crafting armor and weapons becoming more of a checklist than a chase which is very unsatisfying. Friction isn't an inherently bad thing in a video game if it's added with intention it can add a lot of value and satisfaction to an experience and I hope some of these things get reevaluated in the future.

Now for a small list of more minor gripes I personally have with wilds.
- Monster Hunter games feel best with a lot of hit stop on powerful attacks, both Wilds and Rise have a minor amount of hitstop which makes every hit feel less satisfying but its lack hurts especially on things like True Charged Slash on Greatsword, look at World as a baseline for satisfying hitstop.
- Insect Glaive does not play well with charging based mechanics, it makes the weapon feel stiff, slow and immobile which is the antithesis of what Insect glaive is in every other game. (it also hurt my hand trying to play Insect glaive optimally against things like the Arch Tempered monsters)
- No Gammoth????
- Artian weapons as well as Gogmazios weapons are the worst type of grind with astronomical luck required to get just ONE weapon the way you want it, an endgame grind should take effort but it should be actually feasible with a clear end in sight.

I don't want to leave this review only talking about things I don't like so to finish off here's a list of the standout things I like about Wilds.
- The artistry in the map design is absolutely GORGEOUS and awe inspiring, it brings me back to looking into the distance in 4U or GU seeing a giant massive world that couldn't really be explored except this time you actually can explore it pretty thoroughly. I also love how every locale is interconnected and you can run from the Windward Plains all the way to Suja.
- I'm a huge fan of how special Wilds makes Elder Dragons feel by making the couple that are there such a huge deal
- The new monsters are all excellent and a couple have taken spots in my top 10 favorites.
- Nata lets you know he heard from the others (very useful).
- Wilds added the foundations for underwater combat genuinely returning to the series which gives me a great deal of excitement for the future.

Ultimately I have personally enjoyed Wilds despite its flaws. As a final rating I give it an 8.2/10 its a solid and enjoyable video game and when it does something well it does it VERY well but probably wait for a sale because of the technical issues its had.

this game had a lot off issues early on (still a few minor bugs I assume since I have run into one) but it has come a long way stability wise. 

After playing it for 31 hours at time of writing I can whole heartedly reccomend this game, at its heart its still much the same as worlds but with a few key differences that I've noticed which I will now list.

Number one, there is no more clutch claw, well... not exactly anyways. whilst you cannot grapple onto the enemy anymore you can still use it to do things like grab a material from afar.

Number two, the Palamute from rise makes a triumphant return in what I can only really describe as a chocobo like creature called a Seikret, and man is this trusty and noble stead a MASSIVE help, from giving you quick terrain traversal from riding upon its back, your mobile supply box (not item box :c) and carrying a second weapon for you to use, this noble beast is gonna be one of your best friends in these wild and crazy lands.

Number three, a more personal asside I'm sure but I find your palico friend is much more helpful in this game than wilds. They have a wide range of abilities to use ranging from being able to deploy a cage of flashflies, deploying shock traps, status effect clearing abilites, even their vigorwasp game is more on point in my opinion.

Number four? I don;t expilicitly remember this being a thing in any MH game before, being able to actually aim your melee strikes. Just simply hold down LT (seriously play this game on a gamepad, you'll thank me later) and this little reticle pops up and where that reticle is-is where the general spot yer swing is gonna go.

Number five, wounding has changed a bit. you still hit the same spot many times and it generates a wound, but now rather than it being just a temporary damage boost to hitting that same spot more, wounding a target generates a spot for a focus strike that when you hit it with a focus strike it instantly breaks that wound but for a massive hit that will even sometimes ground the target.

it is for these reasons and differences to what I;ve played before in other MH game that I can confidenly give this one a solid 

9/10! the only reason its not higher is because theres no MR and no proper story expansion yet, but be patient my friends! they had to take a good bit of time fixing this one but theres always an expansion that brings MR along with it so its coming!

till then, I say hunt on friends!

"After the sandstorm comes the new spring, when life is restored to all the earth."

If Worlds didn't click with you, you might actually love Wilds for whatever reason.

Forget the optimization and hardware requirements. The first 5 hours of this game will have this terrible washed out look no matter what you do.

However, once you get past the desert, a luscious spring of evergreen awaits.

When a monster starts running away, it's no longer frustrating. Now, you hop on your stallion and herd it into a corner. You feel so powerful when chasing the injured beast, like "where do you think you're going?"

If World is the Sherlock Holmes of Monster Hunter, Wilds is the John Wick.

The Chocobo in this game is like that moment in Halo: Reach where you get ambushed by Elites and Carter says "Five and Six, clear the hole" and Jorge closes the door behind you guys as if saying "We're not trapped in here with you, you're trapped in here with us."

They say Worlds takes 50 hours to get good, but I've been having a blast with Wilds since the moment I started.

Also Laura Post is goated as usual.

[B] I changed this to a positive review [/b] cause the game has some of the most enjoyable combat I've experienced, pretty good map design that makes the environment feel alive, superb attention to detail that might seem insignificant on the surface but adds up to elevate it to a level where it's hard not to respect the love and craft that went into making the game. The issues I had initially can be resolved with (way too much) tinkering and reddit/steam threads, which if you successfully do, gives you a pretty amazing game.

I left the initial complaints below for context.

[hr][/hr]
Insane how a game with graphical fidelity this low, can be so demanding. The post processing makes it look like a dull eyesore, the textures are laughably low quality to the effect that it almost looks like an asset flip, the game feels blurry af even with anti aliasing, upscaling, and motion blur disabled, The only thing I found to combat the awful visuals was forcing HDR on and doing some heavy tweaking, and using FSR native AA to make smaller detail look presentable.

Too bad that the game is so lacking in quality and the 'story' so boring, cause the combat is genuinely fun and the animations for movement and attacking are super good. I wanted to like MH World, but the direction locked combos felt too uncomfortable, so this game fixing the one issue I had makes me feel even more bitter about the state it is in. I have to remember to save every 5 minutes too, because talking to NPCs has a 10% chance of freezing the game, especially the blacksmith.

Also, a minor thing, but why the hell can't I change most of my character's appearance at will? It's not like they're actively storing my save data on their servers anyway alluded to by the fact that I lose progress after every crash. I already paid for the damn game, why are you trying to sell me such a basic feature multiple times??? Same vibe as the BMW heated seat subscription.

Monster Hunter Wilds nails a lot of what I loved about Monster Hunter World.
The gameplay feels familiar in a good way — heavy, satisfying combat with smooth weapon flow. The visuals are gorgeous, and the atmosphere genuinely feels alive thanks to dynamic weather and reactive environments.
But it’s not all perfect.
The story feels forced, like it’s pushing itself forward instead of unfolding naturally. Progression also feels rushed, moving you through gear and story beats before you’ve had time to settle in. And the rideable animal, while convenient, actually hurts exploration. It makes it feel like the game wants you to sprint past the world instead of experiencing it.
Overall:
A beautiful, lively world with great gameplay, held back by pacing issues and design choices that discourage exploration.

Buff hammer.

As for performance, that isn't something reviews really exaggerate. I am aware this is the game post-optimization and it still being unable to hit 60fps on medium graphics on a decent setup is shocking.

One step forward yet one step backward when compared to World and Rise. However, it is the best base game experience as a package compared to the 5th gen games. The difficulty progression of the game is quite questionable as the game is very easy through it's main story, but quite hard for it's endgame quests. The difficulty progression made a lot of monsters that are not the apexes of the areas nor other high-tier monsters (i.e. TU monsters) a bit forgettable unless fought in their event quests. Wilds' version of Rathalos is the best as he now has an exclusive song with the Forest and Hills motif of the old-gen games.

Focus mode is the better implementation of the clutch claw and tenderizing mechanic in MHWorld Iceborne but with how the main story is balanced, trivializes a lot of early game monsters. This is better compared to Iceborne's addition and implementation of clutch claw which affected the base game though most beginner players in World will still need to learn how to use it first. The wounding mechanic feels the most balanced and the best at the higher level quests i.e. 9-10 star quests. 
 
Overall, not my favorite Monster Hunter experience as the Sunbreak DLC is still my personal favorite, with Iceborne as my second. Will update this review when I have played the DLC in the future.

Monster Hunter Wilds Review (a.k.a. “I Came, I Saw, I Got Sat On by a Lizard”) 

So I booted up Monster Hunter Wilds thinking, “Yeah, I’m the apex predator now.”
Five minutes later, I was getting folded like laundry by something that looks like a dragon crossed with a bulldozer and anger issues.

Welcome to Monster Hunter.

First off, the world? Absolutely gorgeous. The environments are so detailed that you almost forget you’re about to be emotionally destroyed by wildlife. One second you're admiring the scenery, the next you're being chased by a creature that clearly skipped breakfast and chose you as brunch.

Combat is back and just as satisfying—and by satisfying, I mean:

You spend 20 minutes tracking a monster
10 minutes fighting it
30 seconds getting absolutely obliterated because you got greedy

Classic.

The weapons feel amazing though. Whether you're swinging a sword the size of a small car or bonking monsters with a hammer like you're late for work, everything has that chef’s kiss impact. You don’t just hit monsters—you emotionally damage them. Until they emotionally damage you back harder.

Also, the monsters? They don’t just exist—they have personalities.
Some are chill.
Some are aggressive.
Some wake up every day and choose violence specifically against you.

Multiplayer is where the real chaos begins. Nothing builds friendship like:

Screaming at your friend to heal you
Watching them run the wrong direction
Then both of you getting flattened simultaneously

10/10 bonding experience.

Now let’s talk difficulty. This game doesn’t hold your hand. It slaps your hand away and says, “Figure it out, champ.” And honestly? Respect.

Final Verdict:
If you enjoy:

Getting bullied by dinosaurs
Crafting armor out of your enemies (petty and fashionable)
Yelling “WHY WON’T YOU DIE?!” at your screen

Then congratulations, this game owns your soul now.

Score: 9/10

This review is not about the performance, too many are. While those are relevant complaints, there is always an underlying "this is a great game but it runs like sh*t therefore not recommended". This isn't a great game, this is an above average action game and a sorry excuse for a monster hunter. I am a MH veteran, and I can safely say this game strays way too much from what made the series fun for me. 

"Wait, what do you mean, isn't that just hunting monsters!?" - no. Well, yes, but also, no. It is about hunting, but in a haulistic sense. It's everything that goes into a hunt that made this series what it is. Learning about a monster, what it does, what it is weak to, what items to use against it, and strategically making decisions on what to bring against it, how to approach it.

Older games were simple, more limiting and also kicked your ass if you didn't engage with its mechanics properly. Every action was commital and carried its weight. Even a weapon like Dual Blades asked you to be incredibly careful when and where you attack. Monsters weren't just your glorified punching bags that keeled over if you so much as looked at them menacingly, and while updates have gone a fair way in making more difficult monsters, other games didn't go from 0 to ♥♥♥♥ in the endgame. I know, other base games are also easy, wait for G rank! Is what people will likely say. Which is also innacurate, because other base games still had you foraging for resources and managing them like a professional hunter bloody would. There was an ebb and flow, it felt like you were just a hunter out on your own living life in this world helping out your community but ultimately just being independent. Want to make sure you got the mega potions and items ready for next hunt? Go out and forage for it, it serves as a nice break between really intense hunts. How many hunts in a row can you go without needing to do that? What about locating the monster and being aware of the maps and your enviornment? 

You could claim this isn't challenge or difficulty or fun, it's just tedium, and if you feel that way, that is your prerogative. To me, it helps with immersion in the world and creates a nice pace. Maybe you just finished an intense 40 minute hunt or multiple hunts that really stressed you out, now you can go and forage some ores or pick some bees or get some honey or herbs to just relax a little before your next hunt. Go to your farm, fry some steak, relax. Talk to the NPCs, get absorbed into the world, sit, have a drink. It could be the coziest and most stressful game in the world, and both halves to this whole helped to create it for what it is. 
Not to mention even low ranked require you to upgrade your gear, sharpen your weapons correctly and learn the monsters. After reaching 90HR in Wilds, I went back to 3U and to my surprise low rank Barroth carted me. I was careless and didn't respect the game. 4U is even more fu*ked up than that, 4U low rank makes the high rank of Wilds look like a joke. 

Well, nearly all of what I've mentioned has been removed. Now you have a near infinite supply of everything you will ever need while your Bird uber brings you to monsters while you assumedly are meant to watch netflix on your second monitor until you get there and then you're on a conveyor belt of monster after monster. You can go mostly crazy stupid outside of a few more endgame threats (I hear Omega is ♥♥♥♥♥♥ up, I haven't fought him yet to be fair, and if he is, cool). After a while, I just grew numb from it, completely apathetic. It doesn't help Low-rank is a B-grade disney movie rip-off of a plot that wastes your time and insults your inteligence. 

For most people this will probl'y be a good little mindless action game you boot up for 2h on a sunday afternoon after you shove your 10 month old infront of an ipad watching paw patrol or some ♥♥♥♥. For me, I had to go back to FU, P3rd, 3U and GU to feel anything again.

Having given it some thought, and having 100%'d it, Monster Hunter Wilds might be my least favorite game.

I'm an old hunter. I started with Monster Hunter Tri back on the Wii and I love that game to bits. But I'm not some jaded old gen guy who hates everything new; Monster Hunter World is another of my favorite games, and though it had its issues, namely the story and pacing, the game was still Monster Hunter at its core and I still enjoy playing World to this day. I'm saying this as context when I say that Wilds was one of the worst gaming experiences I've ever had.

Almost nothing about this game works. I say almost because there are legitimate improvements in Wilds worth recognizing; the ability to use your binoculars to check the sizes of monsters is something that should have been in the series years ago, as is the ability to skip the 60 second end of quest timer. These are good changes. Unfortunately, they're subsumed by a cavalcade of baffling game design choices.

Let's start with the least of my issues; the pacing. To the game's credit, the pacing of the game is, at first, pretty good! Monster Hunter has always had issues with getting players into fighting big monsters and this one does it the fastest, and after the intro you're already fighting a boss. However, afterwards the game's pace rams straight into a brick wall and doesn't pick up again until the third act.

Until then, you are railroaded on these truly - and I do mean this - TRULY boring walk and talk scenes where nothing happens and the characters talk about the most boring things imaginable. If these dialogue segments were written well, I wouldn't have minded so much, but almost every bit of dialogue in this game is stilted and uninteresting to listen to, much like the story. It's not the absolute worst story I've ever had the displeasure of sitting through, but it is still a boring, melodramatic mess that cuts into time I could have been spending slaughtering the local ecosystem for new boots, and while yes, the cutscenes are skippable, the frequent walk and talk scenes are not and you have to sit there on your mount as the characters around to drone on about nature or whatever. Have fun!

Let's get into why this game really grinds my gears. The gameplay of Wilds was gutted from previous games. Hard. Monster Hunter's combat has always been about proper positioning; knowing where you should be to hit a monster's weakspots while not getting killed yourself. It was simple, it was engaging, it was unique and it was fun. Monster Hunter Wilds introduces new mechanics that remove all of that; Focus Mode and the wound system can effectively stunlock monsters to the point where they are a non-threat and almost laughably easy to kill. You don't need proper positioning when the answer is always aim for a wound and pop it with focus mode, which means you can basically just wail on a monster and kill it in five minutes. What made Monster Hunter's combat so unique has been replaced by hack and slash gameplay and I think that is unbelievably disappointing.

Continuing from monsters being a non-threat in this game, returning monsters from older games have been slowed down significantly or gutted of their moves to the point where they're hardly the same monsters anymore. Even new monsters have attacks that just leave them wide open for several seconds, and it comes off as unnatural and video game-y, which is ironic considering the story of Wilds. This is a nitpick, but for as much as folks like to rag on Capcom for reusing assets or recoloring monsters in older titles, this is the game that feels the most like I'm just fighting the same monsters over and over with different body shapes.

Wilds is also painfully easy. I was killed one time during the entire low rank campaign, and it was because of a camera issue. Monsters simply do not do enough damage to you throughout low rank to justify getting new armor past, like, the midway point, it's pathetic. Preparation, a key component in older titles, has been streamlined to the point where it's almost unnecessary. Your cat friend can heal you, cure you of all statuses the instant you get them and buff you, removing any urgency you may have getting poisoned or burned. Luckily the cat can be turned off, but the random endemic life that does the same will always be out in the open for you to use, removing the point of half of this game's items.

This is the big one: Your mount will automatically take you to your quest target, removing the need to learn your environment and understand a monster's behaviours. This is one of the core components of Monster Hunter and it was completely removed: Actually HUNTING the monsters. Now you just always know where your mark is, you go to it and you hunt it. Yes, it's more convenient, yes, it's easier to get into playing the game but it removes so much nuance and charm and uniqueness the older games provided to the point where Monster Hunter Wilds doesn't even feel like Monster Hunter anymore.

It feels like just another action game, its individuality and personality torn to shreds and its mechanics broadened to make Capcom as much money as possible from as many people as possible. And it worked. Capcom will never learn from this because it sold like hot cakes. They will never make another Monster Hunter game again, but they will keep making games like Wilds devoid of what made Monster Hunter, well... Monster Hunter.

And I think that is a genuine shame.

Having crossed the 100 hour mark, I believe it appropriate to give a review. To get the big question out of the way, the current state of its performance is that my laptop with an Nvidia 3050 RTX GPU and 16 GB RAM can handle this game fine, and I can have the most impactful lighting options set to medium quality and texture/model quality set to high. The main shortcoming of how it downscales is that shadows have noticeable artifacts and noise, it clearly wants raytracing enabled. Not great for screenshots, but is not obvious enough to hamper readability of monster attacks. My experience is that the graphics auto-config tool is good about ensuring your settings will have nice framerate so you don't have to spend 10 minutes wrestling with your game operating at 5 FPS.

I do not use frame-generation, I cannot speak to how that impacts the experience. If you use an AMD video card, you may have more problems than what I experienced.

[b]If you hail from previous Monster Hunter games...[/b]

Monster Hunter Wilds will be crazy easy with your past experience, and Focus mode is an absolutely cracked buff. Once you start getting into HR 7+ the monsters will start pushing back and provide due challenge. Arch-Tempered Arkveld's super-enraged state makes Savage Deviljho sound like the voice of reason by comparison. A lot of the apparent easiness, outside of just the player character's handling being buffed by Focus mode, is the absolutely massive gathering radius a player has courtesy of the hook slinger, and the Seikret mount's auto-pursuit means I can grab stuff like Godbugs and Might Seeds while still moving directly towards the hunt target, resource attrition takes much longer to set in. Compared to World, or even Rise, be prepared to be let down by the environment. It lacks the interesting buffs you can collect in Rise and the act of exploration is nowhere near as "tangible" for lack of a better word, as it is in World.

[b]If you're new to Monster Hunter...[/b]

Ignore anything I said about the game being easy, I'm a salty veteran hailing from Monster Hunter World and Monster Hunter Rise. For anyone else, Monster Hunter's complexity is as monstrous as its literal monsters. A Soulsborne comparison is reasonable for how combat works in Monster Hunter, you press dodge (or block) to not die and hit the monster until they die. The primary difference is that you have basically no i-frames unless you build for it, and you will want to go in thinking of your weapon as basically your character class. There are 3 weapons that provide a weapon and a shield, including classic Sword and Shield, Lance, and "Charge Blade" (it's a sword, and a shield, and an axe? And it actually makes sense visually???) and they play profoundly different from one another despite the superficial similarities.

If I were to suggest anything, once you visit the starting town you'll want to visit the Training Area as soon as possible, if you run around the edges of the town you will find it sooner or later. There you can pick a weapon (the game automatically gives you a level 1 version of every weapon type) from a box and beat on a training dummy which will show damage numbers and trigger mechanics. Press all the buttons and see if there's any combos that aren't being explained to you in the upper-right hand side of the HUD. You can talk to the Palico (cat) in the Training Area to configure the training dummy to actually attack you too, so that way your judgment about what weapon feels right to play with is closer to real combat. Also, don't be afraid to wiki-search how things like "Evade Extender" or "Critical Status" work, or even your own weapon combos. While many work as advertised, for some the English tooltip isn't quite enough to explain the nuances.

The other suggestion I would have for a newcomer is [b]do not under any circumstances ignore Optional Quests.[/b] They quite often have permanent and significant upgrades as rewards for completion.

But of course before you hunt you make a character first. "Wait, there's a GAME attached to this character creator?!" applies to Monster Hunter Wilds. Depending on how into it you get you can easily spend an hour or two just getting your character made to spec. And you have a talking humanoid cat (Palico) who you are expected to customize and name just like a player character!

[b]What both newcomers and veterans can commiserate on...[/b]

The story is probably the biggest weak point of Monster Hunter Wilds as an actual game (not including nitty-gritty balance issues as that is always a moving target), it starts out fine and does its job of introducing the explorable zones in an orderly fashion with a narrative connection between them. Trouble comes when it wants to play up its cutscenes and dialogue, which takes the game hostage like it's a Final Fantasy game but it never really gets past "A problem? What could be behind this? Oh it's a monster! Because this is Monster Hunter and of course it is." Cutscenes are often played right before you fight a 6000kg fire breathing chicken or whatever so it necessarily has to be pretty shallow. There is also a notoriously disliked character named Nata, who I half-jokingly refer to as the "plot parasite." I don't hate him like most people do but I don't fault it either. His presence in the plot actually makes a lot of sense in the early to mid game, but without spoilers the most I will say is, oh my God, towards the end of the core storyline he ends up being the victim of some really bizarre characterization that is not explicitly wrong as a storytelling device but is very underdeveloped and consequently makes the character's personality develop in a way that is incredibly unnatural, be it for an adult or youth. However, it does ultimately arc back towards something acceptable, it doesn't stay cringe forever.

[b]The overall takeaway...[/b]

That said Monster Hunter is a game about... hunting monsters, of course. It unquestioningly delivers on that front and has never been so accessible to newcomers. Some parts of that make the game shallow in a way that is casually obvious, the most glaring one being the mount which very much dumbs down the process of exploration and literally auto-moves to the target you have set on your map (and is automatically set to the monster you are hunting!) with full pathfinding. The greatly enhanced mobility of the player character's attacks is much more tastefully done and is the primary strength of Monster Hunter Wilds. Also, once you've completed the core storyline, you get access to a transmogrification system and there is a limited ability to dye armors, Fashion Hunter is the true end-game of Monster Hunter Wilds and if you're big on that you can get 100+ hours out of the game like I have.
