I'd rather give this a neutral rating but since I have to decide I'll go with negative. I enjoy the game and will keep playing it but there are some things that just keep bothering me. The expansion may be able to address some of those but not everything can be fixed. I'd say post Title Updates Wilds has the better endgame than the base games of its modern predecessors but that wasn't exactly a high bar to begin with. Is the game worth a purchase? Honestly Rise and Sunbreak together offer all in all the better experience if you don't mind graphics and multiplayer. However if you're looking for multiplayer and your rig can handle it I'd take Wilds over World and Iceborne.

For the things that bother me:
- Graphics: it's true they patched the performance to some extend but there are things they couldn't patch like the blurriness, the fog and the brutal post processing. The game lacks visual clarity and as consequence it can be hard to tell what's going on. Their pinnacle of design is literally an all grey zone with terrible lighting where you fight a grey monster.

- floaty controls: Capcom has multiple motion cap studios and it shows. They felt like they had to animate everything but as consequence your character feels unresponsive thanks to additional intermediate animations. It's especially bad after getting hit when you're on the ground and try to get up just for some struggle animation to play. People would say old generations were clunky and had idiotic extra animations but even with things like the superman pose the controls itself were crisp and not as muddy as they are in Wilds. While it doesn't affect me frame gen will introduce additional input latency making it even worse.

- Weapons: Some are good, some are bad, at least one is so awful you could just remove it. I hated the counter focus in Rise however there you had alternatives. In Wilds they are forced upon you. I don't even get the point of 14 weapons when everything is a counter stick. A lot of people will point out that focus mode killed positioning but as someone who plays a lot solo I never need to position anyway as I'll just wait for the monster to attack me and then counter it. For me the biggest loser is the Insect Glaive even post patch as Capcom doesn't seem to know what it should be. Hammer is now "what if we turned that youtube hammer slowmotion Tigrex video into a weapon" however offsets become pretty awful once the monster have high hp and buildup resistance and outside of them at least slower weapon have a hard time dealing damage. I wouldn't even say hammer is gameplay wise bad cause I enjoy it but all the extra steps you have to do just to get the same damage as other weapons in total is frustrating. All in all if hunters weren't so absurd strong maybe monsters wouldn't require all this extra crap either.

- Seikret: They wanted a combination of Palamutes and Wirefall but made both strictly worse. Palamutes were more interactive and they weren't forced upon you. You could always bench them and use Wirebug movement as alternative. Something similar isn't possible in Wilds at all and maps are clearly designed around the Seikret. The worst is the Seikret pickup is just bad. In order to pick you up the Seikret has to physically run to you from its current random location while having collision with the monster, can be knocked over by monster attacks and will jump over ledges so the pickup will always fail at a ledge. How long this takes and in what direction the journey will go? WELL you will see. It works in the early game but once monster deal damage it can flat out cart you if you accidentally use it.

- Ecology: I don't care about corpses rotting. I don't care about the pack monster interactions that requires you to stalk them for 10 minutes. Endemics were a nice touch in World but you would see them while traversing the map. Captured endemics you could place in your room and interact with. Here the Seikret strikes  again as it walks you just past all of them with the maps not being designed around seeing endemics anyway unless you search for them. You catch them? They are just an entry that's it. I don't want ecology to be removed as whole but it should be a nice to have and not the main focus. The game is called MONSTER HUNTER and not some pseudo realistic bird watching simulation.

- Maps: the desert is unnecessary big with anything other than the north-eastern area being awful to fight in thanks to slopes resulting in sliding and mini jumps when dodging. Forest and Oilwell are OK but Cliffs and Wyveria are two awful maps as they are cramped, visually monotonous and full with ledges, slopes and other crap.

- Too much random grind: this used to be a franchise about killing monsters and using their skin for armour and weapons. The materials being random drops was plenty honestly. Sure some parts were hard to get but hunting the monster again and again was the game. Now? Random decorations, random talisman, random weapon parts, random weapon bonus, random weapon set bonus. You want to try a new weapon? Too bad now go through absurd amounts of randomness cause the crafted weapons cannot compete with the randomised ones. An appealing grind has progress but here you just bash your head against the wall and hope you will eventually get what you want which can be never.

Please note that I do really enjoy this game, even with some of the changes in this game compared to other modern MH games. 

However I AM leaving a negative review as the game pretty much requires an SSD to run smoothly, it won't matter what's in your rig. You WILL have "loading periods" where all of the models for the pc, npc, armor, weapons, etc. will be just triangles for a good 2-4 minutes, on the higher side if you're unlucky.
Yes, the game appears to run smoothly with shorter "loading periods" on lower settings (i play on medium as, gameplay > graphics, for me always)- it's not that my pc couldn't handle the highest settings, but this shouldn't be an issue at all, considering the rising SSD prices.

Overall, i'd say if you are a die hard monster hunter fan like me, its worth it. If you are a beginner and you want to get into monster hunter, just go play world.

Edit: It's been many weeks since my last review and I'm still logging in almost daily hoping to see any changes, but the performace is still garbage.

Each new update brought performance issues but were more or less ironed out in the following weeks. The February performance patch, however, completely wrecked the game for me and there hasn't been any more patches for over a month. More than 900 hours invested into the game and it suddenly became a stuttery, unplayable mess.

Writing this having just finished the low rank "campaign" --full disclosure I don't usually play MonHun games but my brother loves them so I got this purely so he and I would have something to play together.

I knew going in the story as such wasn't going to be too deep, but it did kind of surprise me that I breezed right through the low rank stuff without needing to get help or really optimize, but the lack of grind that early on was welcome.

The only real complaints I have are:
-Some of the controls in the default mapping are awkward on mouse/kb (R+E for example)
-Driving your mount with separate keys for accel/decel rather than W for go release W to stop is annoying
-Some mechanics exist but aren't introduced well, seeming to expect you to have played the series before

Overall it's pretty fun, but for my taste I'd love to see a game in this style and world with more depthful story (I know MH Stories exists, but they play differently/have different visual style)

This game could have been so much better. I'm by no means a Monster Hunter purist, but the way this game plays has drained all fun for it for me. I even waited until recently before picking it up, because I'm no fan of the drip feeding of content, but in the end I probably will not even experience most of the newer released stuff.

I love Monster Hunter Rise, so unlike some veterans I'm not opposed to more fast and fluid gameplay. Wirebugs are a way superior system than Focus mode though, I felt it was pretty clunky and not much fun at all. Add to this the handholding in the story, the Seikret go-to-target auto mounting, and the way the game handles exploration, I couldn't help but feeling bored after reaching High Rank.

They tried to make the world more detailed, seamless and open world, but it comes of as having way less charm than it's predecessor's maps. This is probably in part due how the gameplay loop consists of pressing up to automatically go to the monster, slay and repeat, so there's no incentive to learn the maps. Due to the auto pathing the maps also feel way more restricted, compared to Rise.

The story is unfortunately also not very inspired and feels more like some boss rush where control is constantly wrestled from the player. I honestly was also not very fond of Monster Hunter: World, so maybe the main line games are just not for me, but I think there definitely was potential that it learned some lessons from Rise to elevate the experience, unfortunately for me that is not the case.

The game looks quite nice in certain parts, and I had no serious performance issues. But I had some issues with texture popping in way too late in some cutscenes making a lot of the more scenic scenes look pretty bad.

All in all a rather disappointing 30 hours for me. Maybe I will come back and the game will click for me all of a sudden, but I don't hold my breath. So if you, like me, loved Rise, I cannot recommend this game.

Having played Monster Hunter: World and Monster Hunter: Rise, I had high expectations for Wilds. While it lives up to the Monster Hunter legacy, it feels like it could have been so much more.

The world is mesmerizingly beautiful, with ever-changing weather and diverse regions. Riding my colorful bird and watching my palico’s clumsy antics are fun, but compared to World, Wilds feels like it’s missing some life. I can’t pinpoint exactly what, but the sense of vibrancy isn’t quite there.

Combat, exploration, monsters, wildlife, weather conditions, different regions, mini-games, and side activities are all interesting—but not enough to justify the $70 price tag. At a 30–50% discount (or more), I’d recommend picking it up. For those wondering whether to leave World/Iceborne behind and jump into Wilds: don’t. Wait until bugs are fixed and new content (DLCs) is released.

Pros:

🌍 Beautiful world with dynamic weather and diverse regions

🐦 Fun mounts and palico companions

🐉 Classic Monster Hunter combat and exploration

Cons:

🕳️ Feels less alive compared to World

💸 Not worth full price at launch

🐞 Bugs and lack of content updates (for now)

Overall Recommendation:  
Worth playing eventually, but only at a discount and after future updates. For now, stick with World/Iceborne until Wilds matures.

After 300+ hours, I can safely say this is one of the most addictive entries in the Monster Hunter series.

The core gameplay loop is as satisfying as ever—tracking, learning monster patterns, and finally taking them down never gets old. The combat feels weighty and rewarding, especially once you really start mastering a weapon. There’s a ton of depth here, and the game does a great job of letting you improve naturally over time.

The monsters are a highlight. They’re well-designed, challenging without feeling unfair, and each hunt feels like a proper fight rather than just a damage check. Multiplayer is also a big plus—hunting with friends or randoms adds a lot of replayability.

That said, it’s not perfect. Some grinds can feel a bit excessive, and there are moments where the pacing slows down, especially when farming specific materials. Performance and minor technical issues can also pop up depending on your setup.

Still, if you enjoy action RPGs with deep combat systems and rewarding progression, this is absolutely worth your time.

TL;DR:
Great combat, awesome monsters, tons of content. A bit grindy, but that’s part of the charm.

Monster Hunter Wilds released in an inarguably terrible state. The performance issues were inexcusable, and even on a fairly powerful rig like mine, I still struggled to maintain a decent framerate. Many also experienced consistent crashes, stuttering, low-poly models appearing, bad pop-in issues and basically any performance issue under the sun. To add to this, the title-update model Capcom has been using for the past few games is merely an excuse to release an unfinished product while still charging full price for it. While I enjoyed Wilds at launch, I would not have recommended anyone buy it at that time. However, in its current state, I would recommend you pick up this game: especially if you are looking to get into Monster Hunter for the first time.

Capcom has almost entirely fixed the performance issues that once plagued the game as well as fleshed out the roster, and they have added many amazing hunts. Archtempered Arkveld alone makes Wilds one of the better Monster Hunter games in my opinion. 

There is no better game for new players to enter the series from; Wilds is by far the easiest game in the modern Monster Hunter era. While that is a detractor for veterans, it undoubtedly eases new players in. Couple that with character controls that are crisper than they have ever been, and you have a recipe for clean, fun gameplay people of all skill levels can enjoy. 

If you are still skeptical, wait for a sale before picking up Wilds, however, I believe this already decent game will become much, much better when the expansion releases. If Capcom can release another expansion on the level of World's Iceborne, we are in for a treat. I'd rate Wilds in its current state a solid 7/10.

This review is being given through the lens of someone who already loves Monster Hunter, but waited an entire year for Wilds to receive its final update and get fixed with multiple patches. If I were to leave a review based on my initial experience though, it would have been an easy thumbs down because the game performed horribly and had an incredible lack of content. I'm honestly glad I waited, because it's really good now and if you were on the fence before, it's a great time to hop in.

Capcom should have released the game in this state though, to be honest. There's no denying or forgiving them for that. It's sad that as a huge MH fan, I (and probably many others) had to wait for such a long time to play the "real" game and I feel especially bad for early adopters who brute forced it anyway during launch because it was a lot worse then than it is now. Totally unfair to them, especially for the price.

Even today though, in its "better" state, the game still feels a bit thin and has some performance issues. For example, there's a noticeable lack of end game content, masked by a boring grind that will probably have the gap filled with this game's version of "Iceborne", whenever that comes out, and it definitely still runs pretty bad, even on high-end hardware without the use of several AI tools (DLSS, frame gen, etc).

Despite its flaws though, I think Wilds strikes the perfect balance between Rise and World. Maybe a hot take, but this is my favorite combat in a MH game so far. It's not too fast or too slow, plenty of quality of life changes have removed the "boring" parts of hunting which keeps the pace going strong and I know this is controversial, but the focus/wound system really resonates with me. I enjoy this combat a lot.

Some people say this is the "easiest" MH so far and I agree, to an extent, but I also think a lot of that sentiment comes from experienced MH veterans who already gained enough knowledge from other MH titles to easily succeed in this one. I can definitely agree that Wilds is more accessible to newbies overall, but it still has that classic MH learning curve that many people can't overcome. I wouldn't say it's an "easy" game by any means, it's just that the core MH playerbase has gotten better over the years, so it's hard to make the game feel difficult for them without totally alienating new players at the same time. Every MH game going forward will probably feel "easy" if you've been playing them since 3DS to now.

Overall though, Wilds is an undeniably good game that's unfortunately being managed by a company who doesn't seem to care for it very much, but I am still having a blast. Even almost 150 hours in, I can't seem to put it down and I can't wait to see what the inevitable expansion brings. Remember, World didn't have the greatest reception either until Iceborne dropped and then it really delivered. Same thing with Rise and Sunbreak. People seem to forget that. Capcom has a formula now and they're not changing it with Wilds.

tl:dr
The game was bad, now it's good, but it still has some issues and gaps to fill that will probably be addressed with the upcoming expansion.

The story is absurd, the characters are insufferable, and the mechanics have been dumbed down to where an IGN game journalist could make progress. Be prepared for railroaded training wheels for the first 6-8 hours before the game allows you to hunt monsters how and when you want to. 

For the core mechanics of the game.... it's Monster Hunter for the low attention span, duel monitor-watch-your-favorite-show-while-you-play. Everything is on auto pilot. Auto movement, auto explore, auto monster tracking, auto crafting, auto material gathering, auto goods harvesting. 

Capcom created a large beautiful words to explore but was so worried your little brains would get bored in the 30 seconds it took to get from point A to B that put in fast travel points everywhere on the map for you to place. The entire game is base on getting you back into the action as fast of possible. 
As an end game mechanic or system, that would be convenient. 
As a core principle of the game, I feel it cheapens the experience. 

All in all, it isn't a horrible game. It's just not a very good monster hunter game. A Monster hunter Lite* or an arcade version of previous monster hunters. It can be fun and I will continue to sink some hours into it but absolutely not worth the $70 asking price.

Monster Hunter Wilds scratches the World itch while adding new features and QoL improvements.

New mechanics such as counters, rushes, and more keep the player engaged and involved in fights better than previous games. Similar great camera work and animation stops on big hits recreate the weightiness of succeeding extensive combos and hitting monsters at just the right time. 

You won't find as many spacing setups as World and even less than Arise, so the name of the game is getting up close and personal for the most part. Wedge beetles exist, but are far less user friendly by being out of sight and in inopportune areas. The camera constantly fighting to stay on the monster doesn't help much either. Air control somehow feels worse than World.

Lance is more pressure based. 
Gunlance now has two wyvern fires and shelling is built more into combos. 
Greatsword now has a parry and counter system, something much needed and rewarding. 
Sword and Shield is still the omnitool. 
Long Sword combines facets of World and Arise with better meter building and leveling combat systems; however, personally I found it less flashy than previous games. Once you learn combo transitioning, it does play smoother than World.

Overall, beyond the lackluster performance on launch, Monster Hunter Wilds is a great successor to World. I do think they need to rerun pretty much all the limited time free challenge quests due to the onset of new players who waited until performance issues were patched.

Much better than at launch. Performance has improved a lot—no more texture pop-in on my end, and the game feels significantly smoother overall.

Core mechanics are heavily simplified. Focus Mode is a godsend, especially for Greatsword users, but thankfully it’s optional if you want the classic feel. The changes also make the game more approachable, especially if you’re not as fast as you used to be.

That said, the game is very forgiving in the early to mid game. The real challenge only shows up in the endgame, where things finally start to get intense.

Overall, Monster Hunter Wild is a mix of accessibility and challenge. Easier than older titles, but still has its frustrating moments—and likely more to come with future updates.

The biggest pain point I've had since day 1 is that there seems to be some kind of global cooldown or input delay after performing almost any action. As a result, the game often feels unresponsive, even when using a wired controller in offline mode. What makes this especially frustrating is that inputs are sometimes registered during the cooldown and executed afterward—but not consistently. For example, after sheathing your weapon, there’s a long period during which you can’t do anything except move. If you try to equip slinger ammo during that window, the game usually ignores the input, but occasionally it will equip the ammo after the cooldown, equip and then immediately unequip it, or equip it only for you to accidentally unequip it because the game wasn’t responding when you pressed the button. The whole system feels clunky and unreliable, and you end up overcorrecting for the game’s sluggish response time.

The camera also needs work. When using the auto lock‑on camera, it frequently gets stuck in walls, floors, and environment structures. Using an item from the radial menu while locked on always causes the camera to snap to a random angle and remain unresponsive until the item animation finishes.

Monster Hunter is easily one of my favorite franchises, and I really want to see it succeed; but it feels like this game was rushed to align with the 20th anniversary of the franchise. Capcom has released several titles recently before they were ready, and left customers to languish in bugs and performance problems.

Maybe some of these issues will be addressed in the upcoming expansion, but I’m not optimistic. Given the poor reception Wilds has received so far, I get the impression that Capcom will cut their losses after the expansion releases and move on to the next title.

Monster Hunter Wilds triumphs in its monster fights and combo-heavy gameplay, something that I rather expected after deeply enjoying the hunts from Worlds and Rise. I was a bit bummed when I learned there wasn't a new class in Wilds, but I still enjoyed picking up my comfort picks (SnS and Lance) and trying out the new combos available. The monsters were very well made and mechanically challenging across the board (i'm pretty slow at learning boss patterns). Gore Magala was probably the most insane fight to me, the first one that I had a lot of trouble dealing with. But besides that, the game was overall great. The progression from low to high rank felt good. The jewel system was familiar and fun to min-max. Farming out gear sets felt rewarding and not too tedious. And the glam system was well-made and much appreciated.

On the flip side, I do have my personal complaints about the game. First, I wish Capcom made a bigger leap with multiplayer lobbies and progression. One of my biggest gripes with Worlds and Rise was that multiplayer/coop was NOT seamless, as I need to constantly invite friends and then remove and repeat if we were to progress through the story together. It seems like this style of choppy progression is what stayed in Wilds, and I personally feel it breaking my immersion (especially when a cutscene ends with the new monster aggro'd onto you and THEN the coop flare becomes available ugh). Additionally, despite the game looking visually better, I had a lot of performance issues running this game on a high-spec PC. Even on mid-low settings, I would receive lag spikes and coop displacement, which kind of sucks. I would rather the game look the way it did in World/Rise and run smoother rather than Capcom trying to achieve the highest graphics.

For now, I'll be waiting for the rest of the title updates and expected expansion. There's obviously no real endgame right now, so I haven't spent too much time grinding out jewels and pushing my rank. I hope they can release something repeatable and rewarding like the Guiding Lands from Worlds. But yea, overall great release. Some things could be better but the game does well at what it does, and I thoroughly enjoyed my time with it.

My Thoughts on Monster Hunter Wilds

Monster Hunter Wilds has found a special place in my heart, even if it leaves a slightly sour aftertaste. The main culprit? Its clunky and overly complicated multiplayer system. Playing with friends shouldn’t feel like solving an ancient riddle or requiring divine intervention, yet somehow it does. Half the time you’re struggling to connect, the other half you’re getting disconnected for no clear reason. It can test your patience more than any monster ever could.

That said, the core of the game is something I truly love.

The hunts, the journey, the steady climb from struggle to accomplishment — that’s where the game shines. Every victory feels earned. There’s something incredibly satisfying about taking down a powerful monster after a long, punishing fight. The kind where you’re barely standing, your resources are gone, and your heart is racing.

One of the funniest realizations I had was discovering that having NPCs on your team actually made the game harder. Suddenly everything clicked. “So that’s why it felt like I was playing on nightmare difficulty!” And yet… I kept doing it. Not because it was harder, but because I loved the challenge. That thrill of not knowing whether I’d succeed or end up getting dragged back to camp by my Palico never gets old.

In a strange way, I’ve come to prefer these solo adventures. Just me, my trusty Palico, and companions who struggle alongside me without questioning every decision. Every win feels personal. Every loss feels like a lesson.

Still, I can’t help but wish the multiplayer experience were smoother and more accessible. If they ever simplify it, the game could reach an entirely new level.

Until then, my advice is simple: give it a chance. It’s not as bad as some people say. Just because something is difficult doesn’t mean it’s unbeatable. Every monster can fall — I’m living proof of that. I made it through even the toughest encounters, unknowingly increasing the difficulty, and still came out on top.

And honestly? That makes the journey even better.

[h2]Yikes! That monster hunt was wild, right Alma?[/h2]

Now, I know what you're thinking: what could a man possibly hope to gain by spending so many hours of his short, pathetic life playing a game he doesn't care for in the slightest? Why, nothing, dear reader. Nothing but the satisfaction of knowing his integrity as a hobbyist hater has been preserved. Indeed, I'm not one to simply rag on games - nay! I dedicate my life to playing them, studying them, completing them so I can hate on them more thoroughly! For you see, I fancy myself a bit of an ecologist. Much like substances and the U.S. government, I believe that each and every game franchise must have been put on this Earth by their developers in order to fill their own wonderfully unique niche. Have you an inkling of where I might be going with this? What game but Monster Hunter should I play to feel like a monster hunter? Can't answer? I'll tell you: it isn't Bayonetta.

I get that most people here had never heard of this series before World, and I'll be the first one to admit that the old titles were anything but perfect. I'll also mention that I did actually enjoy my time in the Forbidden Lands somewhat. Call it Stockholm Syndrome if you like, but if only Steam allowed for mixed reviews, I might have been more lenient. That said, the fact that Capcom made me miss Freedom Unite of all games is a matter of slight concern. Maybe I'm getting old, maybe I'm just burnt out on this series… but, by the time I was reaching the end of my High Rank journey, one specific question kept buzzing around my head, inhibiting my enjoyment of the experience like the proverbially ill-placed Vespoid:

[i]If you spend seven years of your life crafting a big, beautiful video game world, then design all the mechanics around having to interact with it as little and as briefly as possible… what is the point of it all?[/i]

To look pretty? Sure, the game looks pretty, at least for the mathematically-minded among us. I'm sure glad I can count each individual pore on Gemma's nose as she stares at me, expressionless, because we blew our entire animation budget on the low rank cinematics. And the voice acting, dear Gog. I ended up switching the audio to Brazilian Portuguese so I could focus on reading the subtitles. I can't quite put my finger on it, but something about this generation doesn't look right to me. It's less tribal fantasy, more Netflix remake. Perhaps Rise had the right idea.

What about the fights? I'll confirm that the combat does feel smooth as butter, as least if you play Greatsword like I do. Focus mode might be a little too forgiving, but the end-game monsters were issued with a crate of compensatory Dash Juice, so it evens out. The other weapons might not have been so lucky in this department, but overall, balancing seems consistent with its previous iteration: Longsword is good at everything, Bow is a legal cheat code, and only friendless nerds play the Light Bowgun. Hammer mains hang somewhere near the corner, doing the Soulja Boy. If it ain't broke, don't fix it, as they say. It would have been nice if the environmental designers followed this same philosophy. Remember all the little slopes and vines and ledges and walls you can run on? When was the last time you pulled off a slide attack? Mine was in 2024.

The levels were a stride and a half in the wrong direction. I can look back to my stay in the Plains with some fondness, though I cannot say the same for the rest of the game's locales, which I found aggressively drab and uninspired. This feeling only doubles when the Fallow season arrives, and triples when I'm forced to hunt in the Oilwell Basin because of yet another giant ape. Man, some of these maps suck. I grit my teeth and shoulder bash through the most boring volcanic area I've ever seen, only to be rewarded with a glorified corridor with a black-and-white filter tacked on top of it. I get it, guys, it's a helix, a DNA strand. Very clever. It's still an awful map. And you know what makes these maps even more awful? The fact that you need your mount to traverse them. Great. We sacrificed the complexity of World's Great Forest for a taxi on legs.

The reason I'm being so harsh isn't that the game is bad, it's because it isn't the game I want it to be, and I bet there's a subset of players out there who share a similar opinion. It's like every single wish we had over the years had been filtered through some weird monkey's paw-shaped self-pleasuring device which run on arcade coins. We wanted our paintballs back, so paintballs are back, but the monster's location is always displayed on your screen, so they're pointless; We wanted a better story, so we got a marginally better story, with high-fidelity cinematics that we still can't experience alongside another person. We wanted our crafted gear to stay relevant, so we got, like, four different gacha roulettes based on killing the same three monsters. The game is an effigy of completely unrelated mechanics in the shape of the old Monster Hunters. Innovation and convenience above all, like watching your favourite flower patch being turned into a parking lot. A game with great fighting and not much else. And where does that leave us, the ones who liked the hunting part of the game? The part where you pack your whetstones, set off into the unknown and get robbed by a bunch of cats? That'd be the door, old timer. And swing by the gift shop, while you're at it.

I've been playing since mh3, I understood the complaints at launch since this game is expensive, but at this point the people who are still complaining just have to be new gens because this is not even kind of out of the ordinary from this series. 

First off, the performance. I do have a pretty strong pc, but I play on almost max settings with a buttery smooth 144 fps, if you're complaining about performance after all the performance updates, you just gotta upgrade your pc. You can't have a pc as strong as a ps4 and expect the brand new AAA game made for ps5 to run good on your pc, also if you have an amd card that's just on you idk what to tell you. 

The other thing about the people complaining is every single complaint was also an issue with mh world, which at this point is the most glazed monster hunter game. The performance, the small amount of content, the low difficulty, and the amount of changes from the previous games were ALL complaints at the launch of mh world. If you pay full price for a monster hunter game at launch expecting it to be at the same level as iceborne you just have to realize that's not going to happen and that's NEVER been how this series operates. I started playing about a year after the game came out and there's already a good amount to do thanks to title updates. The second the g-rank expansion comes out, everyone will immediately go from saying this is the worst mh game, to the best one. It happened with mh world and mh rise.

TLDR: everyone who's complaining is just complaining that this pre-expansion monster hunter game isn't on par with the ones that already have the g-rank expansion. Play this game without expecting expansion level of content and it's very good. Wait for the expansion to come out if you can't justify the price since it doesn't have as much content yet, which is probably what I'd recommend to people who haven't already played the older games since there's plenty of content on there that's new to you.

When I first started the franchise at MHWorlds, and subsequently moving onto MHWilds, the core essence of gameplay was simply about tracking and slaying massive monsters. However, as the hours piled up, I came to a realization. You are moving through an environment that doesn't revolve around you. Adapting rather than controlling, learning rather than conquering. The endless cycle of hunts started to feel less like progress and more like a reflection on effort, failure, and persistence. Somewhere along the way, it slowly and quietly flips the premise on you: The hardest and most destructive of monsters isn't the one roaring in front of you but the one within. Your impatience, arrogance, and your need to dominate in the cloud of frustration as you battle. In that moment, the hunts stop being about victory. But rather, something closer to self-destruction.

I really like this game. I consider it a great improvement over Rise and World. I will continue playing it and will buy the DLC.

My GPU is the listed minimum requirement. Surely this means I can play the game on low settings, right?

However, as of right now writing this review, all my 126 hours in this game were played on around 25 FPS. I can tolerate this, I grew up on slow computers, but I cannot recommend this game to anyone in this state. I have all my settings on the absolute lowest, I've tried everything from community made config files, playing on the lowest resolution, to various GPU settings and external tooling and using DX11. Nothing. It pains me to leave a bad review on a Monster Hunter game, but I'm sorry, the game runs like complete ♥♥♥♥.

I could play Monster Hunter World, not only on max settings, I also had custom shader mods installed. Yet this game on all lowest settings, while looking like a PS2 game, cant even reach 30FPS. I was hoodwinked into buying this game because friends assured me they "fixed the performance". I can only imagine how bad it was before the patches.

This game may be worth its price, but it is not worth several thousands of dollars to buy a new PC. Nor will I.

Additionally, unrelated to performance, the netcode for this game is worse than World and Rise. Constant "communication error" and friends being disconnected randomly from a quest, or the whole quest party being disconnected. My first time doing the [spoiler]Zoh Shia[/spoiler] fight I had to close a popup saying "communication error" every 30 seconds. I'm not on wifi, ethernet cable directly to my router. My internet is good. Never had these problems while playing any other game.

It is so incessant that I genuinely don't feel like playing the game. Why join an [spoiler]Omega[/spoiler] quest with my friends when there's a good chance we will just get disconnected mid quest?

And what is it with every UI button having a loading screen? Just opening the game and joining a lobby is like 3 loading screens. Sending a link party request is 2 loading screens. You don't need to do that... We had 30 years of UI/UX conventions by now. You can do optimistic updates and if the invite fails you just show a notification later. It boggles my mind how a company can create many technically impressive fully featured video games yet fail at this. It is infuriating to use any multiplayer related UI element.

On the topic of UI, every list of something in this game (armors, decorations, etc) has no text search functionality. None of them are even alphabetically sorted. In some menus like the decoration melding, there isn't even a filter option. If im looking for a specific armor set I have to scroll the whole smithy and scan it with my eyeballs until I see it. Same with decorations. A billion dollar company can afford to hire someone who can code a text search and who can sort items alphabetically. 

Finally, a bit divisive maybe, but get these scripted sequences out of my [b]video game[/b] please? What is it with all these "follow X character" then you let me walk 2 meters and it starts ANOTHER scripted sequence. In most of which I cant even sprint or use my slinger to at least catch endemic life. All i can do is hold forward on my controller and NOTHING ELSE. And the so called "seikret" mechanic is way too forced. Practically every map in this game has terrain that you cannot choose to walk on, you need to get on your seikret and put your controller away as the game automatically for you does cool looking parkour. Every map. The monster is right there 2 meters away but oh no there's stupid gap between where I need to get on my seikret for automatic parkour. Either at least let me do the jumping or just don't put this ♥♥♥♥ in an [b]interactive[/b] video game. This isn't assassins creed with "press F to stab", it's Monster Hunter. Let me play the game.

tl;dr: The game runs bad. Bad netcode. Bad UI. Too many scripted sequences.

P.S: Please remove the word "thang" from the English subtitles for the palico plunderang text. You seem to have accidentally hired a crunchyroll translator. Please don't put brainrot slang in my videogame where I go for escapism.

Let me preface, I absolutely love Monster Hunter.

Monster Hunter Wilds is a fun time, especially if you’re already into the Monster Hunter games. The world feels bigger and more alive than before, with dynamic environments and monsters that actually interact with each other in cool ways. Hunting is still super satisfying. Tracking something down, learning its patterns, and finally taking it out never really gets old. When everything clicks, it’s great.

That said, the game is WAY easier than its previous entries. Performance can be hit or miss too, which kind of pulls you out of the experience, although they have made MAJOR improvements to it. It’s still a solid game and worth playing if you like the series, but it doesn’t feel like a huge leap forward. Overall, I’d give it a 7/10.

I want to play a game, not watch a movie. Out of the 7 hours I’ve played, most of it was wasted skipping cinematics instead of actually playing.

At one point, I just put the controller down and my character kept moving on its own, and I had to wait around 10 minutes because large parts of these sequences force the characters to walk painfully slow.

And the main reason I bought this game: the coop, it’s a mess. The game constantly blocks you from playing with friends. First, you’re forced to finish a main quest. Then, even after completing it, you’re still locked into another story segment and can’t leave the area. So you have to finish that too, just to regain basic freedom. And when you finally can move? No teleport, no quick way to regroup. Just more wasted time.

It honestly feels like the game just doesn’t want you to play with friends.

Performance doesn’t help either. I have a good PC, I can run Resident Evil Requiem at 240–260 fps on ultra settings but this struggles to stay around 40–50 for no good reason. Even after updates, the optimization is just bad.

Overall, this barely feels like a game. It’s hours of forced cinematics, slow walking sections, and systems that constantly get in the way. If you’re here to hunt monsters with friends, you’ll spend more time waiting, skipping, and being locked out than actually playing.

Very fun and addicting. Despite the rough release, CAPCOM managed to fix this game as a whole. Objectively this game is still worse than MH World's base game, but the quality of life added in this game makes me prefer it. It also fixes element matchups, where having weapon with correct element would be beneficial (in contrast to World, where almost all weapons must focus on raw). Wound is a broken mechanic, yet I still prefer this than World's tenderizing. Off-set & power clashes are a huge improvement to the hunt, they're very fun mechanics that rewards experienced players. Seikret is also a nice addition, combining Tailraider (auto) & Palamute (manual). Although... The exact same problem still exist, Seikret's auto-pathing is as r*tarded as Tailraider's. That said, all locales in this game aren't interesting AT ALL, most of the time you just want to auto-pathing with Seikret and get to the hunt target immediately. Small monsters aren't unique and doesn't feel alive, it severely unappealing. Big monsters almost never interact with them (other than Doshaguma), making everything feels bland and unimmersive, doesn't feel like actually exploring the nature. 8.8/10, great game but a step down from its predecessor.

Bought this game after the 50% discount and the optimization update. It runs quite fine with my GTX 1650 with the lowest setting, not really that bad. I do not recommend this game not because the graphics quality and watered down gameplay which a lot of people already mentioned. My main disatisfiers are (1) Wasteful vast world design and (2) Forced story. 

Wilds environment is really huge, I won't deny that. But all of the vastness are just "extended distance/Highways" so Seikret can fit the gameplay. The environment design is also bland, especially wyveria as the "forgotten ancient civilization." Personally, I feel more awe with old gen "Ancient Tower." You don't need high end graphics to depict a long vanished ancient civilization and a forced storyline, just use the "show don't tell" technique and NPCs' speculations.

Personally, I feel that the worldbuilding doesn't fit Monster Hunter. I thought "The Everstream" in World was absurd but this "Dragontorch" and "Wylk" is even more absurd. Maybe future DLC(s?) will uncover this narrative problem. 

With that "Wylk," "Dragontorch," and "Everforge" not even a single tribe know how to make a weapon, defensive structures, even a counter-attack strategy against all those dangerous monster? Human is not that naive. In an environment that harsh, they are more likely to be aggressive and unforgiving. They can even wage war against each other. Just take a look on history of civilizations and religions: Brutality united in separated Identities. Maybe the Monster Hunter want to write Human as a benevolent race. If that's true, then what is the point of writing "Wyveria's Sin (Zoh Shia)" if not of the innate brutal nature of human race as animal (or Monster in this game's context... maybe)?

The storyline is even more absurd. That kid MC... after he had witnessed his tribe almost being annihilated by Arkveld and how brutal Arkveld was, he still persisted on letting Arkveld live freely and "Be Strong"??? This is top tier naivety. How do these tribes even survived prior to the expedition teams (The Hunter) arrival?

More Personal and Biased Comment: Arkveld design is bad, Rey Dau fits more as the primary flagship monster. Some OSTs are decent, but not that memorable. Character creation is much better than World but what's with that "Body Type" nonsense when we are playing a fantasy game with big consideration of Biology?? Just say it as plain as Male and Female. I won't be surprised if in the future Rathalos will be titled as the "Queen" of Skies.

I didn't want to like this game and I do have some design gripes:
~I like the idea of memorizing the maps and researching the quarry. I don't like the idea of your new mount, the seikret, ferrying you everywhere on an automated path. You don't even have to worry when the monster changes zones because you can just whistle, leave to refill your coffee. No need to figure out where the monster sleeps or what it eats- you just stay on it's tail.
~I think there maybe are a few too many tools for the hunters to use. I don't really like the wound system. It takes you out of regular gameplay to jiggle the camera around and find glowing red dots. Simple weapons got a bit more complicated. I'm used to 1 gauge with switch-axe but there's two now. (Kind of.) 
~It seems like there are are mechanics that are completely useless that they were obliged to carry over from previous games. Having the psychic knowledge of knowing where every monster is on the map is overwhelming means the research-to-tracking system obsolete. You can still analyze the occasional footprint for a measly 10 ecology points. There's no point in crafting items in preparation for a hunt anymore. Hungry? Pull a bug off the wall to eat. Low on health? Pull a green bug off the wall to eat. Cold? Pull a red bug off the wall to eat. Hot? Pull a blue bug off the wall to eat. At this point why have survival elements in the game at all? It over-complicates things.
~I think the game looks bad. The sudo-hyperrealism is a stark change from previous monster hunter games. The game isn't nearly as colorful as it used to be and it's strictly an art-style thing. It's a shame because I thought World looked fine and Rise was a nice stet back into the previous monster-hunter art style.

...however...

Despite my complaints there is still a *lot* to like about this game:
~The new open-world design of the game lends itself to the ecological aspect of the game. Small and large monsters migrate as they fight over territory. The did a great job making the terrain readable on the map despite the three-dimensional layout of it.
~Despite all the tools the game is still challenging. Capcom giving players more tools means they can give different kinds of obstacles. The story doesn't warn you about the next monster you'll be fighting so general preparedness is all the more important. I found myself switching to my sword and shield to cover more elemental options and having a block against hyper-aggressive monsters. I was glad that I had some tinged-meat in my inventory when a Rathalos crossed my path. This let to a lot of interesting moments to me I wouldn't have had otherwise.
~Story is good. It continues the theme of mankind needing to harmonize with nature. Characters are good. I was surprised by all the MH4 references and continuing continuity for those in the know. Dialogue and voice acting is fine. Some people have gripes about it but I don't.
~Character creation whips ass. My cat looks good as hell. My hunter looks a little less cool but that's a personal skill issue. I like that you need to earn the layered armor and that you can look cute without having to compromise stats.
~None of my gripes about graphics apply to the food visuals good god how did they render this food to look this tantalizing what the hell


All in all, despite my gripes about all the new stuff, it's still Monster Hunter. It's a good game that keeps to the theme and can still flatten me like a pancake at high rank. I hope they learn from the mistakes in this one so they can make it better in the next one.

I think they can still improve alot like how to navigate the multiplayer aspect of the game and the farming of Talisman and Decorations. With wilds they took the hunt out of monster hunt but honestly that is okay. Coming from over 200 hours on world/iceborne across steam/xbox, wilds is a more streamlined experience. With the added mount system you can traverse maps quicker and fight your target quicker. There really is no more hunting and tracking. The mount system also adds a safety net when your in a dire situation so take that as you may. Some like it some think it makes it less dangerous. You will still get totally rocked so be prepared to get carted a lot. Combat is much more fluid than previous titles and the added wound system is a nice feature. I have tried all weapons and most of them have drastically improved. Some are a bit clunky on fluidity, looking at you charged blade, but overall all the weapons can be viable just need a good build and hours of practice. The lineup of monsters is good and excited to see more when the DLC is released. For me the elder dragon fights are the weakest part of the game. Don't get me wrong they are enjoyable, but can seem too cinematic, Capcom needs to keep it to an epic fight between you and the dragon and not a script based movie fight. The build diversity is decent, once you get to endgame, the diversity lacks a bit. I hope they improve upon that in the DLC and add more (maybe more weapons?!). The transmog system (layered armor/weapons) is a nice feature, there are some great looking armor but also some terrible looking armor. Same goes for Weapon skins. Also please CAPCOM let us re color all armor pieces, some armor piece colors can't be augmented unfortunately. The story is meh but I am really not a story focused player so it doesn't bother me too much. I just want to fight monsters with cool weapons. There are areas to improve the experience for players but overall its a fun game and again more streamlined to go fight a monster or 2 and then log off. Much more friendly to a player like me with less time to play games day to day.

After hours of enduring the game's abysmal performance, I've finally given up.

I've done every fix imaginable to get this absolutely terrible, lazy, unoptimized PC port to work. Give rolled back to AMD 25.3.1, edited the registry to try and delay GPU Pipeline timeouts, disabled overlays, tried full screen, windowed and windowed fullscreen, move the game files to another SSD, checked the game files half a dozen times, and more...

The game itself is a good instalment to the franchise, overall. But my god, it should be scrapped and rebuilt for PC because this god awful port DOES NOT WORK.

The fact Capcom have acknowledged the issue and basically said "Yeah, we'll see what we can do but no guarantees" just shows how lazy and customer-averse they are.

The Pros: This game has massive improvements to the flow of combat compared to Rise and World, with far more monster variety with unique looks and attacks, more frequent unique looking weapons (compared to World), and allows for many more cool mechanics like having a secondary weapon and an improved wound system. Likewise, collecting resources is a lot faster now due to the QOL added so you can collect resources at range. The tracks and research aspect from World didn't make a return sadly, you just kind of find a monster once and then it's always discovered; however, that works in the favor of this game because the maps are huge and could take a while to find where a monster otherwise. Though few monsters are called "Elder dragon" they have numerous monsters that are the same quality, spectacle, and feel of elder dragons from previous games.
The Cons: Most of the cons are small things and not make or break reasons on whether or not to get the game. It still has some optimization issues though nowhere near as bad as before. Switching weapons can become a forgettable mechanic due to you needing to go on your mount to swap them. The game feels a lot easier compared to World and Rise due to the wound system, improved mechanics of weapons, focus system, and ability to call your mount at almost anytime to get you out of danger; though this does make it more accessible to new players, once any player starts getting a grasp of the game, it will be very easy and require little to no prep to take on each monster up until Gog and Omega -- which will hopefully change with the DLC adding harder enemies. The palico consistently likes to heal me after I've taken a health potion and healed near full: there are thankfully an equivalent to palico gadgets you can earn, but the vigorwasp delivery specifically is the bane of my existence; I add that in part because I wasn't even aware that I was able to change the actions of my palico until recently and believed that I was stuck with only the vigorwasp delivery. I'm not sure if I missed some section talking about the ability to change palico actions but I was unaware of it throughout the main story.

A fun addition to the Monster Hunter series, and many of the performance issues have smoothed out.

I say 'smoothed out' and not 'runs well': I have a 1080Ti and this game barely does a consistent 30 fps on the lowest settings, but at least it's a consistent framerate.  For the graphical fidelity of the game, however, I'd expect the resting framerate to be much higher.  Probably best if you have a 20- series or better nVidia card before picking this up.

Wow, this is a polarizing game. I think Monster Hunter Wilds suffers from so many problems, more so than any other game in this series.  Pretty much the only bright spots in this game are the gameplay and certain design elements. There is no expansion yet, so the game ends in High Rank and that's all I can review. I've finished all title update fights so I can speak on them all.

Taking notes from World, Wilds prides itself on full immersion. This time it's actually to the detriment of the game and it's reputation. Each map is a fully simulated environment where monsters can turf war, get shocked and stunned, or fight small monsters off screen. Why? This serves no purpose besides giving modders something to showcase on Youtube for clicks. In game it tanks performance and interactions not seen by the player are a waste of time. These environments, simulated all the way down to the smallest monsters, are novel but made performance on PC so bad on launch and for 8 months after that it killed the reputation of this game. Now they optimized the game a lot better, but I don't think sacrificing performance for these meaningless additions added to the game.

Gameplay wise, just like World stuff is grounded without flashy wirebugs or super attacks, although some weapons actually got some. The single biggest additions in this game are the wound system and focus mode, both of which are terrible additions and should not have even been considered for this series. The wound system is a disaster, you strike a monster's body and create spots that allow you to do special attacks with your weapon on that deal a lot of damage and usually trigger knockdowns. This is a problem because creating wounds and attacking them knocks down a monster leading to more wounds and an endless cycle of destroying the monster. Basically the entire game before updates was easy to beat and monsters got steamrolled because of this. Later on the mechanic was so bad they just made monsters stop getting wounds as often and heal themselves quicker. Terrible design concept. Next feature is focus mode, which lets you directly aim any weapon in the game. This basically kills the need for good positioning near a monster because you can hold focus mode and adjust yourself mid swing. Besides these two mechanics the game at it's core is monster hunter so it's still fun and weapons feel good to use.

Next thing I want to touch on is the new mount, the Seikret. I need to make an entire section discussing this because everything about the Seikret just sucks. The concept and the execution are just the antithesis to everything this series stands for. The Seikret can just auto navigate you to monsters. So why is there the need to make elaborate and pretty maps in the first place? You can let the Seikret walk and go on your phone. It's disengaging and a horrible idea. Another is you can use the Seikret to save you from monster attacks. However, unlike wirebugs it's frustrating and inconsistent and will get you killed a lot of the times because the damn bird runs towards the monster randomly. Awful awful awful.

Now monster roster, even this disappointed me to be honest. I think of any of the new monsters only 3 of them even impressed me and gave me a fun fight: Xu Wu, Rey Dau, and Doshaguma. The rest are either a complete mess, or uninteresting. For the updates, Mizutsune is good, Seregios is a fun dance when not tempered, and Lagiacrus for being so hyped is just not very fun and had very questionable hip check hitboxes and a atrocious underwater section. Omega is a fight I actually really enjoy, it's very technical and brutal to learn and master. Lastly, Gogmazios. It's a fun spectacle but a very boring fight.

So overall, do I think Monster Hunter Wilds is a disaster? Yes. Do I still have enjoyment of the game because it's Monster Hunter? Also yes. Do not ever pay full price for this game, get it on a cheap sale or wait for the expansion. It's just not worth it.

TL;DR: It's... a game. I can't really recommend it to newer players when other Monster Hunter games exist that I think excel in almost every way, but if you're a series veteran who wants a fresh experience it's not the worst thing ever created.

[i]"The sunk cost fallacy is our tendency to follow through with something that we’ve already invested heavily in (be it time, money, effort, or emotional energy), even when giving up is clearly a better idea."[/i] 

Monster Hunter Wilds was launched on the 28th of February 2025, a little over a year ago as of posting this review. I have waited for so long to review this game because I really just wanted to believe Capcom would lock in and get this game into a state where I could at least say that overall it's a positive experience and just leave it on a negative review purely because of the awful performance...

[b]But that day never came.[/b] 

I want to start off just quickly mentioning that when I originally drafted this review it was over 4 pages long and roughly 2000 words. I have a lot to say about this game and unfortunately it is mostly negative but I think I’ve done a good job compressing my points here.

First of all the combat. As with every other game in the franchise it is very fun and addictive to learn and master. There are a variety of new and returning tools given to players such as perfect guards, perfect evades, offsets (basically certain heavy attacks have the ability to interrupt monster actions and send them reeling backwards) and focus mode which makes aiming attacks a lot easier for melee weapons. Most of the 14 weapon types are in a pretty good spot right now, although a few of them do feel like pretty significant downgrades from previous iterations which I hope are improved upon in the expansion.

Next the campaign. This is without question the worst campaign in the franchise… A needlessly drawn out story that interrupts the action at every opportunity, long on-rails dialogue segments and pretty poor balancing that feels like it doesn’t really prepare the player for the sudden spike in difficulty at endgame. Also if you are planning on playing through this game with friends the multiplayer story experience is awful, despite Capcom claiming pre-release that they had made changes from World to ensure it was fluid… Thanks Capcom <3

The endgame itself is… interesting? Skill decorations are randomised again despite literally no-one asking for that to return, but the drop rates are far more relaxed than in World so it's not the worst thing ever. The Artian weapon system absolutely sucked at launch and is honestly even worse now, but the new Gogmazios upgrades added in Title Update 4 at least allow for some cool build variety with all the different set bonuses you can mix together. There are also randomised talismans to grind out if you are a really dedicated player, but they have very little effect on your build overall so that whole system is kind of just there.

The monsters are fantastic. A bunch of really cool new concepts like Rey Dau whose head transforms into a railgun or Jin Dahaad who is literally just a giant walking heat sink. Lots of the returning monsters also got amazing treatment with their fights in this game and it was really great to see Lagiacrus return after so long and come back so strong! (although the underwater section gets old fast…). And the “final” monster in this game, Arch Tempered Arkveld, is one of the greatest fights in the entire series. They honestly did a great job with them and I’m excited to see what they cook up for the expansion!

Finally the performance…

[b][u]It still kinda sucks…[/u][/b] 

And honestly, regardless of how much it’s been improved upon and may be improved upon in the future, the game's state at launch was atrocious and for that alone this game deserves a negative review.

Overall, I was just left feeling incredibly disappointed with this game from the moment I finished its campaign over a year ago now, and even after all the updates, events and balance adjustments the game still felt messy and unsatisfying for the vast majority of the time I spent playing it.

Despite all the negative things I have said about this game, if you have played previous entries in the series and enjoyed them, but have maybe run out of things to do or gotten burnt out, this game does have a sizable chunk of content and there is… [i]something…[/i] in between all of the awful decisions. If you are a new player interested in the series, I would wholeheartedly recommend looking into both Rise and World and deciding which one looks more your style because both are spectacular games and have even greater expansions waiting for you if you enjoy them!

Thank you for reading! :D

Overall I can give this game a solid recommendation for anyone interested in the franchise. Unlike many of it's predecessors, Wilds actually had an endgame at launch, and Capcom has done a great job expanding it. It has hundreds of hours of content with solid combat and great monster designs.

The performance used to be poor for most folk, I never had a lot of issues personally but after a recent patch I'm seeing noticeably higher and consistent framerates which I can imagine is especially fantastic for lower end machines. Capcom was committed to fixing the performance issues and I commend them for that, even if it took them around half a year.

Now for my negatives. This is not a monster hunter game made for previous fans. Wilds is 110% made to widen the series' appeal. This on it's own is not a bad thing, but to get new players they have to shave off some of the rough edges that made some of the older games special. I think MH:World and it's expansion did a much better job of walking the line between not having too many QoL improvements, and having so many QoL improvements that the series loses it's unique approach to gameplay. Wilds just shaves off a little too much and has some redundant design because of it. (The chillmantle and heatmantle bugs completely negate the need to ever bring cold and hot drinks along on a hunt for example)

I also feel Wilds is missing some of the wonder that previous games had. World's hubs Astera and Seliana felt so much more alive than any area in Wilds. They were colorful and every shop you interacted with had it's own huge area with a unique look. Even the older games hubs areas like Kokoto village or Pokke village felt much better to be in than the base camps we get as our hubs in Wilds. Every NPC we interact with is stuck in a tent. I miss that theme park like experience of going around Astera or Seliana and having each area feel like it has a purpose. In Wilds, the base camps are thrown together and talking to the smithy feels less engaging when I'm just talking to a girl in a tent. The Gathering Hub slightly makes up for this but then it goes against the design of being able to freely go out on any hunt from the base camp which the game toted in it's marketing. (Thats a whole other thing because that mechanic is completely ignored almost immediately) The Gathering Hub also just has bizarro versions of previous games' mechanics when they don't fit Wilds at all. In previous games, eating before a hunt cost Zenny, and buffs would be lost when you died but thats besides the point. In Wilds, the main way you eat is from getting ingredients and cooking on a grill, or having an NPC invite you over for a meal. The Hub has a big roundtable not dissimilar to previous game's canteens, but instead of paying with money you pay with tickets, and the meals you get are just DoorDashed from the villages they belong to. It's a way to implement old mechanics into this new system, but to me it just feels clunky and I wish it was just how it was in World with the Meowscular Chef and Grandmeowster Chef.

I admit a lot of this review is negative, and all of it is from personal preference for what I personally think a Monster Hunter game should be, and I know that for some this review will come off with "old woman yells at cloud" energy. But I think that in a vacuum, Wilds is not a horribly offensive game. It has some especially smoothed over edges but new players won't care or notice and most returning players will probably appreciate it. For me though, I see these QoL changes to be a step in the wrong direction, and all I can hope is that Capcom doesn't push the franchise down those steps for the sake of mass market appeal for future mainline games. Monster Hunter is really something special, and seeing it lean away from the things that I personally love about it is hard to watch.

Came for the pretty glasses girl, stayed for the wicked gameplay and drip. Surprisingly, my PC is able to run the game with some stuff on medium settings.

Cons

• Designed with controllers in mind. Mouse and keyboard take some getting used to, but they are serviceable.
• Some texture pop-ins after first loading up the game, but they smooth out over time. There are also low-res textures on a lot of objects for some reason, especially on architecture.
• Hair, scarves, and cloth clip through armour a lot. Kind of distracting, but this game is not the only sinner in this regard.
• The UI is a convoluted mess. There, I said it.
• Much too easy in the first half of the game.
• The story is pure tripe. The villagers don't know what a "weapon" is, but in the very next scene, the elder cuts the bread with a knife. Christ, who wrote this?!
• The intro (i.e., the first 8-10 hours) is a railroad. I almost quit the game because of handholding and endless cutscenes. Listen: We don't want long-winded, spoon-feeding intros. We want to play the game! Don't insult our intelligence by holding our hands through everything. Show, don't tell.

Pros

• The animations, design, and behavior of the monsters are top tier. They really seem alive with unique behaviors and movesets. Great stuff. I sometimes feel bad for killing them.
• I like the feeling of being inside a functioning ecosystem.
• You can use your slinger to grab stuff from afar and manipulate the environment.
• Like other MH games, each weapon makes this feel like a different game.
• Plenty of ways to approach the game, which makes for great replay value.

Conclusion: Why in the world would developers create these fantastic biomes, elegant and diverse monsters, and magnificent environments just to NOT LET THE PLAYER explore them? It is unfathomable to me. A huge misstep on the director's part. However, if you can stomach the story railroad, the game mechanics themselves are very good. 

Score: 7/10

Context: 
I have played every single Monster Hunter all the way back to the PS1 and PSP.
I have watched the series evolve tremendously over the years and make leaps and bounds towards becoming a stronger hunter by utilizing new tools such as wire bugs, clutch claws, mounts, the mounting mechanics etc. 
Each Monster Hunter has had an identity that was unique to it with a dedicated flagship, or on occasion flagships, that built the story to be interesting and captivating. 

With MHWorld, every event quest had something either fun, goofy, or challenging that made them enjoyable. With MHWilds, I cannot say the same. There are some goofy quests such as the recently added slime farming quest but aside from that they are all just standard hunts for, at best, mediocre armor that can't be mixed well into any set without significant sacrifice or even layering. 

Now to talk about the risk vs reward aspect of the hunts. I truly believe that Monster Hunter Wilds has some of the worst design philosophies when it comes to rewarding the player for arising to meet a higher challenge. Of note, grinding a perfect set to minimize your time on a hunt of an apex monster to only receive a slightly cooler pendant  is insulting at best. When compared to the previous titles, it appears as though CAPCOM are just pushing out trinkets without putting any thought into the personality or the essence of what the monster is. Take AT Jin Dahaad as an example, for his armor it is simply the frosted version of his base armor. Comparing that to AT Arkveld there is no contest as to which armor has a closer affinity to the monster it represents. The rewards for rising to the challenge of a 10 star GOG; a title banner.... a 10 star Zoh Shia? literally nothing extra. Fighting 4 AT apexes? A title banner. This is pathetic. 

The 10 stars. I am personally tired of seeing the same 4 monsters getting a new star just because. There are many other monsters in the game I would love to see variants or subspecies of and THEN get those as 10 stars. Now with the precedent being set that the apex monster act as the elder dragons for late game, looking at armor sets here, and then to have Gogmazious come in and have multiple passives, every elder would now have to have multiple passives. The overarching problem with MHWilds is that they are only using the same resources already in the game rather than try to incorporate monsters that we have seen and loved from previous titles because they had a poor launch. They are letting down the community and should really reevaluate what it is that players want. 

TLDR; CAPCOM is getting lazy with the rewards for players who rise to meet the challenge of a higher difficulty quest and Monster Hunter is going downhill if they keep this up.

As with many others, the performance of this game on launch was terrible. Whilst I personally was at "playable" performance at 1440p, many struggled with playing at an enjoyable experience. As a long time MH franchise player I usually play the games extensively after finishing the story but that wasn't the case after this one. I love having high frame rate in all my games so I ended up resorting to 3rd party frame gen at the time like lossless scaling and optimization mods from nexus. It was tedious so after the first playthrough I ended up just putting the game down until they get some optimization updates going.

Although it took a while, they eventually did it and it's been amazing! While I don't speak for all rigs, it worked wonders on mine and the game feels and plays like all the previous gen MH games that I fell in love with. I don't use anymore of my previous optimizations and they finally added DLSS for the older RTX cards which didn't work if you had anything older than a 40 series on launch. Unfortunately, my experience still isn't relatable to alot of people with older rigs... Hopefully in the future they can further optimize it to the point that lower end PC's can fully experience the game smoothly.

If you have a fairly new or decent PC I 100% recommend this game to anyone that enjoyed world, rise, or any previous MH. Otherwise if you have something older or weaker than the latter I'd tread carefully.

I cannot recommend this game. It is by far the worst Monster Hunter to date.

1st: Performance

It is completely indefensible how badly optimized this game is. It looks worse than World and runs insanely bad. Without FrameGen and Mid to High settings (raytracing off) I don't even get stable 60 fps. I recently started playing again after stopping when Lagiacrus released and Youtubers said they "fixed" the performance, but they did not. It runs like ♥♥♥♥ and looks like ♥♥♥♥ and this will never get fixed, because the RE Engine is not made for grand scale games like this. But it probably still runs better than if it were using UE5. 

2nd: Pacing

The game progression to Endgame somehow manages to both feel rushed and way too slow at the same time. How? You have 15 minute story segments where nothing happens, then you get to fight a monster for 5-10 minutes followed by another 15 minute story segment following another 5-10 minute hunt and after that you are immediately rushed to the next biome. Since the story is really bad, those railroaded sequences suck all life joy out of you and the hunts in LR are so fast that the joy doesn't return before the next story sequence. 

3rd: Optional quests/side content

The optional quests are completely useless, since they are just the same monsters you already killed, with nothing to differentiate these quests from the normal ones. There are absolutely no gathering quests... WHY??? I know maybe not many people enjoyed them, but they definitely gave you some time to breathe and learn the new maps, find some shortcuts between areas. None of that is here anymore. World had the best implementation of this system with optional quest chains that rewarded you with important gear (mantles) and upgrades (canteen, plant growth). They also visually upgraded your hub area, which let those quests feel important. Even if you do all the optional quests you get nothing. No visual upgrades to the hub, not even an indicator in the quest menu that you finished all the optional stuff. Nothing. 

4th: UI

The UI for this game is a step down from World, which was already confusing for first time players. 

5th: New monsters:

The new apex monsters for each biome are all enjoyable to fight. Doshaguma is easily the worst new monster, as it is not only visually repulsive, but incredibly annoying to fight too. The new pack mechanic sucks and I seriously hope they discontinue that ♥♥♥♥, because if I have to deal with 3-4 monsters at the same time in G-Rank I'm going to ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ explode. Don't come at me with dung bombs, as this does not help your argument. When the response to packs is to break them up and just fight the monster solo then what was the reason for implementing the pack mechanic. Annoy you? Guardian Doshaguma is immediately more enjoyable to fight, because it doesn't have a pack mechanic. The rest of the normal new monsters are mostly forgettable. Some highlights are Ajarakan as he is simply fun to fight and Quematrice as an enjoyable early monster. Balahara is annoying with Bowguns. The returning monsters are very well done and enjoyable as always. The post game additions (Mizutsune, Lagiacrus and Seregios) are also really enjoyable. I love Mizutsune. Unfortunately, they fumbled with his theme in this game. His music was way better in every other title. I cannot comment on anything after Lagiacrus since I stopped playing then. But I do not give a rats ass about the other content added after Lagi since they are all designed to be fought as a Group and I'm a solo player and since I have constant server disconnections in this game (every 15 - 20 minutes) I will not even try the multiplayer stuff so I won't destroy my set-up out of sheer frustration and anger. And no, this is not a me problem, I do not have these issues with any other game, just the Monster Hunter series and since I've been playing since Freedom Unite I know that the Capcom servers are ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. In World I could not finish even one Safi'Jiva quest without disconnecting. 

6th: Handholding

My God Capcom, I am a grown adult, I do not need to know where all the monsters are on the map at all times, I can go out in the world and find my own iron ore for upgrading my basic gear and what reason is there to fight the same monster multiple times anymore, when new gear and weapons need so few parts from the monsters that I can craft everything after killing the same monster 3 times. Wtf? 

7th: Artian weapons

Why? They suck visually, they are an annoying chore to upgrade while being complete RNG if you made a good weapon. The normal weapons should have received an extra upgrade and be the best in slot weapons until the Post Game additions dropped. 

8th: Conclusion

Some of these issues can be remedied with the G-Rank Expansion, but the core problems will still be there. There are numerous other things that can be critiqued about this game, but this is already getting way too long. The new maps are all a convoluted mess. Even after 65 hours I get lost easily in some areas. The fact that I only have 65 hours in this game is extremely sad, but the ♥♥♥♥ performance just killed all enjoyment I could've had with this game, so I burnt out fast. In every other game I have at least 100 hours, mostly 200-300. World was such a great game, while not being perfect, but giving you a really immersive new experience in the series and Rise and now Wilds completely killed my hope for the mainline games. Stop taking every bit of friction out of the game just so newcomers can perfectly optimize their fastest way to the Endboss. The story sucks ass too. The focus of "here, look at these ethnic minorities with their vibrant ethnic culture and their ethnic minority food" just feels super weird, as if it was written by some fat white women. Also ♥♥♥♥ Nata. 

Will I try out the expansion? Yes. Will I enjoy it? No, probably not. 

I hope Stories 3 delivers.

Have a good day!
