Context: Just hit HR 100 and cleared the last optional quest.

Monster Hunter Wilds is a very fun game. I love how they basically brought back the Guiding Lands concept from World and expanded it to make the world feel more immersive. The downside, though, is the whole “passing the day” mechanic to refresh monsters so you can get the ones you want and save them as investigations.

Personally, I’m not a fan of doing quests with randos unless it’s a dedicated hunting party formed through the official Discord, besides that, I’m mostly a solo hunter, so you can imagine how thrilled I was when I discovered the Support Hunter system, calling in NPC hunters during quests with actually decent AI is a huge win for solo players.

With the introduction of the SF6 and FFXIV collab, Capcom kind of introduce specs to the game in a form of Akuma and the blackest night which I'm all in for, it shows that they’re putting more thought into their collaborations, and I hope we see even more creative ones in the expansion.

I started playing when they announced the final update for the base game, and I can say for sure that the game is FINALLY optimized with no lag whatsoever (high settings on a 3070).

My main issue with Wilds is how easy the early game is. Monsters go down way too quickly, and you don’t really feel that classic “Monster Hunter” challenge until around HR50+. The main story also ends very quickly as you could finish it in under three hours if you speedrun it.

I can definitely understand the criticism the game received at launch, especially when it had abysmal optimization and very little endgame content before the title updates. But now that the base game has reached its last and final update, I can confidently say that Monster Hunter really shines once all the content or DLC have been released.

I’m really looking forward to the expansion, and I hope more people give this game a try.

I have had a lot of fun playing this game

For some context: this is my first MH game. I tried World when it came out but I didn't play long before refunding it.
Wilds has good things, but also quite a few bad ones. If you're looking for a boss simulator, you found it. If you're looking for a story rich game, you did not find it.
Why do I play it? Fashion hunter basically. 

At just over 200 hours, I seriously considered why I was still playing because I was just fighting the same monsters over and over for materials and gear upgrades. Just so I could fight the same monster with a higher difficulty. This might be appealing to some players, but I need a goal outside of this. It's not like I can change my hunter's personality for a different narrative (no dialogue choices), just the appearance.

Speaking of narrative, the main story and the characters are incredibly dry. Cringy even at some points. I feel absolutely nothing for the story and the only character I would enjoy more of is Erik. And maybe Olivia. This is not a story game. It is a fight to get stronger game.

My biggest complaint: graphics. Outside of cinematics, this game looks like it came out 10 years ago.
We all know Wilds had absolutely horrible performance issues at launch. Capcom really has made efforts to improve it, but it's still rough for me at least. I have to sit there for quite a while in a new area for character/monster models to load or it's just polygons. And I mean BASIC polygons. I could attempt to fight the monster before it fully loads, but I'm gonna get smacked by the invisible claw that hasn't loaded yet.
The textures are bad. They just are. I audibly complain every time I play and none of my settings are set to high or ultra (mostly low). I don't dare go down that path if low is struggling. Tried the better textures 'DLC' and I didn't notice a difference.

You'll also see complaints about the UI and menu navigation, and it's very true. 
Very little gets explained and you often just get thrown into a mission with no idea what you're supposed to do. Prime example is the introduction of captures. You don't encounter the concept until later in the story and it literally just throws you into a mission with an NPC saying to do something. And these monsters are the most annoying Fers for a capture. I didn't know I was supposed to open a different window to grab items I've never used before. And of course all my inventory slots of my hotbar were taken (why wouldn't they be) so I had to throw two item stacks away to get those items needed. All while running around with these 3 MFers running wild like toddlers in a small area.
I've had to look up quite a few things online to understand what they even do. Game says 'armor spheres make your armor stronger' is actually 'armor spheres increase the amount of times you can upgrade to make your armor stronger.' 

All these negatives, yet a positive review. I might just be hyper fixated, but I really like the battles and weapons. Charge Blade chainsaw >:) I improve my positioning, find new ways to use the environment, and naming my build 'Move B*tch' with full evade because it's my biggest flaw. It does get a bit monotonous trying to get a particular drop so try to change it up every now and then. As much as I complained, I just can't give a negative recommendation.

But I can't believe this game is still $70. Buy it on a sale, cuz it ain't worth that price.

[h1]Comprehnsive, spoiler-free review of Monster Hunter Wilds (MHWilds):[/h1]

[b]Net Positive[/b] - Monster Hunter (MH) games once they "click" are fantasic, MHWilds is no exception. 
Now that all Monsters for base Game are relased (TU4+) I can [u]recommend this game[/u].

[h2]Preface:[/h2]
MH:World (MHW) was my first entry into the MH Series, where the game only clicked for me when trying out the Gunlance, after almost dropping it playing with Dualblades. This as a whole feels like a common theme with people who drop the game, usually because they didn't click with a weapon.

[h2]Gameplay:[/h2]
Fighting monsters is very fun, and after completing all base game achievements, I [u]still want to keep playing[/u]. The gameplay experience is based around 14 different weapons, which act like "Classes" in a traditional RPG. All weapons have different moves, systems, and learning curves. The gameplay loop boils down to Hunt a Monster, carve it for parts to make better weapons and armour, repeat.

[h2]Difficulty:[/h2]
Variable - a vast range of low to high - [u]recommended for all[/u].
As a person who has played at least one Previous Title, the game [u]starts off very easy[/u]. There are no intro "Small Monster" practice hunts, You are immmediately tasked with a hunting a Large Monster with very low health. The game does pick up the difficulty later on, but in my playthrough of Low Rank (Story) with Sword and Shield (SnS) and Gunlance GL I fainted once, to an attack I didn't read the tutorial message on.

In High Rank (Post-Story) the [u]difficulty picks up slightly[/u], and the game opens up into levelling for progression, with a few Monsters that are only fightable in High Rank, and the Tempered system opens up - increasing health, attack rate, attack power, and on occasion, new moves for Monsters previously fought.

End Game and Title Updates (TU) improved the experience tremendously. The Tempered Monsters feel like where the base Monsters could have been, though for a new player experiece I can appreciate the lower difficulty. Higher Star (9*, 10*) and Arch-Tempered (AT) Monsters [u]can feel like a good challenge[/u], with the End Game Monsters often requiring many attempts and build tweaks to get an initial clear.

[h2]Versus other Entries:[/h2]
Variable - [u]pros and cons[/u].
MHW had a sense of exploration that is lost slightly by MHWilds' streamlining of the gameplay experience - essentially letting the player press a button to be on-rails delivered to a target. 
The environments in MHWilds feel alive, like MHW, and the graphical fidelity has increased, as you would expect from a newer entry. 
The "Gimmick" of MHWilds - akin to the Clutch Claw from MHW: Iceborne (MHW:I) - are the "Wound" and "Focus Mode" systems, which are heavily intertwined.  Wounds - at least in earlier parts of the game are very strong at disrupting Monsters. Focus Mode - essentially lowers the skill ceiling for ensuring attacks hit their target, but also provides access to additional moves not in previous titles.
Fishing, capturing endemic life, and environmental looting (Mining Ores / Bone piles / Plants / Fungus) are all still present, with varying degrees of importance to the gameplay loop.

[h2]Story:[/h2]
Variable - [u]Not important to me[/u], but more in the spotlight than previous titles.
The Story in MHWilds is much more cohesive than MHW in my opinion, though this doesn't affect my enjoyment of either game, as most of my playtime takes place outside of Story quests.

[h2]Technical:[/h2]
[u]Needs a decent system to run[/u].
The launch was rough for this game, performance to graphical quality ratio was not accpetable - most issues [i]seemed[/i] unrelated to graphics. Post TU4 the game runs much better, though on my system (i7-10700K - RTX 3070Ti - 64GB RAM) I maintained 60fps with Medium/Custom Settings. 
I recommed playing on Controller/Gamepad - personally have been using an XBox Series Controller.

[h3]Importance of Categories to me:[/h3] 
[u]Gameplay/Difficulty > VS Previous > Story[/u]. Technical becomes most important in most negative reviews.

[h2]Overall, I recommend MHWilds.[/h2]

Overhaul, it's a great game. The combat is fluid, the monsters are amazing and the graphics are beautiful, although the story is lacking, you don't play monster hunter for the story, but to hunt monsters.

But there it's where is the problem: hunting. The game makes it too easy to engage in combat. You don't have to follow the monster's trail or use other tools to track or bait him, as it doesn't reward that type of gameplay, although it's in the game. Most of the late game monsters are arena fights. Amazing fights. But more like an mmo fight than anything else.

The biggest problem is the artesian weapon. The system came to aid certain weapons that lack an element or status in their trees. The problem is that, they are too strong and makes the actual trees irrelevant, specially when the whole end game grind is for them. After you reach endgame, you don't want to hunt lesser monsters for their material and that made me hunt the same 4 or 5 monsters again and again and again and the fact that to upgrade it is "ramdom" (it uses a pool of abilities and status) is completely dumb and I hope they don't make the end game in expansion like this.

My way to make the artisans better, would be to be able to upgrade the monsters weapon with better augments and give them their respective monster's armour set ability. We have 2-4 slots to activate a set ability, 6 pieces of equipment but only 5 actually count? With 5, we have a piece not being used or kbly counting for group set with that's possible.

Difficulty? The endgame is a great middle finger to players complaining at launch about it. Gog and omega and now arch monsters add a great sense of danger that the base game lacked. They are not perfect! Status didn't keep up with the new gigantic monsters health and many of them move like they weigh like feathers and stun lock you, but nothing impossible when you know what you are doing.

Performance? I have a quite weak laptop and it's working fine. Some dips where the action is very strong, but it got much better lately. At least I don't have to mod to get a proper performance!
I recommend the game, but they still have to work somethings here and there in the gameplay loop

Ps: many of the jewels in the game are completely useless or redundant right now like wind resistance,  many monsters use it but you probably never was hit by it if compared to world for example (rathalos wind pressure while flying was somewhat a problem there and here it doesn't affect your character); tremor resistance, the game right now game has NO monster that makes you need the whole 3 levels, not even gog with its humangus size; flinch free and shock absorber and jump master, they pretty much do the same thing but flinch free do what jump do and you still can hit players, unlike shock abs; climbing and any stealth ability really, since you have a mount and always are hitting the monster and not hiding from it and antismell. Just one monster uses it and thr monster is not that dangerous to waste jewel slot for that

The performance is STILL terrible. I run this game on some high end stuff, by no means should I be getting the severe amount of lag and frame drops and stutters that I do. Nothing is more irritating than the game itself prevent me from playing it.

I do like this game a lot. I enjoy the combat and the monsters, but jesus christ does the performance problems, input delay, input detection and overall just terrible optimization of hardware utilization in this game absolutely kill me love and enjoyment of this game as a franchise.

And this is just ignoring the slew of general problems with the combat with things feeling sluggish for no reason, having confusing as design (explain to me which idiot thought making greatsword due a shuffle after guarding was a good idea?) or in general being so inferior to other options it feels more daunting to convince myself to use hammer than it does to spam dual blade dodges. Seriously the dodge mechanic is completely broken, its better than the long swords special sheath because at least during the dodge, you can negate ALL forms of damage. The special sheath still takes damage through the fat fk known as Uth Duna. I hate that fish.

Frankly, if they want people to stick around for G/Master Rank, they need to balance the weapons to be as actually good as the best options (that being bow and dual blades for aforementioned reasons) and speed things up a bit with weapon animations and remove so many unnecessary shuffles from perfect guards. Playing this game against the 10 star AT's and NOT being a speedrunner is geniunly a miserable experience for so many weapons that I've entirely dropped some. I had done this during Savage Omega and now I have to do it with just the games overall design. This is ultimately unrelated rambling but uhhh 

TL;DR:

Optimize the game. ffs. I shouldn't be stuttering and lagging and having my inputs ate or outright ignored when im running a 4090 and 9900X. 

Balance the weapons in the game to be as useful or have as useful tech as the discerning dodges. 10 star monsters make most other weapons a slog to actually get proficient in. This was a problem with the 9 stars and STILL is. Stop being scared of strong weapons, theres better ways to make monsters challenging instead of forcing you to sit around and do nothing because of chip damage, perfect guards being nearly useless if not outright redundant or outright being slow. Greatsword tackle shouldn't be better than a PERFECT GUARD. Don't make "vital" mechanic and proceed to make every challenging monster completely negate its benefits on everything that doesn't slot Guard/G. Up. Especially since slotting that will shaft most weapons damage and make the hunts even MORE sloggish.

After playing this game since launch i can say that it has come a long way and it was not without issues but is an absolute solid MH to play after all the patches and TU`s, The starter Roster is really solid i personally have no issues with the current monsters that we have 

The Good:
-The Arch Tempered Monsters are a blast to fight, challenging you with new moves from the apexes

-Layered armor system is also way more accessible compared to world which i do like as you can hide 
 some of the armor that does not look that great very easy or dress to an absolute unmatched fit to 
 intimidate the monsters 

-Focus mode is Amazing in my opinion as it allows you to hit the monsters when it otherwise would have 
 been a miss thought it kind of removes the penalty for not learning the monsters pattern which is the 
 core of MH but i am not complaining about it (we wilds players are spoiled with focus mode) 

-Seikrets not being a instand get out of jail free card unlike rise`s emergency move (forgot the name) as 
 the seikrets take time until they get to you dependent on how far away they are and after that take a 
 little time picking you up

-most of the performance issues are gone with a crash being super rare now and performance running 
 rather stable 

The Bad:

First is the Artian weapon system where after getting to Gogmazios, you are grinding for the perfect or decent stats on a weapon/weapons as literally any other weapon from the smithy is utterly useless or inferior to use which sucks, the only reason to craft smithy weapons at the moment is to make layered weapons (as regular artian weapons are kind of ugly, gog weapons are fine imo). which is kinda like WOW that weapon from X monster looks so cool i bet its gonna help in my hunts, NAH grind gog like 17 times in a row for a Chance at a decent weapon and then do it another Dozen times if you want different elements which can get stale very fast. 

Second is more a personal gripe but still a major let down in my eyes that being the Coloration of the world as it appears SUPER washed which is an absolute bummer even though it makes sense in the world building sense like the windward plains being a desert that frequently gets hit by massive sandstorm or Scarlet forest getting hit by massive floods and rain (absolute bummer as the scarlet forest during the Plenty is absolutely gorgeous) 

Third is the Ice shard cliffs which is easily the most boring locale in MH wilds it just feels super bland and the claustrophobic fighting in thin corridors against for example a Gore Magala does not feel great at all. its an absolute let down when compared to the Hoarfrost reach from world (but that locale came with Iceborn and wasn`t  base game so its kind of unknown what an wilds expansion has up its sleeve)

That being said though if you got a decent Rig that can Run this game i do totally recommend you play this one, even though my negatives outweigh the positives i absolutely love MH wilds and am exited what capcom is going to cook up for an expansion

MH Wilds = MH World Slop Edition. At this point, the team responsible for MH should just declare creative bankruptcy and do something else. 4/10

Wilds is a lazy copy of World and is a downgrade in almost every aspect. The camera is still trash, the menus are a cluttered mess and the new "Focus Mode" does more bad than good. Wilds has also managed to make an even more RNG end-game grind than World (how is that even possible).

The biomes/locales in Wilds are a MASSIVE downgrade from World. Akward map layout with too much verticality and cluttered visuals make for an awful experience. Compared to World, the navigation feels awkward and the maps don't have a natural flow or cohesive design to them. The Wyveria map is especially bad.

Then there's the monster roster; besides the Arachnid and Cephalopod variants most of the "new" monsters in Wilds are a copy pasta of a monster from World but worse:

Arkveld < Nergigante (even the lore is almost the same)
Zoh Shia < Xeno Jiiva
Gogmazio < Safi Jiiva
Jin Dahaad < Behemoth
Uth Duna < Namielle
etc.

The best thing about the monsters in Wilds are the bad ones from World that didn't make into Wilds (yet) like the Piscine Wyverns and Alatreon.

Last but not least, there's the unacceptable PC performance at launch that took a whole year to "fix". 

In closing, I remember reading a Wilds review on Steam a couple months back that simply read "Just play MH World". After completing Wilds, I could not agree more.

After 200+ on both SlopStation 5 and PC and after a few updates Wilds is a good game just sucked Capcom didn't delay the pc version so that they could iron out the performance hiccups Wilds faced at launch. My main grips with Wilds mainly the difficulty and some of the accessibility features. For the difficulty I have no problem with low rank being a walk in the park since it seems like they want low rank to be more story driven that is fine but high rank was just a joke up until title update 2 when they introduced seregios and lagiacrus. What also affected the difficulty was the new wound mechanic while I think it's a nice addition the wound system also made monsters topple more frequent and monsters being slower, less aggressive, and smaller health pools (unless you're goremagala) it brought the game down a bit until the recent title updates. The accessibility feature more so stuff like being able to fast travel to camps while in combat this is something I hope they never bring back we already have farcaster for that! also not a big fan of being able to buy monster materials or the weapons it felt like they were removing a big part of monster hunter's gameplay loop, grinding, I only ever use the support ships for consumable items but otherwise it just feels like such a pointless addition it works fine for a mmo like frontier but it shouldn't be in the mainline games. Now for the good stuff the roster of new monsters the monster hunter team keeping up a good job at doing variety and not just dragons and dinosaurs like in world it was also nice to finally see the likes of Lagiacrus and Gog comeback after so long. The new weapon movesets and focus mode while yes I did wish we got a new weapon in Wilds and I did criticize the it to an extent because of the wound mechanic it's a double edge sword because it's a fun edition just needs more fine tuning the focus strikes make the already existing weapons feel refreshing enough along with the new moves. Wilds might have my favorite iteration of the Insect Glaive it's so much fun to use it was already fun to use in world but now it's peak, Gunlance and charge blade got some neat additions too but so far I'd say Insect Glaive is the mvp this time around. While Wilds has it's flaws it's still a good game one I'll be grinding at after I'm done with all of the content for both the base game and the upcoming expansion I'm also glad the mh devs could turn things around for the game's end game and performance the start to the 6th gen of Monster Hunter may have had a rocky but things are starting to look bright I give Wilds a 8/10.

I recommend this game because, in spite of its issues, it's still extremely enjoyable and expansive in terms of content and things to do. There are many positives I can think of, as well as negatives.

The performance, after a few updates attempting to improve it, HAS gotten better... but still nowhere near that good.  The recommended specs for this game feels like an actual lie. I have a 3080 ti as well as a 5800x, and I'm barely pulling an average of 60 fps on Medium settings in some areas. As someone had said in another review, it's like going from ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ yourself to now pissing yourself. I feel as though there are still MANY areas that performance can still improve, and I REALLY hope they keep that flow, but I doubt it. 

The implementation of the wound and focus systems, while good and fun ideas, have led to a very core issue with the gameplay itself. For the base game, excluding the 10 and 9 star monsters, it has led to a very ridiculous easing of the difficulty. I should not be able to kill a 8 star Guardian Rathalos in 3-4 minutes through the sheer power of stun-locking and wound exploiting. While this problem has been addressed with the harder 9 and 10 star quests, I believe, if not balanced properly for the inevitable expansion, it will present another massive issue in terms of difficulty. As stated, the newer higher tier of monsters have at-least addressed this issue... through the usage of one-shots and ketamine, but it is what it is. Another thing that bothers me is the changes to some of the weapons, as they had to make a-lot of changes to match the new systems. Furthermore, I'm a bit iffy on them only letting you put combat decorations on the weapons themselves. I miss the variety I had in previous titles.

Next would be the dulling of mechanics that made the Monster Hunter franchise what it was. This game gets rid of having to search for the monster, knowing your environment to get to areas quicker, overall knowledge like where the monster typically spawns or areas that it often roams, etc. While perhaps not an issue for many people, for me the smaller mechanics such as that is at-least what made World for me. I believe the world itself is detailed and gorgeous, but learning the map through gameplay to better your hunts is gone. At most, you auto-ride your bird until it takes you where you need to go and memorize the environmental stuff like boulders. Perhaps its a sacrifice you have to make to make the world as big as it is (traversing the desert on foot would be abysmal), but I'm not sure I'd take larger maps over automating what was once a big part of the gameplay for me. Perhaps what Rise did with the Palamutes would be a better implementation other than 'Press 'button' to auto-path to the monster'. There are a ton of small things as well, like the chef cats and the quest descriptions being funny or unique, 

I think that, in other areas, the game itself is excellent. It looks nice (imo), the gameplay is extremely fun, there is a ton of content and replayability here, the late game difficulty is fun (also frustrating), the animations are amazing... however, I wish they didn't take away things the old titles had. If they kept the changes, balanced them well, and didn't remove what made the old games what they were, it would be perfect. 
Regardless, I'll recommend this because, in spite of these issues, I still play this game and enjoy it a ton.

Between focus mode, mix-and-match set bonus weapons, Qol improvements, and easy access to layered gear, Wilds has completely pulled ahead after a rocky start plagued by bad optimization.

After a whole YEAR since this game's release, I can finally recommend it to most people. Assuming they have a PC that can run the game (mine just barely can at medium-high graphics, admittedly). With Wilds now in a relatively stable state performance-wise, it lets me see how good this game is overall, arguably one of the best base-game/high rank experiences we've ever gotten. A solid roster of monsters and what might be the best hunt-by-hunt gameplay in the series, it might not be perfect, far from it, but I honestly consider Wilds a must play for any MH fans.

That being said, I can't help but be worried for the expansion. If this is how hard the base game is, I'm worried master rank will be filled with bloated HP and damage numbers, rather than actual difficult fights that require genuine strategy. We'll just have to wait and see.

[h1]Optimization[/h1]
For those concerned about optimization, I pre-ordered Monster Hunter Wilds on day one, and the launch experience was rough. The game was plagued by lag, making gameplay choppy and unsatisfying. I only managed about 30 hours before shelving it. However, after Title Update 4, news of major improvements caught my eye. I jumped back in and racked up another 60 hours over two weeks—with zero issues. It ran buttery smooth at high graphics settings, no crashes, lag, or interruptions, even during online hunts with other players. (For reference: RTX 4070 Super.) Optimization is now excellent.

[h1]Gameplay[/h1]
I've been hooked on the series since Monster Hunter World and Rise. Wilds brings meaningful upgrades like Focus Mode and monster wounds, which make it way easier for a noob like me to target weak points precisely. The new movesets are fantastic—especially the Long Sword's flashy finisher. Weapon designs are top-notch, and the Darkness Shatterseal Greatsword's effects are so cool they convinced me to main Great Sword.

[h1]Graphics[/h1]
Graphics aren't my top priority, but Wilds feels like a solid step up from World. Maps are massive, and new monsters like Rey Dau and Arkveld look incredible. (I won't compare it to Rise, which was built for Nintendo Switch with a different art style.)

[h1]Story[/h1]
Story has never been a draw for me in this series—no one plays Monster Hunter for the narrative. No complaints here.

[h1]Overall[/h1]
This is almost my favorite game of all time. Nothing beats the adrenaline rush of wielding an oversized weapon to perfectly parry a colossal monster's attack and turn the hunt in your favor. With optimization fixed post-Title Update 4, I highly recommend it. 9.5/10

Now that all of the title updates are out and the base game of wilds is "complete" I feel comfortable leaving a review. I wish there was a middle thumb option, but overall I'll leave it as a positive review.

I never had any performance issues luckily, so I have no comments on that aspect of the game.

To make a long story short after ~200 hours I can say I really enjoy the time right after a title update and absolutely loathe the end game grind for this game. Especially with the new 10* AT that came out with title update 4.5. I have never enjoyed monsters that gain difficulty through bloated HP and one shot moves. I do not think it fits the MH franchise, but that is the state of the current end game. Monster hunter has always been about prepping items in advance and adapting your build to the target of your hunt, but for most of these 10* ATs any adaptation I made felt fruitless. It came down to just perfect dodging or guarding for the 15-20 minutes it takes until the monster is dead. Some find that to be fun, I found it to be incredibly boring and just unfair at time if you happen to slightly mis-time something and get one shot. It makes random support hunts nigh impossible (unless you use AI hunters) since people tend to get one shot in quick succession until quest fail. If that is the expectation for the hunt, I'd prefer they wouldn't also bloat the HP and make me sit even longer doing the same dodge / block sequences repetitively across multiple hunts to get the required mats for the armor I want.

I feel the same about Gogmazios and the Gogma Artian grind. I did not like the base Artian system, but at least I could farm many different monsters and get somewhat similar drop rates. The only real way to farm the mutated armaments is to fight Gogmazios repeatedly and hope you get good RNG or its back to the basin for more armaments. The first time I hunted Gogmazios I thought it was spectacular, after the 40th time I got quite sick of his mechanics (especially the third phase...).

The seasonal festivals were great, character customization was quite deep and satisfying, and the majority of hunts I enjoyed and would farm the full armor sets and weapon sets for my weapons of choice. It is just a shame that the end game grind is such a slog of one shot mechanics and gambling.

Another release of Monster Hunter. After World and Rise, it should be instant favourite and a banger of a game. Well, it is not. Even after performance patch, perfomarmance is... mediocre. Game doesnt look actually that good either, if you look carefully (maybe it does with HD textures, but no way I can run those). Especially when you compare to other PC releases. Rise looked questionably before Sunbreak was released, but it has distinct style, making its somewhat lackluster graphics look way better. Wilds went for realistic look, and failed. Game actually looks worse than World, works way, way worse than World. Sure, we get somewhat open zones, which is kinda interesting, but I dont think it is worth its outrageous requirements. But the worst part is probably combat. Wound system is atrocious. Additional controls required by that system are obnoxious. New "more dynamic" combat feels way worse than clunkier, but more responsive combat of World or Rise. New "moves" which are supposed to make weapons feel worse than ever before (weapons i actually used: Lance - which i main, Bow, Heavy Bowgun, Sword and shield). Lance, for me, feel especially atrocious. Monster hitboxes feel all over the place, which, paired with lowish fps (around 47-55) make perfect blocks nigh impossible, perfect counters needless risk (example: i counter jaw attack of the monster, that misses even though im not moving, and im getting hit by front leg, since it has active hitbox, but since perfect counter requires releasing blocking stance, im getting hit). Release defense button mechanic should be removed immedietely. I assume there is plenty weapons that has similar issues, but since lance is what im most familiar with, im using it as main example. Tl:dr; If you liked older monster hunters, there is high chance you might hate this title. If you are new to the series, have NASA based supercomputer for gaming device, and want very dynamic combat, it might be a decent starting title for you, though i would still recommend former two games.

TLDR: A fun experience held back by technical issues and grind.

Before anything, I want to say this is my first game in the Monster Hunter series, and it was a mixed bag.

The learning curve was tough. There was so much thrown at you at the start, and a lot of it wasn’t properly explained. I was still discovering mechanics and interactions 30–40 hours into the game. In a way, that made it more interesting, but it would have been better to have clearer explanations. It often feels like the game forces you to go to Reddit or YouTube to understand basic systems.

That said, I had a blast hunting different monsters and observing the ecosystems. Combat is fun and surprisingly deep. There are many weapons to choose from, and each one changes your playstyle significantly, which keeps things fresh. Progression felt rewarding and enjoyable.

The co-op system, however, is extremely confusing and has a long way to go. The fact that there’s a 20-minute guide on YouTube explaining how to properly play together says a lot.

Story-wise, there isn’t much to say. Some parts were interesting, like exploring new lands and learning about their ecology (I wish there were more missions focused on that). On the other hand, Nata was incredibly annoying I honestly wanted the character gone halfway through the game. I’m not even sure what the main theme of the story was. Growing up? Self-discovery? It felt unclear and poorly executed.

Technical issues were my biggest problem. The game kept crashing during one mission, completely blocking my progression. I had to reinstall the game, update drivers, and search Reddit for solutions before I could fix it despite the issue apparently being known for five months.

Overall, I’ll continue playing as new updates come out, and I would still recommend it to friends, just with some warnings.

I'm sorry but even though I love Monster Hunter, I found this game to be a major disappointment. Don't get me wrong: there are a lot of excellent qualities in this game and I will probably get back to it later. I just feel disappointed. 

I like that it's an ambitious title for trying to make MH almost completely open world. However, I hate the choices the developers took to get there. To me, MH should do what it does best: hunt Monsters without anything to intrude on that experience. Instead, I got a game that fell back on World's worst qualities: having overly intrusive tutorials, NPCs that will not leave you alone, and an in your face "cinematic" story. It was okay in World since it didn't take too much of your time and you could skip most of it. However, it takes up every waking moment of your experience with follow the NPC scenes that force me to listen to them talk. It's not a good sign that I was actively trying to rebel against these scenes and also found myself having to turn off the voice volume just to hear myself think. Oh yeah. I actually didn't notice that the game had music until I tried it again last night. I couldn't hear it partly because on default voice volumes, the music is quiet compared to everything else and partly because I felt that for a fast paced monster game, the music felt very bland. For me, the music was the best part. So, seeing that go was especially disappointing.

It feels so bad writing this because I thought the combat was really fun. It's just that everything I mentioned ruined it for me. So, I'm probably gonna have to give this one a pass and just go back to Rise or Generations.

Personally this game performance feels much better for me even before the recent optimization patch, but it's still quite demanding game and some of my friends are still having troubles with running it, so play it if you can meet its requirement.

Wilds is a great game, tho I cannot for certain say that it's the best MH game ever made, especially comparing with World experience, or Rise gameplay, or MH4U (cuz I havent played it lol). I'd certainly it's an overhyped massive blunder, and this game would hit the mainstream MUCH more if it wasnt for dogsh optimization.

A lot of mechanics that they promised feels really overhyped (like pop up camp, weapon switching, even the openworld itself). In concept, you could do some really cool things with them, but in reality, they dont really offer that much and it's just a normal Monster Hunter game thing.
However because of that, a lot of good QoL systems are removed to trade for immersion: you have worse material farm system so you have to "interact with the locals". I believe the weapon switching system also results in probably the WORST and most restrictive decoration system since World.
The graphic is also really bad. Because it relies on realism, you have to crank your graphic to the max to see what it really offers, which you cant most of the time, cuz high graphic is really demanding. Even so, the artstyle direction is to gray and washed out, so little color to pop out even at the most vibrant area, that everything seems so dark and gray and dirty (not in a good way) and everything just blends in together for a very poor visual clarity. (If you don't trust me, go and fight a Gore Magala in Iceshard Cliffs. You will barely able to see it even though it's right in front of you)
The armor for me personally are really nice in concept, but have a few details that makes them really hard to mix to anything outside to its relative set. This is made worse by the fact that some armor you cant edit a large chunk of their color, because "you cant dye scales"
And that we havent even address the absolute elephant in the room, that is the long ahh page of hundred of DLC microtransactions on steam page.

But after all that one thing still remains is the core gameplay is really good, and Focus mode is a really nice addition to Monster Hunter that makes more clunky and passive weapons (especially GS) feels much more reactive. 
The story I think it's also best since World, with a lot of memorable characters with depth and interesting story to tell rather than just "monster act weird, go hunt monster", tho that's a good or bad thing really depends on what kind of player you are.
I think just the fight experience alone is the pros that outweight the cons.

In all fairness, MHWilds is a reall good game at core, tho I only recommend it when it's on sales or if you can run it. It's a shame that the game launch was such a disaster, but really 1-2 years from now everyone will realize that it's just another normal Monster Hunter title that ever existed.
I hope Capcom learns from this and do better in future Monster Hunter games.

50 hours into Monster Hunter Wilds and I’ve finally realized what it reminds me of: Dauntless and Wildheart… if both went through puberty, joined a biker gang, and started lifting monsters for fun.
As someone new to the MH series, I expected to get folded like a wet napkin. Instead, the gameplay loop grabbed me by the throat (lovingly) and dragged me into hunt after hunt like a toxic relationship I just keep going back to. The combat is addictive, the worldbuilding is weird in a “I probably shouldn’t drink the water here” kind of way, and every monster looks like it wants my insurance details.

The story though? Ehhh. It feels a bit “discount lore pack.” Not awful, just… there. Like the quiet kid in class who watches everything but contributes nothing.
Still, if you like big creatures, bigger weapons, and the emotional damage that comes from carting at 1% boss HP, this game is absolutely worth playing. 10/10 would get bodied again.

I’ve been playing Monster Hunter Wilds since release, and honestly, it’s been a ride. At launch, the game was gorgeous but super demanding — my old rig with an RTX 3070 could barely push 30 FPS. I ended up upgrading my PC just to enjoy it properly, and I’m glad I did because the visuals are stunning.

The early game felt pretty easy, and compared to Monster Hunter: World, the weapon and armor skills seemed limited. But I figured that was just because it was the start of the game. What really blew me away was the aesthetics — every map is beautiful, and the customization options are insane. You can tweak your hunter, your Seikrets, camps, even NPCs. It makes the world feel alive and personal.

Fast forward a year, and the game has improved a ton. Performance is way better (I’d say 20–26% smoother than launch), and the gameplay has gotten harder thanks to new monsters and events. Hunts feel more intense now, and I love that challenge. The best part is how the devs are clearly listening to feedback. They’ve been refining the game nonstop, and it shows. Credit where credit’s due — Capcom nailed the updates.

Now I’m just hyped for the upcoming DLC. If it keeps up the same level of polish and improvement, this game could easily surpass World in the long run.

[h1]Monster Hunter Wilds TU4.5 Review[/h1]

[b]TLDR:[/b] It has been a year since its release, and the game is great and now highly playable. There is so much content you can do in the game. Even if you are new to the series or a veteran try it out. If you played during launch and dropped it because of the performance issues give it a second chance because you might really like it now.

[hr][/hr]

[h2]Introduction & The State of the Game[/h2]
* [b]The Performance Turnaround:[/b] Launch performance was completely abysmal and left a horrible taste in many players mouths. It really should have been in a good state from the beginning. Thankfully after multiple optimization updates leading up to TU4.5 I can finally say the game runs smoothly and is very playable now.
* [b]Value:[/b] The content is so abundant it is almost overwhelming. No matter what price you buy this game at it is an absolute bang for your buck.

[h2]The Good: Combat, Monsters, and Story[/h2]
* [b]The MH Identity & Combat:[/b] The core gameplay has changed so much since MHP3rd but the overall combat is still as satisfying as ever. Optimized gameplay is buttery smooth and the combat is chef's kiss. It is a great evolution for the traditional MH Hack&Slash. Some veteran purists might prefer the original pacing and combo commitment but all I can say is try it and if it clicks then great.
* [b]Phenomenal Music:[/b] The music is phenomenal and fits every situation and environment. The music for each major monster is different and unique making it quite the spectacle.
* [b]Monster Roster & Story:[/b] The roster has amazing variety making it incredibly fun to fight new and returning monsters. The harder endgame bosses are just PEAK—wonderful cinematic and challenging fights blending set pieces and raw gameplay. There is more story now to progress the narrative and introduce the roster, and older fans will definitely love the characterization of returning characters.

[h2]Hunters, Gear, & QoL[/h2]
* [b]The Core Loop & Grind:[/b] The core loop of hunting monsters is satisfying and the endgame respects your time. You can avoid the RNG gear mechanics entirely and still clear the game. The base craftable Artian set and craftable charms are great upgrades if you do not want to farm for god roll talismans. Grinding for perfect stats is not for everyone but it is still a fun goal.
* [b]Equipment Freedom:[/b] There is so much cosplay and cool fashion available. It is a great blend of cool, cute, and silly/funny perfect for different tastes. Color customization is flexible and you are no longer restricted by gendered armor sets. If you want a fully armored gal or a sexy muscle man now you can.
* [b]Character Customization:[/b] The creator is incredibly detailed for making characters from fiction or reality. Even if you are not creative you can just search online for peoples character codes.
* [b]Support & QoL:[/b] You can have NPC support hunters help in battle and their carts do not count towards the quest failure. QoL changes make gameplay less tedious like easily reposting quests. Some might be divided on changes like Focus strike. Some love that you can adjust yourself during combos while others don't like it because it makes it easier to fight monsters.
* [b]Bonus Charm:[/b] The devs added small hidden things like the grandhub diva who gives a small buff after her performance or the secret bird's nest on the grandhub that gives you stuff from time to time.

[h2]The Bad: UI/UX, Network, & Nitpicks[/h2]
* [b]UI/UX Negative:[/b] The menus are confusing to navigate. It might be a skill issue in navigating the ancient forest on my part but it needs a touch up to be more digestible.
* [b]Cluttered Pop ups:[/b] Notification messages are messy. I want to know if I have a specific buff activated but it gets convoluted by notifications for things I don't care about right now. A filter would be great.
* [b]Default Controls:[/b] The default auto run for the Seikret and radial pop up for items feel clunky and need changing. Sometimes you do not even know there are much better settings because the UI/UX is convoluted, and I only knew about it because of youtube shorts on my feed. 
* [b]Investigation Quests:[/b] Great for saving world encounters but the limit of 50 is too small. Getting other players quests is pure RNG making it hard to get specific duo monster arena fights that you want to get. I hope we get a Custom Quest Maker.
* [b]Gathering and Delivery Quests:[/b] These shouldn't have been mostly removed as they gave players a breather to enjoy the environments. MHRise style event quests exploring the map would give more excuses to go straight from the Windward Plains to the Scarlet Forest without needing to fast travel.
* [b]Network Issues:[/b] I am not sure if it is because of crossplay or just server stability but I sometimes get disconnected suddenly leading to carts. We have decent internet and this doesn't happen in other games.
* [b]Missing Personal Rooms:[/b] I loved room customization in MHWorld and MHRise. Now it sucks that catching rare endemic life is just a number and a picture on my profile instead of something to display.

[h2]The Ugly: DLC & Monetization[/h2]
* [b]Overpriced DLC:[/b] Optional cosmetics are great but prices are a bit much. It also did not help that cosmetic DLC were released before the optimization updates which annoyed a lot of people.
* [b]Lore Behind Paywalls:[/b] Items like Alma's table give unique lore dialogue. It sucks that you have to pay to learn this lore. The basic version should have been free.
* [b]Character Edit Vouchers:[/b] Limiting edits to a maximum of 3 times and hiding further changes behind a paywall is very scummy. This should have been a completely free feature.

[hr][/hr]

[b]The Ultimate Verdict:[/b] Despite the issues and nitpicks I have this is a wonderful experience to pick up. Monster Hunter Wilds is a great game held back due to its rough performance and available content on release. The game was clearly rushed but as you can see with the constant updates the devs poured a lot of care and passion into this game. This is my biased view as a fan of this series since I was a kid, but I encourage you to try out the game. If you dropped it during launch because of the performance issues give it another chance to shine as you might just really enjoy it now.

I’ve been a fan of Monster Hunter since the very beginning, and I can honestly say that MH Wilds is very good. The improvements are noticeable compared to the first release. The gameplay feels much smoother now, and I’m not experiencing any lag or delays anymore. Great job on optimizing the performance. ⚔️

However, I noticed that the hammer weapon skins are a bit underwhelming. As a hammer main, it feels disappointing to see other weapons getting better and more detailed designs while the hammer skins seem less impressive. Would it be possible to create better hammer skins something similar in quality and style to the Shattering Greatsword and make them available as cosmetics? It would also be great if more hammer weapons could be added to the list in future updates.

I also have a few suggestions that I think could make the game even more exciting:

1. Guild House System
It would be amazing to have a guild house feature where the guild leader can purchase different house locations (such as Windward, Basin, etc.). Only the guild master, vice master, and officers should have permission to edit the main guild interior.

Additionally, each guild member could have their own personal room inside the guild house. Members could design their rooms however they want one could have a waterfall theme, another a desert theme, and so on. I think this would add more personality and make guilds feel more alive and interactive.

2. Large-Scale Hunt Mode (6–10 Hunters)
It would be exciting to add special quests designed for 6 to 10 hunters fighting a massive monster with better loot rewards. There could also be a competitive mode where teams compete based on how fast they defeat the monster. The top-ranked teams (Rank 1, 2, or 3) could receive exclusive rewards such as rare skins or special loot. This would encourage teamwork and add a competitive endgame activity.

3. Quality of Life Improvements
It might be more convenient to move the melding old woman to the Grand Hub so players can access her more quickly. The same goes for the NPC in Windward who brings the loot (I believe his name is Dogard). Having them closer together would reduce the need to travel back and forth repeatedly.

It would also be great to add mining spots for armor spheres and other materials, similar to how it worked in Monster Hunter World.

Overall, I really enjoy MH Wilds and appreciate the improvements so far. I’m just hoping to see more love for hammer users and some additional features that could make the game even more immersive and engaging.

I don’t think I’ve ever played a game that disrespects my time the way Monster Hunter Wilds does when it comes to RNG. I’ve hunted the same monster so many times I could draw its attack patterns from memory. I’ve broken every single part. I’ve captured it. I’ve carved perfectly. And somehow? STILL no drop.

How is it possible to do everything right and walk away with nothing but scales and disappointment? The “rare” materials don’t feel rare, they feel mythical. Like they don’t actually exist in the loot table and the game is just laughing at me behind the scenes.

You want that one gem? Cool. Be prepared to grind for hours. Days, even. Not because the hunt is challenging. Not because you’re undergeared. But because the drop system feels completely detached from effort. Skill doesn’t matter. Strategy doesn’t matter. Preparation doesn’t matter. You’re just spinning a slot machine after a 15-minute boss fight.

And don’t even get me started on getting duplicates of the same useless material over and over again. Oh great, my 27th plate that I absolutely do not need. Fantastic. Meanwhile, the ONE thing required for progression might as well be coded at 0.0001%.

The combat? Great. The monsters? Incredible. The world? Beautiful.
But the RNG? Absolutely brutal. It turns what should be an exciting loot moment into pure resentment.

At some point it stops feeling rewarding and starts feeling manipulative. Grinding should feel like progress. This just feels like punishment.

Fix the drop rates. Add some form of pity system. Respect players’ time.

Because right now? The real monster isn’t in the hunt. It’s the loot table.

Story: Welcome to the forbidden lands. Find this lost boy's parent and hunt each monster in ascending order of danger while we drag you from locale to locale- Um... You're not supposed to fall asleep during my explanation. (Absolute snoozefest)

Combat: You can stagger or down the monster by using a special move in a new focus mode! What did it cost? Everything got a little quality of life, but general movement and responsiveness went down as a result. If you're fighting multiple monsters, you're at their mercy and will be thrashed around and you will thank Capcom for this traumatic experience.

Performance: I'm having to rewrite this entire segment due to old stability issues resurfacing with a menace. I will put this bluntly so I don't need to rewrite this again, even if you have the top of the line, the threat looms of the gpu driver crashing. I have had a plethora of hunts end abruptly due to my GPU giving out leading to my vexing conclusion that the team behind the driver compatibility must have vibecoded it into being acceptable for the time of testing, only to be a liability later. Of course, this could be wrong, but it doesn't stop the game from throwing the gpu fail error up and wasting my investigation charges. Even on the lowest of settings, this game still manages to find a way to go "Oh oh! I can't wait another millisecond for this pixel to get rendered from this particle system! Time to ruin the experience!". When the game works, it can hold a stable framerate. A shame that it's at the cost of inexplicable crashing unique to this game.

Overall: If you want me to get the future expansion, don't expect to wow me with eye candy and sparkles, as all I'll see are more sources for GPU failures exclusive to the game. You can raise the ante all you want, but if it means the handler is nagging me about how to do the fight, I expect to be able to throw the handler in the fight and have them handle it in my stead. If you're gonna play this game, you'll have to look past the ugly bits and what you'll find is good. You'll need to enjoy it in bits and pieces and there's beauty and dopamine to be gotten in learning the combat (Especially the power clashes and parries). The sound design for ambience is very nice and sometimes it impresses me with how life-like nature looks that it makes me want to go outside.
I won't stop people from enjoying what the experience has to offer, though I would be remiss not to mention the bad.

Monster Hunter Wilds is not worth your time. Go play World. Or rise for extra charm and a faster pace. 

On launch, the performance was completely unacceptable. Textures not loading on low settings and models popping in as floating eyeballs during cutscenes. You could watch details load into the environment step by step during gameplay. Inexcusable. I'm told it's been improved significantly, in part by a patch that required players to manually update shader files in the file explorer.
Monster Hunter has always been weak on story (except 4U), but this is a joke. [Spoilers, I guess], but there's a kid whose entire culture is wiped out by a monster who then constantly places you in more and more danger, first by agitating said monster when multiple people warned him not to and with no plan to win or escape. Then you find out the monster didn't do any of the things the kid said but it is trying to kill you now and the kid stands in the way to defend it and almost gets you both killed again. The kid is wrong and spends all his time placing you in danger, but is also the main character and you're meant to care. I also find the concept of artificial life as described in this game completely uninteresting. [End Spoilers] The overall pace of the story is grueling. Walk and talks are constant and consistently uninteresting. Because you can gather instantaneously from range at full speed on a mount, I assume the walk and talks are meant to force you to smell the roses. I smelled way more roses spamming circle on a crack in the wall than I did listening to some guy (/girl/non-binary individual) spout off about how the ecology is actually really interesting instead of being encouraged to explore and discover all the little things the devs tried to put in an engine that can't handle them.
The gameplay is disappointing, to say the least. I appreciated the methodical pace of earlier Monster Hunter games and Wilds strays about as far from that rhythm as possible. Combat is fast and fluid to the point of becoming effortless and mindless. In the base game, there is nothing that can challenge the player in any way. The wounding system is significantly over tuned to the point that at any time during a fight, you have access to an instant knockdown button. Health values are low too, so a lot of fights feel like they just kind of end before they really even heat up.
Later title update monsters are more difficult. I fought seregios and lagiacrus and both were more challenging than anything up to that point, but neither got there in particularly exciting ways. Seregios felt weightless and the mandatory underwater section with lagiacrus is a pale imitation of 3U underwater combat (which I enjoy though most people do not). You can only really use your slinger to grapple onto lagiacrus, where he hits you no matter what move he does. The player doesn't really get to influence how the section plays out, when choice and personal style should be the thing that keeps you coming back to the same fights.
Speaking of grinding, there was none in the base game. Materials are so plentiful and gear costs are so low, that full armor sets and multiple weapons from any monster could be achieved in just a couple fights. Monster Hunter's gameplay loop has always been the grind and that simply doesn't start until endgame. It feels as if there is nothing to achieve or do until endgame.
I also strongly dislike the randomized artian and now gogmazios gear that are apparently the only gear that matters or is remotely difficult to obtain. There was a sense of purpose in fighting rathalos over and over to get one ruby and upgrade a sword, but the randomized rewards completely eliminate any relationship between the unique properties of monsters and the gear you use. Having a great poison weapon no longer requires mastering the fight with a poison monster and understanding what it's like to be on the other end of the stick. Now it just requires that you pull the slot machine 5 more times.
The minute to minute combat and the rewards for mastering that combat are completely alien to the core that I loved about this franchise. I've been told that archtempered monsters don't wound as easily and one shot you if your gear isn't at the endgame level, but I honestly can't be bothered to care. The reward is slot machine pulls, so why bother. The increased challenge is based on removing access to mechanics that were poorly implemented to begin with, but that the game itself is built around. The game got written into a corner and there's no way out that appeals to me.
I am aware that 64 hours of gameplay is actually a decent amount of time, but I'm telling you, as a 10+ year veteran, I only finished the game out of obligation to my favorite media franchise of all time. I gave the story a chance and listened to the characters. I was bored with hands off the controller for 20 hours. The rest was an unsatisfying substitution for something I should've loved. And for context, I have 200 hours from world and rise each, on steam, not to mention 373 hours on base rise on the switch and 777 hours on ps4 world. I bought both of those games multiple times despite thinking they were already losing some of the old charm and I played world for more than 10 times as long as wilds. The difference is that I could still boot up world and have goals to accomplish, but there's nothing at all pulling me back towards wilds. 64 hours, from someone who was willing to make a new MH game my only hobby for months on end, is an indictment. If you still want more context, I played 4U on the 3ds for 750 hours and 3U on the 3ds for 1000 (+ some wii u hours, but who's counting) and I'm about to boot it up after sending this review. If you're hardcore, my secret alternate recommendation for a better Monster Hunter game is 4U, it's the only game in the franchise with a story that's actually well written and makes a salient point about ecology, namely that rabies exists. That game also maintains the methodical pace I love with a lot of qol that previous titles lacked or honestly spit on. If it was on steam, I would go blue in the face telling you to pay any price for it.
There are some upsides, though. I appreciate the ability to use gendered armor sets on either masculine or feminine character models. Some pieces are a little too bulky or skimpy for my taste, so the flexibility is greatly appreciated. I was also very excited in the leadup to the game's release that they included some real freaks in the roster. The cephalapods are rad designs and some of the other monsters have a sort of alien feel that lets me at least keep a candle lit for the creature designers on the team. There's a dragon with fur, a viking helmet, and chains on his arms, a raptor/mosquito thing covered in air sacs, and a chicken that's just wrinkly and allegedly spanish. Hope the franchise doesn't die its seemingly deserved death before they come back in a title that's actually fun.

MHW is the red-headed stepchild of the recent entries in the Monster Hunter universe. It's attempted to take most of the highlights from the previous games and mend in into some form of a cohesive concept. However, it ends up resulting in a garbled mess that struggles to find a new identity whilst rewarding challenges in any form that makes progression feel warranted or unique. This is not a new issue with the Monster Hunter games, however the traditional MH sense of evolution feels all over the place in Wilds; making some areas (gem hunting is the most obvious with the new investigations) a joke to access while the classic RNG aspects of creating a perfect set have been relegated to a hodgepodge quest hopping for useless jewels and imperfect talismans galore.

In addition, this game only recently found its sea legs in terms of optimization. Most of my friends who played early on, some new to this series, gave up entirely because of how awful this game ran. It took the game devs over a year to address these concerns, but I suppose it's better late than never. This improvement largely came from the massive outlash from fans and newcomers alike, and they've tried to remedy this issue with new monsters and event quests.

With this new addition is Gogmazios. Every MH has "that" hunt which makes or breaks the game. It was Fatalis in Worlds, and Primal Malezeno or Risen Valstrax in Rise. There's even an argument for Behemoth in Worlds before master rank dropped. Wilds fumbles horribly here by making EVERY OPTIMIZED BUILD reliant on fighting Gogmazios. Unlike any of the previous games, you can now craft "Artian" weapons for every weapon in the game. That is to say, you can generally skip out on the massive weapon tree and its large grocery list of required hunts by inevitably heading towards a one stop shop craft for generic looking weapons. There is no reason to fight or hunt monsters for their weapon parts directly. It all goes into a forge and turned into oralbullshite before being consumed by Gemma for a milquetoast advancement of RNG riddled upgrades. It reminds me of the odd parts of an MMO's weapon progression, and it just feels so off especially when THIS PARTICULAR FIGHT is so damn obnoxious and seemingly required if you want to attack any of the big bad evil monsters within a normal time frame. 

Ultimately, this identity crisis leads to a subpar story (they all are bad but this one annoyed the hell out of me with the loud kids), some decent combat, great hunt variety (arguably the best for where its at by comparison to other games), okay weapons, sub-par layered armor, and a very confusing and non-productive gameplay loop which was once the meat and potatoes of "grinding" in these games. The monster parts just don't have as much necessity, which will be doubly true once master rank comes out. Factoring in that it's taken over a year for it to finally get some form of advancement beyond the Arkveld armor set, I'm left scratching my head with what they wanted to do here. They expanded on guiding lands  from World in a big way, but without making the environments worth exploring. They added a two weapon inclusion for each quest start, but there is currently no rhyme or reason for why you'd want to switch between weapons at all. The jewels don't carry over from one weapon to the other, so again it just feels like some small QoL instead of THE mechanic which this game focused on (as compared to wirebug or slinger/mantle customizations). 

Personally, I'd wait even further if you're on the fence. The story progression is ass and I'd - at this point - probably recommend Rise over this one just because its arcade combat felt so much more rewarding. The Great Sword feels amazing in this game if you're a sucker for unga bunga big slams. MHW is ultimately off kilter, and a weird entry that makes me question why they went so hard in some places and completely missed the mark elsewhere. Fun if you can play it with some friends, but the late game is not for casuals, or certain builds, at all; even more so than some of the other entries.

Gameplay is absolute top tier, SSS rank quality.

with 14 weapons, you are bound to find one that makes this game ultra fun to play for you.

3D engine: it's the RE engine, It works well, and scales well. I ran the game on a 6thread cpu with a 2080MaxQ, at 1080p. lowest settings. It's playable; but you do need a stable 60fps to enjoy the game fully.  There were at launch some issues, (I never felt them on my battlestation rig) but there were. A later patch added some settings to ease some very specific power hungry features (say the number of small animals roaming around for exemple) and those have helped a lot for people with rigs on the lower end of the requirements.
As a gamer with a powerful rig, i cannot really say anything other then this: even at 720p lowest setting, the gameplay is so fun the gameloop so addictive,  that the game is worth it. do lower you res if you have to, the game is worth it.

story: servicable, nice enough if you wanna pay attention to it, with a very nice payoff near the end of the MSQ. can be completely skipped and ignored if you just wanna go fight monsters, your loss imo, but enjoying the gameplay and gameloop do not require the story. The story do enhance the fun, for me. ymmv.

characters: they're fine, the game is not woke infested, it's body type A & B, and there is a character male who seems very feminine, but it's just a trait, not a plot point or whatever. it's fine.
woke filter: a tiny bit (body a&b) but it's fine.

sound FX/animation: the weapons sound fx in this game are absolutely insanely good and paired with
the animations of the weapons, landing a blow, on  a monster...  feels.  it just feels. You feel it when your weapon hits your target. There is no other way to describe this: the combination of soundfx and weapon animation in this game is absolute top tier. on par with say Demon's soul. but nothing like a souls game,

monster animation: again, top tier in its category. The ecosystem feels alive, the verisimilitude is perfectly achieved. when you are in that game, you are in that world, everything reinforce the escape and dive into that universe. it's very immersive.

music: i own the soundtrack on CD (6cd boxed set). It's fantastic. within the game it fits perfectly. outside of the game, because of your experiences within the game, listening to the music oustside of the game takes you back to those moments, and it's nice. Listening to the music can be very uplifting. Some tracks are on my gym playlist.

multiplayer:
the community is non-toxic. If you die in a quest, veterans will take the time to point to you things you can improove (gameplay or gear), and will offer to help you out.
the multiplayer gameplay is very satisfying: answering a distress call to help others, or have others answer your distress call feels is nice. 
The hub in this 6th mainline game has been revamped and there is something lost in the upgrade: kinda like the looking for raid option in wow destroyed one aspect of the community, the new hub with dozens of people did the same in monster hunter. Meaning in the 5th mainline game (MH World/Iceborn), I made friends while hunting, we built a clan and often played together. In the 6th mainline game (this one MH Wilds) this does not happen as much..
So gameplay wise multiplayer is good to fantastic ; but community wise it's a regression from Worlds.
IF new player, you'll find it just fine, if veteran player you'll find it a bit frustrating, but not a hindrance.

Ingame store / microtransaction:
THEY ARE PERFECTLY DONE. monster hunter is game as a live service DONE RIGHT. all mtx are cosmetics (visuals or music) none of the mtx impact gameplay, there are no boosters, nothing like that. There are no loot boxes. should you buy something from the ingame store, it will be because you want it, you choose it. no random stuff.


overall:
At full price, the game is worth its price.
And if you know you enjoy this type of game/gameplay or gameloop then the game becomes a must buy.

on sale, the game will be very much worth its price.

there are plenty of youtube videos of people having fun with the game, go watch a few, with hunters using different weapons. see if one weapon appeals to you. if you think it does, then you'll most probably have huge fun learning the weapon and hunting monsters.


one last note:
Monster hunter is a a roughly 20 years old franchise. In this franchise this is the 6th mainline game. The formula for fun has been crafted for 20 years, honed, perfected, tailored. it works, it's fun. it feels real nice. 

btw me I'm a fiver: I came into the franchise at monster hunter world (5th mainline game, 2017) I have since accumulated well over 5000 hours. And I'm still enjoying myself, still discovering new stuff, new adrenaline rush, new setbacks, new loss, and new wins... and I'm still asking for more.

With the recent performance update playing the game has become a much improved experience in terms of quality. It rarely crashes and overall runs much more smoothly than it used to. I'm able to have the game set so that it looks beautiful without frying my PC and having the framerate drop. Great job here!

It's clear that a lot of work and love has been put into this game and it's definitely a nice introduction to people who are new to the series (for example the many improvements in quality of life mechanics are great). Wilds is for sure it's own unique experience (like every Monster Hunter game, really) but personally I've had a ton of fun with this one and I would say it's one of the best ones in the series, especially with the amount of diverse environments and mosters presented as well as the amazing soundtrack that accompanies all of this. 

The story to me was mostly satisfying, though I wish it was a bit longer and more fleshed out in some parts. I was immensely happy with the expansion of the lore and I can't wait to see more of that in a future DLC. The characters in the story are all interesting and unique but once again I wish they had just a little bit more depth to them (though I must admit not all fans of the series care much about the story and npcs that much and that's okay! everyone likes the games for different things after all). I have to say that Wilds' plot to me had a similar vibe as MH4 (which is one of my favorites in the series) so I see that as a great accomplishment of this game!

Some people might say that Wilds is "easier than the previous games in the series" but personally I am satisfied with the difficulty here and I'm glad that I do not have to do the same type of unforgiving grind that was required in some of the older games (especially when playing them solo). I wouldn't say this game is "too easy" since with the new updates and event quests there are definitely some really good challenges. I would say it's just lost that frustrating grind part, which to me is a big improvement. 

Overall this game has a huge offer of things to do and I'm sure most people will be able to find something they enjoy, wheter it's newcomers or long time fans.

I am really disappointed to see this ongoing tendency in the game dev industry, because, to be honest, most of the triple-A titles are not looking better graphics-wise and have ridiculous hardware requirements, and the same problem is related to this game. It is still not optimized enough, and the most interesting thing is that the graphics in this game is not looking better than what Cyberpunk or Elden Ring can offer with their recommended hardware requirements.

Every update has been so underwhelming. I'm happy the game is more stable for those who hadn't been able to paly it, but the issues with the game are at its core. They learned the wrong lessons from World and doubled down on what made it bad.

It's harder than ever to play with your friends, as they continue to shove a story no one cares about down your throat. There is so much railroading that no one know how to play the game anymore. The main story is so easy that when you go online, chances are someone hasn't upgraded their gear at all and gets one-shot by every monster. And I don't blame them, because there are so many messages and explosions going out at the same time that I'm not surprised players get overwhelmed and just stand in the path of incoming death.

But despite the living ecosystem being World's main draw, this game feels the most artificial it's ever been. Every hunt will at some point have a scripted portion where the monster runs under a hanging rock, or a tree, or some other kind of trap. This along with the wound system, telling the player "Attack now" makes it feel like the game is playing you instead. This was a problem in World too, where the maps were so big that you didn't need to learn anything about them, the scoutflies always told you where to go. Every zone was a forgettable wasteland. But here, is it so much worse. Now your mount automatically takes you anywhere you need to go. The maps in Wilds are so unnecessarily large and full of special paths that only mounts can go through, that half of the gameplay is just watching a cutscene. Everything feels scripted and fake. Monster Hunter Rise solved some of these issues perfectly, with smaller maps and mounts that only you can control. I can't for the life of my imagine why they made them worse.

This game is really bad.

I loved Monster Hunter World. It's been a while since I played it so I'm not sure if Wilds took huge steps backwards or if my expectations for games has just gotten a lot higher since then.

This game is infuriatingly unresponsive and inconsistent with inputs. Sometimes you press an input and it goes off first try. Sometimes it doesn't go off until your 19th button press. The UI and inputs are all sooo clunky and laggy and unresponsive it is mind boggling. Did the developers even play their own game at all? I'd love to have one watching me play for even 5 minutes so I could ask them 37 times what the f* is going on between me pressing my button and the game just doing nothing or how I need to go through 5 different animations just to jump from one state to the next or 5 different menus just to swap an item from my mount to my pouch. 

The game is not setup for skill-based combat which is a shocker since this genre (souls-like action-rpg) is loved by its demographic specifically because of the skill-based combat. Fights are not setup to challenge you in any meaningful way. The damage you take over half the time is just random BS that the player really had no reasonable way to skill-check. To account for that fact, the devs decided to make everything trivially easy. Monsters do basically no damage to the player and have almost no HP. So you just close your eyes and mash your buttons and get hit by anything you want because none of it matters and you kill the monster anyways.

There are mods that you can install to increase monster HP and the damage they deal to you which is a good idea in theory, but then you just come back to the problem of half the monster attacks being totally random BS that you aren't able to see coming. It's not a skill-check it's just you taking damage randomly so if you use a mod to crank the difficulty up, you'll just get one-shot randomly.

I play these types of games for the high skill-based challenge, but Monster Hunter Wilds completely ignores players who come to the genre for that purpose. Which is strange since that is the core demographic of people playing games in this genre. Disappointing.
