Here is why:
Don't get me wrong, it's genuinely a good game. 
It just doesn't do it for me anymore, as a series title, like it used to.

The graphics are fine, a bit sluggish here and there. 
Environment looks wonderful, monster design is peak and the soundtrack is in it's best shape.
Our beloved Lagiacrus is back, which is a huge w and so is the original tri OST it comes with.
The weapons are mostly fun and armors looks splendid.

But the whole thing is so overtuned, it drains all the fun in minutes.
Not everyone has a tiktok brainrot head and needs constant stimulation.
The entire time something pops up, side messages being the most annoying.

A new event quest, a merchant is back, collect your items, do this, here is that, don't forget, a new glowing or colorful cosmetic is waiting to blind your face off, the next time you try to enjoy mulitplayer, and try the shop with 100+ items.
It is tiring man.

My biggest problems are things like, everything is shown on the map, like all of it. 
No more searching for herbs and ores. That's too lame, appearently. 
Why hunt monsters when you just can see them all the time?
Oh, I forgot, you don't have to run yourself, there is a Uber called Saikrii that just walks you there. And while you're at it, gather herbs with a slingshot. Bro.
It feels like it is forbidden to enjoy anything close to slower animations while gathering and stuff nowadays.

They made the hunters more mobile. Okay. 
Gave them a focus mode, which is really powerful and the only thing to give monsters their share of advantage is to speed them up on crack, wow.
I love methodical gameplay, this isn't and that's a me problem, I get that.
It doesn't even increase the difficulty, which is a whole other topic. It's just tiring.

The fights are visually impressive, audio is fantastic too but it is just so unnecessarily fast, so, that really no one gets bored of walking a bit between fights or getting to a safe space and sharpen your weapon.
Hop on Saikrii, heal and sharpen, back into action. Non stop. Don't get bored now. It is draining.
And while the majority calls it QOL and enjoys these changes, I'm struggling to.

Performance was never a problem for me, but a lot was talked about it and it seems to have improved I guess.
The multiplayer and group building is weird as hell and gave us some starting problems.
Server connection is bad for the most part, I don't care too much, playing mostly solo.
Story is ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. Nata, and for me personally Alma, is a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ nightmare, like, leave me alone, I don't need your ass 24/7 around me.

Favorit Monster: Arkveld, what a beauty.
Favorit OST: Uth Duna, it has such a majestic middle sequence.
Favorit area: Scarlet Forest, clear daytime. Sun Rays through the woods is just my jam man.

No more rants, just accept defeat and move on.

Swayed by the comments about improved stability and performance, I decided to give the game a go and I must say that I was not disappointed. Capcom sure took their sweet time optimizing the game, I am glad I waited for a year before buying it, especially considering the massive sale going on right now

However, if you are looking to buy the game, I´ll shortly brief you why you should/shouldn´t buy it. I am neither a newbie nor a veteran to the MH series, with several hundred hours in total on MH games I´ve played

First, the good

Story - Unlike MHW, which was the last MH game I´ve played, I must say that they actually took a lot of effort of delivering a nice story this time around. Sure, it´s still going around slaying monsters, but this game has animated cutscenes, voice acting, your character actually talks, as does your Palico. Not exactly story of the year material, but it is good and a massive improvement over MHW

Visuals - Much improved over MHW. The game looks stunning. It is very pretty and I love its visuals. Sure, visuals aren´t everything, but I have yet to see a game this pretty. It truly looks wonderful

Gameplay - IMO much more fluid AND much more quicker than MHW. Good ol´ MHW was already throwing monsters with massive HP pools during the mid-end game, so hunts took wayyy longer compared to Wilds. I averaged most hunts under 10 minutes, with a couple of exceptions that were closer to 15, mostly the final bosses. Most weapons got new moves to play with and while I haven´t mastered even my main weapon (Bow), I can say that it is much more fun and makes for an entertaining experience all around. I also like that resources for crafting are much more common and plentiful, armor spheres and gems and pendants are much easier to get, not to mention that the game gives you a lot more resources from each hunt, meaning that you no longer have to slay a monster 10+ times like some in MHW to get their full set. I usually only needed to kill each monster one to three times to get everything from their tree, which when combined with faster hunts means that the game doesn´t feel grindy and respects your time more than MHW did. At no point did I feel pressured to make a supply run every couple of hunts, I simply grabbed what was in my general direction and that was enough. Try it for yourself, you´ll see

Music - Was alright. I think MHW had a few more memorable tracks, but it was good nonetheless

Performance - As of 24.2.26 when I´m writing this, no issues whatsoever. No crashes. No lag spikes. No fps drops. Sometimes the character doesn´t react to my commands from the radial menu, but I am not sure if that´s on the game or if I´m doing something wrong. I was playing on RTX 3060 with high setting and had plenty of memory to spare. The game shows you how much of your card is used depending on how you set things up, so even if you play on lower-end pc with something like 1650 or 2060, you can still likely play it on low-mid settings. The minimal/recommended settings seem like a tall order and I was also questioning whether playing it on my pc would make for an enjoyable experience, given this game´s reputation for almost a year, but they truly did improve it and you no longer need a 3k dollar setup to have an enjoyable experience

Finally, the bad

The only bad thing I can say about this game is its length. As of writing this, I have finished the main story. Yes, I have not played the epilogue/high rank, but I finished what the game presents to be the main story. If you will stay anyway and will grind monsters until the next game comes out, it doesn´t really matter. If you are someone like me who puts in 50 to 100 hours and then moves onto another game and you see that the story ended just before the 20 hour mark, you might want to wait for a big sale before buying it. 70 dollars for 20-30 hours ain´t horrible nor great since most of HR is pretty much just - "Yeah, this monster, but stronger, drops better loot"

All in all, a 9/10 from me. It improved on all aspects, was very fun, the only critique I have is that there ain´t more of it. I wouldn´t spend more than 40 bucks on it, it´s great but I dislike how pricey the games got last couple of years. 110 bucks for the premium edition is just kekw for me

Great game with fantastic art direction. The gameplay is, though fun, not what I enjoy Monster Hunter for. A shift to timing-focused mechanics and focus mode itself both simultaneously take away emphasis on positioning. In most other games' hunts, the solution to avoiding damage was to simply get out of the way, which I found more immersive than parrying the creature. This gameplay shift is not complete, as that would completely remove Monster Hunter's unique traits of animation commitment paired with positional knowledge, which means that monster tracking isn't like souls tracking; it follows you with an attack, but not to a fuller extent. This means you get to dodge the attack through positioning usually, but the monster can also miss you if you are preparing or executing a perfect-timing mechanic and the aftermath or followup to an attack will hit after you're left an overcommitted sitting duck.
The game offers good monster variety to fight and they all have unique designs and ecological explanations. In all aspects other than the combat and occasional hiccup, the game is as immersive as it can get.
As of Febrary 18th, the game has been running pretty consistently at 60 FPS without framegen with a 3070 TI, so it has finally met my expectations. Now is a good time to play.

as much as I want to like the game, I cannot in good faith recommend it. This is for a litany of issues that I will detail.

PERFORMANCE: The game performs much worse than it should - even after the latest round of performance updates on 02/18/2026. I have a 9070XT, 9800X3D, and 64GB of 6400MHz RAM. I should not be having frame drops, inconsistent frame pacing, freezes, stutters and every other issue under the sun with my specs; but Wilds makes it so.


 GAMEPLAY: Until this latest round of Gogmagios and 10* monsters being added (lol), the game was piss easy. Focus mode trivializes everything in low rank and most of high rank, with the exception of 8*s and above that they added post launch because of how stupid focus mode and wounds you can inflict are.The issues that have arisen from focus mode being so utterly broken have led to monsters already having more HP than 4P Fatalis from Iceborne. It is a stark downgrade from rise and world to attempt to appeal more people, and it has utterly rejected me and many others who have enjoyed Monster Hunter for years prior.

Do not buy this game. Play Rise+Sunbreak or World+Iceborne, or go play one of the old world games like 4U if you have access to a 3DS or an emulator.

I’ve played a lot of Monster Hunter, but Wilds hits different.

The world feels alive in a way the series has never done before. The weather shifts mid-hunt, monsters move naturally across huge open zones, and everything flows seamlessly without breaking immersion. It doesn’t feel like “maps” anymore — it feels like an ecosystem.

Combat is still heavy and satisfying, but smoother. Every hit has weight. Every dodge matters. When a storm rolls in and you’re mid-fight with a massive monster, it genuinely feels cinematic — like you’re inside a next-gen boss battle, not just grinding for parts.

Performance-wise, on a strong PC, it’s insane. The lighting, scale, and particle effects make hunts look AAA-level every single time.

For me, this isn’t just another sequel.

Rating: 9.5/10 — Easily one of the most immersive hunts I’ve ever experienced.
It feels like the future of Monster Hunter.

There are some positives to this game. The way monsters interact with the environment is cool. Monsters fighting is cool. While grossly underutilized, herd monsters like Doshaguma and Hirabami are interesting when they can actually utilize the mechanic, but the alpha Doshaguma is pretty much limited to a heavily scripted fight and Hirabami are very easily separated and rarely coordinate their attacks. Xu Wu and Nu Udra are the most unique monsters the series has ever seen, and are engaging, interesting, and entertaining fights as a result. Arkveld is cool. Offset attacks are a cool feature that I hope returns in future entries. The new monsters in this game all have underutilized/ unique skeletons. Weapon swapping is the only part of the Seikret I will miss. Zoh Shia was so cool that the game launched without his armor set and you could only fight him once.

I waited until a few patches (and a hefty sale) to get this, and I'm glad I did. Because now I can give this a positive rating!

After over 1000 hours in MH:World, I'm finding Wilds a fun experience for the most part. Visually, a side-grade.
Things seem a bit easier than they were in World, but the combat is a definite upgrade.

I was a bow main in World, and the bow feels even better than before with the additional mobility we have in Wilds. You also don't need Crit Ele any more, so you're not locked into the ugly Rath set for half the game. We have a much better jump attack, and popping wounds is a fun (albeit OP) addition. DP-spam is finally a valid strat. Bows are in a fairly decent spot.

Though, FYI: if you're playing multiplayer as a bow user, maybe don't go quite so wound-pop crazy, as other weapon types need wounds to build combos.

Anyhoo....
Performance is fine.
Story is, ya know... usual MH story-level.
I don't like how the camera constantly robs you of control (it does this frequently), but I think that's just during the main campaign. Minor complaint either way.

All in all, it's pretty fun so far!


Edit: end-game is brutal, man. That difficulty curve is quite something!

After the recent performance patch the game runs very smooth with a few occasional crashes. I recommend trying it out, because it's a very pleasant experience now that the game runs smoothly.

As for the game itself, it's Monster Hunter, you probably know what you're getting into. If you're a newcomer however, this is by far the most accessible entry in the series. The sheer amount of quality of life additions make it so new players can have an easier time getting familiar with the game while the veterans can enjoy things that were lacking in the previous games. The story is very well written, albeit quite short. Overall this is one of the better games in the whole series.

Not recommended purely because I think there are better ways to spend your money than this.
If you're new to monster hunter and want to try the series, go ahead check out MH World or MH Rise.
The worst thing about Wilds is the bad, bad main quest. The story is exhaustingly simplistic, but that does not stop the game from serving it to you over and over. I felt like I spent 50% of my playtime wathing mid cutscenes and riding on auto pilot listening to the one-note characters shout their catch-phrases.
This shallowness is likewise reflected in the gameplay experience, with boringly easy monsters and gameplay systems so automatic and streamlined, that I wonder why the gathering and non fast-travel wasn't removed. The  sidequests are few and simplistic, so you effectively have to sit through the boring storyline sequentially with little agency, with NPCs frequently screaming at you "I THINK WE SHOULD GO THIS WAY" and turning your character around, even if you're in the camp area and think you can do something else.

The game is not a horrible experience, the environments are quite stunning and the way the monsters interact with the environmental effects is pretty impressive, as well as the quality of combat animations.
Thankfully the game does open up in the Hunter-Rank, effectively the post game, with new, harder monsters and freedom to choose your assignments.

It's not a horrible game, but I think it's just not that great of an (expensive) purchase. I pretty much recommend this only to to people who've already played World or other games in the series and are simply hungry for more monster hunting. Just keep in mind that it'll take you ~13 hours to get through the slog of the Low-Rank storyline.

I honestly think with all the optimization updates and the completion of base game, Wilds is absolutely a fun time. I found I'm actually enjoying it more than World's base game, and when I tried going back to it, I missed my seikret so much. I'm really hoping the expansion lives up to the hype.

Despite my praises, this is definitely a game you're going to want to buy on sale. If you manage that, it's definitely worth it if your PC can run it.

After playing the game for a year since launch, I can safely say that this is a great Monster Hunter game. It is a mix of World and Rise. Lots of mobility and QoL changes. The new wounding system is a neat addition and you can still get away with no using it at all. Compared to the other games, the story is more indepth. So take account that there will be more cutscenes that what past games presented. 

Now I am not blind to the technically issues that plagued the game. I suppose I consider myself lucky as the only issues I ran into were the weird triangles and shadows appearing in my game. After a month those issues vanished. The so called "the more DLC you have, the better your game runs" is also false. As I had none of those issues and I didn't the big DLC pack until Jan. From what I have heard, the recent patch that came close to the 1st anniversary solved a lot of technically issues for PC players. 

So if you're a veteran or a new player, Monster Hunter Wilds is a great addition to the series. Just have fun!

With the final AT monster out and the game going into stand-by and focus going towards the expansion; I can review atleast the base game.

TLDR; Atleast it performs great now, but it is not my favorite MH, but base game Monster Hunter games rarely are. Not even World was that great at the start, there was just alot more hype behind it so it felt like you were apart of a community.

And I think that's where Wilds ultimately falls short for me, the game was initially designed with the idea of going out into the WILD, so the game didn't have things like the Gathering Hub at the start and the premise was supposed to be a more out in the wilds surviving-game, but as development progressed I think even the devs realized that the engine just couldn't handle whatever they wanted to introduce, that's my feeling atleast.

And with updates they had to refocus the game back to the old systems of going into quests from a quest counter instead of hunting investigations in the wild and all of that was at a pretty big cost.

The removal of player hosted lobbies AND limiting how many players you CAN see at any given time at a lobby has made the game incredibly hollow. There is no feeling of a community almost anywhere unless you specifically search for it via Twitch Streams, Discord groups or make your own Squad and hope people discover it.

And to me Monster Hunter has always been at it's best when you have a feeling of a community, you are able to host custom lobbies where there's specific goals in mind. You can't do that in Wilds because every lobby is a bunch of random people doing a bunch of random things, so it starts to feel more like a solo experience.

Now, I am glad for the fact that I have a pretty tight friend group who loves Monster Hunter, so I was able to experience that community driven feeling through them, but as time passes and people's interests on the game wanes, so does my interest to play the game more actively, even though I would love nothing more than meet new people online and help out players in specific needs, but you can't really do that in Wilds. In a sense the game feels like it's the most isolating MH games to-date, despite it having lobbies that hold 100 people.

As for the games design, as I said they really designed the game to be more of a survival game at some point in development, this shows in the pop-up camp system being largely phased out currently with updates making them not only more robust, but for you to have more of them available. Pop-up camps were a relic of a system for a game that was never there, it would have made sense in a more open-worldly game, but as I said that clearly became a limiting factor.

I still do not like that they've phased out the need to actually keep track of monsters, I just can't get behind the idea of the hunter magically knowing constantly where the monster is. I disliked it in Rise, I dislike it here. The paintballs are in Wilds, but they really should have been for tracking, not pinging.

Gameplay is solid 9/10. I think Focus Mode is a mixed bag where alot of weapons either HAVE TO use it or suffer a huge DPS loss and on other weapons focus mode just allows purely more mobility for like 360movement during long windup animations and it really is poorly balanced, not only in terms of weapons, but in terms of what type of controls you use. Focus Mode has a very clear advantage on certain aspects; if you use mouse and keyboard as opposed to a controller, where it's really hard to do something on a controller and incredibly easy on a KB.

Wound mechanic is largely a bad game design, not necessarily because monsters get wounds, but because the wounds unlock weapons true potential. Certain weapons like Charge Blade, Insect Glaive, Hunting Horn just lean too heavily on wound breaks being their source of gauge fill or unlocking weapons unique playstyle more consistently. I was afraid of this during the beta and it's still in the game, and it just  ruins alot of the fun when you're just wound hunting to keep Pizza slicer up or something similar. The only thing Wounds needed to be were spots on monsters where the player dfidn't need Weakness Exploit to do big damage. But here we are...

In the end, the game has more potential than any other base game MH todate. That is a given, not only is the end-game of Wilds incredibly deep and you actually have to do some math if you want to figure out the best sets for yourself and not rely on online guides or community META sets.

So I am excited for the expansion, my only hope is that they refocus some of the community stuff into the game and one way to do that is to allow players to host lobbies with actual targets like World&Iceborne had. The endgame of the expansion is going to be out of this world insane, because we are already getting to the levels that Sunbreak had at end-game, not quite, but it's close, so I am hopeful.

In a perfect world all players would get housing back and housing could be used as a player made lobbies for 8-16players at a time and lobby host could allow all players to show their own house designs if they want to. That'd be amazing.

I'll be honest, I was hopeful for the optimization changes made by the Devs for this game. They did fix a lot of the issues from the launch and managed to improve the overall performance.

That being said, if we're talking about a normal PC port, the game went from complete utter garbage optimization to overall a bad one.

It's STILL very CPU demanding, 
STILL has huge frame drops in areas where nothing is happening,
STILL have bad texture streaming,
STILL need to play with settings to have constant frames, despite the game looking horrible with these same changes.

I do commend Capcom for trying to fix these problems, but fundamentally these will NEVER be truly fixed because REengine is amazing for linear games but AWFUL for open world ones. Dragons Dogma 2 died because of this.

If you only care about gameplay, I guess now you can play this game smoothly on a range of cards on the lower settings.

a great game, but not a great monster hunter. world and rise were indeed a departure to the franchise, but a much needed one, and still kept aspect of MH that made it still feel within the series. this game does not feel like monster hunter at all. the inertia of your character is all out of whack, focus mode cancels out a core skill in the franchise that is positioning and timing, animations of moves are all different in polarizing ways, like how superman dive spamming needs a weird stumble animation where you dont have i frames to finish before you can dive again/run away, (same thing for laying on the ground), and just sprinting in general has your character moving in arcs instead of straight into the button you press, BUT, for some reason, despite all these 'realistic' changes to movement, YOU CAN JUST TURN 180 DEGREES AROUND MID TRUE CHARGED SLASH. INSTANTLY. it does not make sense. along side this, directional aiming mid combo/move without focus mode, something MH has always had, just feels off. cant put my finger on it, but its like they want to force you to use focus mode to aim any attack. the UI is awful. completely cluttered on almost every screen, mid hunt with buffs/debuffs/shoutouts completely spamming the chat, elements just moving all over the place, important things like the hunt time moved, the quest log just being awful with no contrast and the grid is just so hard to look at and understand what quest is what without double takes, the weapon tree/armor list being cramped and needing horizontal scroll bars, and the sub menus on so many options that should just either be present information, or a keybind. not to mention the map, which previously was something you can use to just click on things in World, uses a controller scheme where you have to drag the map onto the cursor and also you can only see like one third of the map because of how much clutter there is. there are so many weird issues with this game, and honestly i dont feel like talking about how auto-play the game feels (people talk about how trackflys made the game too easy. dawg ive seen so many people get lost with TRACKFLYS, you cannot tell me that the seikret does not make the game much worse.), but i will say that almost EVERYTHING could be forgiven IF THE GAME ATLEAST RAN WELL AND LOOKED GOOD ALONGSIDE IT. Tl:dr; Game was mid. Devs were and are making updates. Game is now good (not amazing, just good). For veteran players of the franchise, this may not be the "definitive new generation Monster Hunter" game you are looking for. For new players that have never played Monster Hunter before, get the game. There are a lot of quality of life changes and difficulty (lowering) changes that make the game more accessible than ever. No need to worry about skill or knowledge, there is a great community willing to help new players.

As of posting this review (Febuary 20th 2026) the performance issues have been fixed.

The game is in a good state. However, I would personally rate this currently as a "wait for a sale" or a "wait for the incomming expansion" for veteran monster hunter players.

If you have not played monster hunter before then this is a good game to get you into the franchise.

The reason for this review is to point out a few things. These points are not things I view as necissarily good or bad, just things that should be noted.

1. The gameplay/story is extrememly linear. Despite one of the game's features being describeds as "open world exploration" the story is extremely linear and "railroaded". There are literal sections where you cannot do anything but watch as your character auto-paths and talks to npcs

2. The difficulty of the game is much easier compared to previous monster hunter titles. Part of this is due to the new "focus mode" mechanic, part is due to actual "boss" design. This is not to say that there are no difficult bosses. (Savage) Omega, [spoiler]Lagiacrus[/spoiler], and [spoiler]Seregios[/spoiler] are fun and engaging fights, but thats about it. The other "bosses" are not very challenging

3. Auto-pathing & Map design. While a lot of hate for the auto-pathing function I am personally indifferent. My problem is that I believe the auto-pathing is used as an exuse for "poor"/"lazy" map design. I have never hated manually nagivating maps this much. A lot of the maps have winding "branches" and "formations" that you climb on. [spoiler]ruins of wyveria[/spoiler] is basically just a spiral staircase and [spoiler]oilwell basin[/spoiler] has a literal fully functional elevator built into the middle of the map. Not to mention that a lot of the maps can get very dull and ugly depending on the "weather". Though it should be noted that the maps are conversly gorgeous during the "season of plenty" weather.

4. Wounds. the "wound" mechanic is essentially a weakspot a player can create on the boss if they repeatedly attack them in the same place. Players can also "pop"/"detonate" the wound to knock down the boss and create a dps opening. This adds to the second point, where the game is too easy. certain bosses can be chain staggered to a point where they literally spend almost the entire fight on the floor.

5. HP bloat. The point touches up on points 2 & 4. The developers seem to be listening to the players and are adding more difficult boss fights (which is good). But hp values of bosses seem to be going up at a staggering rate. Most end game bosses have more hp than Fatalis from "Monster Hunter World: Iceborne". Note that the game is currently still in high rank and curently does not have an expasion. Also a lot of the end game bosses ignore the knockdown of the "wound" mechanic or straight up heal their wounds. So I wonder, why even have a wound mechanic to begin with?
6. Sliding. If you hated the sliding attacks from "Monster Hunter World" they have also added it to this game.

7. Weapons swapping. Yes, you can bring more than 1 weapon into a hunt at a time. Yes, you can swap weapon on the fly whenever you want. No this does not change as much as you thing. A majority of players maximize their endgame builds for damage. This means that most players just end up bringing two of the same weapons as each weapon uses a different build to maximize for damage. There are currently only two bosses that recommend using the weapon swapping mechanic: [spoiler]omega[/spoiler] and [spoiler]gogmazios[/spoiler]. Both of them however requiring different elements. So most of the time people still bring two of the same weapon, just in different elements.

8. Skill tax. Certain weapons require certain skill to "feel" comfortable. Other weapons do not reequire certain skills to feel comfortable (this has always been the case for monster hunter, dmg vs comfort). This game seperates weapon and armor skills. As a result, certain weapons (specifically any weapon that can block) must fully commit to either be fully damage vs comfort. While other weapons do not have such restrictions and can fully build for damage.

9. The map tool. You know where all monsters are at all times now AND in the future. The map shows monsters that will spawn and where they will spawn. Again, I personally am indifferent but there are some people that dont like this. Personally, I'm curious as to how "invaders" are going to work considering you will know where and when they will spawn.

Genuinely the most disappointing Monster Hunter game I've ever played.

I've been playing monster hunter since 3u on the original 3ds. To say that these games have helped shape my personality would be an understatement. Monster Hunter is one of the only major series of games that managed to keep me attached by being good at its core principles: amazing combat, and incredibly distinct style and worldbuilding. Monster Hunter remains the core game of its genre, the king of the castle of hunting games. It blends its world to be cohesive with the act of hunting these massive creatures, and while its not always completely stable from a ludo-narrative perspective, its as close as you could possibly get to a perfect blend. 

Until this game came along and ♥♥♥♥♥♥ it all up. 

Monster Hunter Wilds is not a destruction of the game on a mass scale, but it does create so many cracks in the tried and true formulae of the series that it's impossible to ignore as a returning player. There are upsides to this game, yes, the onboarding process has been significantly improved, but it has come with detriments to nearly every system since World came out nearly ten years ago.

I even upgraded my PC to play this on medium graphics, only to have its slew of updates and drm render it nearly impossible to play for me now. It runs like ♥♥♥♥, as monster hunter games famously do. So I am also skewed against this for personal reasons, but I still beat the campaign, so I have enough experience in this to say that yes, this game sucks compared to its peers. 

The game's combat has been shafted with the edition of the Wound system, the main gimmick of Wild's combat. The ability to chain stun and go into uninterruptable animations while the monster sits there and freaks out is genuinely terrible. People have made it a point to say that the hunts last the same 'time' as any other, but the monsters have never felt more helpless than in this game. The solution? Add gimmicks. One hit kill moves, bad damage scaling, raid mechanics. These are the only true challenges that the game presents, ♥♥♥♥♥♥ gimmick mechanics that do not fit into the core system of a standard hunt. It's unbalanced dreck in both directions. At the same time!

The map design is the worst its ever been. Monster hunter maps all have specific and distinct color pallet that give the feel of unique biomes rife with flora and fauna that FIT INTO the setting. They're so memorable that I can name them off the top of my head: World had Green/Brown, Orange/Green, Red/Black/Yellow, Blue/Pink, and White/Orange/Red. Each map used each piece for different functions and hunts to create a cohesive ecosystem. Worlds went further and even added endemic life, which stood out in the environment, but still remained cohesive.

Wilds attempt at creating a cohesive ecosystem was to build a map and add seasons to them. This, if implemented properly, would have been the best addition to the games in general: creating essentially separate maps within maps to add more monsters to them, ones that you wouldn't normally see in the 'standard' map. 

Wilds did not put them in properly. Seasons range from unnoticeable to literally nothing, and the only real thing they bring to the table is neutral grays and browns on an already mediocre smattering of color pallets. The endemic life in Wilds as well is more obtrusive than it is natural. It seems like 90% of endemic life was added solely to provide buffs to you while hunting. In conception, you'd think this would make them more endearing, but all it does is add large glowing distractions that stand out in a near cartoonish capacity to the environment. Endemic life didn't NEED to benefit you while hunting, it was set dressing and an extra bit of content to do while playing. By trying to tie it closer to the core game, both create unwanted friction that pulls you out of your immersion. 

More still, the streamlining of tracking monsters has been trivialized and made obnoxious. You never develop an actual feel for the zones you're traveling through, you just ride along, bumping through every invisible wall in existence. It sucks.

Finally, the story. Oh my god the story.

So monster hunter is a long running series, and like any long running series, there's a set of in-jokes and playful sentiments that form around the games. One such sentiment is the idea that monster hunter games are 'all the same' as far as story goes, wherein there's a big monster ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ things up that you have to go kill, and that's it. Another such sentiment is that the setting exists in a world 'on the shoulders of giants' where there was a civilization connected to the monsters that existed in the past, but is gone now. The games tease this, the player base tease this, its all copacetic. 

Wilds story was like watching a newly hired writing team blunder in on 20 years of this sentiment and decide to push it to the forefront of the games in the most obnoxious way possible. Characters are annoying, unlikeable, and pointless. Your Handler Alma(the companion character that gives you quests) is so much nothing that it makes me wish for World's handler, because at least she has a personality. Every single person in this game is this upbeat peppy drivel that feels canned beyond imagination. Your own character included, because for some reason every annoying person in existence just couldn't stand the idea of a silent protagonist over "I'll save the day!" "Don't worry, I got this!" every twelve seconds. 

The single most cringe edition to this game is the Handlers new use of 'authorization': where every. single. cutscene of a monster or being in danger has them point like they're this awe-stunning power and saying "I permit you to kill this thing!" It's nonsense. It's treated with such reverence and its NONSENSE! It's a defiling of the whole core premise. You aren't on a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ nature reserve, you're in the untamed, untouched wilderness about to be attacked by monsters, and you need a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ bureaucrat to come over and go "erhm, you're all good bro!"?! In previous games, the handlers served as ecological researchers that observed and took notes on what you were doing, and that was perfect, it was the most "if its not broke, don't fix it" thing that monster hunter ever did. Trying to solve the minor issue of the dissonance of killing hundreds of these things isn't worth lampooning every cool emotion you could possibly feel.

And that's the game. Monster Hunter Wilds, for this returning player, was taking every cool emotion I wanted to feel and lampooning them beneath bloated mechanics, unwieldly dialogue, and bad design. This will be the first monster hunter I've ever skipped the expansion for. This is the first monster hunter I will not play more than a hundred hours of. I could go on for days with how many pieces of the puzzle they ruined for this series, but these main points should be enough for you to get the picture. 

If you're a new player and this vibes with you, or you thought this game was great, don't feel bad. This isn't meant to detract from your experience, especially if you enjoyed your time. But when I say it could be better, I say it with every piece of my love for the series.

For a more in-depth review:
I'm a monster hunter veteran from Tri and especially 3U and can say I've been enjoying the hell out of this game. Some things aren't perfect but overall I think it's a fantastic entry in the series and even better than World in a lot of of ways.

The hunter controls better and more smoothly than ever, and all the different weapon types feel very effective and great to use and have awesome new moves and improvements (really happy they went back to World's bow moves after Rise kind of re-nerfed the bow...). 

All the different environments are great and feel very distinct, and the new weather and season system can really change up how hunts go, as well as give a nice change of scenery. There seems to be even more of a focus on environmental tools, traps, and endemic life you can use during hunts too which I always enjoy. Even the Grand Hub feels awesome and is gorgeous, though I do miss the house from World that you could customize pretty extensively.

As for monsters, I feel like the roster is pretty solid, even if a bit sparse still. Lots of my favorites are back, like Gore Magala, Lagiacrus, and Nerscylla, and some of the new monsters are fantastic, especially the Apexes and some others like Chatacabra, Ajarakan and Lala Barina. Plus, the new Cephalopod class is very cool and certainly different. Zoh Shia and Gogmazios are awesome too but we definitely need more Elder Dragons. 
There are some monsters I'm not super impressed by though, like Quematrice (decent design but such a forgotten pushover), Doshaguma (surprisingly fun and challenging to fight, especially the Guardian version, but the design is just a lump of hair...), and Hirabami (interesting design but does it even attack?? I just completely steamroll it every time). Some of the older gen 1 and 2 returners are just ok too, it's good to see them again but there's nothing really impressive going on there.

There are a few relatively minor things I don't like about the game though:
First, I'm kind of mixed on the Seikret. It's great to have a mount to get around faster and to get through tough terrain, as well as carrying a secondary weapon for you, but it's kind of insane that it can just autopilot you to the monster. I liked the Palamutes better in that regard; they're fast and agile but you still actually need to drive it yourself (also doggies are the best.)
Second, the game really seems to shower you in materials, even rare ones all the time. This could be seen as a good thing as it reduces the grind, but that's always been a part of Monster Hunter and getting enough materials to make a full armor set after like 2 or 3 hunts just feels wrong. Wounds a big part of this I think, since breaking one always gives you some materials.
Lastly, the aforementioned sparse roster. There are a lot of cool monsters here, but it doesn't feel like many, and some are just reskins/harder versions of the same monster. Guardian versions are cool but don't really feel that much different from the normal monster, and Tempered and Arch-Tempered are really just the same monster but with bigger numbers (and OCCASIONALLY some new moves).

Overall though I love the game and will continue to play quite a bit!! I'm super looking forward to the upcoming expansion too; I can't wait to hear more!

This game is both a must-play for long time veterans of the Monster Hunter series as well as a fantastic intro point for new players. The Focus Mode mechanic both adds another layer of complexity and QOL to the game and makes certain weapons easier to play for new players (looking at you Greatsword). While the game started out with some pretty major performance issues on PC, these have all been resolved, and now with the final Festival going on before the long wait for the DLC it's a perfect time for new players to engage with all the game's content while the multiplayer base is still going strong. Only ding I give is the same ding I've given to every main series game since World: if you're a returning veteran trying to get through the main game story as fast as possible, it's tedious as hell.
there was kind of a rough release and performance issues but at least for myself those have been mostly resolved and this is a fun monster hunting experience.

I bought this on sale for 45% off and it was very much worth it.
I played since Tri and while a fairly different game now it still is a great experience. There is a lot of quality of life now and not a lot of meaningless early grinding (there still is lategame grind) which some people dislike but honestly im glad to skip two hours of gathering blue mushrooms and honey in the ancestral steppe...
Switching weapons mid fight is a great addition in my opinion and adds a lot of flexibility to a hunt while not feeling OP. You can bring a experimental weapon build or something you are not comfortable with to try out and if it doesnt work out you can just use ol reliable!
The new weapon moves and general fluidity is just amazing and i love it all.
New monster designs are pretty nice although some new monsters kinda lack a face for lack of a better description? They kinda feel a bit like souls or bloodborne bosses rather than proper monster sized animals.
Performance was always an issue with this game since launch but now it runs on the steamdeck (turn upscaling off so it doesnt look like a smudge) At a reasonable 30 fps! On my pc it runs great as well.

Overall, its good, go get it while on sale (I HATE NATA)

I've held off on making a review despite loving this game because previously I felt like its performance issues were unreasonable for the average person, now however the game in its content complete form with the recent performance patches is an excellent experience. It isn't without its failings, however there is no perfect Monster Hunter game, as much as some would like to convince you their particular favourite one is without flaw.

This game has a large amount of negative buzz around it due its messy release and 20+ years of series expectations, you have many other reviews to check for that so instead I will sing my praises of this game: It has one of the best base game monster rosters in the series and it feels like combat design has been adjusted to make things flow in a satisfying way ; The focus system adds further player combat expression with a particular highlight of the system being wounds that only appear after monsters perform certain attacks which further incentivises learning monster moves/behaviour ; Lots of fantastic new music with some outstanding new additions to a series with a staggering musical history ; A step in the right direction for Monster Hunter storytelling, there is still plenty of room for improvement (especially in execution while playing the game in a co-op format) but I appreciate the effort.

I have great expectations for the upcoming DLC as this game is a superb foundation to build upon and I look forward to the future of Monster Hunter in general. I do hope for a return to some of the classic friction from older titles but I imagine it may be hard from a design philosophy standpoint to reverse the quality of life they've brought into the series recently seeing as it has coincided with a big uptick in sales, we shall have to see what the future holds.

Now that the base game is finished, I can finally write a small review.
This game has ruined MH and has no redeeming qualities for the Ultimate version.
Where to begin is genuinely a difficult question; let's start with the -limited- qualities of the game.
I love this game Hammer. The Cephalopods are a really nice addition, new skeletons are always welcome.
Andddd that's it !
Now let's take a look at the bad : focus mode, wounds, monsters AI, maps, quests, content, atmosphere/world building... Let's stop here. I think this game have no qualities and being an MH just makes it worse, since when have the player base expectations been set so low ?
I will only mention the performance issues since it's an external factor to the core game design, which is the real problem here... So yeah the game doesn't run, at all.
The addition of a new weapon is also lacking, for 5 gen I can understand, now there is no excuses.

Starting with the most problematic aspect of the game, the gameplay.
Let's start by understanding what makes MH gameplay good in the first place : positioning (where your character stands), spacing (how will you use your weapon moveset according to the monster moveset) and timing. This is the core gameplay of ALL the games before Wilds; this is why the games are deep and interesting in the first place.
What does focus mode do to this ? Well, since you don't have a reason to NOT attack anymore, you just spam buttons nonstop and move around the hitboxes while attacking. 
Dealing damage is a noncommittal action. Let's take AED (thanks Capcom for killing CB once again btw) for example, a really slow attack making your character go forward. Before Wilds, you needed to adjust yourself in advance so you could snipe hitzones without getting punished. In Wilds, you just throw it randomly, your initial position doesn't matter, just focus for the head. An hitboxe is coming ? Well your AED is moving, so just focus mode out of the hitboxe. You're late ? Don't worry, just keep attacking to dodge. Every ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ interaction in the game is just focus mode out of your problems. Play without it ? Yeah, I agree, just play another MH ;)
This is part of why the game is so uninteresting, I won't cover the control you have over the monsters with wounds and environment.

I have to speak a few words about the bestiary of this game : all the ATs are just worse versions of the base monsters, Omega and Gogmazios are EXTREMELY badly designed monsters (both are solid contenders for worst fights ever), frenzy is not a fun joke and... Hirabami ???
The monsters also have terrible illnesses, they can't stay for more than 2 seconds in the same area due to their ADHD and when they're low HP they develop hyperactive behaviour and start running to the other side of the map. Don't even think about stopping them, if you try to use lure pods, they will grow tired of you quickly. If you try flash pods, it turns out the monsters are blind. Only the gentle caress of death (or actually sleep most of the time) will stop them.

The maps are the worst ever made, I suspect because of Seikret. Those are gigantic, empty and without any identity; most of the time even ugly thanks to the cool feature of turning off all the colours on your screen, "weather".
Gameplay wise it's not better, dunes, ledges, added interactions ruining the flow of the fights, tiny arenas... You have every issue you could ever imagine ! Enjoy poor gameplay with bonus free struggles fighting the maps.
I actually do really love the feature of pop up camps, you can set spawns on set locations, but not all of them, and sometimes they will get destroyed for no reason. In other words, you have multiple spawns, like before, except they disappear for fun I guess ??? Great idea.
Seamless is a great idea for immersion, no one ever said (except on twitter of course).
The world have never seemed so tiny since then, you have no room for imagination since you can "explore" (huge word for how bad the level design is) everything.
The villages are just 3 tiny boxes with the usual facilities, no unseen, no identity, nothing. There is Suja and the hub, which are the only 2 okay villages, the rest is a joke.
They are the same template with different ground texture, I can't believe in it, I'm just out.
Look at Yukumo, a tiny village lost in the mountain with hotsprings and cozy music. Look at Val Habar, a huge portuary city in the desert, so huge and crowded you see npcs walk offscreen. I can dream standing in any village except the Wilds ones.

This is also duo to the ost of the game being so poor, the only bad MH in terms of music, they are even annoying. I quickly get a headache hearing the Arkveld theme. At least Zoh Shia have the only good one in the game.

The endgame system is once again random generated quests with impossible RNG turning the game into a cheat mandator... A casino.
For the love of gods please stop with the random generated quests, it's been atrocious since their introduction in MH4. No one wants quests lock behind a useless farming wall.
It's even the worst ones by the way, resets by sleeping is by far the stupidest mechanic ever made.
All of this to get quests without a cool thematic title and description behind.
At least there is now some quests in the game, not a lot but they exist, only after a year of lifespan ^^

On the topic, the TUs are not free, the game at release was 70€ to get access to way less content and a worse game. TUs are included in the price since World, so they better keep doing them and raise the quality since the game is even more expensive than before... With a LOT of micro transactions.

I could go on for hours thinking about how bad everything is in this game, but this small review was just a cathartic exercise for me. I've been playing MH for more than 10 years almost permanently. I did speedruns, irls, tournaments, community events... I made a lot of friends along the way.
Seeing MH dying this way is something hard to accept for me.
Please do not buy this game, do not support it. Go play Rise, Portable 3rd, Frontier or any other game deserving love instead.
By the way, if you want a good story, play a visual novel like Tsukihime/Steins;Gate or read a light novel like Mushoku Tensei/Re:Zero, do not play a gameplay orientated game. It sounds obvious to me but not for everyone I guess, especially Capcom it seems.

[h1] My Monster Hunter Wilds Experience [/h1]

[h2] The Demo [/h2]
When the demo for this game became playable, I was worried that I'd never be able to play the full game due to how bad the performance was, even at the lowest settings. The game felt super under-baked and clunky which was also a worry due to the game not being super far off from release. Despite the demo scaring me, I still bought the most expensive pre-order bundle, as I've been a fan of the franchise for years and had hope that the full game would be better.

[h2] Full Release [/h2]
Upon release, the full game didn't run very well no matter what I did. It was more playable than the demo, and I could live with it because I enjoy Monster Hunter for the gameplay, not the graphics. Besides the performance, this game does feel really easy at times, some of the UI is clunky, and some parts of the game just don't feel fully baked yet. This game does do a lot of things well, though, and some things are done even better than in the previous titles.

[h2] Improvement Over Time [/h2]
This game has never by any means been the perfect Monster Hunter game; however, Capcom has been going out of their way to improve on a lot of the issues that players have had, as well as pushing lots of new content and higher difficulty parts of the game to make the experience better for veterans and more hardcore players. Just recently they even released a pretty large optimization update that not only made the performance better than it ever has been, but it also added a ton of new content to the mix. Prior to the recent update, I struggled to hit above 30 FPS on anything higher than medium/low settings; now I can play at a consistent 60+ FPS on primarily high settings, which is absolutely amazing!

[h2] Final Thoughts [/h2]
Overall, this game started off in a bad place, and a lot of players will continue to despise it because of that; however, it has evolved so much since launch, and I believe that Capcom will continue to try their best and improve where they've stumbled. We still have a lot to look forward to with this game, and I'm sure Capcom has a lot cooking in the oven right now. Previously, both World and Rise had massive content expansions that were released a while after release, those being Iceborne and Sunbreak. Those expansions added a ton of content but also fixed issues and improved the performance of both games. Wilds will most likely be getting an expansion eventually, and I'm hoping that Capcom continues to deliver on improving Monster Hunter: Wilds and making it the best game it can be!

Being straightforward, as of February 2026 the game is a solid entry in the franchise and has enough content now post-launch that any player looking to get it has enough to chew on for a while.
A lot of the criticisms are still valid in regards to things like the wounding system, difficulty or lack thereof until endgame, and things like multiplayer connectivity.  However, many of the issues we saw on PC as far as performance, optimization, and crashing, seem to have gotten a lot better with the updates over the course of the first year.  Perfect? No, but it's a good game as it is and it runs well enough for me to feel comfortable getting new friends into it.  The additional paid content doesn't provide any sort of advantage and the gameplay is consistent with what I'd expect from a Monster Hunter game. Cross-play is a plus when you're not dropping connection to the host, so no matter where you or your friends are you can all hunt together.  We'll see what the expansion has in store for us at this point.  Happy Hunting!

I am aware of the terrible optimization issues that this game had on launch, but now that it has been almost completely sorted, this game has shaped up into a game that eclipses base World in quality, both in area-design and roster diversity. While it has a slightly smaller monster-pool compared to base World, it has far less forgettable wyverns, thanks to its goofy event quests and unique armor transcension system. My only real complaint is the Seikret. Less reliable than wirefall, and disengages you from the areas that are so lovingly built with so much detail. Some of my favourite moments are just wandering around the cliffs or the forest, with no real goal. I found some really neat areas that normally you just wouldn't find. It saddens me that I don't know the scarlet forest like the back of my hand, an area which I love, in the same vein as I do the ancient forest from World, an area which I hate. I kinda wish they just ripped the wirebug mechanic from Rise, and maybe even the palimute.

Monster hunter wilds is a good game, but not a good monster hunter.

There are numerous things that makes the game fun (like improvement on weapons move set, barrel bowling, returning of beloved monsters and so on) but equally if not more so, removes the engagement that the hunter has in their environment.

 There is no sense to prepare yourself onto a hunt in wilds unlike previous titles. The games normally punishes you for not being prepared for the fight that you can encounter with some of the monsters, but in this game its a non-stop boss rush and you don't need anything to stop you to going to one monster to another. 

The only time the real challenge comes to play is at the post end game segment meaning that if the player really wants to find something that has to test their skills with the monster whether that be of their attacks or setting traps and bombs ahead of time they would have to grind it out in almost autopilot state of being. This may be my criticism for the game feeling just too numbing, that could also be the seikret problem as well since it literally autopilots you to whatever destination you marked on the map.

I will also mentioned that this is the worst low rank experience in a monster hunter game I've played to date. I hate how linear the game feels not letting me make my own choices with what monsters I want to hunt. I miss rise's mechanic where you can immediately hop onto a hub with friends and pick whatever monster you want for key quests not wanting to bother with the story because at the end of the day I just want to hunt some cool monsters with friends and how this game sets up it really doesn't feel like the game encourages you to do so.

(I don't think my words would relate to many people but that's how i feel, I still like the game I just wish the game gave me the freedom and critical think to approach the problem with a monster and be rewarded with a smooth hunt when having the right preparation. I'm also an individual that likes monster hunter freedom, so take that with what its worth.)

Hopefully the G rank expansion will help aid the problems I've brought up, if not I'm still looking forward towards new monster hunter titles and expansions, just please make sure its well optimized when at launched.

TLDR:
Games fun, but feels too autopilot and because of that my brain goes numb.

I have been a fan of the monster hunter franchise from since 4U released, with hundreds of hours spent between that game and worldborne. While in the run up to wilds' release I was anticipating to play it more than I have for any other game before (I went out of my way to build a new rig in part so I could run the thing), after 100 hours of playing, I am sick of contending with the continued issues that prevent me enjoying this title. At the start of the game's launch crashes were infrequent but impactful enough to sour my opinion slightly, but the bulk of these went away as time went on, and I was quite happy with most of what the game had to offer (the story and voice acting I felt were quite weak, but nothing worth getting hung up over, those are not really why most people play the games in this franchise). 

However, around the release of TU3 I stopped playing for several months, due to not liking the content introduced (purely personal preference). After a few months I came back in Feburary 2026 to try the final Title Update and see how much the game had improved, but was slightly annoyed to see that now the game is regularly crashing for me, even when I am merely adjusting graphical settings in the main menu. I am disapointed that even after efforts to improve the poor perfomance, the playability of the game is now somehow in a worse state than it was for me 6 months ago. I am giving this review as a hope that these performance issues will be solved before the release of the expansion, as there are some aspects of Wilds that are genuienly some of the best realised in the Monster Hunter series (visuals, music, monster variety). Yet I cannot recommend this title to many interested newcomers as of now, thanks largely to the dumbfounding inability to get the performance working consistently for many people, which is a shame.

Wilds is a fundamentally broken game that shifts the gameplay from Monster Hunter to Monster May Cry. It's cool that they've kinda fixed the performance, but it's still not great. But that's unimportant, because we have 40x framegen and can upscale from 240p for a definitely equivalent experience. What is important is the gameplay. It's been completely neutered down from previous entries into nothing more than a boss rush title, but because hunters now have so much available in their kits that's not as fun either. I've still gotten a lot out of Wilds just because at a base level it is entertaining and well made, but that alone does not make something good. 

The game is completely noncommittal. With almost zero exception, there is no fight taking place where you cannot simply run away to restock on items. This isn't necessarily an issue, but when you have a Seikret that allows you to ignore just about everything in the battle and heal up and sharpen at your own pleasure, the pacing takes a hit. Nothing is really dangerous unless you have a severe case of tunnel vision or are being faced with an instakill aoe attack, And then, when you play completely braindead for that entire time and take so much damage you run through all your potions, you can continue to ignore the fight, fast travel to a camp, and restock on literally everything. With the open world format this is kind of an unavoidable issue but it's made worse by the convenience of being able to place camps close to wherever you think you'll be fighting the most, reducing the amount of time that it takes to get back in the fight when you do restock. So because of all that, Wilds refuses to punish the player for playing poorly with a failed mission and instead pats them on the back as long as they can figure out how to almost never die because resource scarcity is a thing that just doesn't happen.

And then I still end up playing with people who have never played a video game before and end up failing quests anyways.

I never elaborated on the other elements, so I'm editing this now cause I remembered. The story is fine, I liked it and it's fun to go through but I'd be hard pressed to call it good. Music is great as always. But I have a bigger thing I want to ♥♥♥♥♥ about now: the gathering and preparation loop. It's been neutered with convenience after convenience to make it less of a friction point than ever and while I can appreciate wanting to reduce the friction since it can be a pain of the ass, it turns into a death by a thousand cuts scenario where you just don't need to ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ gather anything any more. No paintballs or psychoserum because you already know the monster's location 24/7, infinite whetstone use, and because hunters are already so powerful and have so many methods of toppling or staggering monsters you don't need traps for anything but capturing and flash/sonic bombs are worthless to bother getting. On top of that, gathering itself is trivial now. It's borderline instant to do and you can pick up just about everything from 20 feet away, and the seikret is so ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ fast that density isn't an issue.

This isn't necessarily an issue brand new to Wilds. World "solved" tracking by turning it into a legitimately engaging and more realistic loop of tracking down the monster, albeit highly simplified, and Rise just tells you where the monster is. But like I said, every convenience and simplification just gets piled on all the rest, and it makes it nonexistent. I'm not gonna pretend like the preparation loop is anything more than friction. The game wouldn't be better if we all returned to MH2 and spent 5x longer gathering and combining ♥♥♥♥ than actually hunting, but it still represents a gradual whittling down of the series' identity into AAA sludge.

Do I recommend this game to anyone.....I would give it a rating between “kinda” and “it depends”. This game is sort of a mess if you are looking at it as a game alone. I am NOT going to praise this game that took about a year from its release to actually be in a playable state, something that should have been the case when it was released. Is this just the culture now? Come out with a game that definitely shows aspects of being unfinished, and hope that your fan base turns a blind eye to it, or at least just deals with it.

You have individuals saying, “Well, it's playable, so stop talking objectively about it now.” Honestly, no, I won't. I can be a fan and still give criticism. There are some clear design choices about this game that I find questionable as a whole, from this base Monster Hunter to its last base title update. The overtuned wounding system, the balance between monster and hunter, the lack of a gathering hall on release, the slot-machine-induced endgame, and the overall tone of what monster hunter is now. This is coming from someone whose first Monster Hunter game was Monster Hunter Freedom Unite. 

Overall, it's a fun game, but frustrating. Time will tell if it actually becomes a “Great ” monster hunter game. Soon enough, the expansion will release, and they will either fix some of the design choices or maybe they will double down. Who knows. But for the product we have now, it's a fun, at times messy, game. 

Also forgot to mention that the monster cash shop won't get better over time. Locking things behind a paywall that used to be obtainable through event quests in other Monster Hunter games is scummy as hell. But since many people do purchase from it because of FOMO, it's only going to get worse.

Wilds while not a perfect game (no game is perfect), still a very interesting and full of potential. As the flagship of 6th generation of a such longstanding series like Monster Hunter, Wilds with a ton of new features never seen before in the series along with a new game engine. Open world map (kinda), new combat mechanic in Focus Mode, a new method in traveling with the Seikret, weather system etc etc make Wilds a fresh air for the series. Monster lineup for Wilds also very very good even at base version and especially now after 4 Title Update bringing 5 new monster (4 MH original + 1 Collab) creating a very good lineup for a base game version. The combat also felt great even if focus mode kinda make earlier monster very very easy, but they always designed to be very easy to begin with so no problem.

Wilds biggest sin is not in monster lineup or new feature or anything like that, it's biggest sin is egeregious and terrible terrible performance, the price it's need to pay for using a new engine designed for Resident Evil type of map to create an open world one. Thankfully, the issue can be said to be solved but the damage has been done. Wilds terrible performance will contiune to haunt it at least until the expansion release, at best.

With all that said, I believe this game has potential to cement Monster Hunter series as a mainstream media (whether that is good thing or not, let time be the judge) and especially with the expansion, I hope this game will become better and better.

[TLDR: Best MH so far, Better combat, Better Graphics, values your time, doesn't expect you to hunt for 45 Minutes on a single monster like older titles, doesn't run nearly as bad as people complain about (probably potato pc)]

Performance:
Don't understand the hate for the game RTX 5070 (NON TI) 5600X NVME 16GB ram, Max graphics High Ray Tracing and HD Texture pack 90+ frames per second no upscaling no dips, using Frame Gen gives me crazy smoothness and no blurry look.

Values your time:
Shorter more impactful hunts & better RNG for loot, food buffs last multiple hunts instead of once off per.
I also prefer being able to auto-pilot my mount to the Monster to stop me from spending 30 Minutes on hunting a single monster like World did, I have gotten further in less time than any other MH game (gear wise) and that is a good thing, older titles forcing you to re-grind a 45 minute monster 6 times for 1 armor set and then grind again for a weapon which you throw away within a few hunts was absolutely boring. don't even get me started on Gems or Legendary Weapons in older titles.

The Seamless world blew my mind being able to run right out of camp to hunt a monster or gather materials is amazing, all older MH games were loading screen simulators.

Wilds Vs World Content:
Can't compare the content to World as it was launched in 2018 and had massive updates and a expansion, but Knowing Capcom, Wilds will get to Worlds level of content very quickly when the DLC drops, what is there now is pretty good, easy 100+ hour time sink of content ingame right now.

Difficulty:
All Monster Hunter games are super easy until HR10 (after the main cinematic campaign ends) more so if you know how to play a MH game and muscle memory kicks in. This game just modernized The Monster Hunter experience and I am all for it I have played every single MH since the PSP era, fluid combat, blocking is rewarding instead of punishing, parrying is doable and not sweat level only for no life MH players anymore.

My Gripes:
They could've really slotted in the Original MH sound track into the last fight opening scene instead, and given us a proper Main Hub along side the camps earlier in the campaign, as I preferred the main hub areas of all the other games. 

please bring back paying for Cat Chef meals with Zenny I prefer those animations over the hunter making his own meals.

Overall:
The game itself is a really good Monster Hunter, combat is the best it has ever been and after the Cinematic Story you start getting High Rank hunts with decently difficult monsters, content is coming but what is in now is more than enough to keep anyone interested until the DLC drops. 17 Hours in finished campaign and doing the "Meat" of the experience now and I am enjoying every minute spent.

[b][h1]Monster Hunter Wilds: A Wild Start[/h1][/b]

  I held off posting a review for this game for a while since while I enjoyed it myself at launch, the amount of issues the game had made it difficult for me to recommend it. Since then, the game has been updated many times fixing or otherwise applying band-aids to a lot of these issues.

[b][h1]The issues at launch[/h1][/b]

[h2][i]Optimization[/i][/h2]
  
  The most talked about issue was the optimization of the game. This topic has dominated most Wilds discussion since launch and for good reason. [b] The game simply did not run acceptably at all.[/b] Multiple stutters, crashes, poor FPS, and even not launching were some of the most common issues everyone had. 

  How has Capcom responded to this? It took many months, but now there has been multiple patches addressing performance. I can say from my own experience on my hardware the game runs smoothly. I do not get nearly the same stutters I did before, I do not have any game crashes, and lastly the FPS is more than acceptable for me. I cannot say though for certain this will be everyone's experience however. With PC gaming there are literal thousands of possible combinations to account for. I would suggest trying to lookup videos showcasing performance on current patch that best matches your hardware. All in all, I am happy with the current performance of the game and it is much better than its launch state.

[h2][i]Difficulty[/i][/h2]

  
  The next most discussed criticism of Wilds was its difficulty, or rather, lacking thereof. I will say that this topic is rather complex, and I will not be able to give the full breakdown it deserves. Discussing difficulty in games is difficult as it's something that is subjective. Everyone has a different level of difficulty they desire from a game, and differing ideas of how to tune it. 

  What I can say without a doubt is the main story progression did felt a little too easy, and this was a common complaint. What I think was the problem overall was the tuning of game mechanics compared to how the monsters can fight back. Wilds introduced 2 new mechanics for combat called focus mode and the wound system. 

  Focus mode allows us to change the direction our character faces even while attacking. This makes it so it is much easier to keep uptime on a monster and maximize DPS. During the main progression the monsters lack the HP or the move set to deal with us constantly being able to hit it. 

  The wound system allows us to create wounds on the monster that we can initiate special moves on that inflict hefty damage and even stagger the monster. The wounds were very abundant however, leading us to being bale to constantly combine stuns, knockdowns, and wound staggers to basically stunk lock the monster to death.

  When you combine the above mechanics it lead to every hunt feeling like you went in, and stun locked a poor monster for 5 minutes and left. Leading to a bland experience where it all felt the same.

  Capcom did respond to this by adding more challenging monsters over the title updates. We've seen buffs to 8* monsters, addition of 9* monsters, arch tempered monsters, and in the last update we got 10* arch tempered monsters.  I can say for myself the game now has plenty of fun challenging hunts in the endgame. While it is a shame the story progression remains the same, the fact that at the end has so many fun and hard quests makes up for it.

[i][h2]Lack of things to do at the end[/h2][/i]

  The last issue I had was lack of endgame at launch. We did have an endgame system with artian parts, however it didn't do a good job really promoting grinding, and it actually hurt the game as all the best weapons were (in my opinion) ugly green artian weapons.

  Luckily, with the title updates the endgame is a lot more expansive and fun. There is a endgame loop that has expanded artian weapons to include grinding multiple challenging monster quests. And on top of that, we have transcendence which allows us to even further improve lower tier armor. Overall, there is a lot more to do and more variety of things to hunt

[h2][i]Closing thoughts[/i][/h2]

  
  This is a great monster hunter game. While it had a lot of issues at launch, Capcom did not abandon it, and slowly over a year has vastly improved the experience. Is it perfect? No, there are still things that could improve, but I am more than happy to say this is a Monster Hunter game I would easily recommend now. It is a shame it took this long, but at least it is here now.

I've waited an entire year to make a thorough review of Monster Hunter Wild's base game + Title Updates, and it's safe to say, I can now definitely recommend this game! Despite CAPCOM potentially rushing the publish time, the developers did not give up to deliver performance updates as well as amazing Title Updates (as always.) This most recent update really delivered on that Monster Hunter charm that I was yearning for.
-
[b] Pros: [/b]
[u]-Combat[/u] feels fresh and exciting, with most weapons receiving a major playstyle change. Every weapon is now viable (yes, even hunting horn, my beloved.)
[u]-Monster skeleton diversity and creativity[/u] is very good in Wilds. This was a gripe I had with Monster Hunter World's base game that I think Wilds definitely improved on. The monsters also feel more alive, with idle/endemic animations that really show off their character.
[u]-Endemic life makes the world feel bustling and alive.[/u] The attention the detail is amazing.
[u]-The score.[/u] The music and sound effects are phenomenal!
[u]-QOL improvements[/u] such as mix and match armor, movable barrel bombs and traps, weapon flexibility, environmental links, and immediate layered armor/weapons available.
[u]-Endgame.[/u] While still never as good as expansion endgames, the tailsmans and gogma weapons are very fun and rewarding to grind.
[u]-The love and care put into this game is deeply felt[/u] personally. The environmental interactions, diva performances, lighthearted event quests, nostalgic music brought back from previous titles, transparent developers, and the [spoiler] little handcarved gift Alma gives to you for completing the final optional quest [/spoiler] made me feel rewarded for dedicating my time into this game and the community as a whole. 
[u]-Mizutsune :)[/u]

[b] Cons: [/b]
[u]-Auto mount movement[/u] being turned on by default and scoutflies makes exploration obsolete and unimmersive by default. Many players just will never see the beauty of this game because hunts are mounted to a railroad track. It feels you need to go out of your way to explore.
[u]-Lack of ailments[/u] or ailment impact. Monsters in this game give less friction than in past games when it comes to applying ailments to the hunter. I would love to see monsters use more than just raw power as difficulty in the expansion.
[u]-The main story's difficulty is much too easy.[/u] This makes for an odd difficulty curve later when fighting more difficult monsters such as [spoiler] Gore Magala, Mizutsune, Lagiacrus, Seregios, Omega, Gogmazios, and the Arch Tempereds [/spoiler] . Proper friction is key.
[u]-NPC/Tutorial dialogue.[/u] It is VERY irritating and immersion breaking when every NPC shouts exactly what a monster is about to do. There needs to be an option to turn these things OFF. Probably my personal biggest gripe about the entire game is just how hard it feels it is trying to prevent players making ANY mistakes during hunts. Friction is good, please let go of our hands a bit.
-
In the end, I fully recommend this game, especially to creature enthusiasts, build craft lovers, and Monster Hunter fans. You just may need to wait until the endgame for the really challenging hunts.

What a painful experience. I am a long time Monster Hunter fan. This was not a Monster Hunter game.

Now, not to be all negative, the game play of the fighting monsters? Great. Peak. Love it. The art is amazing. Feels gritty and looks amazing. Herds of wild beats roaming large open areas. Amazing. Weapons feel good and play smooth. Super exited about that stuff.

But the problem comes in at the rails and the story. The whole game is on rails. I feel like I'm being lead around on a leash from monster fight to monster fight. If I wanted to play a fighting game, there are better options.
There's no building up my base. There's no gathering materials. No replanning traps. No grinding materials. It feels like one great long tutorial. Told to marvel at the sights. Told to focus on the programmed set pieces. I know how to stare in awe on my own. I don't need to be guided.

And the story is painful. I'm being led around by some pretentious college educated woman who just hangs around doing nothing but yapping. Telling me how pretty everything is. I guess there's no real danger. No survival. No grit and urgency. I'm just chaperoning a fieldtrip for an undergrad. It's safe for this little weaponless woman to be having a good time here. It's a petting zoo, apparently.

So far we have rescued two different little ethnic children and all the hunting feels like protecting morons too stupid to protect themselves. They shouldn't be here, but sure, got to keep them safe. But then even while trying to keep them safe you are told "you're authorized to hunt this monster." You want to ruin any feeling of hunting? have an organization that gives you permission to do it. We're not in the wild, apparently. We're just park rangers culling the deer herd. Could not be more lame.

And what's with all the multiethnic tribes? You're telling me there's a pacifist tribe that doesn't even know what a weapon is and use wind chimes to keep monsters away which don't even work because you quickly have to save them from being attacked anyway? Is everyone stupid? Apparently.

It's all just so overpoweringly lame. Who was this made for? Someone's mom, it seems.
