Started out a little rough at launch performance-wise, plus the end-game content left something to be desired, but such is the case for most Monster Hunter games at the start (post-World). That said, I still had an absolute blast with the game then, and even more so now.

There are still problems with the game, for example, multiplayer is more complicated to set up than it needs to be (though it used to be even worse) and I find the idea that a lot of end-game hunts essentially require a group of four, assuming you're not min-maxing, to be a little annoying, at least for a High Rank experience. Also, I tend to agree with those who say that more recent Monster Hunter games would be better described as "Monster Fighter" but this game does enough interesting things with its world for me to believe Capcom still has some appreciation for the ecology, atmosphere and somewhat calculated planning when it comes to interacting with the world and its monsters.

Wilds is still very much Monster Hunter, with its addicting, grindy gameplay loop, extremely well designed monsters and combat that is more fun now than it has ever been. The weapon designs are all fantastic, no more ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥-slapped-on-bones designs from World, the maps are all incredibly put together and while this is an unpopular opinion to many, I actually found the story to be very engaging and felt actual narrative drive to hunt most of the monsters, particularly the flagship and final boss.

The title update monsters have all been amazing to fight, and the monster roster only got better with time. That, along with the massive performance patch that came out recently, makes this a game I will absolutely be returning to, not only for its expansion, but for future full playthroughs even after the fact. 

Anyway Capcom I NEED you to give a ♥♥♥♥ about your old mh games too, please MHGU on PC please please please thank you

Wilds is a flawed yet fun addition to the Monster Hunter series. 

It had a VERY rocky journey to the end of it's content, as we wait for the DLC, but I personally, found it to be a fun one. The game for the longest time suffered from performance issues on most systems, and actively barred players from playing. Because I knew from World and Rise, and both of their DLC's, that all that stuff would get fixed with time. And currently:

- The hardest quests in the games now fully feel as such, pushing even experienced hunters to the edge
- The event quest page is filled, with most being there permanently, giving players a wide variety of silly rewards and trying challenges
- And as of the last update, optimization has improved notably, with more players able to play, without the need for external assistance

All that on top of, quite fun combat and monsters, and I think it is safe to say, that Wilds has earned it's stripes among the series. It does have a few flaws here and there, but we'll see what the DLC brings in terms of answering them. Besides, none of the games in the series is perfect, but we love them all the same.

Excited to see what is to come for this game, and planning on dropping double the time I have in the game already when it does come out.

Despite everything, I'm having fun.

Yes, I'm on (relatively) old hardware, in the form of a NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1080. Cranking everything down as low as it'll go and turning on FSR at Balanced, I can hold about 45 real frames and render at 60 with framegen. The game looks like crap, but it's playable. Genuinely playable. But that's not what this review is about.

The problem is... optimization. In the form of disk drive occupancy.

Every Title Update, no matter how much new content is added, comes in the form of a patch file, which RE Engine loads atop the basegame file. These patch files describe changes. The current Title Update includes a crossover with Stories, adds a few cosmetics, prepares to return past Events for the next month's time, and begins rolling out Arch-Tempered hunts for already existing monsters.

What size is the patch file for this update? 20 GB.

It's a 20 GB patch file describing new quest data (which should probably take up a handful of megabytes at worst), some minor new cosmetic content (between models, textures, VFX, and the related, I'm only expecting a gig max here), and the Stories crossover, which... adds a handful of models, textures, VFX, cutscene data, quests...

The new content contained within this title update certainly is not 20GB worth of content. But because it's Patch Data atop Patch Data atop Patch Data, the packed data has to be shuffled around rather violently.

Optimize how you're serving game content to us, developers. Storage space for PATCH DATA doesn't grow on trees. You've ballooned to over 100GB storage requirements for a collection of assets that reasonably should take less than half that.

Fix this.

This game has come a long way since launch. There are some negatives to it still but the performance is no longer one of them. 

The gameplay is as smooth and accessible as you can ask for and is a great entrance to the franchise while still having challenging and rewarding fights. On the content side of things Monster hunter always feels a too small to begin with.

 It suffers from the same issues that large scale games with constant update cycles all fall victim too. The last game will always have so much more to do than the most recent simply because it had time to grow. 

 Personally I find it disappoints a little on the graphics front but not in regards to fidelity.  Monster Hunter in my short time enjoying the franchise has always has their air of whimsy too it. Not that its a parody or it doesn't take itself seriously, it does. It just comes across as genuine. In this world there are cute talking cats you can dress up and fat little birds who look like cotton balls. It just so happens there are also apocalyptic terrors and walking natural disasters sharing it with them.

If you have ever been subjected to the torture that is the monster hunter movie you'll know what I'm talking about. The tone and vibes of monster hunter are completely unique and unapologetically so. Bright colours and gorgeous music and bipedal cats serving you food porn quality meals. I feel like wilds misses that a bit. 

Capcom found the mid 2000's Call of Duty piss filter and now everything is a little drab and washed out and a touch more lifeless feeling. Monster Hunter is as much the vibes of the world as it is the act of hunting monsters and engaging in the gameplay loop itself. 

 This is easily fixed with a few quick scripts of course, I highly recommend "Disable Post Processing Effects" by TonWonton and "Tweak Volumetric Fog" by TheFuzzy51 for a light face lift that keeps the game feeling vanilla and brightens everything up a bit. Its just sad you have to do that to get a little colour back in the game.

All that said I'm super hopeful for the future of the game and its expansion when it gets released. I would like to see it grow into a much bigger more content rich experience.

If you're on the fence about getting this game, as of February 17th, 2026, I would say it's well worth the money, but definitely try and see if you can get it on sale as I still think the full year wait to get this game to run the way it was supposed to, without Frame Generation (♥♥♥♥♥) still leaves a bad taste in my mouth.  They need to deliver an Iceborne quality experience for the DLC WITHOUT any performance issues, Period.

I’ve put a lot of time into Wilds across multiple title updates, and at this point I honestly think this is the best base game any modern Monster Hunter has launched with. A lot of the early criticism wasn’t completely wrong, but it also didn’t tell the whole story. The game people were judging at launch is not the same game that exists now.

First, performance. Yes, it was bad. On PC especially, optimization was rough and not really acceptable for how the game looks. Some of the outrage was definitely inflated by people trying to run it on outdated hardware, but that still doesn’t excuse the launch state. The good news is that the title updates made a real difference over time. On my old 3060 Ti, I originally needed AMD FSR frame generation to sit around 90 FPS. After updates, that went up to about 110 with framegen, and eventually I could run 60 to 70 without framegen at all. I’ve since upgraded to a 5070 Ti and now I can max out my 162 Hz monitor with 2x framegen. So the game does run the way it should now if you have appropriate hardware, it just took longer than it should have.

The “this game is too easy” criticism is also pretty misleading. A lot of people clearly never reached late game. Early progression is accessible, sure, but endgame monsters absolutely hit back. 9* hunts like  [spoiler]Seregios, Lagiacrus, and Gore [/spoiler] are legitimately challenging and honestly comparable to some Master Rank fights from older games. A lot of what people remember as difficulty from older Monster Hunter titles was really just jank. Clunky controls, awkward UI, bad hitboxes, or spending forever running around a map trying to find the monster. Personally I would rather spend my time actually hunting than fighting menus or wandering in circles.

Endgame also deserves more credit than it gets. After all the title updates there’s a good amount of variety, and you’re not forced to grind the same monster over and over. There are multiple things worth hunting depending on what you need.  [spoiler]Gogmazios did get repetitive for a bit, but the addition of 10* quests helped a lot and the loop feels much healthier now. [/spoiler]

As for whether this still “feels like Monster Hunter,” that’s subjective, but to me it absolutely does. The core loop is still there. Learn the monster, improve your execution, optimize your build, repeat. 

Story has never been the main appeal for me, but I did like some of the ideas here.  [spoiler]The Gore Magala situation was interesting, and Gogmazios being hidden under the Oilwell Basin was just cool worldbuilding. That’s about all I personally need from Monster Hunter narrative. [/spoiler]

On the gameplay side, most weapons feel amazing. Combat is smooth, responsive, and satisfying. There are a couple unfortunate exceptions, sorry to Charge Blade and HBG players, but overall weapon design is one of the strongest parts of the game. The Wilds versions of returning monsters are also done really well.  [spoiler]Mizutsune is a great example of a fun returning monster. [/spoiler]

Some complaints like auto navigation on the Seikret are weird to me because it’s optional. If you don’t like it, just don’t use it. The game gives you tools and lets you decide how much help you want.

Overall, Wilds definitely had problems, especially technical ones, but the foundation was always strong. After multiple updates, it now has a challenging endgame, a varied hunt loop, and some of the best moment to moment combat in the series. If you actually stick with it past the early hours, there’s a lot here to enjoy.

[h1]A Weary 'Thumbs Up'[/h1]

I started playing with MH1 when I was 11, stopped, and then started again on MH3U later on in highschool and was hooked ever since. From 3U to 4U, to Generations Ultimate, to World, to Rise, and I can say emphatically without question, this was one of the weakest initial launches of any Monster Hunter I have ever played. Initially, my brother and I played the first 75 hours of the game, and after making it to high rank with really only Aarkvald, and Gore Magala to look forward to for real difficulty and challenge, and Zoh Shia only having the option to repeatedly hunt as an "expansion update" we were insanely disappointed. 

It was not just the optimization issues, which were glaring, and horrendous at times.

There are so many aspects of this game that should have had its features already in it, especially Zoh Shia, online gathering hubs, and so much more. We reached high rank, and we basically just felt like there was nothing to do. We finished everything. In other games, we at least had fun and felt motivated to finish every quest together, especially the multiplayer guild hall quests. 

With all of that being said, after the Gogmazios update, I have become much more happy with the game. It is a little bit better optimized, but now we actually have more challenging fights that I am happy with. Arch Tempered Jin Dahaad, Omega Planetes, and of course Gog Mazios are very welcome additions and have been loads of fun. This is how I wish the end game had been in the beginning. Hopefully this stride keeps going and Wilds can really get its second wind.

I do have other issues with some of the combat moves of the other monsters, and how you have these beautiful, or very interesting designs like La La Barina that are completely wasted because the combat is absolutely braindead, and boring, which is sad for such creative designs like her. Chatacabra being a kind of boxing frog seems very cool as a design, but his combat mechanics/ ai is so simple and dumbed down that it is god awful. I remember almost falling asleep fighting him. Why make such interesting designs only to make the fight a total snooze-fest even in high rank?

Anyways, I initially would not recommend the game at all unless you were a hardcore fan of the franchise and wanted to continue support, but even then it was still terrible. But now, I give a somewhat weary thumbs up.

Okay this review might change down the line once the expansion for Wilds releases so it'll be up until then and until I actually manage to finish it. In the games current state I can at least recommend people try Wilds and see how they feel about it before trying to get the expansion. 

Starting off however, I might be a bit discouraged to even say anything about the state of the game as the launch was ROUGH and filled with performance issues which took several months to almost a year to fix. I don't even want to say "Thanks Capcom for taking the time to fix the game" as these issues were present in a previous game that used the RE Engine for open world and currently still runs WORSE and hardly gets any updates at all to improve on its stability and performance. Only reason Monster Hunter Wilds runs better is because it's more important as an IP to Capcom than DD2. 

https://store.steampowered.com/app/2054970/Dragons_Dogma_2/ 

The funny part however though is that the open world that was marketed to us isn't at all as interesting when you transition between regions. Personally I'd trade loading screens and larger file size if it meant the game ran better but I do appreciate Capcom being ambitious with their game in trying to make it more immersive. Which, speaking of immersion, while visually the game is stunning the UI and Menu and HUD is honestly the WORST it has been in some time. Constant notifications of the changes in environment, weird placement of functions in the menu that you might even need a google search to figure out. SOS quests and quest settings being the largest offenders with now multiplayer quests flooded with people not knowing how to use the SOS system so when large title updates roll out expect to join quests where the host can't be bothered to set it to private or create a link party for their friends if you want to have fun with randoms because they will keep you hanging instead of either accepting or rejecting your quest request. All the stuff you have to read and pay attention to in the UI might be overstimulating especially when hunting which is a shame because part of the charm of say, the chat, was the callout system and stickers which mostly get ignored now as people either conditioned themselves to not looking at it or have mods to silence the chat itself. 

Now to what I actually enjoy about the game, especially as a solo experience because solo? The game is great and close to the best base game monster hunter experience, The monster roster features some amazing new monsters and old ones from previous games and some flagships of course, the ecosystem is vibrant and detailed especially with how beautiful weather changes can be in every region. The story itself may be a hit or miss depending on how you might feel about the MH Lore (personally I'm okay with it) and what direction Capcom seem to be going with it. I do also enjoy the characterization of our hunters as well leaving enough room as well for people to still identify with their character and the flagship monster Arkveld is amazing to fight. 

The combat is still smooth enough to enjoy, even better with the movement improvements and animations to a lot of weapons in the game that make dodging and engaging with the monster with your weapon have that added weight many enjoyed that they felt might have been missing in Rise. Some weapons however might have had their kit simplified which funneled a lot people to stick to one playstyle to have more impact in a hunt and I unfortunately main one of those weapons but again maybe the expansion might bring along changes that address how the weapons function as well. Overall most weapons have changed for the better, the focus system might make hunts feel a bit too easy and seem a bit disruptive when playing with someone who needs focus wounds for their weapon too but I'm hoping with the upcoming expansion the changes to the weapon balancing and abilities addresses that. 

The game is pricey, but I do recommend to fans of Monster Hunter, a lot of stuff has been streamlined and the UI is a mess but the core game is still fun and and the performance since launch has vastly improved thank goodness.

So I will start out by saying that this is my first Monster Hunter game pretty much, I tried Monster Hunter: World way back when that came out for Xbox but my Xbox couldn't really handle it so I gave up.

So far at the time of writing this I have 34.1 hours played in this game, I completed the Low Rank portion of the game and have now progressed onto the High Rank portion. Up until this point my opinion on the game was that it played nice, Monster hunts were fun, The loot made sense and the difficulty kept me interested. 

However, When i got to Mizutsune, I hit a wall. Simply put, I could not kill it or even get it to retreat to a new area once before I died 3 times, Failing the mission. So i went to the drawing board and wanted to improve my build a little bit. That's when I started to learn about the Artian Weapon system. 

From here on you will only read complaints from me: The fact that you need to not only grind the parts to MAKE the weapon in the first place, But you need to grind even MORE to get material to upgrade this weapon so that you can outperform (Even if barely) regular monster weapons.

This would have been salvageable, And at the time I thought maybe I might sink some time into this, Crafting my ideal weapon to be able to push myself further. However, That's when i learned that the Artian Weapon upgrade system is not based on RNG, This is based on a predetermined seed that you get from the moment you create your first character. That is dumb as HELL to put it in the most polite way I can.

I commend all of you that are able to muscle through that horrendous system to continue playing the game and even those of you who choose to not even interact with the system at all, Sticking with regular monster weapons. I just cant do it, I'm not going to spend how ever many hours grinding the materials to make the weapon, Grinding the upgrade material and then save scumming for 10, 20, 50, 200, 500 weapons or whatever it takes to fish for that ideal seed to get what I want. Its just not worth it. At least if this system was based on a random model, I can live with that. I play TONS of games that use an RNG system to acquire things, I'm no stranger to that but this is a first.

After having some back and forth with myself about maybe just muscling through it like people do, I found a subset of people, Like me, Who think this system is utter slop and decide to use external tools like mods to get their ideal rolls. This would have made me continue to play this game, If it wasn't for the fact that Capcom doesn't directly address whether this is bannable. What i'm referring to is a mod called Artian Editor on Nexus Mods. It allows you to pick exactly what stats you want your weapon to have. Naturally, You CAN use this mod to gain an unfair advantage by adding things to your weapon that aren't normally obtainable within the vanilla unmodded game. I did not want to go this direction, I wanted to create my ideal, One time craft, Perfect weapon that is otherwise NATURALLY OBTAINABLE WITHIN THE UNMODIFIED GAME without having to save scum hundreds of hours to get it, Like many others do. But I do not like the risk of ban.

So for now as it is, It does not seem like this system will be changed as they've updated this system to incorporate even higher tier weapons but with the same seed based model.

My rating of the game BEFORE learning about this slop system was a solid 8/10, The gameplay loop was enjoyable and challenging to me and that kept me playing this game exclusively out of my entire library. Not to mention the game is very visually appealing. 

After learning of this system, My review drops to probably a 4/10. If youre okay with save scumming and spending hundreds of hours fighting the same monsters for the same parts to make the same weapons over and over until you get what you need or modding the game and risking the ban OR avoiding the system entirely sticking to monster weapons only, Then you can disregard my entire review and call me a moron. For those of you with limited play time however, Avoid this crap at all costs, I received this game at a discount and I wouldn't even recommend it then.

If this system was implemented this way on a seed based model to retain players then I seriously question the mental capacity of the person who's idea this was. This does NOT respect player time, And is borderline predatory practice.

So I just picked up Monster Hunter Wilds and have been really enjoying the game. I haven't played any Monster Hunter since Worlds. I would recommend the game to any Monster Hunter fan to get it now while Capcom is having it's sale. 

Beyond this I have some more specific feedback below if you're interested but its mostly opinionated.

[b]Performance[/b]: I didn't play before the "optimization patch" specifically because of what I read and heard about the games performance at the time. But my experience with the patch has been the game running exceptionally well with no stutters and easily over 90 fps.

The caveat to this is I am using DLSS and AMD FRS (Frame Gen) with Ray Tracing on and motion blur off. Game settings on high so far but only because I haven't tried "ultra" yet. Either way the game looks phenomenal at these settings for a Monster Hunter game.

[b]Focus Mode[/b]: I know there is a bit of distaste for this mode. Me personally it makes the game feel more like a traditional MMO versus what is normally the Monster Hunter experience that feels closer to a Dark Souls system. Either way it seems like you don't have to use it if you just chad your movement and attacks either way.

[b]Chocobo[/b]: I straight up don't like it - but this is just a me thing as I don't like auto moving mounts. I know you can stop it but it just feels bad to me coming from more traditional MMO mount systems.

Anyways - that's all I have and if you're a Monster Hunter fan that may have not been playing due to fear of performance issues, I personally think that's in a good place now and you should check this game out.

Good Luck out there.

[h1] It's a fine game, but it's not Monster Hunter [/h1]

I'm writing this review immediately after completing Low Rank. Whether or not the game improves in High Rank is irrelevant, because the game has already failed. A game that only gets good after 10 hours is a bad game.

[i]EDIT: Having now finished all the story missions in High Rank in another mere 10 hours, I can confidently say that Low Rank was the best part of the game. I am so glad I bought this on sale; I cannot imagine paying $70 for a 20 hour Monster Hunter game. Capcom should be ashamed.[/i]


[h3]Part 1: The Maps, the Bird, and the Claw[/h3]
The maps in this game are huge, and very detailed. However, as a tradeoff, they are a [i]nightmare[/i] to navigate. Every area looks functionally indistinguishable from on another, and getting from point A to point B is a massive pain. 

You will understand very quickly that the Seikret and the claw shot were not born out of a desire to create more interesting systems, but out of necessity. Yet at the same time, both of those additions only exacerbate the problem. The Seikret's [strike]taxi mode[/strike] auto-tracking takes your hands off the wheel and makes you a passenger rather than an explorer. You won't learn the maps unless you actually walk around them, but reminder, they're [i]huge[/i]. Huge, but largely empty of actual things you would want to interact with, so you use the Seikret just to save time. Anything you see that you would want to interact with you can just grab with the claw shot. You may say, "Well why not just manually control the Seikret while exploring?" The answer is simply that the manual Seikret controls are awful.

[h3]Part 2: Cutscenes and Monster designs [/h3]
This game has too many cutscenes. While the plot is better than World and Rise by my standards, and I liked seeing some aspects of the monster hunting world in more detail, it very much overstayed its welcome. The Disney shoot-em-up dark ride portions where you get to listen to characters yap while clawing resources off the ground are kind of ridiculous. You [i]can[/i] skip the cutscenes. However, the point still stands.

I have mixed opinions on a voiced player character. I did not mind the voice, in fact, I really appreciated not having some annoying secondary character (*cough* handler *cough cough*) acting as my mouthpiece. I'm not a fan of my hunter being John Monsterhunter straight out of the gate (or at all, frankly. It doesn't feel earned.) But I think they did an OK job not making him obnoxious, even if they had to give him a cardboard personality to do it. The cutscene interactions with Nata, while generic, were fine. 

On the subject of Nata, I didn't end up hating him like many other reviews I'd read previously did. Yes, he's annoying, but he's also a traumatized 12 year old who lived in an isolated village his whole life. I took him with a grain of salt, and it was fine.

I have even more mixed opinions on the monster designs. I like most of them, but some fit in very poorly design-wise with some of the established monsters, and some of the established monsters look weird with their grittier reskins. Like, holy hell Congalala looks [i]so wrong.[/i] They also sound significantly more alien/artificial than past monsters. It was super cool to see my beloved Nerscylla again, though.

[h3]Part 3: The Monster Hunting, or Lack Thereof[/h3]
This is the biggest problem I have with Wilds.

You don't hunt monsters in this game. You fight them. The bird tracks the monster for you. The monster's location is always marked on the map, and in an effort to one-up Rise's attempt to kill Mohun's identity, a [i]giant waypoint[/i] is displayed above the monster whenever it gets more than 15 feet away from you. 
Monsters spend significantly more time downed than previous entries (and I am tactfully ignoring the Clutch Claw in this discussion). This is largely due to the new wound system.

The preparation is also gone. Just craft whatever armor pieces you have parts for. You [i]will[/i] have parts for most of them, due to the overabundance of monster drops given by wound breaks, as well as the cheaper material costs. I never once made gear because I needed an edge over a new monster, I just made whatever was available. There is zero need to do quests more than once, and as a result, I competed all of Low Rank in 10 hours.

What about the item pouch? More like I-don't-care-what's-in-my pouch. Hot/cold drinks? Eat a bug from the wall. Low health? Eat a bug off the wall. Statused? Good thing your cat is carrying the entirety of a hospital's medical stockpile on him at all times (there's also an endemic bug for that, believe it or not).

*Sigh.*

Okay, look. I have enjoyed Monster Hunter's previous entries for their unique take on what basically boils down to a "boss-fighting game." Wilds does not [i]remove[/i] these aspects. They're still there. There's just no reason to engage with them. If this changes during my venture into High Rank, it doesn't matter, because [i]you can't half-ass a Monster Hunter game[/i].

[h3]Part 4: Positives [/h3]
Despite what my recommendation says, there [i]are[/i] parts of this game that I like.

I enjoy the seamlessness of leaving camp directly into the wilds. I love the new cephalopod monsters.

I don't hate 90% of the NPCs, which is more than I can say for either of the 5th gen games. In fact, I really enjoyed fighting alongside Olivia in some of the story fights, which I've honestly always wanted from a Mohun game (Sunbreak doesn't count because I couldn't give a damn about any of those NPCs). The facial rigging and voice acting is leagues better than World. Some of the reunion scenes between NPCs after a hunt were actually touching. And dare I say, the plot as a whole is the least egregious compared with Worldborne and Risebreak. Also, the transition from walking simulator/cutscene to combat feels really good and gets me pumped to fight.

I didn't discuss combat beyond wounds, because I don't have much to say about the new mechanics, but I will say that I was apprehensive about the offset attacks, and I actually enjoyed them more than I was expecting. (Focus mode on Greatsword is stupid as hell though, lmao.)

[h3]Part 5: Final Words[/h3]
I really hope Monster Hunter can regain some of its identity with the upcoming Master Rank expansion. There are more little nuisances here and there that I found annoying, but this review is already approaching the character limit.

I did not touch on performance, because at this point, it's just beating a dead horse. It's not good. The engine Capcom has been re-using for almost the past decade is not up to this sort of task. The game, while now more optimized than at release, should never have been released at all. Unacceptable.

I did not touch on online play because I have not tried it.

[h3]In conclusion: there's a good reason why World is still competitive with Wilds in terms of player counts despite being 8 years older.[/h3]

Monster Hunter Wilds: A Beautiful Disaster
Rating: 4/5 (Gameplay) | 1.5/5 (Technical State)

The Fun: The Peak of the Franchise
When the game actually stays open, Wilds is nothing short of incredible. The transition to a fully seamless open world isn't just a gimmick; it changes the rhythm of the hunt entirely.

The Ecosystem: Seeing a weather event like the Sandtide roll in while you’re mid-fight—forcing both you and the monster to adapt—is a level of immersion the series has never reached before.

The Seikret: The new mount is a game-changer. Being able to sharpen your blade or swap weapons on the move keeps the momentum at an all-time high.

Combat: Every weapon feels like it has more weight and "crunch" than ever. The new Focus Mode adds a layer of precision that makes breaking parts feel more intentional and satisfying.

The Frustration: Fighting the Engine, Not the Monster
Unfortunately, the "True Flagship" of this game isn't Arkveld—it’s the Crash to Desktop.

Poor Optimization: Even on high-end rigs, the game is a resource hog. CPU bottlenecks are frequent, and the VRAM management is aggressive, often leading to "origami" textures or massive frame drops in the Scarlet Forest.

Stability Issues: Frequent crashes during transitions or high-particle fights (like the lightning storms) turn a 20-minute epic hunt into a total waste of time.

Upscaling Dependency: The game relies way too heavily on DLSS/FSR to be playable, which often leaves the image looking blurry or "shimmery" unless you have top-tier hardware.

They definitely fixed the performance problems that plenty of people had with the game. But "optimizing" is a double-edged sword and instead of creating a more granular set of graphical options to better include a variety of hardware, they made a LOT of downgrades the the graphics. A lot of people are seeing an increased 10~20 fps, but I'm seeing a lot fewer polygons, all around lower textures even on the high res texture pack (which they cut the size of in half, and not by compression, mind you), and all around made the game graphically look like it belongs a generation behind where the game was at during release.

I'm not gonna comment on performance because you'll likely read more than enough about it in the reviews. All I'm gonna say is I have a pretty beefy rig myself and experienced some pretty bad drops in performance which isn't exactly ideal when the game looks fairly muddy when you start looking up close. That being said.

I think what killed MH: Wilds the most for me was how trimmed down everything felt compared to previous titles. I'm not one for artificial difficulty but if I don't have at least some challenge, then parts of the game just feel boring. There's no researching monsters to identify more things about them to aid in hunts, there's no expeditions to stock on needed supplies for hunts, there's just nothing to add to the hunt other than breaking out a stick and whacking away at it. This might work for the people who want nothing other than a monster arena style game but I don't think those types dominate the franchise.

Another glowing problem with the game is the absolutely boring level design, it's not even close to other games and those who say otherwise are straight up lying. Windward Plains is a wide and empty desert with very little visual variety, you have a small oasis, some caves, lots of wide open spaces, and just overall nothing else going on. Compare this to Wildspire Waste which has the same desert style areas and caves, but also a riverlands, an isolated swamp, some steppes, and more. On the surface they seem similar but it comes back down to how EMPTY the worldspaces of Wilds feel. You have the same issue with Scarlet Forest, Oilwell Basin, Iceshard Cliffs, and the Ruins of Wyveria. But what feels worse is that each respective map starts to feel small and more like a corridor than a map which is a stark contrast to the prior games which were either much more open or at least had more flow to them with intertwining paths. Not to mention a total lack of unique locales such as Coral Highlands or Rotten Vale. It took me a relatively short time to become bored of the world it was making me inhabit.

I hope the expansion truly breathes some life back in the game but I honestly don't have much faith that they will. What a monumental fumble of what could've been a title that surpassed everything before it.

I'm a huge fan of the franchise, back to the original (2004). It was such a diamond in the rough. I was lucky enough to meet one of the head developers and despite a big language barrier, you could tell this was his baby. There was legit glint of happiness when I cooked in the forest and laughed. Trying to figure out great swords, more like driving a semi than a the standard immediately responsive "spam buttons for combos" of the time. The dev struggled explaining timing and pacing in English. I said "oh ♥♥♥♥" multiple times as Rathalos wrecked me and he just looked at me like "Yeah, he's tough right?". As if this was the point of the demo. To punish but make it clear, victory was always within my grasp. And that was new.  You could tell this was a passion project made by lifetime gamers.

Fast forward to mega corporate sales and dlc era where AAA need to keep up with EA or die, a world where most indy shops introduce innovation and captivating stories, with low overhead and 1, 5, less than 100 man teams. Where is MH now? In many ways it's more accessible and Quality of Life seems to be a staple. Some complexity remains. I'm concerned that "accessibility" is taking more of a front seat, or I'm just seasoned now and the game feels "easy". Either way, it feels "easy". 

Gathering as a chore, for example helps with pacing. Now, just spam the collection key. Don't worry about crafting, you can just auto craft. Cook perfectly? Get 12 perfect stakes, not one. Most probably like the QoL add, but I liked feeling a bit like a juxtaposed gun toting caveman in MHW and prior. This iteration definitely pushes you to more of the "hunt giant monsters quickly" path.

Despite that, I'm only 8 hours in and hoping the difficulty scale increases, so it goes back to those punishing roots as the game goes on. Not just because "one hit you die" but because the monster pattern and range of attacks keep me on my toes and careful with overextending.

While I consider this to be one of the weaker entries to the franchise the performance gain from one of the recent updates did solidify this game as a game worth playing overall. As with every modern monster hunter game, the game isn't really complete until the release of an expansion which has yet to come for Wilds.

I do think the game lost some aspects that made the prior games special. As well as new additions not really serving the monster hunter series as we love it. Some of it is stylistic choices and some are gameplay.
+ Gameplay itself is smoother than ever. Game feels for the most part appropriately responsive where it needs to.

+ Anything animated, like monsters or weapon attacks, is better than it's ever been. This is the one thing Capcom consistently nails and improves upon each entry.

+ Audio design is great and music is stellar.

+/- Visuals are great, art direction is pretty good but not as special as MHWorlds. However Capcom has completely ignored performance until quite recently and even then it's severely at the cost of fidelity. It will always be a balancing act between smooth framerate vs good visuals but in this case you have to massively sacrifice one aspect for the other, there's no balance to speak of.

+/- The quests/hunting is for the most part almost entirely seamless, you don't get sent back to the hub after every hunt but that is as much of a blessing as it is a curse. You tend to have to fast travel back anyways to restock on supplies, perhaps build/upgrade weapons/armor and put away the extra accumulated items you've picked up along the way that is just taking up your inventory. The idea of reducing downtime as much as possible is set back by the reality of it.

- The food prep mechanic feels like it lost it's magic. It went from a buff cat serving you the best grilled meat ever and an old grandma cat making the best stew to just you alone with a frying pan. This kind of also has been affected by the less downtime approach I spoke of earlier.

- The game has lost some of its apparent difficulty and it's due to a lot of small adjustments everywhere that ultimately have made it easier. Heavy weapons feel less clunky and faster (so they're easier to use and require less strategy). Monsters in general feel more predictable in attacks and design. Being staggered or knocked down provides you with temporary invulnerability which makes it more fair in a sense but it means you'll always get out of a bad situation. Difficulty tends to go up with expansions both in fair and unfair ways so this might change in the future.

- The environment and world design doesn't really feel alive. They nailed this with MHWorld but I'm starting to believe that was a fluke, which is a shame because this is the same dev team as that game.

- They technically made an interconnected world this time around for the most part but don't utilize it, like at all. It's like how The Last of Us technically has no loading screen but it's because they lock you into a cutscene or animation that masks a level transition. MHWorld had the guiding lands which was an endgame map that was the combination of every previous location and while execution was shoddy, the concept isn't. Make the world expansive and interconnected with paths to other locations spread across it.

- The linear bombastic story is genuinely the worst part of the entire MH franchise and they've doubled down on it. MH Rise in my opinion did it better but it's likely due to their more limited budget and thus it wasn't as deeply developed. The world itself is interesting, the monsters are interesting, some dumb poorly written child being the chosen one isn't. The protagonists have never really been the main character of Monster Hunter so to speak, it's always been about the world, the monsters, the hunt and the gear you can create from hunting said monsters.

Overall by now it's an entry worth playing and my negative points of the game is from the lens of someone who's grown up with the series. 

The perfect MH game doesn't exist and will never exist because they insist on changing things up between every entry and that will never not be frustrating lol but it is what it is. Enjoy what makes this entry uniquely good and say goodbye to it in the next entry even if it's by the same studio like World and Wilds (Rise was made by the secondary studio). The end result will always be Monster Hunter but they will be different flavors of Monster Hunter, and Capcom will probably ignore or forget the special ingredients that made the previous games uniquely good. I didn't find this entry as good and addicting as World or Rise but that doesn't rule out that I might love the next one.

[h1] Monster Hunter Wilds - Veteran Review (High-Crit Taste) [/h1]

[b]Veteran players:[/b] I’m genuinely impressed — they did it again.
Monster Hunter: World was the only other game in the franchise I played, and once again the [b]level of production, care, and attention to detail[/b], especially in the [b]Areas[/b], is something you rarely see today.
Everything feels [b]alive[/b].

Last year I had been busy with other things and only returned to the game now in February 2026. From what I read at some point last year (2025), there was a period where an update caused problems for many players, but I was lucky I wasn’t exposed to that window — and from what I can see, whatever happened there is not present anymore. [b]Nothing negative stood out[/b] to me when I came back.

The solid foundation — gameplay, Areas, Quality Of Life for mechanics, diverse ways of fight — is all [b]there[/b], and it’s strong enough to carry the entire experience.

[h2] PROS [/h2]

[b]– The Areas are a MASTERPIECE.[/b]

Honestly, [b]Top 2 / Top 3 of all time[/b], together with
Red Dead Redemption 2 and Monster Hunter: World.
They’re alive, extremely well planned, crafted, and produced.
If you have high standards and good taste, you’ll immediately understand what I mean (together with the other points I mention).

[b]– Gameplay, map system, and playstyle variety are excellent.[/b]

Everything feels intentional and flexible. The combat system is very interesting and dynamic and TOTALLY changes according to the weapon of choice, it's another game and another set of combo options for each weapon,  It's a noticeable improvement vs Monster Hunter World, that wasn't that bad. It takes a little bit of time to adapt tho, if it's your first Monster Hunter game, but when it becomes second nature to you, the fight vibe, fun, and etc. becomes so good and cinematic, man.

[b]– Quality of Life (QoL) for the menu mechanics is solid.[/b]

There are a few small points that could be improved, but overall they gave a very interesting amount of attention to it, and the system supports the experience instead of fighting against it.

[b]– The game is already great, and only needs small refinements to be PERFECT.[/b]

[h2] CONS (nuances — not exactly "flaws", but points that would make this game Top 3 of all time) [/h2]

[b]– More Areas at this quality level would elevate the game even further.[/b]

If Capcom releases one new Area every ~6 months with the same craftsmanship and attention to detail?
I’d gladly pay for it without thinking twice! — and I’m not someone who buys DLC unless it’s well-earned.
And this would be one of those cases.
From a writing perspective, it could be called Expeditions, Exploration trips, etc., or another region, I don't know. But the expansion of this world would be amazing. 

[b]– The Map as whole is impressive and truly remarkable. But Map filtering could be more efficient.[/b]

Filtering for things like traps or gathering points currently takes 4–5 clicks (with controller).
If the icons could be displayed horizontally instead of stacked vertically, this could drop to 2–3 clicks (with controller).
A simple toggle between compact and expanded styles would make the map perfect. And don’t get me wrong, the map is AMAZING — if not the most impressive one in the game industry. Easily Top 3 of all time, not only in terms of visuals, but system-wise too. But there’s still room for improvement, like this point right here; without it, the experience kind of breaks sometimes because, as an example, you need to switch from 'Traps' filtering to 'Gathering' filtering, and this flow could be way better with this subtle ‘vertical compact to horizontal expanded’ toggle suggestion.

[b]– Multiplayer improved vs World, but lacks the old MMO-social feeling.[/b]

I miss when players actually lived in the world together. Entering the city together or whatever.
And a good reference, could be a system like Dying Light’s co-op (to activate the next part of the story, etc, there's only one way: everyone standing in the activation zone, 2m square, to progress missions) would solve the issue of one impatient player triggering things for the group, and everyone missing the context and ruining the "Co-Op" experience — the Borderlands 2 problem.

Maybe something for the next entry in the World/Wilds branch, but the feedback stands.

[h2] FINAL THOUGHTS [/h2]

All my “cons” are micro-refinements.
This is already an [b]excellent game[/b], and the foundation is strong enough that a few improvements would push it into [b]legendary territory[/b].

They genuinely crafted something special here.

I'm newgen and this is my first MonHun game, mainly played on my PS5. I own all MonHun back to Gu now, this game really introduced me to the amazing fandom of hunters.

This game might be not just my favorite Monster Hunter, but [b] one of my favorite video games of all time. [/b]

Most of the problems people seem to have with this game come from the fact that this game just does things very differently than World, most notably the sanding-down or outright removal of a lot of mechanics. While enough people seem to have those opinions that I don't feel right making fun of them, I do feel like they're wrong.

My first Monster Hunter WAS World. I tried to give it a fair shake, but kept getting bogged down by the mechanics that Wilds sanded down. Why do I have to pick at poop and tracks just for the game to outright tell me where the monster is? Why do the fights last so long that I wonder if the game is genuinely broken? Why does just hitting the monster feel clumsy and unintuitive?
When I brought these up to the friend that was trying to introduce me to the game, they responded "it'll click for you after the first 20 hours of gameplay."

[b] EXCUSE ME? [/b] If someone tried to say that about any other game, I'd throw a cinderblock at them for being stupid and defending an objectively bad game. Wilds is absolutely correct for addressing all of those mechanics.

But enough about World, let's talk about why I love Wilds.
-The fights START easy and properly get harder at a steady pace instead of just having a series of difficulty spikes, mirroring a player's growing experience. It might be TOO easy at first, but the challenge is absolutely present in the late game with monsters like Omega Planetes.
-Every weapon is fun and responsive, large in part due to Focus Mode. I don't feel like a bumbling idiot just trying to swing a sword, but hitting things like True Charged Slash still feels earned because the commitment to each hit isn't any smaller.
-The grind is a lot shorter for almost all gear, which I like. This is a pain point for some, but to that I say: How many times did you have to fight that same monster for its 1-2% drop in older games, and how much of that was spent wanting to do literally ANY other content? You've ALL had that moment, don't act like it didn't happen. And it's STILL a thing in Wilds with Gog Artian weapons and grinding for random god rolls if you want a wall to bash your head on.
-The game is just approachable. I want to play this WITH someone, to share the game with them. It's a good "first Monster Hunter" for people wanting to try it, which World absolutely is not.

I have enjoyed this game. It has had it's ups and downs, especially when it first released. However it runs so much smoother than it did. The focus system is a great addition, and being able to swap between two different weapons during a hunt is great. Really fun, loved the characters, gameplay and visuals for the main story and really looking forward to playing more.

FINALLY the PC optimizations have happened. 
The game didn't run well before the multiple performance patches, requiring framegens AND upscaling, and was bottlenecked on the most powerful CPUs that you could buy (and that was BEFORE the CPU shortage!)
Now that Capcom has put in some work to make it run, it runs well. 

TIME FOR THE REVIEW.
STORY-
The story is ass. But you're not here for it, are you? There's a bunch of unskippable cutscenes with some kid whining about monsters and circle of life and all. Sure it's realistic; a sheltered kid who knows next to nothing about nature is going to draw some erroneous conclusions.
Still annoying as hell, and Nata needs to let the professionals do their job.
It's basically a similar story to World and MH4 but done WAY worse. The entire plot can be summed up as "Who would've thought that this natural creature we found in the ecosystem... was a natural part of the ecosystem!? It's a mystery!"

GAMEPLAY-
Every weapon feels absolutely amazing. There's some pain points here and there, like Great Sword not being able to Overhead > Slinger > True Charge like they could in World, or LBG/HBG having NO recoil skills, so their spread 3 still makes you fall flat on your butt every shot. But every weapon has some incredible new moves, including their Wound mechanic moves which do a large chunk of damage and almost always guarantees a good 10s knockdown.
Some weapons rely on wounds much more than others- Great Sword gets a free True Charge, Longsword gets an attack boost, but others like Dual Blades only get a cool animation, and lance just deals slightly more damage.
Knowing which weapons most benefit from a wound and which can pass them up will be the biggest multiplayer friction point for a while, I think.
Also, the whole "Capture/Kill" debate. At this point, with how easy it is to get Carver, and how often it procs, killing is going to be WAY better 99 times out of 100.

DIFFICULTY/GRIND-
Until you hit endgame, the game is a cakewalk. People have made youtube videos showing that the NPC helpers can play and win the game for you.
Once you hit endgame, the difficulty spikes hard, and the grind becomes insane. If you want to make 10 weapons, you need 50 hunter tickets. At 1-2 per quest (3 if you're lucky/use a voucher) that's going to take a LONG while.
Of course, if you look up a guide on what is "The meta", and make ONLY that armor set + your 1 meta weapon, you'll be sitting around whining there's nothing to do in short order, aside from your deco RNG grind.

FUN-
The game is super fun. Even after putting 200+ hours into this on PC (and another 100 on console), I still log in just to help randoms with their low rank quests just for the fun of it, trying out new weapons and having a good ol' time.
Plus I'm sure it feels great for the low level guy to be struggling on that one monster, dying over and over, getting frustrated, and then someone comes in and gives it the beatdown of a lifetime. I know it felt that way for me when I was new to the game.

As a New-gen hunter (getting fairly decently into world and iceborne) and putting in a solid amount of time into wilds (450 hours as of the time of writing), I feel very comfortable in saying that this game is truly solid and great and would be an excellent starting point.

The fluidity of combat with the help of Focus Mode, while not something that was emphasized in previous entries (which focused more on your exact positioning), is something that really adds and emphasizes the character fantasy aspect of each of the weapons. To me, each weapon has something that makes it feel COOL to go at each of the monsters in this game, so that when you master the weapon, the monster, or even both, its a wonderful dance each time you hunt.

I think visually it is a bit hit or miss. I might have to fiddle with my settings more but the colors are a bit too washed out for me at time, or that the way the game is stylized is maybe a TAD too realistic. The game was truly incredible visuals when it wants to though. Rey Dau's blue crystals on its wings, Uth Duna's body is a very intoxicating and envigorating shades of red and blue, and certain enviroments like the Windward plains during the day time can be very breathtaking. 

I personally have not had an insanely big issue with the games performance throughout it's entire time being out, not in a way that impeded what I wanted from the game (hunting and feeling cool as I practiced and mastered certain weapons and monsters). But even on that front they've improved it so much with the recent patches.

If you enjoy your grinding and you enjoy feeling cool, with maybe some multiplayer on the side, its worth your time.

I truly wanted to like this game. I’ve loved monster hunter since I first played GU as a child. I was so excited for older monsters to return, and the designs and movement of the newer monster was so promising. However, there came a point where I was no longer playing because I enjoyed it, and only played because I was convinced that surely, surely high rank would be fun. Surely getting past the story is the answer. Surely being free of the railroad story that doesn’t allow me to explore would mean I would have fun. Surely there would come a point where the Seikret wouldn’t autopilot to the monster and I could actually hunt again. Surely the modifications and ammo for the light bow gun, which has been my main weapon since I was a child, were just locked behind high rank. Surely I could just breeze through low rank in less than a day. Despite how easy it is, despite how much it forces you to play the story mode, to not “waste time” refighting monsters or exploring the area, it felt like the longest slog of my life to just get to arkveld. And when I did, it was in the most pathetically small arena, clearly not designed with the monsters body and attacks in mind. The two of us ran around that stupid pole in the middle of the room, arkveld never even getting a hit on me, and I knew I was done. I hope the monsters get used for a game that actually cares about them, and I’m sorry to the development team who was so clearly forced to insert a monster hunter game into the same AAA rpg that can be found in any other franchise. I’m still a monster hunter fan, but whoever was in charge here clearly wasn’t

If you asked me, say... Six months ago if Monster Hunter Wilds was worth the money, I would've told you no. I've been playing this series for twenty years, played every version that was localized to the west and even played some that weren't. Capcom still has a long, LONG way to go before they've earned my trust back as a whole, but that said, they have made great strides in optimizing the game and making it actually, genuinely playable without me needing to shell out several hundred dollars just to even upgrade my GPU to something from as late as a 30XX series-era card. If they can manage to release more games in this series to PC in an actual polished, optimized state (looking at you DLC frame checks...) in the future, then I will be more than happy to buy those as well. That said, this is about Wilds, and honestly Monster Hunter has never been better, even as a grumpy old veteran hunter who remembers when 'these games were hard, blahblahwahwah'. This is genuinely a bonified treat, the story is a little weak but it's still the best monster hunter's ever had (outside of Stories, of course). Making a build for your favorite weapon has never been so approachable, and even if the early game feels like you're just kinda slapping these poor beasties around, eventually it'll be your turn. With the DLC news coming this summer, there's never been a better time to get in and get hunting, so grab a friend or three and become Monster Hunters.

As someone who has loved monster hunter since 3U I do think wilds is a very very good game, but one that is sadly flawed in a lot of small ways that stem from their desire to Modernize the gameplay

Combat: The weapons feel truly at their best in wilds, bouncing from one to the other left me very interested in almost every weapon, HOWEVER. Monster health, especially in Low Rank tends to leave you hungry for more action as the monster plops dead around 7 minutes in. I have no gripes with the Difficulty of low rank, I am an old hand, obviously to me its going to be a cake walk, but I do believe it is an active disservice to new players to give them monsters that act as simple punching bags instead of something to make them plant their feet a little more and adapt

Map Design: The focus on Verticality reaaaaally hurts this games maps man, the map i most enjoy playing on is the Windward Plains, a map that feels much more like World's map design, a large sprawling landscape with small secrets and areas in areas that trail up and down. The heavy focus on this makes maps a bit of a pain to navigate and makes readibility kind of a challenge when just trying to find items or things like Campsites

Story: Its alright, I dont have as much of a hate boner for it as most others, its quaint and simple as all monhun stories tend to be, HOWEVER. What I absolutely despise about this AND World's stories is the utter insistence that your hunter is an old hand at hunting. Ripe with experience and a hard-assed personality. Only to get mopped a low rank monster. This concept to me, is sooooo boring, alot of the charm of old monhun was being a greenhorn, fresh off the boat, in nothing but your skinnies or Iron armor and everyone viewing you as this fresh meat to be chewed up and spit out only to work your way up through the ranks and prove them all wrong for doubting you. As well, Giving the hunter a personality was a POOR DECISION. In a series known for allowing you to create literal monsters in character creator why would you shoe horn such lovingly crafted freaks of nature to be the Straight man in any situation. Finally, STOP RELEASING THESE GAMES WITH NO REPEATABLE FINAL BOSS QUEST OH MY ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ GOD, This is the SECOND ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ GAME WHERE THE FINAL BOSS WAS LACKLUSTER, THIS IS A GAME LITERALLY ONLY ABOUT BOSS FIGHTS FOR ♥♥♥♥ SAKE GIVE THE DEVS MORE TIME TO PROPERLY FINISH THEIR FINAL CHALLENGE I LOVE ZOH SHIA BUT JESUS.

Visual Design: Genuinely, all the monsters look so ♥♥♥♥♥♥ fantastic, I adore the overall alienistic vibe the whole of the Forbidden Lands gives off, you really do feel like youre stepping into unfamiliar territory and that these new beasts are something that no hunter before has even had the chance to spar against.

Characters: Simple, fun, Alma needs to stop talking sometimes after i cart though I be getting tilted. But i genuinely do like most of them. Its cool that Werner looks like OneyPlays

Music/Sound Design: Oh my ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ goooooooooooooooooooooooooood radiant cry of life is soooooooooo good. Wilds compositions are top notch It really brings the whole thing together. Monster noises? Top notch as always, every noise these freaks creak out really brings it home just how obtuse the Forbidden Lands are, I mean, the SOUNDS Rey Dau makes as he flies are almost hypnotic, theyre so strangely pleasant to the ear. We always be praising MonHun Sound Effects team.

Graphics: Look Gorgeous after the most recent update! But before? Yikes dude... i feel like it was sony more pushing to really make this series a graphical powerhouse so I truly cant blame them but still I woulda been fine if this game was just a slightly more polished World Look than Computer Melter 8 Million

The Horse: I Like Seikrets. I love my stupid Dog/Horse/Dinosaur 

Endemic Life: WHY EVEN HAVE THEM BE CAPTURABLE WITHOUT A ROOM??? ROOMS WERE LITERALLY PERFECT IN WORLD??? WHY CATCH THE BIGGEST WHOPPERS I CAN WHEN IT LITERALLY DOESNT MATTER??? CAPCOM!!! CMON DUDE!!!!

Crossovers: ITS PEAK!!!! I LOVE AKUMA!!!! I LOOOOVE OMEGA!!!! GENUINELY THE BEST TO EVERY DO IT PLEASE MAKE AKUMA'S PLAYSTYLE THE NEXT WEAPON WE NEED A SUPER FAST AND LOOSE IMPACT WEAPON TYPE IM ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ BEGGING CAPCOM PLEEEEEEEEEEEEASE ILL DO ANYTHING DUDE THIS IS TOO GOOD TO JUST LEAVE IN THE DUST

But yeah, This game IS great, Fantastic event, but I feel like it also throws the baby out with the bathwater in way too many regards for me to be able to forgive. I love you guys Mon Hun dev team so PLEASE, I beg the capcom higher ups, let these guys just DO THEIR JOB. You can definitely feel they had to rush a couple things because of some due dates.

I've abstained from giving my thoughts publicly for sometime on Wilds as I am a hugely biased MH fan. But I can say with how many of my friends I have introduced to not only Wilds but the series as a whole that this game is massively worth it for anyone even mildly interested in the franchise. One of the best starting points for a new hunter, narratively its weak but has points that makes it much more interesting than previous titles. The weapon system/focus system are as advanced as ever and yet seamless in gameplay, to the point I dare call it 'immersive' despite having the reticle in front of you. The ecology aspect is a huge selling point and it delivers better than previous titles but falls short due to the lackluster 'hunting' mechanics that were present in its predecessor (MH World).

All in all this game rates a non-biased 7/10, and a personal score of 9/10. I love every aspect of this game, the monetization is a bit much & they took forever to make sure that it is playable to a larger audience due to performance, but I am at a point where I can guilt free recommend this game to others.

My honest review of the game so far. As a Monster Hunter fan since MH: World, it's a great entry, especially after all the title updates for the base game. Capcom listens to the community of this game and I am excited for the expansion--whenever that may be...
Pros: 
- Pretty lighting 
- Extremely fun combat
- Great animation
- Pretty good story for a main MH entry
- Really good base game
- Honorable mention: the expansion will probably be incredible if its as good as MH World: Iceborne and provide so much more content, including higher difficulty (Master Rank or G-Rank). 

Cons: 
- Map designs are fine, but can feel a bit dull (especially compared to World). Also, the whole "open world" aspect that they pushed is pointless because the maps are just connected via long corridors that you truly never will use except for story. I fear this takes away much needed performance but it may not so idk. 
- Game is pretty heavy for low-end systems so good luck (it is a lot better than at launch)

I wanted to like the game.  I loved "Rise."  "Wild," however...maps are too big.  That's not a bad thing, but...they're boring.  Sand..sand...sand.  Or ash,..ash...ash.  Or water...water...water.   They're too big (essentially) to learn routes around...and why bother when the mount will automatically take you where you need to go.  I play Insect Glaive...love it.  BUT---it's nigh impossible to get all my colors.  I aim the bug at the body part of the color I want...but by the time my bug gets there, the mob has moved ALL OVER THE PLACE!  Yes, I know "marking" but that's (for me) shaky at best and hard to do given the mob NEVER STOPS MOVING.  Right now, I'm doing the alien robot and the "oil dragon."  WHEN WILL DEVS LEARN?!?!?  Putting the players in a space they cannot escape, against a monster that can cross that space in 1 jump in 1/2 a second (while it takes me 7+ seconds to make the same journey on foot)--is not "fun" and is not "balance."  And that monster does inescapable area-wide AoE blasts.  Can I do that?  No...I cannot.  That is not "fun" nor is it "balance."  And when I have to stop and drink a healing potion...that takes 7+ seconds to achieve, during which time the mob leaps across the room 3x and does 3 inescapable AoE blasts on each landing....That is not "fun" nor is it "balance."

Challenge is one thing.  Stupid "punish the players for playing" is quite another.

As someone who played every Main Title since 3U I really dont get the hate for the game.

The campaign is ok, but MH was never meant to have deep story. The game structure is more in line with MH: World.

You have to play the story and unlock more and more hunts for the multiplayer hub. It might feel more like the campaign is a huge tutorial but for those who like to coop campaigns it is a bit easier to do than in World (no longer need to both start the mission separatly and than rejoin after the unskipable monster cinematic). 

I like the old structure with separate story and multiplayer hubs but at least the cinematics are skipable now :-)

Base Wilds is a bit easier than Base World difficultywise and more in line with Base Rise.

I like the monster lineup alot. Battle feel more fluid. Weapons got some good quality of life. Early Decoration Armor (I hate to look like a clown until after the base game or worse after the expansion).

You now get deco armor mid story and deco weapon near the end of the story.

Farming for gear and other mats got alot easier but as someone who has a job and responsibilities I welcome that ;-)

I am eager to see what the true endgame after master rank looks like.

The aesthetics of the game are very nice. It tends to look a bit blurry in wide areas though.

I can guess the minimal requirements are a bit off though. You should at least bring some rig that is comparable to a PS5.

Have some mid class pc myself and after updating drivers the game ran smooth (even better now after the recent patch) from the get go.

Have the game on the PS5 too and they look and run comparable.

On a side note: Set your game language to Japanese and the text to whatever you a comfortable with. Japanese Palicos are cuteness overload :-)

Monster hunter wilds feels like it sanded down every rough edge monster hunter had until all that's left is combat. Grinding has been reduced to the point where farming gear takes around four hunts for an entire set. Exploration and map knowledge where made irrelevant by the Seikret autopiloting you to the monster. The focus mode makes positioning irrelevant because you can track your attacks in any direction all the time. Gathering is made irrelevant by the ability to gather at range while auto piloting. Making weapons is irrelevant because they added RNG weapons that outclass everything else at the third village.  Every possible sticking point between you and doing the story quest is gone and without it you are left with a short easy boring campaign. Friction in games is good, you need barriers to overcome and goals to work towards. Part of the satisfaction of monster hunter is learning how to get around these hurdles. Making a set to gather ingredients and doing side quests to unlock the farm was infinitely more satisfying than yoinking them from Seikret back. Grinding to get gear slows you down makes you learn the monster movesets and adds to the play time. Learning the map makes you feel more immersed and rewards people who put in the effort. Wilds looked to MH: World and MH: Rise and learned the wrong lessons from both. The immersion, charm, and attention to detail pail in comparison to World. The combat and buildcraft can't hold a candle to Rise. MH: Wilds, is a caricature of Monster Hunter with a boring story, forgettable character and a pathetic runtime. The game also runs terribly even if in the future this game is patched into a playable state we can't forget that this is what people spent 70$ on. Just play Rise and World they are infinitely better than this sludge.

Played it on release for a week or two, was fun but it ran so bad it made me feel motion sick so I put it down for months. Recently there was a patch supposed to update the performance and I heard that it was great, reinstalled it to try it myself and... it runs slightly better, but still not what I'd consider acceptable. On 3060 and 5700x3D running at 2560x1080 (21:9) I can barely get stable 60fps when I set absolutely everything to lowest with the most intense upscaling. At which point the game looks like a blurry muddy mess. And the 60fps is only stable when there's nothing happening, in combat it dips to 40 and even below that. If I slap FSR framegen on top of it I can get 90ish frames out of combat and 60-70 in combat but the blurry mess turns into a smeary blurry mess to the point where my eyes hurt when I try to play it for more than a hunt or two. I guess it will either look bad or run bad, most likely both at once. Not as bad as on release but even with the performance "fixes" I still regret buying it and would not recommend it to anyone. A pity because there are some gameplay systems I really do enjoy relatively to earlier entries, but I can't see myself picking this over Rise or GU when I feel like playing some MonHun.
Like it

Gogmazios fight sucks in general, but whoever came up with phase 3 should never come up with ideas for the rest of their lives. The monster constantly plants bombs on the ground, but you have to look up to attack it. If you don't attack enough, the DPS check will wipe you out. But if you focus on shooting, the bombs on the ground will kill you. This requires fast camera movement, but the game is designed to be played with a controller, which sucks at camera movement. Not to mention you have to aim to damage the monster, and aiming with a controller sucks. 

Also, many weapons are designed to make use of timing moves with monster attacks, either with offsets or well-timed dodging. This monster rarely targets you for attacks. As a DB user, I spend like 70% of the fight without the dodge buff because the monster almost never targets me, and I have to go out of my way to wherever it's attacking to dodge and get the buff. Who thought having 8 hunters in the same fight was a good idea? I can't even get rid of them to solo it.

"proof of a hero" being played in phase 3 is an insult to the great soundtrack.

The rest of the game is decent, although "hunting" elements that were in older games are almost nonexistent here. The game used to be about tracking, studying and making use of the monsters and the environment, and equally about fighting. It is now about riding a bird taxi to the monster then hitting it until it can't move anymore. Still fun, but way less fun than the former. I don't even remember the last time I went on an expedition to gather minerals and bones. The constant fighting is wearing me out very quickly.

Also, water monsters changing areas by entering water is one of the worst things in the game. Huge waste of time because they always move to the area above the waterfall, which is usually very far.

It's not a bad game, but it's a disappointing experience that feels like Monster Hunter with the teeth filed off. Combat feels stale and unrewarding, unable to decide if monster fights should be a cake-walk or a 1-hit-kill slog. Gearing is at it's worst in the end game, as it depends on rolling well on the gear as you make it, which sucked in World and they doubled down on it here thinking they could do wrong.

 Stutters, crashes, inconsistent frame rates; Wilds launched like it was intended not for modern hardware, but some sort of dream rig that doesn't exist (and probably will never thanks to component shortages.) The game devs still haven't really been able to sort this out, and basically blew it's initial good will with some fans waiting for a fix

I would love to have to eat my words here, but I don't even think a future expansion is going to be able to turn this game around. Many of these problems are core to this entry. As a whole; Wilds is a miss for me. I'd much rather play Rise, World, or even Generations.

Monster Hunter Wilds – A Wild, Rewarding, and Deep Hunting Experience

Recommended ✔

TL;DR:
Monster Hunter Wilds delivers amazing combat, huge weapon and build variety, creative monsters, challenging hunts, smart use of environments, full offline play, and a crazy-good story. Despite some pacing issues with monster travel, occasional forced party content, and minor performance problems (now fixed), the overall experience is extremely rewarding. Beating a tough creature never gets old.

Monster Hunter Wilds is one of those games that truly understands what progression and gratification mean.

From the very beginning, you’re thrown into a massive world full of incredible creatures, each with unique behaviors, weaknesses, and fighting styles. The diversity of weapons alone is insane — every weapon feels like a completely different game. Combine that with armor skills, decorations, and builds, and you get near-endless customization.

You’re never locked into one playstyle. Want to be aggressive? Defensive? Support? Pure DPS? Everything is viable if you learn your weapon.

 What I Loved

Huge weapon and build diversity
Every weapon has depth, personality, and mastery curves. Builds matter a lot and reward experimentation.

Creative monsters and encounters
The creatures feel alive. Each hunt is memorable, and learning their patterns is part of the fun.

Smart use of the environment
Traps, terrain, verticality, and map features actually matter. Positioning and awareness are rewarded.

Offline mode (big win for me)
Being able to play fully offline is a massive positive. Not everyone wants or can play online all the time.

Crazy but awesome story (no spoilers)
The story is surprisingly engaging — weird in the best way — and blends perfectly with the gameplay loop.

Real sense of achievement
Defeating a hard monster after multiple attempts feels AMAZING. Few games deliver this level of satisfaction.

Difficulty that feels fair
The game challenges you, but rarely feels cheap. Success comes from learning, not luck.

What Could Be Better

Monster travel downtime
Sometimes monsters spend too long moving between map areas, which hurts pacing.

Some content feels party-dependent
A few hunts are noticeably harder solo, almost pushing you into multiplayer.

Performance issues (mostly fixed now)
There was noticeable slowdown caused by DLC-related problems, but this has been corrected after discovery.

Unnecessary mod usage by some players
Not a game issue directly, but some players use mods that feel pointless or immersion-breaking.

Final Thoughts

Monster Hunter Wilds is a deep, challenging, and incredibly rewarding experience. It respects your time, your skill, and your creativity. If you enjoy mastering systems, experimenting with builds, and earning your victories through effort, this game is absolutely worth it.

Despite a few pacing and technical hiccups, the core experience is rock solid.

Highly recommended for both veterans and newcomers to the series.
