Beautiful. The staff have outdone themselves. I bought a 4060 RTX laptop for this and it didn't disappoint me. Lots of things to do for oldies like me (though the GogArtian grind is a little steep).

Don't get me wrong, I'm still very miffed that it took a year to get the game into a playable state for everyone. While I did not experience performance issues big enough to prevent me from playing at all, I know that was not the case for a lot of people. Launching in that state and then taking a year to fix it was completely unacceptable. Not only for the obvious reasons but it also sets a precedence to other game developers that this kind of behavior is ok. The only reason I am giving a pass this time, is because the latest patch to fix the performance issues was so good that I can now play at 4k at max settings and still get 120 FPS. Whatever you guys did, you did it very well and the game runs INCREDIBLY well. this wasn't just a stability patch, it feels like the game was completely fixed and tuned up from the inside out. a year ago, I finished the main story and did a little post game but i have been sitting here and WAITING for a reason to get back into the game and dive full force into what the rest has to offer.

As for the rest of the game, its fun, it's addicting and I know the bar was low to begin with, but this was the most tolerable plot to a monster hunter game by far. past games had decent plots but INSUFFERABLE characters like the Handler. Or decent characters but crap plots that didn't matter or grab any interest.
This game is much improved on many fronts. Alma is a sweetheart, Gemma is awesome and I normally find the cutesy animal sidekick annoying in most cases but my palico is pretty cool so I'm fine with him. he needs to get off his ass and heal me more in battle though. Hot take, but I don't hate Nata. I get why others do, he can be annoying but I've seen far worse and there are a couple heart touching moments later in the story. The story, while nothing to write home about, serves it's purpose and isn't too intrusive to the fun of the gameplay. Except for the cats in the jungle, i hate those guys they're annoying, but their section of the game is pretty easy to power through and then you don't really have to think about them ever again.

There are two core monster hunter aspects thought that have prevented me from being able to play previous games that are still present here:
1) The UI is THE WORST UI IN GAMING HISTORY. I'm mostly talking about past Monster Hunter games here but the UI in this entry is barely better.
Even after having a friend guide me through the horrific amount of poorly designed menus and charts, my eye still has trouble figuring out where to land on these screens and there are far too many design choices that make it hard to even figure out what I'm looking at half the time. This game is slightly better than all previous games, but oh my god, there has to be a better way to organize the sheer volume of information in a way that's actually digestible.
Finally the sheer atrocious volume of text has thankfully been toned way down for this game. Past games used to have more reading in it than just about any other game i can think of, and that includes interactive novel games. All to teach you basic mechanics. What one game would take a few sentences and a little GIF or photo to teach the player, past Monster Hunter games would take 4 full pages of pure text for some reason. There are still a few moments like that in the beginning of the game, but it's far more tolerable in this entry.
2) The grind. Again, I'm more talking the previous Monster Hunter games here, the grind in this game is better than past entries but it's still a big ask Even if you don't try to create sets for all the weapons and just focus on a few like i did, there is still a lot of grinding you have to do just to make future hunts slightly more manageable. If you want to explore everything in this game, be prepared to put in some serious hours. if you want to max out more than a handful of weapons and armor sets, be prepared to square that number of hours...im exaggerating but the crazy thing is, not by much...

I know it sounds like I've been complaining a lot and I have, but all this to say; Monster Hunter games used to be the least welcoming franchise to new players in the past and this game finally learned how to not turn away newcomers and I respect the effort for that. If you've always wanted to try a Monster Hunter game, this is your best bet to learn the gameplay loop and mechanics without being overwhelmed.
This is easily my favorite entry in the franchise and while I'm still not happy it took a year to fix it, I am happy the wait is finally over and I can start the post game and do some grinding and just enjoy the game again going forward.

GAMEPLAY:

PERFORMANCE:

Alright. Time to leave my review now. Seeing how Capcom handled most of World, I expected issues to settle eventually and I'm glad to say they lived up to that expectation. I'm writing this review to contribute to the wealth of positive reviews claiming significantly improved performance, finally. 

I feel my case is worth stating among them as I'm running a Ryzen 5600x, 16 DDR4 RAM rated for 3200mhz, and a 1070 ti. I have a feeling many might be in my position and wanting to know if their aged rig can run this game. I'll tell you this TL;DR yes. Longer answer, depends on what you can be content with. I run with most setting medium to low with low-impact settings such as bloom on high. I used to get only around 15-20 frames without AMD frame gen and a rough 30-40 on absolute lowest settings anywhere I could squeeze performance. Now I'm getting 24-30 fps no frame gen, and a very solid 48-55 fps with frame gen on low-medium settings. I'd say that's a huge improvement. 

If you haven't been following the development, good news is that the team is rolling out even more updates after this one at the time of writing. At this point, any rig with relatively updated CPU and GPU could run this just fine and at solid settings at 1080p. 

GAMEPLAY:

As an experienced Monster Hunter player who's been following since MH Freedom Unite, the feel of this game is a breath of fresh air. If you played MH Rise/Sunbreak and didn't enjoy it, but rather enjoyed World, you'll be delighted to know that this game is built on the skeleton of World and takes it to a whole new level with the active environments switching not only day/night cycles, but also weather, a persistently changing ecosystem that feels alive, and a buttery smooth quest system that feels natural to move in and out of. You have the option of taking on quests the old way; pick a paper from your handler and initiate, but now you have the option of opening your map and selecting any monster you see (showing bonus reward info) and creating an investigation right then and there! You can immediately choose to hunt it from the map. You can also encounter the large monsters in the wild which will initiate a hunt as well. 

Combat feels pretty much as weighty and polished as World does. If you played World and enjoyed the combat, you'll enjoy it here most likely as well. Not much to say in detail for the newcomers other than the combat is top notch. This is why you play Monster Hunter games. If there's any room for doubt about whether or not to pick up the game, it shouldn't be here. 10/10, won't find another game like it that does this point nearly as well, yet. 

Graphics, assuming your PC can handle them, are stunning. The world is so vibrant and dynamic. Fights feel like watching an epic movie. Battling the flagship monster feels like a movie every time it uses its strongest attack. The shifting weather, sand, storms, special effects from monsters, and textures for the most part, all look amazing. Apart from possibly not being able to run this (if you have a 2600x, 1070ti or lower/equivalent don't bother), you won't find much room for doubt here. This game looks magical and highly immersive.

Music, OH 10/10 don't even get me started. Absolute zero room for doubt. If you hate the sound track, I hate you. Your opinion is factually wrong and you stinky.

Difficulty, hard to say. Right now the game is not difficult as of high rank quests. I'm HR37, but I'm also experienced with these games. Comparing to other Monster Hunter games, I'd say World at times was harder. This is so far one of the easiest Monster Hunter games unfortunately. This is where a lot of the room for doubt could be for many. If you're used to souls like games, Action RPG as a whole, or have played other Monster Hunter games, you're gonna have to make your own difficulty here. There's not much of a challenge for much of the game if you're keeping up with your equipment that's available to you at any given time.

VERDICT:
Game is a certified 8/10 at the lowest. Easy 10/10 at the highest points. This is the kind of game you could put hundreds of hours into and not even realize it. The story is not a horrible slog to get through like World's story was. The environment and feel of the game is nothing short of amazing. You won't experienced anything else like it. Go in with a healthy expectation that the game will not be that hard for experienced ARPG players or that it will have a relatively steep learning curve at times for new ARPG players and you're in for an amazing ride with one of the best series of games there is to offer.

It's really, really good. Most fun I've had in Monster Hunter, having previous experience with Generations Ultimate, Rise, and World. I recommend it entirely.

Pre optimization patch, I was getting 30 frames on medium settings at 1080p. Post patch, it's at a constant 60 on high at 1440p. my PC has a 5060, and I imagine anyone who's only previous concern was performance now has nothing holding them back from enjoying this great game.

I will say though;
- The game is very easy. To be fair, I haven't seen G rank, but I'm maybe 60% through the main content playing solo and I haven't even been close to carting. I feel like this is due to a major compilation of minor "quality of life" features, which I'll get into, rather than an actual removal of difficulty, but for me it's very thumbs-to-the-side. Maybe a plus for new players.
- Not sure how I feel about focus mode. It allows easy access to most weapons coolest moves, like that thing with dual blades where you spin all the way down the length of a monster. It's fun and definitely flashy, but focus strikes on wounds provide such high damage, stun, and potential to create more wounds that it speeds up hunts by a lot. I found most on-tier fights lasting 10 minutes or less.
- The story is very good. At times it feels slow, but I prefer it to the boss rush simulator of previous titles. Nata is a very compelling character, chasing the box art monster through story missions feels great, and I like that my hunter is voice-acted. Play in Japanese with Palico language turned on and you'll have fun.
- Your seikret basically eliminates the danger from anything that doesn't one shot you. Fall on the ground? It's fine, press up on the d-pad and your chocobo companion whisks you away from (almost) all harm. It literally auto-runs by default so you can focus entirely on healing. It's like the wolf companion from Rise (but more OP) and I think that's a big reason why the community finds that game so easy.
- The grind is gone. Completely. Monsters provide so many materials that if you do your job and break enough parts, you WILL be able to craft a monster's weapon after fighting it once. Even high rank gems and plates and stuff almost always show as investigation rewards, which are no longer RNG but rather always available if a monster's in the locale. Use a lucky ticket and you can craft a whole armor set after one hunt. Big negative for me.
- Open world is good. It's not an overbearing open world, but rather a seamlessness between camp and the outside environment. Directly riding into the hunt from camp rather than dropping in via bird delivery is a great feeling. Sometimes this makes the map feel a bit small, but the way the environments change fixes that pretty quickly.
- Monster variety is incredible. My biggest unpopular opinion about World was that it felt like Jurassic Park. Too many stereotypical dinosaur-like brute wyverns. In Wilds though, there's huge arachnids, cephalopods, TRUE water dwelling creatures, and so much more. It's really unique, and the spectacle of seeing so many of these monsters for the first time is unmatched.

Overall, I think this game is really amazing, and for a new player, it's the best monster hunter experience you'll have. Most of my problems lie with the decreased difficulty, but again, I haven't seen G rank or the post-game event quests. If you're a returning veteran, I think the nuance has been taken away. No more tracking or grinding makes it feel like a lot of the true monster hunter experience has been removed in favor of convenience in excess. If you're one of those super hardcore guys playing first gen in 2026, you probably won't like Wilds.

Monster Hunter veteran with 2,000+ hours across the series.
Monster Hunter Wilds is an outstanding entry with some questionable design choices 

The attention to detail in monster behavior, environments, and animation is genuinely next level. The world feels alive in a way the series has never achieved before. Watching monsters interact with each other and their surroundings adds a huge amount of immersion, and I don’t think this aspect is being praised enough.

Monster and equipment design is fire. Some of the best in the franchise in my opinion 

The story is a mixed. as a lore goblin in any game I appreciate the effort, and outside of Nata it’s actually pretty solid i think Nata’s anime style personality was cringe inducing . That said the cutscenes are really damn good, and the monster encounters in them are cinematic. every time the handler authorizes a hunt i bust a lil 

I really like the Focus system and the new wound system conceptually, but they currently feel overtuned.
Hunters are way more op than before but many monsters don’t seem to have been given enough new tools or reaction speed to compensate. Some monsters feel too slow and end up getting stun locked for large portions of the fight due to constant wound breaks 

This is most noticeable in lowrank but Once the real game begins after finishing the low rank story, difficulty does improve and monsters become more threatening  but the Focus system still feels stronger than it probably should be.

Good systems overall, but they need tuning.

Specs: RTX 4080 / AMD 5950X 64 gigs of DDR4

At launch performance was rough even on highend hardware. After the January patch, it’s a completely different experience. I’m now running at a smooth 120–140 FPS at max settings, native resolution. Major improvement.

 here are some Criticisms i have 

I don’t like the Seikret mount.

Default controls are poorly mapped and require adjustment.

Auto movement being enabled by default is ass.

I dislike the removal of traditional tracking mechanics.

Always seeing monster locations makes paintballs feel pointless.

The pack mechanic is a good idea but feels underutilized.

I haven’t reached endgame yet, so I can’t comment on that portion of the experience.

Despite some questionable changes to the classic formula, Capcom has improved what they built in world/iceborne with this release. i would say Wilds improves upon Monster Hunter in most areas that matter.

Highly recommended. Hyped for the next expansion

Compared to other MH, I really enjoy the flow of combat and wealth of endgame content for a base-game. Of course, having played on launch, it wasn't always that way, but title updates felt paced well with enough content to keep me satiated. At first, there wasn't too many challenging hunts, but with the addition of Omega and 9* monsters, there are difficult encounters worth doing as well. If you like MH in general, you're sure to enjoy the game. 

Of course, there's things I could criticize, like the GPS seikret or too many people to a lobby, and I hope Capcom is listening to those criticism, but I do want to be a voice for the positive because this game gets more hate than it deserves.

As for performance, at first I had to use a cloud gaming service, as the game was un-runnable on my laptop. For reference, I have a 3050 Ti laptop GPU and a AMD Ryzen 7 5800U CPU as well as 16 GB of RAM. I didn't expect to be able to run it, and was grateful for the wealth of platforms that allowed me to. However, with the most recent performance update, my janky laptop with only 4 GB of VRAM is able to run the game with consistent 40-60 fps, albiet on the lowest graphical settings. I am happy to see that they are addressing these issues before the expansion, and can only hope that they learned their lesson and delay any future releases long enough to iron out these issues ahead of time. 

But yeah, overall, solid addition to the MH roster of games.

I recommend this game with an asterisk beside it. I started with World and fell in love. Bought Icebourne as soon as it released. Wilds is a great game, however it's missing a lot of elements that I loved in World. 

For instance, in World, you had to research the monster, investigate it, find it's trail before it would show up on the map. This made it feel more immersive and wasn't just a rinse and repeat hunt, like it feels with Wilds. The Seikret is an overpowered tool, IMO. It's too easy to get around the map. In World you had to run or fast travel, which if you ran, forced you to slow down and really look at the map. It slowed the game down so it didn't feel overly repetitive during hunts. On the Seikret, it's so easy to get around and with it's auto navigate feature, it makes the hunts go even faster since you no longer have to look for the monster.

I don't think the difficulty of the game is a problem. Some people say the monsters are too easy. Well, I guarantee those people have played a Monster Hunter game before and don't seem to understand that once you learn how to play the game (using your weapons, monster timing, counters/guard/buffs) it becomes easier. No matter which MH game you play. For example, I solely play with the Insect Glaive. I have used other weapons but that is my go-to weapon. When I first started, I sucked lol, but eventually I mastered the weapon in World. When Wilds came out, there were some new moves to the weapon and some new things to learn but for the most part, the weapon operated the same. Same for all the other weapons as well. I don't think people understand they've just gotten good at the game and choose to complain about how easy it is. Also, if you look at the history of MH games, Wilds is no different from the others. The base game is never supposed to be super difficult. It's supposed to help you learn how to play and basically get your feet wet for the real game - the DLC. The DLC is always where the games get challenging.

I have loved all of the Title updates and appreciate the fact that Capcom does it for free instead of making you pay for it like other companies do. The customization of weapons and armor are great. I do wish Capcom would bring back the "Apartment/Room" feature they had in World. I loved having my own space to show off all my trophies and all of the endemic life I had caught. I know my friends loved it too. Please bring this back for the DLC Capcom!

Now for the asterisk part of my recommendation...performance issues. I did not have any issues until the update after TU4. I am running a i9 12900k CPU, RTX 3090 GPU, and DDR5 64GB RAM. Respectfully, I should not being having issues. I check for driver updates about twice a month and update accordingly. Now my wife runs almost the same setup as I do, except her RTX 3090 died two years ago and so I replaced it with a 3070 Ti. Same CPU, same RAM, all of it. She has had nothing but performance issues since the launch of the game. Somewhere around TU3 the game became unplayable for her. It would freeze, crash, polygons wouldn't load, etc. Even when we turned all the settings down, nothing helped. Being perfectly honest, we have not played since the new patches came out (after TU4), so I can't speak to if they have fixed these issues or not. I just want people to be aware it's not just people with old outdated PCs having issues. If you buy the game, you might have issues, you might not. 

With all my gripes and the serious performance issues, I would still recommend this game. Even with it's problems, it's a lot of fun to play, especially with friends. There is definitely replay value and you will get your money's worth. My wife and I will be buying the DLC when it comes out.

Monster Hunter Wilds Review

Monster Hunter Wilds feels like the bold evolution the series has been building toward for years. Capcom has taken the core hunt–craft–conquer loop that fans love and dropped it into a living, breathing world that feels more dynamic and immersive than ever before.

The environments are the real star here. Wilds doesn’t just give you beautiful maps—it gives you ecosystems. Weather shifts mid-hunt, monsters interact with each other in natural ways, and the world feels unpredictable in the best sense. Hunts no longer feel isolated; they feel like you’re stepping into an ongoing struggle for survival where anything can happen.

Combat remains deeply satisfying, with each weapon retaining its unique identity while benefiting from smoother animations and smarter monster AI. Battles feel more intense and reactive, rewarding positioning, preparation, and adaptability. Whether you’re a longtime Great Sword main or experimenting with something new, every hunt feels earned.

Quality-of-life improvements make the experience more accessible without sacrificing depth. Traversal is faster and more fluid, tracking monsters feels more intuitive, and the game does a better job respecting your time—something both veterans and newcomers will appreciate.

Most importantly, Monster Hunter Wilds captures the spirit of discovery. It brings back that sense of awe you feel when facing a massive creature for the first time, learning its patterns, and finally bringing it down after a hard-fought battle. It’s familiar enough to feel like Monster Hunter, yet ambitious enough to feel genuinely fresh.

If this is the future of the franchise, Monster Hunter fans have a lot to be excited about.

I’ve been playing Wilds since launch, and I agree with the optimization complaints at release. I’m running on RTX 4070 with an AMD Ryzen 7800X3D, and I experienced noticeable frame rate drops and rendering glitches early on.

Despite the PC optimization issues, I stuck with the game because it’s genuinely fun. I also held off on posting a review until I had spent enough time with it. Now, at 498 hours of playtime, I feel it’s finally time to share my thoughts.

[u]Quality of Life Improvements I Noticed:[/u]
1. Palico AI - My Palico actually feels useful now instead of idling around so much. 
2. NPCs on Quests - They finally brought back the Rise feature where NPCs can assist you on hunts. It’s a great addition and made me care more about the supporting characters.
3. Improved Handler - This is a personal pet peeve, but I’m glad Alma is far less irritating than the handler from MHW. She actually helps and doesn’t constantly put herself in danger like the MHW handler did.
4. Map Improvements - The maps feel much more detailed and thoughtfully designed.
5. Character Voice Acting - Having our hunter fully voice-acted during quests and cutscenes is a welcome change after years of voiceless protagonists.
6. Gold Crown Hunting - They definitely improved this area unlike in previous titles where you have to manually observe and measure a monster if its up to par for gold crown size. With Wilds, you just have to use the binoculars and an indication will appear on the monster if its gold crown. I personally like this improvement because it saves a lot of time and back and forth. 

[u]Things I Still Don’t Like[/u]
1. Main Quest - The main story was honestly pretty mid. And don’t get me started on Nata—I was genuinely relieved when he got grounded and stopped showing up in side quests after the story ended.
2. Artisan and Gogma Weapons - You really have to pray to the RNG gods to get a good roll. Because of this system, there’s very little motivation to hunt specific monsters just to craft their weapons anymore.
3. Open World Design - I can see what the developers were going for with the interconnected open-world structure, but outside of the main quest, I barely used it. Most of the time, I just fast-traveled between camps.

[u]Things I Miss from MHWorld:[/u]
1. Quest Board - I miss being able to grab quests directly from the board instead of relying solely on the handler. Alma tends to wander around camp when idle and I think keeping both options would’ve been ideal.
2. Canteen / Cafeteria - Now meals are handled inside your tent or palicos deliver meals in the Gathering Hub. The dedicated canteen area is gone. I really miss having Palicos cook meals for us —and yes, I miss Granny Meowster.
3. Poogie - I miss having Poogie appear in each town like in MHWorld. Now, he is only in the Suja area. 

We haven’t received the endgame expansion yet (similar to Iceborne in World), so my opinion may change over time. That said, the game has improved significantly since the optimization updates. I no longer experience rendering glitches, and performance has been stable. I plan to keep playing.

Most of my review is written mostly with MH:World as comparison in mind, with some other and smaller stuff I noticed and felt like mentioning, my review elements are all over the place, sorry about that. 
The more open open-world of wilds is a bit disappointing, it still feels very much like areas just that they were stretched out to appear larger, and feel relatively empty to me at times. MH:World here maybe wasn't as open, but it was a lot denser which to me felt nicer. Many of the areas also feel quite flat. In the Scarlet Forest the water is just a bent plane for slopes... the idle water in general doesnt really look particularly nice in the forest especially in the flat areas higher up. I never felt the loading screens in older titles to break the immersion, I feel like loading screens here would've been fine, considering more than the other half of travel actions still have loading screens. 

A minor issue: On occasion running down a slope the hunter will have the animations and sounds like they are about to slide, but the slope is not nearly steep enough for that to happen, it seems very random, even happens in the Hub.
The hub is fine, seasonal event designs are very nice, as always. I also like the small animations of everything throughout the hub. Wondering why it was only released in TU1 and not on release though, constantly hearing the same camp theme everywhere kind of killed the momentum a bit for me at release, but it was mainly the the awful FPS at the time.

This is another minor issue but, for the Gogmazios fight, the player is told to bring dragon element weapons, amongst fire weapons, for the first phases. This is clearly noted to be an important aspect by the game, however playing it solo with only support hunters made me wonder: why do all the support hunters not use any dragon weapons? 
In general I find the Gogmazios fight to be a bit lame, or annoying even. It doesnt quite feel like a good fight, though I'm just simply not a fan of suddenly having to switch to this "slingshot minigame". First phase felt a bit lame/slow, but that's all I guess. Overall, imo not a good quest for grinding current endgame materials. Also why is the quest successfully completed music of the event quest version different from the assignment version? Would be cool to be able to set custom music for that using existing tracks so that the one MH4U theme is not just present in the assignment version which is not replayable unless you join an SOS quest.

Omega fight was hard, but pretty fun and quite challenging, I liked it. The support hunters on that quest also do a nice job. Though in the last phase I feel like Omega can spam the charge/dash forward attack far too much. The music was also good, for all the phases.

On the topic of music, I don't have much to complain about, though overall I feel like the music in Wilds sounds rather soft and, in my opinion, not fitting the game. There are outliers like Rey Dau's theme which is pretty good, but otherwise it feels like the music doesn't have much of an impact during fighting as it probably could have had.

With that said, I am quite disappointed that each main camp in each region just has the exact same theme and there aren't villages or village-like areas that is the "base of operations" for a specific area with their own unique theme like in old monster hunter titles, especially ones before MH:World. The game is amazing, the game I always wanted Monster Hunter to be.
Open World, seemless questing, smooth combat, interesting story and locations, gameplay loops that keep you hooked, incredible support from the developers through it's lifecycle and acceptance of community feedback. Conquering forces of nature and utilise natural ressources to your advantage. Like restocking on more herbs and honey for potions while chasing after a behemoth that is disturbing the ecosystem. What makes it more immersive, seeing the weather change seasons or day to night cycle - creatures react to it, new monsters come that adapt to this, they start fighting more for scarce ressources when the season is less plentiful. 7/10, but I'd recommend picking MH World or Rise instead until things improve.

Wilds is fairly bad on performance, and unkind with AMD graphics cards, but I do not recommend moreso for the game's overall feel.

The game's story is alright, though it's tedious and not built for multiplayer. Better than MH Rise in the story, probably on par with MH World's base game story. It's fine.

Monsters are alright, getting better in the post-story high rank quests with returning monsters. Some of the new monsters are fun, while others are less, or need to get to high-rank to become more interesting. Monster difficulty in base Wilds are pretty mild, maybe easier than Rise's monster, until the stronger monsters in high rank, with a massive difficulty increase for title update monsters, in contrast to World's steady difficulty increase or Rise's consistent low difficulty with some more challenging end-game monsters. Wilds does continue the trend of 'nova'-style attacks World liked, one of those things you either like or hate.

The overall premise of a contiguous world is largely this game's biggest handicap. Though the game makes a point of being able to move from biome to biome, but in practice, there's no reason to, and it ends up more like other games in the series. Maps are large, but are reliant on Seikret auto-run features and fast travel as the maps are more naturalistic and less truly navigable from a gameplay aspect.

The Seikret is interesting, fun with having two weapons to cycle through, a travelling supply pouch, and a pretty easy get-out-of-jail-free card, but they aren't really more deeply integrated. They are, at least, not as throw-away as Palamutes.

The visual style is also less vibrant and interesting compared to World or Rise, far more muted. Graphics are nice, as many RE Engine games are, but the emphasis on graphics has clearly taken its toll on performance, and the drab artstyle means compromising graphics to improve performance leaves a lot to be desired in the world-feel.

Combat is solid, as World was. The new gimmick is the Wound mechanic, but it's not to the detriment of combat pace like the Clutch Claw was. Wounds are certainly the most mild of the latest gimmicks (Hunter Arts, Clutch Claw, and Wirebugs), and give the weapons some extra flair and boosts, lowering the barrier to some of the more technical weapons.

Wilds isn't a bad game by any means, and this review being pre-expansion, things could get better. But it's just not as fun as World or Rise were, and until the game gets its expansion, and some performance fixes, I can't recommend the game at full-price. If it goes 50% off or more, it's probably fine, especially if you're coming back to the series. As for new players, World or Rise are just better experiences.

Now that performance had been addressed, I think it's about time to give the game some praise. I'll keep it short, and also preface by saying that I am very biased as a big fan of the Monster Hunter series; especially fifth gen (World, Iceborne, Sunbreak). 

In the game's current state, this is undoubtedly THE BEST base game we have EVER had in the Monster Hunter series. The combat is fantastic, the roster is diverse, and the music is genuinely incredible. I am also going to make an argument that the graphics are gorgeous, which may be a bit of a hot take, but I find that a majority of players do NOT configure their brightness settings leading to a washed out look. Please do so, and if you have HDR, enable it for an even better experience. 

That being said, it is not without flaws. Just like World, the beginning of the main story is incredibly slow and quite boring up until you get to the "fun" part (fighting the monster). Generally, Wilds takes a little bit of time before firing on all cylinders, which can scare off new players. In contrast to World, though, it is still much better paced and has interesting moments (and skippable cutscenes!).  There is also a solid endgame here. I agree with the idea that there could be a bit too much RNG, but it is nothing that will make a dramatic difference for your average player. 

In short, it is a better experience than base game World 100%. It is at least worth checking out, and I look forward to the expansion.

Monster Hunter Wilds is the kind of game that makes an absurd thought feel reasonable: if I could only play one game for the rest of my life, this would be it (assuming the servers are up and there are fellow hunters around).

The first thing Wilds gets right is combat depth. The system is approachable, but not shallow: weapon movesets have real texture, monster behaviors feel surprisingly “alive” and the world itself is bursting with flora and fauna in a way that constantly sells the ecosystem fantasy. The new “Focus Mode” (the ability to more deliberately aim actions) is a genuinely great addition.

Once the credits roll (the main story basically functions as a tutorial), the long-term loop fully opens up: hunts, build crafting, and mastering your weapon of choice. I put around 250 hours into the PS5 version, then bought the game again on PC, and after nearly another 250 hours on a 21:9 ultrawide setup, I’m even more impressed. Native ultrawide support, excellent visuals, a soundtrack that’s genuinely worth listening to outside the game, and strong overall performance make the PC version especially outstanding.

A year in, Capcom is still feeding the game with meaningful updates, and with DLC and Master Rank still ahead, it’s far from finished. Between armor sets and decorations that trigger specific skills, there’s plenty of room to experiment and fine-tune playstyles.

Some long-time fans argue that the series has shifted from Monster Hunter to something closer to “Monster Slayer”. Hunts are faster, monster tracking is more streamlined, and autopilot via mount straight to the mon. That criticism isn’t entirely unfounded, but for me, the faster pacing is not a downside. It keeps momentum high, respects your time, and still leaves room for preparation and coordination, especially once Master Rank content becomes relevant. Personally, I simply enjoy the quicker pace.

Despite only using two weapon types myself (you can carry two into a hunt), the game never feels repetitive. Switching from Long Sword to Great Sword alone completely changed how the game plays. The offset system for countering feels incredibly satisfying, and even after hundreds of hours, mastering it remains fun. And if that ever stops being enough, there are still 12 other weapons waiting.

Not least, I really appreciate the game’s strong emphasis on personalization. The customizable Hunter Profile “cards” are a fun and great way to introduce yourself to other players, and features like layered armor allow you to fully express your style without compromising your build.

With continued support and an active community, this is a game I can easily see myself playing for many years to come.

Happy hunting!

I’ve been a monster hunter fan for about 15 years now, and this game was extremely exciting for me. 

However, the game definitely had massive performance issues, and it felt unfinished like the shareholders pushed the Monster Hunter team to release the game before it was ready.

Even so, now they have managed to improve the performance SIGNIFICANTLY, to the point that it can be run at 30fps on steam deck, and the game will be getting more performance updates. I can finally recommend this game because of the recent optimization patch. for reference I have a RX 6750XT GPU and a Ryzen 5800X3D CPU also 32GB of DDR4 ram. Before the patch without frame gen on with high settings and FSR set to Quality I could get around 30-40 fps. After the patch that number has gone to 50 on average but sometimes reaches 60 with the same settings. Reducing FSR to a lower setting means I can get a stable 60fps. I don't mind the way the frame gen looks in this game so I've opted to run it at FSR Quality with frame gen on and pretty much all other settings on high. I waited a year to give Capcom the time needed to fix the problems plaguing their newest titles, and they failed me at least. TLDR: I can't recommend this game because:
- I am honestly happy that the biggest negative, performance, is seemingly fixed for so many even if mine has degraded. 
-Many players are happy at the increase in difficulty; I don't think there is a difficulty problem in fighting even the hardest monsters they keep releasing but I find the difficulty in getting the gear rolls.
-Story is lacking in ways that make it forgettable.
-Too much RNG farming for no rewards, in my instance.
-Unresponsive game inputs leading to frustration.

Performance for me, on high end equipment has degraded with each update that has somehow improved it for other users. 9800X3D, 9070XT, 64GB DDR5 6000 RAM, m.2 SSD and 1% lows drop below 20 so frequently that it is headache inducing. Even when using upscaling, the 1% lows are still below 20 when doing anything besides standing still.  Merely turning my camera left or right, less than 90 degrees, makes the 1% lows plummet. And this is even the case in the last areas of the game where the textures look like they are 180p. Regardless of the area, if I do anything besides standing still and looking in a single direction, the frame time graph looks like a volatile stock market graph. RE Engine needs to be retired from all open world game decisions. Capcom has definitely fixed some things that affected performance like the overly aggressive DLC checks, but it isn't enough.

Capcom said they were focusing on the story, but where is that at? The story is forgettable at best. I honestly can't even remember the purpose of this game story/lore wise. Characters' mouth animations look like they are just moving to the sound coming out, nothing looks like it was animated for the purpose of the dialogue. Characters feel lifeless even in the intense moments. For a world that they were trying to make feel so alive, it feels absolutely lifeless and soulless. 

Gameplay is just farming, but not in the way farming in Worlds/Iceborne felt which was rewarding. I mean you have to even farm for basic food ingredients just to be able to eat and get normal food buffs (world had unlimited food as long as you could pay for it while wilds you have to farm the quantity). You have to farm monsters for rng drops that then turn in to rng rolls on top tier equipment which also has rng rolls built in to them twice in the form of buffs and skills. All of that to then go rng farm for another piece of equipment that has so abysmally low rng to get good rolls that I downloaded a save editor, gave myself 9999 vouchers to use at an in game shop, spent 6 hours using 5000 vouchers to get the gear rolls, and literally got 0 top tier/god tier equipment pieces. I am all for farming, but when there is no reward, it just becomes a second job that doesn't pay anything. I put in over 1200 hours in World/Iceborne, 900 of which for World alone, but I can't be motivated enough to farm Wilds.

I also have to deal with the game just not reading any inputs. I tell my character to dodge? Half the time nothing happens. I get hit with an attack and am on low health? Takes multiple sheathing inputs and up to 10, yes I counted, inputs of using a potion before the character starts drinking a potion.

Hardware : RTX 3060 12gb / 16gig Memory / I7 11700 Intel Core 2.50ghz. (GHz does run higher during gaming) 

So I been holding out on my review of Monster Hunter Wilds. I have nearly 500 hours in the game, I have also done everything that is currently released and has been updated to this time. 

Performance: Is ok on my hardware. I normally get around 30+ FPS into slightly higher FPS near 40 in some areas and before the Steam Patch on Jan 27 my FPS would drop into the 20s in some areas during fights. Well that doesn't happen any longer. The patch Didn't really increase my FPS but it is far smoother now to play, no more stutters and it doesn't drop into the 20s FPS. 

Story: I enjoyed the story for what it was. It wasn't (Amazing) but it also wasn't... Bad? It was good enough for me to watch and stay connected through to the end of it. So that is a Plus in my book. 

Monsters: Like all other Monster Hunter Titles. We get a Flagship Monster for that Generational of Hunters to come. In this case it is [spoiler] Arkveled [/spoiler] and it is a great flagship for this Generation's Hunters. The rest of the roster as always is returning Monster from previous series that has been ramped up in the form of 9* Monsters and it's nice fighting them at this tier. [i] This ramp up of monsters was because all of us seasoned / old hunters wanted Harder Monsters and really can you blame us? [/i] Over all it made even the newer Hunters even better and more prepared for what is to come in the Expansion that is G-rank / Master Rank. 

I say that because all though we have gotten a mass of new Hunters in Wilds, a LOT of Hunters are seasoned Hunters and returning from Monster Hunter World Iceborne and Monster Hunter Rise Sunbreak they (Including myself) Wanted harder monsters to extend our time in the game, so for us it is easier and actually regardless of us seasoned Hunters, the game is easier at the start in general as being un-bias as I can be [b] (That isn't a bad thing.) [/b] It allows a new Generation of Hunters to come into our loved series and learn what Monster Hunter is and what it is about. To that I say that is [b] NEVER [/b] a bad thing. [i](The game will ramp up in the form of G rank / Masters Rank) keep your eyes on that. [/i] 

Only Negative: I have is the performance was rough and not just on my hard ware, there are many reports on very good Rigs that had been reported were having issues and it took a whole Year to finally see actual improvements. And having to play through that sore spot of the game's current life has left a metaphorical scar in my heart for the series..

But a Scar that is being healed since the patch of Jan 27th 2026.

Overall: Buy the game if you haven't, and if you are on the fence before but was waiting for the game to be in a better state and fleshed out hunter system, now is the time to get in and hunt. It is a great entry point for new Hunters and a welcomed one for Old Hunters. The Monster's have been expanded on, the graphic visuals of these monsters are the best it has ever been to date. That is no small feat. Capcom has done a great job with the game and as of the recent patch Jan 27th 2026.. You really can't go wrong in your purchase now as a old Hunter or a New one.

With the recent performance update and solid end game arrivals (at least for a base high rank game) I can definitely recommend Monhun Wilds in its current state. 
The gameplay at it's foundational core builds off of the system World introduced while including some aspects of Rise without getting into the more crazy territory that it was in. It's "grounded" but not too annoyingly so. Focus mode and Offset attacks are amazing additions and I hope going forward that Focus mode becomes a staple of the series, some will say being able to aim your attacks is ezmode, I say it's a welcome addition and adds another methodical layer to the game. Offsets are your "parries" that purely just enable those cool moments in monhun games where you turn the tide of battle in your favor, some are easy to land, others not so much, it's a solid risk vs reward gameplay mechanic. 

I have a small few nitpicks, namely Beta armor sets losing out on having slightly different aesthetics and decoration farming, the latter is fairly easy to circumvent by just hunting tempered monsters and doing certain event quests, however you are still at the mercy of RNG, vastly preferred the classic/Rise method of crafting decorations personally. Regarding beta armor sets, I hope that's something that will get addressed in the future Master Rank expansion but I won't hold my breath.
Another major con I would like to add is this constant focus on the story aspect for the more "realistic and grounded" monhun games. I flat out don't like it, we do not need walk and talk segments, the game's structure and pace really do not do the story any favors whatsoever, Rise handled it perfectly, have some cutscenes, have a monster intro, then fight the monster. They try to appease the more gameplay oriented crowd by allowing some cutscenes (not all) to be skipped, but you cannot do anything about the BORING forced walking segments that serve as GTA driving info dump segments.

Regardless of these nitpicks, they didn't ultimately deter my enjoyment of this game, and I am more hopeful about it's future going forward, it's just a shame it took them a year to really address some of the problems, but hey at least we got there.

Performance improvements are nice, but game is still a technical mess and unplayable for some. In particular AMD RX 6xxx series users with DCGI_ERROR_DEVICE_REMOVED crashes since a title update in the first half of last year. 

Beyond the technical problems; hunting itself has been mostly flattened to letting your mount autopilot to the monster as soon as the quest starts, the main story and it's cutscenes are generally bad (though this improves in the postgame) and multiple other gripes such as the removal of MonHun language make it a frequently disappointing game.

With the removal of several postprocessing effects the game can look nice and the fashion is decent, but your time would be much better spent with MonHun World or Rise.

TLDR; Completely unplayable a year after first release. I'd love to play it, I love Monster Hunter and this game adds and brings back a lot of great things, but in it's current state it's absolutely unplayable for anyone not using advanced (and increasingly difficult to obtain) hardware. As sad as it is, I just don't really see Capcom actually taking the time to make this game function as it should have upon launch, and don't see that changing any time soon.

Still completely unplayable nearly a year after release. No digs at the creative team for this game, the monster and environmental designs are gorgeous, and I cannot express how much I would love for this game to function. Problem is, it just doesn't. I've seen people gushing about how title update four has seemingly fixed performance issues, but after reinstalling to give it another chance after several more months of waiting for anything to happen, its almost indistinguishable to the experience I had on launch. 

The actual game mechanics, new monsters, area layout, etc., are incredible but as of right now the abomination that is performance and optimization is just inexcusable, especially from a triple A developer like Capcom. That and the continued bombardment of microtransactions completely spoils this highly promising continuation of the game series. Even if they make this game function after another year of waiting and I can finally enjoy a game I purchased over a year ago, I still don't think I could ever justify changing my review from negative to positive. With every release Capcom continues to obliterate promising titles, over and over, seemingly never learning from their past mistakes. Maybe they will somehow manage to repair this game like they did with Monster Hunter World upon its release, but it still wouldn't make up for their poor practices. 

It's been incredibly disappointing as it really seems like it'd be a banger but with Capcom continuing to blunder with every release it doesn't seem like they will ever learn or take the time to repair the games they horrendously butcher before they're ready to be released. If it ever does actually function you can bet I'm gonna play this for hundreds of hours, but even then I won't change this review unless some serious changes are made and they show they can actually be responsible enough to release finished projects and not prioritize just adding more microtransactions over releasing things in complete states as if it makes up for it. Bad Capcom, bad.

Also, just gonna put this out there, Monster Hunter Rise still looks fantastic for being "lower resolution". Still a very enjoyable experience, and doesn't require the use of frame devouring fog particles, skin with pores that'd trigger a trypophobic, and over the top rendering of visual effects that could be achieved by means that didn't try to turn your computer into a volatile combustion engine. Games can be very fun and successful without "hyper-realistic graphics", and if Capcom didn't try to push it into every corner of their games this would most likely would've functioned wonderfully on release. The texture compression is simply so terrible because it doesn't make things look stylized low res, it just potato-ifies immediately. 

Prioritize fun, interactions, quality of life and variety over needlessly complex graphical fidelity. Actually learn from this Capcom, that'd be really cool and might regain a little respect from your players. Or don't I guess I'm not your dad, I'll just swear off buying any more of your games if you keep going the way you are.

Ok, after roughly a year, the performance issues are overall fixed, and I feel I can give this game a solid thumbs up now.

It's not totally without reservations- I am a Monster Hunter superfan, but I admit that World-Iceborne is a better entry point to the franchise than this game, and I'd recommend starting there instead of this one. The price will be better, the expansion's already out, and I think it's a better first time experience.

But, for the price point, I think Wilds is worth it, you get more than your money's worth given the price point and Monster Hunter is always fundamentally a good action RPG series.

[h1]The Issues[/h1]

[list]
[*] [b]"It's now Monster Fighter, not Monster Hunter".[/b] World's QoL additions had detractors, but they were drowned out by A. The Fivers who hadn't played those older games completely outnumbering the rest of the community and B. On balance, World's changes were good. Infinite restocking is the only thing I think might be worth serious reconsideration, and even then I think it's an improvement from the old system. Wilds has been way more controversial though, in Capcom trying to avoid all "bounce off" still present in World, they ended up dragging down the experience for other players. [b]The result is that Wilds is great at getting people to finish the campaign, but worse at keeping you afterwards.[/b] More specific critiques will have to be in the other bullet points:

[*] [b]Seikret auto-travel just sucks.[/b] Press a button and you'll be Ubered to the monster on total autopilot, you don't need to unlock this feature, you have it at all times and every main quest will have a big ol' prompt telling you to use it. [b]PLEASE DON'T.[/b] Just use manual Seikret traversal. Auto travel can be a nice utility, but if you let the game convince you to use it as a default, especially when starting out, all sense of players getting feedback from the environment fades and it just becomes sludge.

[*] [b]HP/Wound balancing for monsters was badly implemented.[/b] Now, pre-expansion content will always be easier than post-expansion content, and the Low Rank campaign is supposed to be easy in every MH game. However, in Wilds, if you are even an intermediate player, you will stunlock monsters to death through most of Low Rank. At endgame High Rank, that's much the same if you're fighting most vanilla monsters- You need to explore the Title Update monsters, or the extra difficulty options like 9-star quests, to actually start seeing balanced fights. [i](note; "balanced" by the standards of MH veterans. for Sixers who started with this game, uh, good luck)[/i] I think much of the root cause for this is that Focus Mode only seems to have increased the DPS gap between veterans and new players, the latter of which often forget Focus Mode exists while learning the game. This isn't even getting into the Frenzied fights, which are actually bugged to not benefit from quest HP modifiers and so will make any player feel like a speedrunner.

[*] [b]The game is uninterested in teaching new players anything.[/b] One of the clearest examples is that the FIRST Capture quest was implemented in Title Update 2. Whereas World was very clearly built with the idea of players spending a long time in Low Rank if they needed it, gradually introducing new features and ideas, giving them a wide range of Optional quests if people need practice before the next main Assignment, Wilds just wants you to get to the credits already, which is why Quest variety is down the toilet, Low Rank has the absolute barest minimum Optional Quests, and you don't even unlock the flagship dynamic weather system until High Rank. This means, rather than engaging with the world on your own terms and becoming invested, you are blazed through from hunt to hunt without a chance to breathe.

[*] [b]The art direction does need to be criticised.[/b] While I love a lot of the art in this game, there was a clear lack of planning on how to effectively use that artwork due to the dynamic weather system. I think the Basin is the only zone with a Fallow season I like. This game looks GREAT during Plenty, but that's only 1/3rd of the game. I understand Fallow is supposed to look somewhat deprived and wasteland like, but you don't need to throw a giant grey filter over the screen. World also had some strange reservations about using colours too much, I didn't understand it then and still don't now.

[*] [b]This was a problem with World as well, but it's NUTS Capcom have not gotten the memo people want to play MH campaigns with their friends.[/b] Rise benefitted so much from fixing this, Wilds reverted things, and in general understanding how MP works is excessively complicated. There's simply no excuse for messing this up again.
[/list]


[h1]So wait, what do you like?[/h1]

[list]
[*] [b]The combat.[/b] I main Hunting Horn, so I'm biased, but I think I prefer Wilds combat over both versions of 5th Gen's combat. I don't think Focus Mode is perfect, but I think the critiques in some corners of the community are overblown, I don't think it as fundamentally interferes with combat in the way Wirefalling or Clutch Claw did. Overall, Wilds has shown especially through TUs that monsters are capable of producing some phenomenal fight design in this game and the core of the combat is super fun.

[*] [b]The story.[/b] Your mileage will absolutely vary here, this story is nice IMO [i]by Monster Hunter standards,[/i] which means the bar is well below the Earth's crust. There is one particular important character and their arc which, yes, is excessively rushed and it does really annoy some people, but I am personally able to look past that and really appreciate what's there, I think the story makes this game overall notably better than it would've been without one, which I definitely couldn't say about World, and given base Rise would struggle to say about Rise as well.

[*] [b]The roster.[/b] Is it too soon to call this the best roster in a non-expansion MH game ever? Maybe it is, 4 is stiff competition, but there isn't a single miss out of the original monsters IMO, and 4 didn't have Lagiacrus, so it can suck it. My biggest critiques (besides the HP scaling) are that Yian Kut Ku isn't adapted well and Gravios feels a bit rushed, that's pretty much it. So many monsters in this game I could easily see being someone's all time favourite.

[*] [b]The ecology.[/b] When I first booted up MH: Tri as a kid, that opening cutscene might as well have been a cult recruitment video or a brainwashing program. When I was quietly fishing in this game a few months ago, and had my heart jump spotting a Lagiacrus swim through the water, then steadily watch as it looked for a meal... That's it, man. That's Monster Hunter, that's why these games exist. As long as they keep striving for this vision as their main priority, I'm along for the ride.
[/list]


And, well... Those are the most important things in the game, right? [b]On the fundamentals, I think Wilds got pretty much everything right,[/b] the problem was A. The performance issues, which sort of are a fundamental problem in their own right, and B. Design decisions that either overshadowed those fundamentals or lessened their positive benefits on the game.

An amazing monster roster is less important if you don't have good options to fight most of them with balanced HP pools. A good combat system doesn't matter as much if you can only really appreciate it in endgame. And ecology doesn't matter as much in a game that doesn't have the confidence to let people get lost in its world.


Though that's a somewhat somber note, it is one that leaves me optimistic about Wilds' future and its MR expansion. Wilds' development has been nothing if not responsive to feedback, and I think the MR expansion, Portable 6th and Gen 7 will be shaped by Wilds' reception, hopefully for the better.

I will start with the most important thing which is the performance. The patch that came out on 28.01 boosted my performance by @20fps, from mid 60's to mid 80's, without frame gen. I have a 4080 and a i9-13980HX (yes, i run this on a laptop). All in all I'm satisfied with how the game runs on my machine so that's that. Better late than never i guess... but let's be very clear and real, i don't blame the Dev's for the state that this game was released in, i blame the suits which wanted to maximize profits before the end of Capcom's fiscal year. Dev's have no say in such matter's as long as consumers come secondary to shareholders. Sadly this is the world that we currently live in and I'm already resigned with the situation as i expect it to happen more and more, especially for public traded companies like Capcom.

Now on the game itself. For me personally, is the best iteration of a MH game, especially when it comes to the combat aspect of the game. All weapons are tons of fun, giving you lot's of options to deal with the monsters (offsets, counters, perfect dodges, blocking etc.). They’re incredibly satisfying to learn and master. Yes, the low rank and a big chunk of high rank is easy if you have experience with the series, but this also facilitates a good point of entry for new people that want to get into it, which is great. 9 Star Monsters/AT Monsters/Savage Omega is where the difficulty lies if that’s still not enough for you, you can always make your own suffering: use no refills, no Seikret, or go full caveman and fight them naked. 

Honestly, in my opinion, this is the best end game of any base MH game and is not even close. Some of the higher-difficulty monsters already feel like Master Rank with how aggressive they are. I expect them to be on crack when the actual Master Rank expansion comes around, considering how OP some of our end game builds already are. It also gave some of the best new monster additions in a long time, again in my opinion, with monsters like Rey Dau, Jhin Dahaad, Arkveld, Nu Udra etc. as well as some of the coolest looking gear in the series (you can also combine the intergender sets now, no more F or M only gear). 

I have 436 hours at this moment and i will continue to play it, not necessarily for the grind (which there is a lot of), but to learn new weapons I never touched in previous games. (i play 6 weapons atm, so 7 more to go). What else can i say... i can go on and on talking about the attention to detail for the map designs, ecology, world building and all other aspects of the game but at the end of the day, I just love fighting big ass monsters. Is that simple.

Truly one of the most disappointing releases for me this decade at the start. I paid full price on release day and had such a hard time playing this game. As of the 1/27/26 update the performance is now up to standard and you can actually play it.

The gameplay itself is immaculate. I personally really enjoy focus mode even if the wound system and deflated health values of monsters makes it so you can sub 10 most monsters on your first try. It was unplayable for so long due to performance but again it now seems to be mostly taken care of, and I can only hope this is indicative of an upward trend for this game.

The online experience is 10 steps back from previous entries, and World was itself a failure in this department. No excuse to not let people watch the same cutscene so that they can progress thru the game co-op. Full stop. The developer intent for you to sit thru the story is farcical. We were able to accomplish this with the best story in the franchise on MH4U. This decision is mind-boggling. This is still my largest gripe with this game and it is a large one in my opinion. This series was always intended to be enjoyed best in a multiplayer environment and I yearn for more MMO style features, hunts and QOL. However Capcapom seems intent on making some weird hybrid of a singleplayer-multiplayer title. It does not really work in either directions favor. They incentivize hunting in groups but you cannot hunt any new monster without watching the cutscene, backing out, and starting again with everyone at the right progress point.

Endgame is fine if not incredibly boring and unrewarding. Just as unengaging as Rise's anomaly system. I sleep. Gog artian is just another layer to the endless "grind this one monster until the next update" loop. I wish there was a bit more variety in what we can do pre-expansion but I guess I might be asking for too much.

Writing this review on the 28th of January 2026, the day of the performance patch for clarity.

I feel like I can now recommend this game, it really did improve the game and from what I have been gathering online most people are noticing a significant improvement in terms of performance and stability, noting the 1% lows to be significantly improved.

Just recommending based on performance is kind of stupid, there are many other reasons I did want to recommend it for before said Performance patch.

Many of the weapons have gotten updates to their movesets, each feels fun and rewarding, over time some got buffed to be on par or even better than others.
The combat flow is great and rewards mastery over the movesets and the monsters attack patterns, when it clicks, it clicks hard and you feel invincible, countering every move, dodging into yet another high damage combo, it just feels very great.

Monsters of this title are also good now, they did tackle the diversity issues of the release, where farming one monster was optimal for you to progress your endgame meta sets, but now you have a wide choice of things to do, especially if you just picked up this game.
And the monsters of this title, including the apex monsters are all great, they look and play out very differently, perfect to invest your time into mastering each of these and truly demolish them.

The soundtrack is as always great, they kind of perfected it ages ago and it hasnt let off.

The endgame, as mentioned is varied and you have a couple options, you can farm the latest big title update monster Gogmazios to further improve your weapons, or choose to hunt 9 star monsters for a chance of getting a great talisman to adjust your build further, you can decide to do the Arc-Tempered variants, a super strong version of the apex monsters, for better gear or help out other hunters.
Obviously this doesnt last you forever, but for what its worth it already lasted me about 180 hrs of playtime.

It was very depressing to see this game received badly due to performance issues, but it was rightfully so and I am very happy to see it improved so much that I can now recommend this game fully and finally say it has been fixed for most people.

I would also like to add that I have stuck with this game series since Monster Hunter Freedom Unite on the PSP, so if I say this games combat is smooth, then oh yes its smooooth.
Also there is an expansion to look forward to, which will likely be a great addition to the game and introduce a lot of new things to do and see.

After playing for 65 hours and experiencing every single monster quest in the game (minus Gogmazios), I feel like I can safely say that this is a good game. It managed to ignite my love for Monster Hunter in a way that World couldn't. I've liked Monster Hunter for a while now, but after this game I can now definitively say that I love it. 

I would recommend this game, but with a large asterisk.

Like everyone else has said, this game is horribly optimized. If you don't have a PC that can brute force it, you're gonna be playing at a solid 30-45 FPS for the entire game. I have an RTX 5050, and it runs the game at 45-54ish FPS with a mix of high/max settings without DLSS or regular Frame Generation enabled.

Gameplay wise, I have very few complaints! It offers the most dynamic fight system yet, possibly barring Sunbreak. It offers similar movement to World, but the addition of Seikrets makes the game feel much more fluid and less clunky. There are many minor adjustments that make the combat extremely addicting. 

However, I must say that with the inclusion of a fast mount that auto-targets the monster's location, this game feels less like you're hunting the monsters and more like you're terrorizing them. Chasing after them is fun and cuts out the menial job of looking for the monster, but having to pick up trails and sample organic evidence to locate the monster in previous games like World really made me feel like I was tracking a wild animal in order to study it (and then kill it). This is really my only complaint about the core gameplay. I've seen other players mention that the Wound/Focus system feels brainless and rewards unskilled gameplay, which I agree with, but it looks and feels so badass that I can't bring myself to dislike it. Dragging a giant sword through a monster's belly is a feeling that only this game will ever give me. 

This isn't classic Monster Hunter by any means, but the future is now, old man, and that future looks like hopping on monsters and spiraling down through their spine.

Monster Hunter Wilds can be considered a great entry to the series with a lot of wasted potential. Although the combat is the best ever in the series, the monsters are all great, and the story is decent, all the new systems that were added really feel like they don't matter. Also, the fact that you have massive maps with dozens of monsters but 90% of the quests are "hunt 1/2 monsters" that end super quickly when compared to previous games in the series is an incredible let down aspect.
 
But the main issue this game always had was related to performance. Since launch, performance was a mess, plagued by fps stutters that made the game run poorly on pcs from low to mid specs (with some high end pcs also having issues). And with every update it felt like it was just getting worse.

After a wave of negative reviews, seems like they finally decided to address the main issue. With the latest update, the game's performance is beyond anything it ever was since launch. It's still not perfect, but props to them for actually delivering on this promise.

This game has potential, but is bogged down by extremely questionable design choices. At its best, this game is fantastic, but it is basically never at its best. I’m not gonna talk about performance issues because that point has been beaten into the ground, but it is still a massive problem. The few positives are consistent among every Monster Hunter game, and where Wilds succeeds, previous titles like World and Rise are still far ahead of it. 
	Enemy design is fantastic, all the monsters ooze with personality and style, from funny designs, to creepy designs, to threatening designs, every monster in the game has a different personality. Visually this game is very impressive, but I’m not talking about graphics, many of the monsters have massive spectacle attacks that look absolutely amazing(Again, this is true for every Monster Hunter game, not just Wilds). And watching the monster ready some insane attack that covers your entire screen is always exciting.  
	The combat, when it actually works, is also very satisfying. Attacks are always fair and readable, even in more difficult fights. And learning the ins and outs of every weapon, and mastering the mechanics is really fun. At its best, I’d even say this combat system is comparable to games like Elden Ring or God of War when it comes to quality. Unfortunately, those games' combat systems are far more consistent, and Wilds has so many bugs and inconsistencies that I can’t even really list all of them. You will definitely have times where you get hit, and you have absolutely no clue how it even happened, which never feels good.
	Difficulty is questionable, Wilds gives the player many more options than its predecessors, with focus mode and the wound system. Which allows the player to do much more risky things, and play more aggressively, which would have opened the door for the monsters in Wilds to be some of the fastest, most aggressive and difficult monsters the series has ever seen, but that just didn’t happen.
	Before release, this game was advertised as being far more open than previous  Monster Hunter games, it was never explicitly stated to be open world(which it wasn’t) but all the trailers were portraying this game as having a focus on vast open landscapes. But other than the Windward Plains, every other map is pretty standard compared to previous games. The Iceshard Cliffs are especially egregious. For a game supposedly having a focus on open sprawling maps, this map is one of the most tightly cramped the series has ever seen. Which makes combat way more difficult, but also way more annoying.
	To accommodate the “larger” maps, they added mounts in the form of Seikrets, which I have nothing positive to say about, the best part about Seikrets is that there are guardian versions that you’re allowed to murder to vent your frustrations. The controls for them are clunky and feel terrible, they have an auto move option to help with this, but the pathing is also so bad that they’ll just run in the complete wrong direction for like 5 seconds before going the right way. You can also call for them to come pick you up, which can be useful to get out of a bad situation in a hunt, but because they actually have to run to you, how long it takes them to get to you is never consistent, I’ve had hunts where I’m fighting near a cliff, and for some ungodly reason my Seikret just decided to jump off of it, and then when I call for it, it has to scale the entire cliff before it can get to me. Then, after I’ve already escaped without its help, it finally shows up and carries me away when I don't need it any more.
	The endgame is also really bad. In Monster Hunter World, the decoration grind was brutal, it was basically impossible to get the build you wanted because of how rare some of the decorations were, this game fixes that. Decorations are far more plentiful, and I’ve rarely had times where I needed a decoration I didn’t have, but they just replaced this with the Artian weapons and Talisman systems, that honestly make the RNG grind in this game worse than the one in World. 
	The story in basically every Monster Hunter game is terrible and not worth getting invested in, and Wilds is no different, but at least they added skippable cutscenes. Unfortunately there are several parts in the game where you have to follow a character while they dump useless exposition you don’t care about in your face, which is basically the same thing as a cutscene, and you can’t skip it.
	A few small complaints
	-Palico’s talking, Why?
	-There was a filter search for skills in World, but not in this game.
	-When finishing a time trial, let me immediately retry instead of having to go back to the hub.
	-A lot of descriptions for skills are really unclear
	-Uth Duna

While the Link system (co-op) is a little clunky at first to learn, playing with others is easy, and even the support hunters make this an easy to learn entry into the series. With many events, different builds, and other features this game is fun. I have played all the Monster Hunter games so far, and this one is up there for me (MHGU will always be my favorite). The magic is in a vibrant, colorful world, a large variety of fear‑inducing monsters, and the challenge and satisfaction of beating these monsters. You want to metagame the hunting into oblivion, optimize builds while beating your previous score by a nanosecond? Sure, you can do that; you don't care about that? Good news, you don't have to. Hunt at your own pace, enjoy the tranquil world while fishing, gathering resources needed for hunting, or capturing cute rare animals. To me, this is the reason I'm always coming back to the series: the mix of a good challenge with an interesting world to explore accompanied by a cast of (some) lovable characters.

Monster Hunter Wilds is an evolution, not a revolution, of that series. The world is bigger, more detailed, weapons have some interesting updates. Hunts are a little bit faster and more action‑packed. You can breeze through the story in 30–40 hours and then easily spend another hundred creating the most fashionable hunter, beating every monster naked, or just gathering honey.

Pretty much hits all the major beats you love about Monster Hunter. My major gripe (other than performance and graphic issues you have likely read about in a dozen other reviews by now) has to do with the fact you don't really "hunt" the monsters anymore. You can literally press one button upon spawning into a hunt, and your character will automatically mount up and ride you directly to the monster, no matter where it is on the map. Even though the tracking and hunting aspect was fairly limited in the past games, it helped add to the immersion for me personally, and they completely removed all aspects of that for the sake of speeding up the gameplay. You can technically still go hunting for some of the smaller endemic life with nets, but there's no point other than filling out the log and a couple side quests. A nitpick for sure, game is great otherwise, a well deserved entry into the series. For sure grab it if it's on sale and you're still on the fence. I don't regret spending the $70 at launch!
