-I've been playing the monster hunter series since Tri, and I've played enough of Wilds that I can finally say I would rather go back to either Iceborne, Sunbreak, or Generations Ultimate if I ever had the itch to hunt. There are multiple issues with the game, some that the developers have directly addressed and others that have gone untouched for an entire year and only dealt with tangentially, but the bottom line is that the game is filled with problems.

-First, to get it out of the way, the game is still horribly optimized despite the developers attempts at making the game more accessible to lower end computers. I personally had a massive upgrade to my PC and I went from horrible audio issues with massive stuttering and slow rendering times with random crashes even in villages to just random crashes and stuttering. It's a mess plain and simple.

-Wilds' new gimmick during fights is the wound mechanic, which each weapon reacts differently to when doing focus strikes. Attacking a specific monster part to open up a target that when struck deals extra damage and staggers or even topples the monster is good on paper but much less so in practice. For non-tempered monsters below 9 stars, there will be so many wounds that you can easily stun lock the monster to death. For all the others, the wounds barely appear until the end of the hunt- which isnt so bad until I bring up the fact that some weapon move sets are DEPENDENT on "popping" a wound. Using hammer? Congratulations you get to deal more damage. Using Charge Blade? Well that's the only way to access Savage Axe mode. So if you are playing with a team mate who is eagerly attacking all those wounds, you may be locked out of some options for your own weapon. That is the most glaring issue, but ask anyone what their weapon main is and I'm sure they can find something to complain about that was changed. Attacks don't have as much weight to them anymore, which is disappointing when this is the same game that has offset attacks.

-The areas themselves are beautiful and stunning, but for some awful reason the developers decided that the endgame monsters you hunt only show up when the weather is bad, which doesn't mean climatic fights around thunderstorms or torrential rain. No. It means everything is horrendously grey and I can't even see how gorgeous the monsters that my computer struggled to render really are! This is probably the best time to mention collecting and Sekreit stuff as well. I don't mind the Sekreit for the most part, it's a better taxi that fits in with the style of Monster Hunter more than our doggos from Rise, or our scaly doggos from Iceborne. The autopilot is optional and can be turned off, and I do like how I can yoink a mushroom or honey along the way. What I don't like is how my hunter became omnipotent and knows exactly where the monsters are and where they are going. Is it better than the old games where you had to paintball the monster or run around blindly for ten minutes? Probably. But I think World had the better idea where you tracked the monster and your scoutflies figured out where it was after enough tracks. It felt like I was truly HUNTING something.

And speaking about hunting, this game feels more like Monster Fighter rather than Monster Hunter. I'm an experienced player (touch grass) and I didn't have to develop any other build besides my DPS set. I miss the days where I would need to hard counter a monster by bringing in a set to give myself all the advantages I needed in the hunt, because quite frankly, you don't in Wilds. Either you use your super dodge to become invincible, or you bring a shield weapon with Guard 3 and stop caring about dodging because you take barely any damage. The fights are massively skewed where if I hold up my gunlance shield, I can not be killed, but if I lower it my health bar does the giant red spazz warning that I can be one-shot from full health with fully upgraded armor from one attack. Sunbreak had some massive damaging moves in master rank, but that was the expansion! Fighting 8 star monsters is a breeze, while 9 star monsters feel like Arch Tempered monsters from Iceborne. So things are a bit polarized to say the least.

And finally, the monetization of the game needs to be addressed. I loved the event quests in previous games and the fun armor, weapons and hunts they would bring. Tiny monsters and massive monsters and arena quests themed to different video games. It was wonderful and silly and everything the current era of Monster Hunter is completely missing. Special rewards from event quests are just the seasonal armor sets that are to be expected in the game, while Capcom continues to lock more and more weapon skins and armors and more behind the ever growing wall of DLC. It's in such bad taste to still have such a poorly optimized game and be adding in unique weapon designs that transform into accessories on your hunter at the same time. Previous titles would have given that for free. But maybe that's what is to be expected when the game is released early, and cut content is reintroduced as a "Title Update" (looking at you lagiacrus and seregios).

-Overall the game does have fun elements to it, but I feel like the game is drifting away from the complex and confusing challenge that previous titles embraced. I want to have to catch bugs and hunt for a part repeatedly. I want to claw my way up the rankings to finally flex at the top. But I don't have that experience with Wilds, and if playing a game simply makes me wish I was playing an older one... isn't that a problem? Anyways I would wait till the game is massively fixed in optimization, and even then wait longer still because I know that the DLC will reintroduce the same problems.

I've been a diehard Monster Hunter fan since MHFU. I've always loved the franchise, but I cannot recommend this game. The elephant in the room is the performance. A Ryzen 9 5900X andn RTX 4070 Ti Super not even reaching 50 FPS without framegen is just a big fat no. The other reason is that the game is too dumbed down for newcomers to the franchise. I'm all for QoL features, but there is such a thing as too much QoL. You lose the essence of the games for the sake of bringing in new players, alienating the existing fans. If you want a modernized Monster Hunter game, go buy MHWorld, hell go play MHGU, at least you'll have 60 FPS and better content. When this game reaches a stable 60 FPS without framegen, then I'll consider the performance issues fixed. In the meantime, from someone who loves MH, don't buy this game.

16/02 UPDATE: So after a couple updates the performance is better. But it is still a far cry from good optimization. Another thing I forgot to mention is the absolutely horrendous textures. This game has some insane pop-in, as well as completely broken texture streaming. When a texture comes into view, it will always be a blurry mess, and then take 3-4 seconds before is gets scaled back up to the original resolution. Also the texture resolution on high (without the high-res texture pack) just look like a complete mess. Skyrim, a game that came out in 2012, has textures that have a higher resolution than this game. 

Speaking of the high-res texture pack, people were complaining about VRAM usage and stutter issues when using it, so what did they do to fix it ? They cut the texture resolution of the pack in half... The entire point of the HIGH RESOLUTION TEXTURE PACK is negated by cutting the resolution in half. I have spent so many hours modifying the config file, in the hopes it would fix the many issues this game has, and that has made a difference, but I shouldn't; as a user; have to do this. I am lucky to be experienced in a field where I can read and modify the game's config files and more, but other people aren't. They can only copy-paste stuff from the internet having no clue what those things do. One should be able to download the game and play it, none of this crap. Shame on you CAPCOM.

Another thing they did to "fix" optimization was give us more CPU options to turn MORE stuff down. If you thought turning everything down to low wasn't enough, now you have MORE options to turn down. This level of unoptimized slop is unacceptable from a company this big in this day and age.

I’ve put thousands of hours into the Monster Hunter series, and this is the first entry I stopped playing early. After about 40 hours, it became clear that the game prioritizes scale and visuals over responsiveness and consistency — and that hurts the core experience.

Combat often feels less precise than it should, performance is uneven, and even when the game runs at high frame rates it doesn’t feel smooth or reliable. The huge open environments sound exciting, but they come at the cost of clarity and pacing, which are essential to Monster Hunter.

Previous games like Rise proved you can modernize the series without sacrificing feel or performance. Wilds doesn’t strike that balance.

If you care more about how a game plays than how it looks in trailers, this entry may be disappointing.

For the purpose of this review I'm going to ignore the inconsistent performance and the fact that if you own an AMD card the game will just crash on you randomly and make you lose progress during quests. Thank you Capcom please bear my children.

[h1]Monster Hunter Wilds is a game that betrays every core value in the series.[/h1]

Monster Hunter is a series about facing powerful monsters in an unfair enviroment where preparation and knowledge are your most powerful weapons against the indifferent violence of nature. But none of those elements are in this game. You never have to stop hunting to grind materials to prepare potions, traps or bullets because you passively gather so many marials while on top of your Seikret with your slingshot that for the entire game I didn't even bother unlocking the "farm" that this game has. Arguably you don't even need to craft potions because you could just eat a bug, you don't need rations either because you could just eat a bug, you don't even need to make hot/cold potions because you could just EAT A BUG.

Speaking of the Seikret, you could just just call this game Monster Wilds because that damn psychic bird eliminates any semblance of hunting this series had left. While gathering tracks in World was admitedly combersome after a while, it was an absolute flavour win, tracking down the monster, studying it and getting to know it allowed you to immerse yourself into the world and was also way more fitting of the new games's themes of discovering an unknown land. But for some reason the ugly chicken not only knows where every monster is at any time but also the location of every single resource and every zone in the map. The only reason left to explore the admittedly beautiful locations is to find new camps (which is kinda useless because the bird is so fast and efficient you are saving 5 seconds max) or to complete some fishing and endemic life sidequests. Did I mention the bird just takes you to the monster automatically? Who in the dev team believed that getting to the monster was an hassle to fix??

The game is also seriously devoid of content. Before 5th gen, every new game built on top of each other, a good portion of the content from a previous entry was carried over to the next to make a more complete product (and meet the yearly release schedule, no one said those games were perfect). However with Wilds they decided to start over again with an almost completely new roster. On paper it sounds nice to have a completely new slew of monsters to slay but in practice it makes the game feel devoid of content. There are no real endgame monsters, after finishing the high rank quests the only thing left to do is hunt the same monsters you've already killed over and over to level up you HR, it's mindless and repetitive and it takes a while to get to HR 100 where supposedly the real challenge resides, but good luck getting there without dropping the game out of boredom because that's what this game ultimately is: BORING.

The monsters drop like flies, their patterns are incredibly easy to exploit and they drop so many materials that it only takes 4 hunts to get enough resources for a full set and a weapon. Compunded with all the other issues I talked so far it makes for a deeply unenjoyable, unengaging experience.

Someone is gonna point out that X title update added difficult fights or fixed performance or whatever but that is also an issue, not just of this game but the entirety of modern gaming. Releasing an half baked, incomplete product to "fix" later is now a sad staple of the industry, but in Wilds it feels especially insulting because you know they could have added all the content at launch but decided to stagger the release of content for a year to give the illusion of caring about their product until the big master rank expansion. There are absolutely no good reasons for the arch-tempered monsters to not be there at release.

This was less of a review and more of a rant on the state of one of my favourite franchises. Most of the stuff I said on this game could be applied to the previous 2 entries, and this game was the final nail in the coffin for me on a series that I used to love, and after this entry I have no hope for this franchise anymore.

Unlike a lot of people I've not had any issues with stuttering on my gaming laptop nor pc. I do not own all of the DLCs however I will likely purchase upcoming expansions based on my fantasy-creature-game bias lol. 

The story of this game felt a lot more shallow than previous titles, and also incredibly short. There is a significant lack of newer creatures that are actually interesting mechanic wise in the main story and a lot of the interesting monsters were glossed over too fast in my opinion. 
A huge appeal of monster hunter is the Elder dragons which this game just... doesn't have? TU4 brought us Gogmazios which was definitely nice but it is still lacking just random open-world events for endgame hunters. 
Zoh Shia was a really nice fight and I would love more grand scale fights like that. I feel like the plains has some great potential areas for these fights and it could really take use of the wide spaces there. 
Some of the dragon cutscenes I wish were longer, because a lot of the designs are so nice but it can be hard to admire their looks while they're beating you into the floor.

The open world did feel really small, and as cute as the seikret is feels wasted on such empty maps. A lot of the biomes are also extremely boring in comparison to some of the other games, so I am really hoping for a DLC which adds some cooler environments, 

I feel like a lot of the characters weren't really appealing (beyond their looks , cough) like the previous games, and it would be great to see more side content that gives them more personality. I also really miss the chef cat from monster hunter world.. but I will say the new food does look amazing. I really like how it changes based on the ingredients you put into the meal. 

The customisation for your character is still locked behind layered armour , which is fine. I like a lot of the combos, I do still hope they add more because it's really fun to reward yourself with a new outfit after grinding. 
I will however say : where the hell is my dorm/guild house??? I loved catching creatures and fish in worlds because it felt rewarding placing rare ones into my home - now it feels like I'm catching them for no reason since I don't really care for the quest rewards.

I'm past the end of the game, grinding crowns and catching small wildlife. The only thing I can really do now is spend the rest of my resources gambling for good dps rolls to max out my hunter which is not something I find all that fun. I'll finish trophy grinding and return when hot content arrives. If you and your homies need something good to do for a month or so tho this is totally worth it, maybe pick it up on sale though and if it runs well pick up some dlcs to spruce up your mount and character.. or the gemma skin. 

Happy hunting!

I'm not really angry about this since there's already a mod that can fix this already, but I'm leaving a negative review until Capcom patches this because this is a absurd bug to ship with and I imagine a very easy bug to fix. Not to mention that the game will run noticeably better once it's patched so that'll help Campcom fix Wild's rep.

Gonna leave negative for now but will switch to positive if fixed because Wilds is a goated game and I'm not just jumping on a bangwagon.

So as someone who has 169 hours on record for this game I will mention a few key things people don't notice about why it's so over priced and how it HAS to have this price...

This game has a total of: 140 or more creatures (not including the baby versions) each with their own animation, behavior and so on.

Everything in this game as I have discovered, HAS a part in each ecosystem. from catching insects, to latching onto creatures to hunt and eat. There has to be over 200 animations for each of these creatures, Then you have to also put into perspective the amount of audio mixing + ecosystem building they physically had to do for this game possibly from the ground up. Then you have to also put into perspective the IK of them. What's IK? It's the tiny tiny detail when a creature is off a cliff and looks silly sometimes. It's trying to stick to the ground mesh if its on the ground, and doesn't if it's flying. That can take A LONG time to fidget with. On top of this they have "Migrating Territory" and "Territoral disputes" but they're considered "Rivalries" in game. NOT TO MENTION! They have to run servers and the DLC stuff is more of a flare than anything serious. Sure you could get the DLC stuff but considering how much they probably have to do for server maintanence is a lot so yeah. $60 USD is 100% reasonable to me considering how much love and effort IS put into the game.

Sure some of the mechanics can be wonky but considering they're trying to be PHYSICALLY realistic? Yeah I'm 100% going to support their efforts for this game as long as they don't mess it up.


I will say however, that I DO like helping people if they are stuck on a boss, large monster, or so on, I will do my best to give advice on the boss. My comments are on for those who need tips.

First of all, I don't believe review bombing is really effective at improving a game. This has to be the most flawed game I ultimately decided to give a Thumbs Up to. I will detail everything I don't like about the game as well as what I do.

[h1]TL;DR[/h1] 
An MH game with great gameplay but plagued with swarms of issues from the notorious framerates to predatory monetization practices. The amount of monsters and quests felt sparse for the asking price at launch. Regular updates have delivered enough to warrant recommending the game on sale. Hopefully the framerate get fixed and a dozen more monsters get added.

[h1] What's awesome, Hunty![/h1] 
My personal favorite weapons play really well(Great Sword, Lance, Sword & Shield, Hunting Horn, Bow, Gun Lance) so I'm pretty pleased with how the base gameplay has evolved since I started playing Monster Hunter on the PSP. 
Each weapon is practically a whole new set of actions and becoming a real pro can take a lot of time and dedication.

Solid MH Action
Monster Hunter is the original "clunky combat" game but as difficult as it is, it's never felt unfair. Monster behavior is full of subtle tells and hints as to what you need to do. Greed is punished hard, but use some observation and deduction to become a master hunter. Basic weapon combat is great and a lot of fun. I hope the developers will add more monsters so we can enjoy more of the depth and technicality weapon combat has to offer. 

Lot of great QoL Improvements
I know Monster Hunter is supposed to be difficult but this game has made some smart decisions taking away frustration without nerfing the game. Your Sekiret is a great companion that really helps one learn the maps or just let players take a breather while collecting. 

The Seasons and Ecosystem
Each area has 3 distinct seasons; Plenty, Fallow and Inclemency. Each season has a distinct visual look, natural supplies like berries and honey vary in availability from season to season, as do Monster presence. Seasons seamlessly transition like night and day in the game. I wish they had some more distinct effects on gameplay beyond what boss monsters show up in each environment. It's an interesting system with great potential, hope it gets built upon more.

[h1]As Mediocre as Kutku Pallet Swaps[/h1]
Some of the ancillary combat systems from other games are gone. I really liked riding and controlling mid-boss level monsters in Rise. Mounting monsters in Wilds lets you open 1-2 wounds or so and that's about it. The really big boss monsters like Lao-Shan Lung and Jhen Mohran are nowhere to be found.  

UI/UX is dreadful but improving. Slowly.
Repeating a quest or boss has gotten a bit easier since late 2025. A lot of the default radial menus are useless, yes I am aware they can be customized and I'm still trying to make a load out that works. Equipment Loadouts are fantastic and I'm glad they're giving me loads of presets but dedicate some easier keys on controller to use them. I don't use the other functions anywhere nearly as much as I use presets. I'm glad I can reuse armor and weapons and save precious gem configurations.

With about 880 hours invested in MH World (on PS4), I was excited for Wilds. All the negative steam reviews held me back from getting it.

[b]Performance:[/b] I started playing after title update 4, so I cannot talk about how the performance was previously and during launch time. But currently the game runs great at and looks great. I've had zero issues so far with framerate or anything of the sort. I have an ok PC at best, nothing crazy.

[b]Difficulty:[/b] I could not agree more that difficulty is integral for MH to be MH and for it to be rewarding and challenging. That being said, I honestly do not see much difference (if any at all) between Wilds and World. I remember getting wrecked by Anjanath in MH worlds until I finally figured out how to play with different weapons and learning monster attack patterns etc. If you are a veteran of other MH games, you will most likely feel that it's easy, because you are overly familiar with all the mechanics, weapons and even monsters. And if you are new and interested in getting into Monster Hunter, there is no better time than now. You can play it at your own pace and figure it out as you go.

[b]Weapons:[/b] Are still as fun as varied as I remember, with some new moves added. I am not sure if I am missing something. But the weapons are still feel and play great.

[b]Overall:[/b] I've been having a blast getting back into MH. It re ignited the magic of working on lots of different builds, everything is viable, you can play your own way. Fighting is amazing, and all the monsters really steal the show, they've done a really good job with them, I am excited to see what new additions to the roster will be, new and old. And lastly, they finally have a fully functioning layered armor system, I've see some really cool combinations so far. As we all know fashion is the true end game of many games. Monster Hunter is no different.

I don't understand how a game could be released and maintained for a year in such an unoptimized state. 

Monster Hunter World, a game that was praised for its visual style and graphical fidelity, runs like butter on my admittedly older hardware.

Wilds, in comparison, a game that doesn't look all that much more computationally intense, and SHOUDLN'T be so intense in comparison to World, given said previous entry's sheer scale, runs at 15 frames per second OR LESS on the same hardware that has, in the past and currently, run Monster Hunter World effortlessly.

I do not recommend this product for anyone that is on any type of budget. 4gb VRAM, no go. 8gb RAM, not a chance. Intel integrated graphics? What are you, insane? Even as a fledgling Monster Hunter veteran, If you do not have a NASA supercomputer, steer clear of this missed opportunity of a game.

However, even if you ARE running the latest and greatest in terms of hardware, reports have shown that CAPCOM's efforts in optimization have proven less than marginal, meaning that even those who ARE running top specs have shoddy performance, as well.

Please weigh your options when considering your purchase. In its current state, this game is not worth SEVENTY DOLLARS.

I'm one of the many people who initially played this game at release and came out of it pretty underwhelmed. I recently returned to the game (with Title Update 4) and am honestly very pleasantly surprised! 

PERFORMANCE
I know this is the big one for like everyone on here. Personally, I am not experiencing any major performance issues at this point (beyond little hiccups here or there as I would expect with any modern game on a new mid-level PC). The game runs smoothly for me and I can still play it at graphic levels that are very pretty without maxing out every single setting. That said, I've also never been as passionate about performance as many other reviewers I see here, so take this as the opinion of a "normie": The performance is fine.

GAMEPLAY & CREATIVE EXPRESSION
While it's not my favorite of the series, Wilds hits enough of the Monster Hunter patterns to feel satisfying while also introducing a number of welcome quality of life improvements. I'm not entirely positive on this point though, as I am disappointed in the more restrictive buildcrafting in this game compared to the more freeform approach to armor/weapon/skill combos in previous titles. THIS was the main issue that initially made me drop the game after launch once I had finished the main story. Now, luckily, with a number of Title Updates under our belts, a lot of this has improved for me, as naturally with more content you'll get more stuff to play around with. It could definitely still be better, but this makes me hopeful for future updates / Master Rank expansions.
The hunts themselves are solid and most weapons feel good to use, though I'll also mention that I usually play solo, so I can't comment on the multiplayer aspects here. Personally I enjoyed the high-speed frantic battles of MH Rise a little more, but Wilds still offers enough action to feel satisfying. I'd love to see more moveset variations for weapons in the future though (really liked the optional weapon move swaps in Rise).

STORY & VIBES
I'll be honest, this is probably the weakest point for me. I do really like a good number of the story NPCs in Wilds (very much appreciate the designs of Alma and Olivia, for instance), but the story is at times somewhat hard to follow especially towards the latter parts of the main campaign (I still don't know what "wylk" is, maybe that's just me though). I know that many players don't really play these games for the story, but as someone who, again, often plays these games essentially as single player experiences, it is a sticking point for me. It's far from being offensively bad, just kind of... forgettable.
The vibes of the world as a whole are quite solid though! I appreciate the details they put into representing several different and distinct cultures of humans, each with their own customs, clothing styles, etc., which makes the world feel a little more lived in. Especially when compared to some other titles that mainly have one or two hub zones and the rest is mostly uninhabited wilderness.

BOTTOM LINE
At this point (Jan 2026), I would absolutely recommend this game to any Monster Hunter fan, as well as to new players. Will it be your favorite title in the series? Maybe not, but IMO it definitely has enough going for it to scratch that MH itch. And it still has so much room to grow - it's always a little unfair to compare a "new" game like this to something like World/Iceborn with its complete offering of years of content updates and expansions. I for one am excited to see where they will take MH Wilds in the future.

And yeah, just to reiterate, performance is fine for me, so take that for what it's worth.
Weapon skill set bonuses and talismans are RNG as hell and a complete waste of time. Save yourself from fighting hundreds of Gogmazios for hundreds of bad rolls.

Game is great. It's a shame people are having such issues with the performance. There's a mod that's easy to install that uncompresses the textures which actually doubled my framerate (I hit consistently 120+ now). I don't know why Capcom couldn't just provide uncompressed textures as an optional download for those with poor performance. Onto some thoughts on the game itself.

The Good:
* A lot of weapons feel better than they ever have. Dual blades are very fluid to use and the perfect dodge mechanics provide an engaging way to add to the risk-reward.
* The new monsters are mostly great. I love all of the apexes (especially Nu Udra), and a lot of the lesser monsters are fun too.
* While the story of the game is incredibly easy, the endgame has a good challenge with arch-tempered monsters and 9 star quests.
* The split between weapon and armor skills is a lot more interesting than I initially anticipated and makes buildcrafting pretty fun. I don't know how it's going to scale into Master Rank, though. Weapons can't really get much stronger than they already are, and armor will reach a limit without the ability to slap point-sink skills like critical eye and attack boost onto them (it already feels like small jewel slots can be pretty useless if you have more than a couple).
* They've added a lot of things to grind for in the end-game that can keep you playing even after beating the most challenging hunts.
* The transcending system gives us a reason to hunt weaker monsters and makes them relevant for end-game gear, which is something the series has desperately needed. I always disliked the power-creep of title updates.
* Lots of QoL features and changes.
* The most interesting story of any Monster Hunter I've played. You can tell they actually tried to make a real story with real characters this time, instead of just a few action setpieces with generic setup.
* Overall, the gameplay is really fun, the monsters are cool, and the grind is engaging. One of the best monster hunter games to date.

The bad:
* The maps are nowhere near as good as the maps in World. None of them are as visually engaging, they're too large and empty, and there's not enough variety between areas in them (especially since most monsters will only fight in a few specific areas; there are many maps where you almost never see certain areas). The maps are physically bigger but feel smaller, as most of the map you just ride through on your Seikret while chasing a monster. Half of every map is a transitionary setpiece between designated battle zones. In World it felt like there was something to do in every part of the map, and monsters would jump you anywhere, even in corridors or pathways.
* Some weapons feel like they've lost their identity, as QoL homogenizes some of the similar weapon types.
* Quality of Life has gone too far in this game. They put a bigger emphasis on the open world in this game, but there's no reason to go out into it. Everything you need can be collected automatically by Nata and the villagers. You get more rewards for starting an investigation from the menu than from wandering the open world and fighting monsters. There's really no reason to ever go out into the open world and collect materials, which has the side effect of making it so you don't really go around and do the fun side activities like fishing or catching animals anymore.
* It is way too easy to get materials from monsters. You used to have to grind for hours to build a full set, but now you can easily get guaranteed gems from investigations. You can easily get all the parts you need for a full armor and weapon set in 3-4 hunts. The only time I had to grind for a set was Zoh Shia, because it doesn't show up like normal monsters. The drop rate is too high in this game.
* The lack of grind for normal equipment has caused Capcom to add many endgame grinds that honestly go too far. Custom talismans are cool but the RNG is pretty awful. They're not ridiculously impactful, so that's not a huge deal, though. What really goes too far is the Gogmazios weapons. The grind for the set/group bonuses is despicable. The Gogmazios fight is more of a setpiece than a fight you want to grind repeatedly. It's easily the worst fight in the game, but it's the one you have to grind over and over. I hope the endgame grind in Master Rank is more fun than Gogmazios.
* Lack of a place to display the creatures you've caught makes me sad. Also the lack of chef cat is disturbing.
* Overall, Capcom went a bit too far on the quality of life features for standard gameplay, which has caused them to go too far in the other direction for endgame content. A lot of the charm of the classic monster hunter experience has been optimized out with quality of life improvements, but it's still a very fun game.

Monster Hunter Wilds isn’t just a great game—it’s perfect. And not “pretty good” perfect. I’m talking Perfect Cell standing in the arena, arms crossed, smug grin, orchestra swelling kind of perfect.
From the moment you step into the Wilds, the game radiates confidence. The world feels alive in that terrifying, “this ecosystem could wipe me out and look cool doing it” way. Monsters don’t just exist to be hunted—they perform. Every roar, ambush, and turf war feels like a calculated flex, as if the game itself is saying, “Behold… my perfect form.”
Combat? Flawless. Every weapon feels tuned to surgical precision, rewarding mastery without ever feeling unfair. When a hunt clicks—dodges clean, counters crisp, teamwork synced—it feels exactly like Cell explaining his plan mid-fight because he already knows he’s winning. You’re not button-mashing. You’re evolving.
The environments deserve their own villain monologue. Dynamic weather, shifting terrain, and monsters interacting naturally with the world make each hunt feel unique. The Wilds don’t wait for you—they adapt. Much like Cell absorbing Androids, the game constantly layers mechanics on top of mechanics until it reaches its final, terrifyingly polished state.
And the presentation? Stylish without trying too hard. Confident. Self-aware. Monster Hunter Wilds doesn’t beg for your attention—it expects it. Because it knows what it is.
By the end, you’re not just playing Monster Hunter Wilds. You’re participating in its perfect design. Win or lose, the game stands there, unshaken, basically saying:
“Now then… shall we begin the next hunt?”
Verdict:
Monster Hunter Wilds achieved its perfect form. Resistance is futile.

Além disso, senti que teve bem menos monstros diferentes se comparado ao monster hunter world, mesmo sem a DLC. Pensei que dragons dogma 2 fosse um alerta para a capcom para tomar cuidado com a engine do jogo, mas esse jogo conseguiu ficar pior neste sentido.

Por fim, este jogo é muito bom, trouxe várias inovações com relação aos outros jogos da franquia, mas ainda assim senti que teve pouco conteúdo para o preço que ele custa. Além é claro da péssima otmização que ele tem para pc.

1)  I have no idea what people are complaining about.  I run this game at 4K with max settings.  Yes I have DLSS enabled.  I get 80-100FPS and it looks gorgeous.

4)  Inventory, crafting, and item management is less insane than the past.  

Now for the bad.  

This is the stupidest multiplayer design I have seen.  Ever.  In 1995 I dialed up to my friends over a phone line to play Doom and that was easier than this.  I'll just throw these words at you so you can digest the insanity.  Want to play with your friend(s) or family?  Forget Steam join.  You will need to create a Squad.  Or don't.  Or create a Link Party.  Probably.  Or do an environment link, but that will break if you hunt a monster.  Trying to co-op the story?  LOL.

That said the game is gorgeous and the combat is truly great and super fun and satisfying.  Ignore the haters and if you're a MH fan, don't miss this.

I'm not going to touch on PC optimization because that topic has been beat to death with this game, but we all know it is a serious issue. The reason I'm refraining from that discussion is because I don't want to detract from the serious issues that would have still been present even if performance was stellar. 

Monster Hunter Wilds is a clear diversion from the series' core identity in favor of reaching a wider audience (and wider profit). Now we've finally landed on a game that has tried so desperately to reach so many, that it's become a game that truly appeases so few. 

"Hunter! There is a new dangerous monster in this unknown land that you need to investigate!"

- Jump on the Uber-Seikret, set your drop-off pin to the monster's location, look out the window a few times if you wish (can't have the inconvenience of you getting to learn and appreciate a map!), arrive at said "mystery monster"
- Sneak attack monster, get hit by monster only to have Uber-Seikret save you from the following death blow, make wound, focus strike wound to STOP THE MONSTER AND BECOME INVINSIBLE FOR A TIME, more than likely cause a knockdown or additional flinch, repeat process, yay you've forgotten what you even fought
-Carve monster, look at quest rewards, make full low rank armor. Sweet now I don't have to fight what's-its-face again, learn its moves, get a taste of its character, or accidentally stumble upon its ecological actions

Anecdotes aside, this game has 3 major issues

1. REMOVE THE SEIKRET. The Seikret has ruined traditional Monster Hunter gameplay. It provides Uber like services that guard you from using any braincells when traversing a map. Wilds has great looking maps that no one will remember due to this feature (although I do have issue with the "open-world" style maps for the sake of "open-world" hype and it necessitating the Seikret in some ways). On top of it's brain dead navigating, the Seikret also changes the hunt in a major way. The Seikret emergency pickup removes urgency when getting hit by a monster. Before we had to decide if it is better to remain on the ground or get up immediately. The Seikret also provides endless jump attacks, safe sharpening/healing/buffing while running in circles around the monster.

2. REMOVE OR CHANGE THE FOCUS/WOUND SYSTEM. The focus system in combination with the wounding system is way to abuseable... And before people complain that "You should play it now! It's hard!" I have... The new monsters are challenging, but you can't leave ALL of the challenge to the end game. Having walls in Monster Hunter that make you think, grind lower rank monsters, change gear or strategy is important. There are so many cool systems in this game that you don't have to learn because the game is easy as is. I say remove OR change because I do think it's a cool system... It just needs to have more risk/reward involved or simply make it less abuseable. I struggle to think of easy ways in which that 

3. GET BACK TO MONSTER HUNTER CHARACTER/CHARM. Just drop the attempts at a mainstream "story" with mainstream "characters"... That's never been what Monster Hunter is about and at this rate (even with wasted production dollars to this effort in Wilds) it never will be. Bring back the MH charm and silliness, don't take yourself too seriously. The Head Farmer in MH Tri left more of an impression on me than any of these characters and all he ever said was "Hmmmg hay hay". While we're at it... Stop giving us an annoying character that constantly chats to us about what to do... WE LIKE FIGURING IT OUT, NOT BEING TOLD.

I waited until the end of this game’s update cycle before commenting. Now that no new monsters will be added, here’s what went wrong:
- Engine & Motion System(will never change, is fundamental design and engine problem)
Capcom forced inverse kinematics into an action game, causing unstable hitboxes and inconsistent movement depending on terrain. On slopes, moves like charge blade steps vary wildly compared to flat ground(count it by side step on flat land and on slope, on wilds and on world, you would be able to see that in wilds the distance is 60~70% less than that on world, counting by comparison on flat land of each title, also you can randomly flies up into the sky due to monster attack which you blocked successfully, the hitbox just squeezed you onto the sky instead of staying on ground, and it also happens to be in an attack combo of the monsters a lot, enjoys). Combined with late-game monsters having oversized AoE hitboxes and multi-hits, this creates unfair difficulty. The IK system also makes the game hardware-heavy by design, not due to poor optimization.
- Visuals
Excessive particle effects (smoke, clouds) and heavy terrain interaction strain GPUs. Capcom spent much of the cycle optimizing performance, mostly by limiting map loading.
- Weapon Balance
Weapons were constantly nerfed without proper testing: long sword, charge blade, bowguns, sword & shield, bow, etc. Classic DPS combos from older titles were removed, leaving combat dull. Some mechanics even punish players (e.g., insect glaive guard point). Endgame armor finally avoids one-shots, but weapons remain inefficient unless you grind hundreds of hours.
- Skill System
Compared to World/Rise, Wilds degenerates customization. Skills are locked to weapon/armor slots, while charms randomly override them, forcing endless RNG farming. Shield weapons especially lack slots for essential guard skills, making builds restrictive. I see no points of limiting it at the first place as random charms as an added feature breaks such limitation. Charm is considered one of the end game life cycle farming for players, enjoy your another 500+ hours farming for that one charm you need to get a hold of better time in speed run, as for non speed runners, enjoy your end game experience be something you dont even care, which is farming 9 stars missions.
- Anti-Cheat & Mods
first of all i dont even play mods or cheats, but anticheat affects normal players as well. From what i have read, anti cheat in this game would screenshot your plays and then decide whether you cheated or not, even without considering the privacy issues of screenshot your screens, this is a straight up performance barrier, i have heard that this behavior have been changed since last patch but i do not have enough information to confirm, so you can take this as a fair warning and you need to do a bit of your own homework to decide whether to believe it or not. As for using mods, this game would ban you if you modify anything that is quest reward related or having your hand on dlc stuff that you didnt purchase, leaving it here for player who likes to mod their game, but for average gamer, you really only need to care about the performance part of this.
- Farming & Rewards
Late-game farming is miserable: monsters have bloated HP, quests drag on, rerolls are limited, and RNG charms/weapons demand hundreds of hours. Capcom even holds a patent (JP7389377B2) on selective rewards based on player behavior, showing this grind-heavy design is intentional. Wilds has fewer monsters than past titles, with many updates just recycling old ones.
- Network Issues
Connectivity problems persist, especially for AMD users, with some unable to join sessions since launch. And quests are locked behind online session, like savage omega and repeated farming of Gogmazios. You also would keep getting disconnected from sos, indicating its a problem of capcom's server, that and they decided you cant send sos(even to npc) and leaving yourself with a quest that is you soloing it or having to leave, and believe me, soloing Gogmazios is not a fun experience if you havent build a set to solo it at first place.

There is however some goods on this game, mainly the combat motion is very clean and is a well balance between world and rise level of motion(if its on flat ground ofcuz), but still you wouldnt want to try keyboard on this game if you are someone who likes a lot of micro adjustment during your hunt. The focus mode simply have many things that they didnt catch, such as it would force your inventory to be on throwables only(you can disable this by manually disable all throwable in your inventory, but then you cant bring flashes or other throwable as well), focus mode disable some moves as there is move conflicts(you can see why in speed run focus mode is not used as much as you would have thought due to this). hunting horn still would read wrong input if you setup a keybind in setting for clicking both left and right click during moves such as attack follow up with rolling or initial attack note chain. Unable to use inventory by clicking use while in focus mode etc., these at the time of review have no settings or any method of get around with, its a much more fundamental porting issue of the game(at least its not very common issue for non micro adjustment players).

my final rating of this game would be that of 3/10, comparing to world which would be a 9/10, or rise being 8/10, i wont recommend this game unless you just want a recent game to test your hardware on, mine is ryzen 9 7950x,128G ddr5 6000mhz(boot at 3600mhz) ram,rtx4090, and my hardware runs this game at max setting comfortably on 4k 144hz at current stage, i would recommending playing other game if you like a hardware test.

I leave a positive review purely based on my experience with my setup.

With this setup, I’m getting solid performance at medium–high settings, with ray tracing on low. That said: the optimization is poor, especially considering the resources available to the developer. It’s disappointing to see a release in this state.

If your PC can brute-force decent performance, the game itself is excellent—a worthy successor to Monster Hunter: World. The core gameplay, atmosphere, and overall experience are strong, and I’m excited to see how it evolves through future title updates.

However, performance issues are not downplayed. Many other reviews express this, and if smooth performance is important to you, I’d strongly recommend thinking carefully before purchasing at the time of this review.

I won’t pretend to be an expert on game optimization, but for a studio the size of CAPCOM, expectations should be higher. Releasing the game in this condition is unfortunate and avoidable.

— A lifelong Monster Hunter fan

[h1]Monster Hunter Wilds – A Technical Mess Wrapped in Baby-Mode Gameplay[/h1]

I genuinely don’t understand how Capcom shipped this in its current state.

I’m running an [b]RTX 5090 + 9800X3D[/b] — literal flagship hardware — and this game runs like absolute garbage.

[hr][/hr]

[h2]PC Performance & Stability (or lack thereof)[/h2]

The moment I toggle [b]DLSS or Frame Generation[/b], the game [b]hard crashes to desktop[/b] with an exception error.

Not stutters.
Not visual bugs.
[b]An instant crash.[/b]

It doesn’t even matter if DLSS is ON or OFF — simply changing the setting crashes the game.

This is unacceptable for a modern PC release, especially one actively advertising DLSS support.

This is not “edge-case hardware.”
This is [b]top-tier, flagship hardware[/b].

If your game can’t survive a graphics toggle without exploding, something is fundamentally wrong with your engine or QA.

[u]And before anyone says “user error”:[/u]

[list]
[*]Drivers updated
[*]Game reinstalled
[*]Cache cleared
[*]Different launch options tested
[*]Background apps and overlays disabled
[/list]

The crashes persist.
[b]This is not on the user. This is developer incompetence.[/b]

[hr][/hr]

[h2]Updates Made the Game Worse[/h2]

What makes this even more frustrating is that [b]this was NOT a launch issue[/b] for me.

The game ran fine at release. After returning post-Title Updates, performance has clearly degraded and instability has increased. Crashes and issues now exist that simply didn’t before.

Updates are supposed to improve the game — not break it.

[hr][/hr]

[h2]Worse Than an 8-Year-Old Game[/h2]

What’s genuinely embarrassing is that [b]Monster Hunter Wilds runs and looks worse than Monster Hunter World[/b], a game released [b]8 years ago[/b].

How a newer entry manages to look flatter, run worse, and crash more often is beyond comprehension.

[hr][/hr]

[h2]DENUVO: Self-Inflicted Damage[/h2]

And then there’s [b]DENUVO[/b].

DENUVO absolutely tanks performance, easily cutting [b]50% FPS or more[/b].

Yes, it might stop a handful of pirates — a tiny minority — but it actively harms paying customers.

[b]The result:[/b]

[list]
[*]Worse performance
[*]More stutters
[*]More crashes
[*]Terrible PC reviews
[/list]

All to stop a few pirates, while damaging the game’s reputation and revenue far more than piracy ever could.
[b]It’s short-sighted and anti-consumer.[/b]

[hr][/hr]

[h2]Gameplay: Monster Hunter on Training Wheels[/h2]

The gameplay itself isn’t much better.

[list]
[*]Monsters are [b]auto-revealed on the map[/b], completely killing the core idea of [i]hunting[/i].
[*][b]There’s auto-pathing straight to monsters[/b], so why even pretend this is Monster Hunter anymore? You’re not tracking, learning routes, or preparing — you’re just following GPS like it’s an Uber delivery.
[*]The [b]Seikret can pick you up off the ground[/b], removing punishment for mistakes
[*]You can [b]heal while riding the Seikret[/b], eliminating risk and tension
[/list]

Between mounts, auto-tracking, and constant safety nets, the game feels designed so even a toddler could clear hunts without thinking.

Older Monster Hunter games were about:

[list]
[*]Preparation
[*]Positioning
[*]Consequences
[/list]

[b]Wilds feels terrified of letting the player fail.[/b]

[hr][/hr]

[h2]Multiplayer & Content Issues[/h2]

Multiplayer is also a mess:

[list]
[*]Multiple co-op entry methods buried in menus
[*]Half-baked lobby system locked to specific regions/maps
[*]Maps aren’t connected, forcing loading screens just to change biomes
[*][b]Extremely low monster variety[/b], especially compared to Monster Hunter World
[/list]

Capcom already had an expansive roster in World — could they really not port and re-adapt more monsters?

The story is bland and forgettable, worse than both World and Rise.

[h3]The Wound Mechanic Is Actively Bad[/h3]

The wound system is one of the most uninspired mechanics they’ve added:

[list]
[*]Weapon mechanics are locked behind popping wounds
[*]In multiplayer, if another player triggers them first, you lose access to your own mechanics
[*]It punishes co-op play and feels actively unfun
[/list]

[hr][/hr]

[h2]Final Verdict[/h2]

So what do we have?

[list]
[*]AAA PC game that crashes when changing graphics settings
[*]A game that became less stable after updates
[*]Worse performance and visuals than an 8-year-old entry
[*]A Monster Hunter game that removed the “hunting”
[*]Low monster variety
[*]A boring story
[*]DENUVO crippling performance
[/list]

This feels less like an evolution and more like Capcom chasing mass appeal at the expense of technical competence and the franchise’s identity.

[b]Fix your game.[/b]
[b]Stop baby-proofing the franchise.[/b]
[b]Remember why people fell in love with Monster Hunter.[/b]

**Overview**

7.5/10 currently.

Monster Hunter Wilds is a great game, of course it has issues, all games do. The optimization was always the main problem with the game but it's tremendously improved since then, my personal increase being a little over double of the frames I used to get (30-45 frames to almost 90 during quests). Not only that they continue to improve on it's optimization till the foreseeable February.

**Reason for Hate**

Most of the people hating on this game either have yet to reinstall it or are over-exaggerating the issues in my belief, although I'll concede with any other reasonings if commented. A lot of this hate from what I've seen stems from people who started with World, as someone who's started with World I can say a lot of the people are somewhat pretentious and have elitism for it, refusing to play things like Rise which is arguably one of the best Monster Hunters to release once Sunbreak came out. Of course it's not all World players, but they most certainly are the loud bunch.

**Cons**

Of course there's issues with Wilds still, namely online is completely in flames, the UI is arduous to navigate through to a degree even if you've used it a lot. As well as the issue of disconnecting in hunts more than 2 players, which has been an issue since release which I hope they fix massively (although the multi-player hunt issue only recently occurred for me). Another con is some of the weapons themselves such as Light Bowgun and Heavy Bowgun and partly Lance although Lance has received significant improvements. I get it some weapons are simply better than others however the bowguns as so below the curve in this case compared to everything else, still useable and viable thankfully at least. The wound system is what also brings down the game, just not a good system, still leagues ahead of World clutch claw though. Focus Mode actually had potential if built like how Generations Ultimate's Art Gauge was.

**Difficulty**

Another scene problem is the difficulty, this debate is wiggly. If you truly compare it you can argue that Wilds is harder than World in terms of damage received, looking at stats you can narrow down that Wilds monsters deal more damage, the health bars are just longer compared to World hence the discrepancy. In fact, you can argue that Wilds High Rank is harder that World High Rank easily. Low Rank I can certainly give to World in difficulty however. High Rank in Wilds is having similarity to health in World's Master Rank when it comes to monsters. You can argue that World is harder, not mechanically wise but actually because the AI for World monsters was just...abysmal; World monsters ran away a lot more, more backstepping, etc. Wilds doesn't have this problem so I'd argue that Wilds is "easier" not because they made it so, but because they actually made the monster AI not terrible.

**Final Thoughts**

Wilds is really fun, potential to be a top three in the franchise. It certainly has it's cons but it most certainly isn't mostly to do with performance now. If you disagree with me on any of these points I'd be happy to read comments as to why and go more in depth or agree with you if you bring up things I didn't know.

This is from a relatively new player of this sort of game.  Most of my previous experience was with Subnautica and its frigid sister.  I am still enjoying the game about 40 hours in.  Getting the settings sorted continues, given my inexperience, but I am getting there.  I have not encountered any bugs, and have been thoroughly impressed with the open world environments that I have spent time in so far.  The creativity of both monsters and environments is stellar.  It is obvious to me as a retired biologist that there is a large amount of biological homework behind the design of the game.  

While I understand the thinking behind criticism regarding performance and hardware requirements, I am inclined to give CapCom some slack here.  This project was hugely expensive in a viciously competitive market.  Much of the criticism is about top performance requiring more recent hardware.  What, hypothetically speaking, is a company like Capcom supposed to do in this market?  Design a new entry into the market with an inability to make use of current hardware?  And spend an extra year getting all details just right?  As if that was even doable given the massively varied hardware configurations that have to be worked with in the PC market?  That, sadly, is not the way the market works, and no individual company can change that.  Come in too late and too low in the graphics game in this kind of open world game, and you are unlikely to compete long enough to return investment.  

In any case, I am glad to have bought the game on sale, and am very much enjoying it so far even given my relative inexperience.  I look forward to lots more entertaining hours playing with it.

Personally enjoyed this more than Worlds from a casual standpoint - combat is more streamlined and simpler. Getting help via SOS signals is also now more convenient with bots (support hunters) joining first and then players with the option to choose multiplayer or not beforehand (and which bot to bring too). Exploration is also more convenient now with the grappling ability of the slinger and the tent feature for fast travelling within each map (helps for specific material gathering and catching up to bosses). Character customisation is probably one of the most extensive I've experienced yet, it really allows you to create your ideal character and being able to import designs was a convenient factor. Though layered weapon customisation in game can take a while as they're limited to rarity 8 (highest rarity - endgame), the system is still just as good with the ability to modify individual pieces and their colour palletes. The character movement animation and physics are incredible.

This is definitely a game to sink your teeth in. Have fun!

A good game overall. Some of the best graphics around when you turn everything to maximum settings, plays really well, has a lot of variation given by the various weapons and builds, character customization is decent, combat is enjoyable, bosses encompass all difficulty categories, from easy to very difficulty. 

This is the only game I played where the equipment skins obtainable via farming/crafting looks better than what the shop offers. Usually online and single player games tend to uglify skins obtainable by playing the game to incentivize spending money for the premium ones. Not this game. The premium edition of this game came with a skin that I quickly ditched in favor of the ones I crafted.

MH Wilds is my first introduction to the Monster Hunter universe. The game can be very finnicky and tedious unless you play with someone willing to walk you through your journey, which I fortunately had. I picked up a support build with hunting horn as a weapon just to make my life easier and my friend's life easier as well in the process.

Regarding the game's performance, other than some occasional random crashes, I did not encounter any issues and I play this game on max settings. My PC specs are:

CPU: AMD Ryzen 7 9800X3D 8-Core Processor (4.70 GHz)
GPU: GIGABYTE GeForce RTX 5090 GAMING OC 32GB GDDR7 512-bit DLSS 4.0
RAM: 64GB DDR5 6000MHz CL40 Dual Channel Kit

Needless to say, this is a demanding game on your hardware, so you do need a beefy PC to run it smoothly. Not as beefed up as mine, but you get the drill.

Lots of people complain that there isn't much to do in the game and that it has no content. This is a single player game with multiplayer elements. It is not meant to be played as an MMO. There is a limit to how much you need to farm, albeit the end game BiS stuff are RNG based so the grind can go endlessly, but the game's replayability comes from its diversity of weapons and builds that you can try out. Every weapon that you choose to play has its own play style and identity. Combat changes radically when you switch from one weapon to another. It is like you are playing an entirely different character class.

So far the game experience has been a blast. I beat Gogmazios a couple of times as well as Omega. Will for sure attempt Savage Omega as well. The lesser stuff are also enjoyable. Fishing can be pretty fun, as well as hunting small, rare creatures.

[h1]I got this during Winter Sale (which cut 45% of the price) and It's Really worth it![/h1]

[h2]Performance[/h2]
Im running on Ultra setting with RTX 4070 ti super,
i can say compared to the Demo this game run a lot lot smoother (TU4 was the latest update when im writting this review) i got on avg 50 - 70 fps without dlss or frame gen, and around 180 - 200 fps with dlss performance and frame gen on 
For comparison i only get around 110 fps with dlss performance and frame gen on when i played the demo during OBT 1 and OBT 2

yes some location still can tank your performance but this issue can be counted with fingers and not something that always appearing
To take note i have no idea what will happen if you play it using their lowest or recommended specs listed, but some search on youtube shows some people manage to grap around 50 - 80 fps on a laptop 1660ti with frame gen on

[h2]Monster roster, difficulty and grinding[/h2]
The monster roster is fun to fight on this one, even without the expansion releases yet the current roster give me a fun nostalgia feeling like when i played Monster hunter FU and Freedom 2 (like seeing new species of a monster and what they do on their locale) 
Additionally i would say the current roster is already too good for the base game (compared to previous series) so i dont know what their approach on topping the current roster when the expansion release

The difficulty as of right now is i would say it's on the sweet spot, the weapon move set improvement is the main thing that i would say bring the biggest change, where some of the more offensive weapon now have a counter and offset move which bring a better defensive mechanism other than just evading the monster's attack
Those defensive mechanic will make Veteran hunter say that the difficulty is way too easy but i don't think it will be that easy for someone who entered the series on 5th gen (MHW + Iceborne and MHR + Sunbreak) or even first timer, the learning curve is pretty steep once you want to master certain weapon

The grinding side is pretty chill with this one, most of the time you can make full set of certain armor after you grind the monster 3 - 4 times, the only hard times you'll face is prolly on the end game upgrade mechanic [spoiler]grinding the rng to get the best combination on your artian weapon and/or talisman[/spoiler]

[h2]Final Take[/h2]
Can definitely say the game is really worth to purchase and play right now [b][i]only if you bought it during sale[/i][/b], i would say it's still a bit of overprice if you buy with on full price but it will really depend for the future improvement (also we're still waiting for the next 2 additional performance update)

I wanted to get the this game runs bad stereotype out of the review right away. I do think capcom did drop the ball with performance at release, but it was never as bad as people claimed it was. A lot of people going as far as to say wilds looks worse than world, "this looks like a PS2 game", people complaining of "only 60fps" on max settings or worse settings when they have a pc even older than mine. A lot of the complaints are from people who have even better specs than I do, stating the same things I mentioned above. The game at release, and the game demo in Jan 2025 did have some issues for sure, and on capcoms press release about the game in November they said that performance was not their biggest worry at the time, they expected to have more sales on PS5 than on pc and more buyers in the Japanese market. They purposely ignored PC players, and while that's not great, I still feel the whole "this game runs bad" stereotype is not accurate anymore. I will say though, I do play on PS5 Pro for a reason, the game looks a lot better than a lot of my friends who plays on pc with "okish" builds, so take that as you will.

I have over 200 hours on PS5 and I bought on PC basically to test performance. People say the game runs very bad, I have no issues running the game in 4k ultra settings, ray tracing, no frame gen, with "old" hardware. I personally think my specs are decent but the average person online says my computer is out dated, probably because the GPU Is not a 40-50 series, and to be fair it is 5 years old at this point. Not only did the demo run great back in January of 2025, the game also runs great at these settings never dropping under 60fps during game play. title screen and loading screens I have gotten down to 54.

specs now:
Intel i7 14700k 5.4ghz 64gb ram, and a RTX 3080.
Old specs in January when I was on the demo:
i7 8700k 4.7ghz 32gb ram, and a RTX 3080.

There is one more issue I did want to mention with the game that's a bit of a problem. IF you use frame gen with a 40 series card, or FSR, or play on PS5 pro, there will be input issues. Some fights require a lot of quick response times, and if you have frame gen turned on you lose "real" frames, meaning you lose button inputs and responsiveness. This has killed me plenty of times, I would recommend keeping it turned off.

Anyways, enough with all the performance stuff onto the actual game.

Monster hunter wilds is just like any other monster hunter game but its a bit more "serious" and they tried to keep with the whole uncharted territory theme of the game. Due to this the world and maps are a lot different, and a lot of the "charm" of the older games is gone. The original sound affects, world layouts, questing, grinding times, they have all been changed significantly with this title. However, we have been having these small changes starting back in world, like the fire pit sound being changed, and now in wilds the pit has been removed completely, or their being a camp on each map instead of a general town. Although at the end of the day the game play loop is still the same, so it all works out. The game is beautiful, and while its very easy to over look a lot if not almost all of the detail due to certain game mechanics... its still has an amazing world and environment with plenty to explore and secrets to find.

As much as I hate to say it, I think wilds is the best entry to the series. I have played Every game now, even going as far as to buy older consoles to get the original feel of earlier titles. While the older games are full of charm, and I think wilds got rid of a nearly all of it, the game play makes up for it. At this point of the review we have nearly every main series game poster monster, a hub, meals added back into the game, layered armour, and a lot of other stuff we expected to have at release, or really we SHOULD have had at release. I don't want to spoil any of the game details so I won't say too much more, but I highly recommend giving the game a try if you can get it on sale.

I understand MH today is a live service game, and while all the games have had online play, and the majority of the content being locked behind online, I still think games should be released in a "completed" state. A lot of the negative reviews on here may be harsh, but if written near release its pretty deserved due to missing almost every "monster hunter" feature that we have come to expect in the series.

Last thing I wanted to talk about is the price and sales of this game. Monster hunter came in 2nd best game sales of 2025 (as of December), right after FC 2026. Capcom did consider this games sale numbers to be a failure. That is to be expected though with a $70 price tag. This game, along with the nearly $400 of DLC is really over priced. I do have a rule though with $1 to 1hr of game play to beat the game, and well, the game can be completed from start to finish within 70 hours, meaning the game in my eyes, isnt worth the cost. I do have well over 200 hours, but that is as a major fan of the series, not everyone is going to play the game as much as me. The average play time for wilds is only 61 hours, and with the full game already being at the $500 mark, that is kinda crazy.... I recommend getting the game on sale as an average player. I would say its worth $40-50.

This is the first MH game I've picked up, and I'm so glad I got it on sale, because I cannot recommend buying this game at full price. The core mechanics are fun, the hunts are interesting, the weapons are a challenge for someone like me; and none of that matters because every fight is a 50-50 chance of CTD. My rig gets ~60FPS in this game. consistently close to 100 with frame gen. And yet in the less than 12 hours I have spent playing, I've probably had close to 20 CTD. Almost all of which were during monster hunts, during which you cannot save the game, and which are the only way to progress the crafting system. So I have been content locked because of crashes. in 48 hours, I completed 2 main story quests and rage quit twice. What even was the point of putting this game on PC, if PC players can barely play it?

Combat-wise, the best in the franchise imo. Focus mode made playing MH on mouse and keyboard feel as viable as using a controller. For me, the story was also much more engaging than previous games. The environment and terrain are a step up from even World and the interactions are unreal. Where the game falls severely short is the optimization. It is absolutely ridiculous that the recommended system specs only net you 60fps on medium settings and frame generation on. I am fortunate enough to have recent enough hardware that this isn't a concern for me, but Capcom really should have delayed the release to address issues. I've been playing Monster Hunter since 3U and I think this is definitely the best game in the series, but only when it works. For $70 I would really have preferred to wait an extra year and have a game that I could recommend everyone play, but at this point only people with high-end systems can really experience the game at it's full potential.

Phenomenal, Holy sh!t award. This is the kind of game other triple A studios look at and think "what the fock are we even doing over here". You'll see more creativity, charm and ideas in a beautifully fleshed out world here than any other monster hunter title. Its the biggest and most ambitious game yet from this team and they have delivered masterfully. But before we get into the I'll take a moment to address the elephant,

[b] Performance [/b] I played the game after TU 4 on an RTX 3080 and 13600k and I'd say I had a pretty good experience. With AMD FSR and DLSS on i was consistently over 110fps and with it off around 70+ with max settings. Yes i know that we don't like upscaled, frame genned frames but unfortunately it looks like this is the direction the industry is heading in, and if that's the price of entry to play a title of this quality I'll gladly pay it every time. In TU 4 the game is very stable and I didn't experience any crashes or pop in, mind you this thing yearns to be run on an nvme ssd. 

That being said visually this game is stunning, the environments are gorgeous to look and inhabit. The locales will transform from dusty hellscapes into gorgeous grasslands, herds of ceratonoth will graze in the plains while a pack of doshaguma chase you around. The oil well basin is a dark and sinister environ of long abandoned industrial works rotting away spewing oil, impossible floating ice structures and grand designs of a fallen civilization make up the ruins of wyveria and ice shard cliffs. The much lauded level design of world has been turned up to eleven, there is so much verticality in the traversal and the addition of siekrets is a masterstroke, little pathways and hidden connections dot the landscape. Monsters will interact with the environment and climb hazard to blast you from, big ass floating jellyfish will suddenly appear in a cave, wudwuds will be chilling in the forests. You gotta see it all and you gotta beat it all.  

This is a big game even by Monster hunter standards, by far the most fleshed out and most extensive HR campaign in any MH game yet. Yes theres some dumbahh chatacabras fights in here but by and large the monster designs are great and very fun to fight. There's monsters in here that'll piss you off and nuke you from orbit or freeze you when you have 2hp left or kill you with some bullsh!t, but hey you can beat em up with a big ass sword and then turn them into cool hats and dresses, so who's really winning? 

The story is the story its ok its nothing moving or even memorable its just competent, it serves its purpose in getting you to the next fight and making whatever sense it can of the world its trying to explain. That being said the world is so well realized you'll feel like these npcs and characters actually live there, people will offer you meals in there homes and your reward is getting to watch the mouthwatering food animations, guild member npcs will move around the basecamp just sightseeing, there will be musical performances in the grand hub, festival events will change up the entire look of the place. The cats will put on little costumes and around in between the players in the hub. Geniuses make this game.

The way monster hunter games works is that once you beat a guy you turn him into a belt to fight the next guy but its not enough to barely win with the help of players or npc hunters the true draw of these games is mastering the monsters you gotta show this mf who's boss, and there's some big, big ones to conquer in wilds. I love the 9* jin dahad, laigiacruz, mizutsune, nu udra and omega fights and for its finisher the game reaches a crescendo with Gogmazios. This fight is a masterpiece, you are the size of this guy's toenail and you gotta bring him down, every phase is amazing and in the final act when you are about to prevail and the Main Monster hunter theme music comes on man its enough to make you cry. If you don't feel some kind of way after beating this guy, wipe yourself off man cuz you are dead. 

Games like this are few and far between and they need to be celebrated as the crowning achievements that they are. In a time when most triple A studios are at a complete disconnect from the concept of fun, This team has delivered a masterpiece. i can't wait to see what they do for masterrank DLC. 

Thank you for making this game.

Wilds needed another year or two in the oven but would still feel rushed and half baked in the end. Even after the performance fixes you're still left with a shell of a Monster Hunter game that lacks any meaningful friction for the sake of shoehorning new gameplay elements that don't feel like they belong in a Mon-Hun title.

Weapon move-sets were expanded but they all feel like they lost some of their identity. The new focus attack system strips skill and variety from weapons since this gimmick is how you maximize damage output. All attacks can be aimed on the fly which on the surface seems like a nice change, but it removes the element of prediction and planning your attacks which feels like the antithesis of the gameplay language of previous titles.

Most of what Wilds has to offer is braindead easy, the game is constantly holding your hand, preventing you from doing any meaningful exploration in an "open world". The story is forced and tedious, the UI and notifications are intrusive and give me a headache, and in general the sauce of the previous games is lost. 

Hopefully Capcom can address the criticisms and turn this game around with updates and an expansion, I desperately want to like this game as a longtime series fan!

This game is deeply disappointing, not because of its ideas, but because it barely functions. The most glaring issue is the constant crashing. Whether it’s during hunts, loading screens, or even menus, the game feels unstable to the point of being unplayable. Losing progress repeatedly kills any sense of immersion or motivation to keep playing.

What makes this worse is that the core gameplay COULD be great. The environments look impressive, and the concept of a more dynamic, open world is promising—but none of that matters when the game can’t stay running long enough to enjoy it. A Monster Hunter game demands long, focused sessions, and Wilds simply doesn’t respect the player’s time.

Performance issues go beyond crashes. Frame drops, stuttering, and inconsistent behavior make the game feel unpolished and rushed. It feels like something that needed more optimization and testing before being released. For a major franchise with years of experience behind it, this level of technical instability is unacceptable.

Until the crashing and performance problems are seriously addressed, Monster Hunter Wilds is hard to recommend. Right now, it feels less like a finished game and more like a broken beta that players are paying to test.

Monster Hunter Wilds feels like a game that strips away everything that made Monster Hunter feel like a hunt. While it adds new systems and features, most of them end up making the gameplay less engaging rather than more.

Hunting and Exploration Are Dead

The biggest problem is how little you actually need to know the maps. With Seikret’s auto-run, the game basically does the navigation for you. There’s no need to learn paths, shortcuts, or monster behavior Seikret just takes you straight to your target. Tracking monsters, a core part of Monster Hunter’s identity, has basically vanished.

On top of that, gathering and environmental interaction are almost pointless. Mining, picking plants, catching bugs, or harvesting materials is gone. so much Resources just appear in your inventory, which completely removes the sense of exploration and preparation that made past games feel alive.

Combat Feels Shallow

Combat has also taken a step backward. The wound system reduces fights to spamming attacks rather than rewarding timing, positioning, and strategy. You can endlessly stun-lock monsters without worrying about where you stand or how you attack. What should be tense, tactical encounters now feel repetitive and dull.

Pack Hunts Are Broken

Pack hunts are almost completely meaningless. A single large dung pod trivializes the mechanic, and monsters don’t coordinate their attacks  they just flail around randomly. Compare this to MH4U, where multi-monster hunts with creatures like Seltas and Seltas Queen felt dynamic and challenging.

Weather and Seasons Don’t Matter

Weather and seasons sound like they would make hunts feel dynamic, but they barely affect gameplay. Maybe monster spawns change, or you trigger a trap here or there, but that’s about it. There’s no real environmental danger or challenge no sandstorms reducing visibility, no flooded paths from heavy rain, no lava pillars erupting during firestorms. It’s a wasted opportunity.

Investigations and Rare Drops Are Pointless

Investigations have become a “rest in tent” loop. Instead of staying in the field hunting continuously, you just reset and repeat. There’s no luring system, no incentive to track monsters in the wild, and rare drops are no longer earned through skill or persistence they’re just handed to you as quest rewards. The sense of accomplishment from snagging a 1% drop is gone.

Field Cooking Doesn’t Matter

Cooking in the field might sound like a neat feature, but it’s almost irrelevant. Village meals at the Grand Hub are easier, better, and more convenient, making field cooking pointless.

Final Thoughts

Monster Hunter Wilds prioritizes convenience and spectacle over depth and mastery. By automating navigation, simplifying combat, and removing meaningful hunting and gathering, it loses the identity that made Monster Hunter feel like an adventure. What’s left is a game where you fight monsters — but rarely hunt them.
