For most people my issues with this game don't matter and are unimportant. I know for a fact that if this or monster hunter world are your first monster hunter games you will probably think great addition, no complaints (besides the issues with running the game). For me who has been with this series since monster hunter 1 on the ps2, I have some complaints. If you've never played them before, the early games are a lot different then the latest ones. For example, in the early games crafting items was not as simple as honey + potion = mega potion, there used to be a chance to fail and you would end up with garbage. You would also have to mix things randomly and try to remember what made what. Collecting items wasn't as simple as a giant pop-up on your screen as your run by telling you exactly what it is. You used to have to walk up to a mushroom and collect it to see what it was, then try to remember where that was for later. Small monsters used to actually feel alive and do something, if you walked into there area they would attack you on site or run away. They were easy to deal with, but they were more than just background like they are in Wilds. Wilds is a good game don't get me wrong, but it feels way too easy, way too hand holding. They built such a beautiful vibrant living ecosystem, but gave you no reason to ever explore it. Why would I ever walk around and explore if you tell me exactly what monsters are on each map, for how long, their strength, their weaknesses, and exactly where they are. This problem was none existent in World, because of the tracks system so why did they get rid of it. At its core Monster Hunter is a boss rush grinding game with hints of exploration and beautiful environments, so why did they get rid of the need for grinding and exploration. I hope they realize this even through the issues with optimization and fix this in the future. I know a lot of the quality of life fixes are to help integrate new players to the series, but they have taken it WAY too far. I hope Master Rank is kick my ass hard and make me quit my job grindy. Everyone that doesn't like that doesn't have to play after that.

♥♥♥♥ this game man.
I have more than 1000 hours in World, and while I didn't like Rise in the beginning, it grew on me and I 100% it after 420hours. Both games were very enjoyable in their own rights.

Wilds though? 44hours on my account, and I couldn't even give a ♥♥♥♥ to continue whatsoever. I have highend pc, so the performance itself isn't an issue, but that doesn't excuse the atrocious launch that made Cyberpunk perfect in comparison.

But the worst part for me is how everything lost its charm. My first MH game ever was Tri back on the Wii, and all of the games since then have had that magical charm MH has. This is the first game in the series that is completely void of what made MH a beloved series for me. 

Everything is automated, it feels like you are no longer in control with what is going on, to the point that you dont even need to position yourself any longer, a single button solves that issue completely, removing the need to actually improve and learn movesets to the extend you once had to. You also aren't even moving around on the maps on your own any longer. The Secret is nothing like the dog you had from Rise, it is instead an automatic running pet that usually finds the fastest route on the map (some areas can only be accessed using said pet), without you ever having to do anything, so the large maps goes unexplored as a result. Not there is much to explore in the first place, since in comparion to even Rise, the maps are dead, there is nothing interesting to them at all. 

Call me oldschool, but I liked the grinding aspect of the games too, this has removed that completely. Everything is discovered instantly upon seeing a monster the first time, be it hitboxes, its movement, drop rates for specific items etc, there is no sense of discovery or interest in hunting the same thing again, with all of the generous item tables there are for all the monsters in this game, it just becomes boring and dull really fast.

The maps are empty and hollow, and honestly too big for their own good. I hated the ancient forest from World, but at least it was pretty and fun exploring outside of the hunting aspect, Rise was pretty much the opposite with limited exploring (still way better than wilds), but great areas to fight monsters in. 
This game though? I dislike both exploring and the hunts themselves, and honestly I dont even really know why, but it just doesn't feel good, and I hate it. Maybe it is because the monsters themselves have too low hp and feels weak, but this game is just not fun at all.

Monster Hunter Wilds has come a long way. Performances are better now, although it's still not perfect. And the last title update before the DLC (Ttitle Update 4) sets the base game in a good state when it comes to the end game grind in my opinion. If the expansion makes Wilds much better like Iceborne did to Worlds or Sunbreak to Rise, this will probably become my favorite games out of those 3 (the only 3 I played).

Coming from world, having the KBM controls swapped around isn't very fun. Took me 4 hours just to rebind them without any button conflicts.

Also coming from world, having to rely on FSR frame generation to have any reliable framerate also isn't great.
Disabling raytracing can double your framerate, at least. You can also try restarting your game to see if you get better perf, if you've edited the settings too much. It seems the render engine tries to "hang onto" some settings without freeing them.
Expect weird screen sloshing if you're in that boat either way. It's acceptable since the game doesn't rely on pixel-perfect precision, but the ghosting can put some players off.

Wound mechanics + the seikret are OK imo, since the title changes several key factors about the gameplay loop, and you can anticipate that the difficulty scales well once you exit low rank.

Overall this is best enjoyed as an interactive movie unless you really like killing things that can't fight back

After 6 hours of initial impression of the game I got a couple ideas on how I feel about the game.

Let's get the most important part out of the way, under all the jank and concessions to establish a satisfactory performance Wilds is a fantastic sequel to what made worlds great. The weapons feel fluid, the environment is packed with opportunities that can be exploited and the new additions like wounds are for now very fun to exploit and use against the monsters. The artificial tools to create a playable game are working as of writing this review, with me having on medium settings a near to perfect 60fps and seamless experience running a 4060

But, the concessions I had to make to establish such a parameter are not acceptable for a product of any kind. The tools to enable smooth performance work, but in turn make every texture look like papermashe. During exceptionally intense environmental sequences the resolution scaling makes the game so blurry, it turns into a "I lost my glasses" simulator. It's impossible to recommend this game to someone who expects a functioning game out of the box for a price that SHOULD BY ALL MEANS GIVE YOU THAT. I wish to remind you, this is with heavy concessions, medium settings, no ray tracing, very down the middle to low settings for a card that achieves more with contemporary games such as Where Winds Meet, which in my opinion meets MH:Wilds in terms of graphical fidelity.

A personal nitpick that is the direct result of the above mentioned critique: First impressions matter, when you enter MH:World you are greeted with a lush, vibrant and living forest that really invites you into the setting... MH:Wilds wants to establish the changing climate of the areas which I understand, but combine the drab, grey, empty sand dunes with the papermashe texture flickering caused by the dependency on upscaling tools, the game doesn't look inviting at all, in fact, the first hours in the desolate environment and bland visuals were quite off-putting... I can see how this environment might have looked great had it the capability to be rendered in the intended capacity, but we live in the post release reality which they are not. 

In conclusion, if you can accept a AAA game looking like it's made out of cardboard for what I perceive as fantastic MH gameplay, get it, I bought it on sale, it's not a purchase I regret. Otherwise I'd skip this one until either gets the performance in order or more powerful hardware can outperform the janky optimisation sometime in future. It is sad to see that the devs were busy undoing their neglect nearly the entire year, instead of releasing new content. I don't see Wilds getting nearly as much support as World had and whatever it could have gotten was cut short due to mentioned time spent optimising what should've released in a playable state. I won't be surprised if the entire support is dropped as we know they are most likely already working on the next title. I would encourage the MH fans to ask themselves what could've been if it wasn't for this time wasted on fixing the game, instead of lashing out at valid criticism with pointless vitriol.

I think this game is sad.

It has potential to be the best game i have ever played, but it just doesn't quite manage. It's too easy, hunters are too strong compared to monsters and a lot of skills required in previous games are just extra here, or completely unnecessary. There are too many tools meant for survivability, and the monsters have too few, if any, ways to counter the things hunters are able to do.

Focus mode completely negates skills like positioning or aiming in a way i don't think is healthy. The seikret is a very powerful tool for treversal, and very versatile in a hunt. It can pick you up from the ground, get you away from bad situations very quickly, and being able to use pretty much any item on it for little to no risk is very powerful.

The focus on fighting has become more and more pronounced. It no longer feels like a hunt, but rather just a fighting simulator. In World, you could choose to track down monsters by using the environment, and for a good portion of the game you had to do this, as the monsters icon wouldn't show up on the map until you had gathered enough research points. In this game, you just have to hunt the monster once and then you'll always know where it is. The seikret is also a step towarsds this direction of getting to the fight as soon as possible, as it lets you do things like sharpening your weapon while you're pursuing the monster.

In addition, the idea of just granting you items from wounds and part breaks instead of dropping them on the ground makes it so you have to do less active decision making during a hunt. There is never a time where you have to consider the risk of picking up an item instead of focusing your attention on the monster, except for tail carves. Speaking of part breaks, they were pushed aside in favour of wounds, which i think is a shame. Yes, some monsters have part breaks as a core feature of their fight, mainly Jin Dahaad, but this is really only the case for under half the roster. Some returning monsters have fewer parts to break, like Rathalos and Rathian no longer having breakable backs or heads. Part breaks usually affect the fight in an interesting way, while wounds just serve as easily available weak spots you can create on demand, which grant an item, and lets you use the very powerful focus strikes. It's just a less interesing mechanic than part breaks.

Many of the mechanics this game has feel very undercooked or underutilized. Take pack monsters as an example. There are only really three large monsters that really gather in herds of other large monsters, those being Doshaguma, Ajarakan and Hirabami. These monsters are far from guaranteed to travel in packs, and Doshaguma can only do so in the plains. This makes it feel less like an actual feature of pack behaviour, and more like the monsters just so happen to be in the same area. Another extremely underutilized feature is Alpha monsters. Currently, the only one in the game is Doshaguma. It is a very cool idea, but the way it was implemented makes it very forgettable.

Leading up to the release of Wilds, Capcom made sure to show off features like pack behaviour, Alpha monsters and other things, like power clashes. Things like this were very exciting and interesting, as we had rarely, if ever, seen these things in previous games in the series. Yet, at least at the time of this review, before a Master Rank expansion, these features are barely relevant. The pack mechanic is rare to see and there are no relevant high-ranking monsters that exhibit this behaviour, power clashes are only on a select few monsters as a glorified QTE, and there is only one single Alpha monster. On the other hand, things that really shouldn't (in my humble opinion) be as relevant as they are, are made to be core features and are very much supposed to be utilized heavily, even though the monsters aren't really built for it. No monster has a good way of hitting a seikret that has even decent positioning in mind, for example.


All of the decisions to make the game easier is meant for new players, i get that. However, i think that in this pursuit of more new players, Capcom is starting to alienate some veterans of the series, who liked the slow pace and who liked the more deliberate combat style the older games had.

All i want to say is that, despite my complaints i still like this game, it's good enough. But it could have been so much more than just good enough, which is why i can't say i love this game.

This is a 10/10 game which is hindered behind poor optimization and porting.

I still stand by my 10/10 rating, I always love seeing the MH team explore new things, and I will continue to have faith in them and their vision. :)

Recommended — with reservations

I really like Monster Hunter Wilds. I want to be clear about that up front.

The story works. The themes work. The idea that sometimes hunting is something you have to do even when you don’t want to is very much in line with what Monster Hunter is supposed to be about. The Arkveld arc in particular hit hard—conceptually it’s tragic, restrained, and morally consistent with the series’ core message about ecological balance.

But after playing Monster Hunter Rise, I realized why Wilds doesn’t quite land for me emotionally, even when the writing is strong.

Rise makes hunts feel meaningful because the world participates in them.
Wilds makes hunts feel meaningful mostly through cinematography.

That difference matters more than I expected.

In Rise, when you hunt a new monster, the village acknowledges it. Elder Fugen talks about the threat, the cost, and the necessity. You feel like the hunt is a collective decision—something no one wants, but everyone understands must be done. You carry the burden, but you don’t carry it alone.

In Wilds, the tragedy is often framed beautifully, but once the hunt begins, it feels lonely. The world observes. Characters react afterward. During the hunt itself, it can feel like you’re executing an obligation in silence. The story tells you this is hard—but the game rarely lets you share that weight with the people affected by it.

This isn’t a complaint about realism or tone. Wilds’ grounded approach is valid, and in many ways impressive. The ecology feels alive. The monsters feel like animals, not villains. The problem isn’t what the game is saying—it’s how distant the player feels while carrying out those ideas.

Rise prioritizes participation and ceremony.
Wilds prioritizes observation and immersion.

Personally, I connect more with the former. Monster Hunter, at its best, isn’t about killing monsters—it’s about protecting balance at a cost, and that cost feels heavier (and more meaningful) when the community acknowledges it with you.

I still recommend Wilds. There’s a lot to admire here.
I just hope future Monster Hunter titles—or even updates—find a way to blend Wilds’ realism with Rise’s sense of shared responsibility.

Because when Monster Hunter remembers that hunting is a reluctant necessity, not a spectacle, it hits harder than any cutscene ever could.

After nearly 400 hrs in this game, I can finally say I do not recommend it. And this is coming from someone who has put roughly 1500 hrs into Monster Hunter games in the last 10 years. I love this series and want the best for it.
This game would be a 13/10 for me and would be in my opinion the greatest MH game ever made, if not for one fatal flaw; the game crashes constantly, especially after TU4, rendering the game virtually unplayable.
I built a new PC in Feb 2025 specifically to play this game. I went based off what Capcom says on the Steam page for the game is the recommended hardware. I did not build a PC with the "latest and greatest parts" because I have a budget like the vast majority of people. I bought a Ryzen 5 5600x 6-Core CPU and an AMD Radeon RX 6600 GPU. 
Prior to TU4, I could run the game on Medium graphics and experience a crash roughly every two weeks. Not great, but workable. After TU4, I can't run the game on the lowest settings without the crashing every hunt or every other hunt. I can't tell you how many investigations with lots of rewards I have lost because the game removes an investigation attempt before you depart and saves the game. Capcom should change this to avoid punishing players whose game crashes mid-hunt. 
I have no mods installed and have tried every fix under the sun to no avail (update drivers, roll back drivers, verify game files, create exception for anti-virus, update BIOS, input Steam launch commands, adjust game settings, etc.). Literally nothing has worked. 
I sincerely hope they fix this game, the game is great when it can be played. I just hope developers haven't moved in a direction of only making their games playable on the newest, most expensive parts.

As a casual hunter with fond memories from Freedom Unite to Iceborne, I am convinced that Wilds deserves a positive review.

There has been a lot of doom saying in the Steam reviews and discussions. Much of that is relatable and justified. Thus, I'd like to reflect on that, to outline how this goes together.

[b] Lack of content and difficulty[/b]  - Wilds may have the lowest entry barrier in the franchise and the difficulty curve remains shallow for much of the high rank content. However, title updates 3 and 4 have delivered a much enjoyable variety of encounters that are reasonably challenging. For a MonHun base game, the end game content subjectively feels nicely diverse and memorable.

[b]Wound and focus mechanics[/b]  - this has contributed significantly to the perception of the low difficulty. Monsters are staggered easily and frequently, while heavy combos need less commitment due to the flexible aim. Still, it is a cool mechanic, that feels much smoother than e.g. Iceborne's clutch claw. Also, this works reasonably well with the higher wound resistances of the end game content.

[b]Multiplayer[/b]  - this has not been much of a topic in recent reviews, but the menus and options to play with others are oddly obscure. But once you got the hang of that, multiplayer can be a great experience, especially if you can play in arranged teams with friends. This really has kept me interested for a while longer than anticipated.

[b]Performance and stability[/b] - this certainly is the elephant in the room. I guess the message from an overwhelming portion of the negative reviews has been clear. However, things are not universally terrible. Sure, the game is CPU-intensive and heavily relies on a fast SSD and some sensible use of upscaling. If you can life with that and have somewhat modern hardware, Wilds can run without hiccups. In all this time, I have seen no stutter and exactly one crash. I am curious what future updates will bring, as the devs are getting more experience with the new engine.

[b]Visual fidelity[/b]  - Wilds can look very neat at times and with cranked up settings. And then again, the visuals can be rather terrible, e.g. when low res textures and polygons pop in from LoD glitches - which seem fixed with recent updates, though. Also, poor default settings for post processing effects can sometimes kill the sharpness of the scenery. Generally, the visuals do not appear to scale well at lower settings. Note that high resolution textures are available as a free DLC, but require somewhat modern hardware. For more details on this, refer to my review of said DLC.

[b]Lack of exploration and tracking[/b]  - I agree the fully automatic riding navigation with the Seikret has taken away much from us. It lets new players catch up effortlessly during a hunt, but I miss the experience of tracking down monsters and exploring the environments to find and learn effective routes. For those who care to look closer, the game world with its wildlife and weather variants is worth some free exploration time, but sadly, that gets mostly overlooked. The non-combat aspects of hunting have been dumbed down indeed, but the refined weapon mechanics and nicely designed monsters compensate for much of that.

To close this review: I had a good time with Wilds so far and hope for some great master rank content to come. Let us see how this will evolve. Cheers and happy hunting

写在2025年的最后一天，TU4更新之后，王锁刃更新之前。

虽然到现在为止，游戏在我的电脑上的优化表现依然无法令人满意。但是本次更新终于解决了游戏各种读取慢的问题，大幅提升了游玩本身的便利性和整体体验，期待官方承诺的后续的进一步的优化。

游戏内容本身——
高清化以来，系列放弃了相对慢节奏的走位立回战斗设计，顺着高性能弹反目押这条路一路飞奔而去。有人会喜欢有人肯定会讨厌，如果不喜欢这个设计方向的话，本作玩起来可能会比想象中的还要痛苦一些。我本人倒是觉得还不错。出招更精准，动作更流畅的怪物，看起来还是很有压迫感的。

怪物阵容、探索内容、装备技能搭配、可肝的系统以及难度曲线而言，在经过了数个版本的迭代更新之后，我认为本作已经称得上是最豪华的本体游戏了。在上位就提供了各种不同风格的狩猎体验和高难度任务挑战，如果想要肝的话也有很丰富的可以肝的内容。装备强化、幻化、随机护石、机械武器、随机词条…可以说是应有尽有，肝度直逼MHR曙光。

一直都没来写评测，因为起初我一直觉得本作的难度曲线和系统深度有些难评。但是现在我可以发自内心的说，我推荐这个游戏，它是一个优秀的怪物猎人续作，并且可能是目前为止最豪华的上位体验。如果玩家的电脑足够强大的话，应该玩起来会很爽。

this game sucks

[h1] It's good but get it on sale, not worth full price. And I'll go into why; [/h1]

I like this game, but it feels sooo lazy in some aspects but well done in others. It feels like one half of the team made it their life's mission to make a MH game, and the other half just used ChatGPT for it all. 100% has some caveats to it that make it not great. Also, the only other MH game I've played was MH: World so I'm going to be comparing the two since I LOVED that game. I've dabbled in MH: Rise, but god, that wasn't worth my time in the slightest. 

[h2] Let's get into the positives first.  [/h2] 

So, visually, its great. But, that isn't hard to do. 

- The locations are also stunning. So far I've seen the first area. Desert, and the forest. Both are spectacular. I can't wait to see what others there are. But that being said, this doesn't touch MH:W in terms of visual world style. It gets close. WAY better than Rise. Rise was a waste of money imo. They most certainly went back to the MH:W type of game. And that's what I've been waiting for, for years.

- Character models, very well done. But I'll touch more on this later.

- Monsters, They're nice. None jump out at me so far as being "OH MY GOSH THATS GREAT" excluding the Doshaguma. That one is very cool. I haven't seen any dinosaurs, or dragons yet. I really hope that changes soon. As far as the others, I really do like them, but they're "average" if that makes sense. 

- The Seikrat is an amazing addition. And the customization is really good.

- The cutscenes are great.

- The ability to aim your attacks toward a reticle on the screen. Very helpful and I like this alot


[h2] Now for the negatives, the epitome of laziness. I may just be nitpicking but here they are. [/h2]

- The performance. It doesn't run great. I've tweaked the settings to where I like, but still. Could be way better. In terms of MH:W, it doesn't look [i] too [/i] much better than World, but runs way worse. Optimizations people, optimizations. 

- The Seikrat auto-move and movement is wonky, and I can tell they were going with a sort of Red Dead Redemption style horse movement, but something doesn't feel 100%. Functions well enough though a bit difficult to get used to.

- There's a distinct difference between pre-rendered cutscenes, and in engine cutscenes. Visuals are fine, but god the facial animations. Its like they didn't even try to match up the lips to what the VA is saying. It kills my immersion and looks lazy af. I don't remember encountering this issue in MH:W. And if I did, it was so minor it didn't bother me. Every dialogue its horrible. EDIT: I figured it out. In MH:W the lips didn't match the voices either, but the lips actually moved. Almost like you were watching an old kung fu movie where the actors spoke Japanese but it was dubbed over in English. In Wilds, the lips move, but barely. 

- The story. I don't really care for it too much so far. I can follow it, but they haven't made me care about it. That's about all I can say about that. 

- The Monster names oh my god. GIven I've only put 6 hours in but this is what I was referring to when I mentioned AI. Lala Barina. Congalala. Rompopolo. Balahara. Who tf has a fetish with the letters L A. This is obviously a nitpick, and the monsters themselves are cool (except for the Congalala that dude literally ♥♥♥♥♥ on you) but c'mon. Make up some better names for goodness sake. Like, MH:W has Anjanath, Rathian, Barroth, Diablos, Tobi-Kadachi, Rathalos, Radobaan. These sound like some crazy monsters. These strike fear into the very name, and the monsters back the names up. So perfect. 

- I don't remember the palico being so prominent in the story back in the day. Like, yeah thats cool you've got a little cat dude running around helping you, but why is he so far up my crack. In cutscenes my dude speaks so often and it just kills my immersion. I have them speaking english because how does my guy know what "purr purr meow mrruow" means. They speak as if we understand them. And the english version, the palico is so... Intrusive. Two characters having a meaningful conversation, and this cat is just like "Hey don't forget I'm here"

- Some monster's move sets are just. There. The Lala Barina for example. Its literally just; Stab *wait* Stab *wait* Twirl *wait* Stab *wait* Like c'mon dude. Am I fighting a microwave. But other monsters, like the Doshaguma are fun af to fight. Tons of different movesets, AoE attacks. The difference between the two is like Pong, then Dark Souls.

I'm sure there are other things I can say, but I've typed long enough. I'm gonna make my wife and I some steak.

I held off on buying this for a long time despite being a huge Monster Hunter fan because of all the performance complaints. I’m running a 4070 Super, Ryzen 7 5700X3D, and 32GB of RAM, and I’m honestly not seeing the issues people were talking about.

Outside of some minor stutter and the occasional cutscene desync, performance has been solid. I’m hitting around 140 FPS with frame gen and roughly 60 FPS without it on high settings. Maybe the recent performance patch helped, but it’s frustrating that the complaints kept me away for so long, because the game itself is peak Monster Hunter.

Performance:
I've played this on both a PC with a 5080, in 4k, highest settings, at and least 60fps constantly.
Also, on a laptop with a Nvidia 2050 on basically lowest everything settings.

The game has been perfectly playable on both, the only problem I had was screen tearing on the 5080.
If you have the same issue as me, lower your max frame rate to 59fps (I have a 60fps 4k screen with gsync) and disable frame generation.

Graphics:
If you are able to play on higher settings like I was the game does look amazing.

Gameplay:
The game is your usual return to a monster hunter game, I did not play rise, so my comparison is with world.

The sekiret mount is a interesting addition and I thought overall improved the game play by auto following your target and being able to heal up and sharpen on the way.
The controls are ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ though, would be improved if they just let you move the sekiret around without having to toggle it running.

Almost all materials can be gathered with the hook which speeds things up and can be done while ridding on back of sekiret.

I dislike how temporary the camps feels, there's no doing side quests to improve them, no growing plants, insects, fishing expeditions, etc..

Meal buffs.... mehhh
The felynes don't cook you meals anymore which I think is a negative and you have to forage for ingredients they aren't permanently unlocked.
You can cook your meal anywhere though which is handy.

Story is better than world and characters aren't as annoying, but overall follows the same premise as world and all previous monster hunter games I've played.

Overall the game has been enjoyable but perhaps a little too easy until the very end of the game.
Weapons have some new moves.

New monsters had some nice variance to them and were pretty fun hunts, aren't just all dragons.

Wound mechanic is new and really fun, I really enjoyed it as a way to deal additional damage and stagger enemies and interrupt big moves and punish monsters if you evade big attacks.

Could be improved:
You get too much money with nothing to spend it on.
Same with hunting points, basically nothing to spend them on.
Nothing in camps to be upgraded or invested in.
Bring back the felyne cooks.
Post story completion Nata genuinely looks sad and bored as a trade broker just sat in your tent. Would be so much better if they were running a stall and actively engaging with traders and npcs and actually looked happy?! Possibly also some quest with them to help them become a hunter which I think is what they want to be.
I dislike that you don't build up knowledge of monsters by investigating tracks etc.. monster are always displayed on map which is easier and investigations are now randomly generated based on what's in the area at the time so I guess it was removed because of that.

Other:
Lots of mods for this game.
Bring back Palico Prowlers. They'd print money.

Please fix optimization... I beg you. This should have been a fix before monsters were released. That is a major disappointment.

Otherwise it has been a fun game, great for returning hunters, and new ones. Good challenges, (though I wish Congalala was not here...) and I am pleased with mostly everything save for optimization issues not being an immediate fix.

Understandably, the biggest drawback of the game is it's definitive dogwater performance and optimization hell. Although in my experience, I haven't encountered any significant performance issues, only just a few stutters here and there but nothing game-breaking. Though, I am just one of among the few in the minority of this game's population, as majority of the playerbase are suffering from massive performance issues.

Getting through the performance issues though;

Absolutely addicting gameplay loop. Combat feels more fluid and responsive than ever, especially flashier than MH:Worlds, taking some inspiration from MH:Rise. Although, the combat and gameplay is much similar to Worlds than it is to Rise. I genuinely enjoy each and every hunt I do, and I'm more than excited to test each weapon and build ideal sets and playstyles around them.


EDIT: The recent update MASSIVELY improved performance. Game is in a great state now.

Visuals are absolutely astounding. If the game doesn't melt your PC, the graphics fidelity is insane. I thought Worlds had peak visuals, but oh man Wilds absolutely takes the podium. Environments feel so vibrant and life-like, and character models look so gorgeous. This was probably the game I spent the most on character customization simply because of how much I adore the character visuals. Also, the awesome designs on the weapons and armor sets definitely leave so much room for fashion opportunities.

Content-wise, the base game itself has a good amount of content to enjoy, even though it released fewer monsters compared to Worlds on release. After TU4, the endgame now bears more reason to sink scores of hours onto. Obviously it isn't as fleshed out yet compared to MH:W or MH:Rise, since both those games already have their expansion DLCs. I have high hopes that once Wilds gets it's expansion, the already amazing base game would be elevated hundredfold in content enjoyement.

Majority of the negative reviews on the game mostly lie on performance issues, which are completely justified. Capcom IS making efforts to stabilize the performance, but recent updates still present issues and justified backlash. If you think your PC can't run, then it most likely won't run it.

Best to get the game on sale, but still somewhat worth it at full price if you have no performance issues.

By far the best Monster Hunter in the series. If you've played Monster Hunter World, this game is the equivalent of a sequel, a Monster Hunter World 2. Mechanics are refined, movement is smoother, and combat is so incredibly satisfying. Gone are the days of clunkily moving around and attempting to sheathe but the game won't let you, or praying to God that the character is in fact aimed in the direction that you want.

It started off a little rough around the edges. Performance issues, weird looking glitched models (often due to people using frame generation wrong though), underperforming weapons... but now, with some Title Updates™ and refining, the game is in its true prime.

When the DLC drops, my hours will most likely go from 445 to 1500.

As much as people are hating on the game for its performance and "difficulty," the game is great. Nowadays, everyone just wants to hate on new games for one reason or another, but honestly, the game did have some rough moments at the start (which I didn't experience personally but saw), and it has gotten better. It would be a waste not to experience what the game has to offer.

Also, the difficulty isn't that bad. If you went through your whole playthrough without upgrading your armor and then came into high rank, you could just get one-shotted or almost one-shotted by anything. If you're not using a weapon that’s good for dodging, you can easily make an armor set with high defense and enough damage-related skills to roll through any monster. The game is not about overthinking. You can guess what skills to pick just by looking at their names (sometimes), and if you really care about it, you can try to match the monster's elemental weaknesses, even if you don’t fully understand how it works. It might seem worthless at first, but it all depends on the weapon you’re using.

Anyway, the game is good. Stop hating for no reason and just enjoy it if your PC can handle it at a stable framerate. You’ll definitely need that framerate for certain monsters (or collab monsters, looking at you Omega).

The game play in an overall sense, story-wise, is absolutely infuriating, you are constantly forced into locked walking simulators and listening to a ton of irrelevant dialogue, which you can thankfully skip, but then you don't know what's going on, as you're whisked from area to area.

Wilds is a great Monster Hunter game. The team clearly put in a lot of work reclaiming some of the identity of the series that was lost a bit in the move to the 5th generation games. I think there's still plenty of room to reincorporate elements, and certainly the balance isn't perfect across the board, but I would happily tell someone to pick this up over World or Rise.

Except the performance even now, post-Title Update 4, is still absolutely inexcusable for a flagship game from a company as big as Capcom. There is so much to love about Wilds but it's impossible to see it through the abysmal post-processing stack and the genuinely confounding stutters and low frame rates. Even now, I cannot for the life of me figure out what exactly is so resource hungry about this game.

I want to believe that by the end of it's life (after the last expansion update) things will be in a better place, but I was hoping that Dragon's Dogma 2, with it's similar issues, would've led the team to course correct MH Wilds before launch and that never happened. Such being the case, I cannot remain hopeful that we will ever see any improvement beyond what newer pc hardware will offer.

If you're a diehard MonHun fan (and lets be honest, you've already bought the game by now if that was the case) and feel you absolutely cannot skip this game, my recommendation would be to look into REFramework and some mods to fix the god awful visuals. This game actually can look beautiful, but (at least at 1080p) there is no option in game that you can tweak to get it there.

Monster Hunter Wilds started off with bugs, crashes and a lot of problems. Many have been solved since the launch and the team seems to be determined to make it a very ambitious project. It's hefty for PC and I needed to upgrade it to keep up. But that would have been an issue anyhow.  It's a work-in-progress and shaping up to become better rather than worse. So I always give such games the benefit-of-the-doubt. It was made by humans, for humans. And as we all know, nothing is perfect. 

Where to begin? I started playing at around launch day and I have yet to stop playing despite those issues. Why? Several reasons. The Fights against the Monsters are the first. The fighting style is very enjoyable and smooth, to the point where I don't want to miss it. I have played World to the end and have 1000 hr+ in the game. They are really trying to make it a worthy successor to that title. From tiny details like the environmental influence on habitats and interactions between monsters, to Event Missions. World was in comparison easier and harder for me. It was my first game of this series, and yet, it took me three days of figuring out the moves of Omega Planetes to learn how to defeat it. I love the challenge. It was probably meant to shu* up people complaining about the difficulty level, and how it was too easy. Well, now they complain how the game is too hard. Ah, well. Can't please everybody anyhow. 

Multiplayer missions are certainly an experience. There is more likely to be something strange if others are involved, but for the most part, it's an enjoyable experience. What I do like a lot is how I DON'T have to eat a meal for EVERY single mission separately! Yes, you can let that timer run its course. The HUB is shaping up to be pretty nice, even if I still feel like I need to run around inside of it. And finding the Smithy after a mission was a fun struggle at first for me personally. Always turned the other way, heh. 

The Diva is certainly memorable and I enjoy most of her songs. Her prayer also adds a little boost to your stats for your next mission. Of course Fashion Hunter is the most important part of the game for many, you should see the crazy combinations players use if you visit a full Hub. Personally, I'm Crown Hunting right now before they add even more monsters and I never get that Achievement in this lifetime. 

Crafting armours, weapons, decoration, Palico armour... Refining, colouring, or hit the switch to conceal the piece entirely. It's all possible and some of it from the beginning of Low Rank. Oh, another difference to World is how you can actually join missions BEFORE you have completed them yourself if the Hunter Level fits. Great, isn't it? It's friendlier to new players certainly. And you can support your friends starting out from the beginning. 

The community is mixed. Some shouldn't play a multiplayer coop game if they end up flaming others. But that is thankfully not the norm. The idiots are sadly louder than the silent players who only want to have a good time. Thankfully, reporting people who try to drag others down to their level is possible on their Hunter Profile. Personally, I don't hesitate there if they make toxic comments. Otherwise? I have 'met' a lot of nice people by searching for teammates for the harder Event missions. And I have yet to regret it. It's hilarious if you are playing with hunter friends, perhaps even via Discord voicechat in background. 

Trying out different weapons has never been easier for me. It was harder in World. But in Wilds, the fighting style is so smooth, you can't help but enjoy even an unfamiliar weapon pretty fast. 

Well, I don't want to spoiler too much. The Monsters should be experienced firsthand. They are, after all, the star of the series!

This game has been amazing so far, and is by far the best combat in the series (coming from someone who started at TRI on the Wii, and played everything since then)

but the performance is absolutely awful. sub 60fps at 1080p on an RTX 3080 laptop with 16gb VRAM is really  starting to get me questioning if this is truly a next gen title, or just poor optimization.

I understand I am running it on a laptop, but on both Windows and Arch Linux, with temps under 80C on both the CPU and GPU, less then 70% CPU utilization and settings ranging from low to ultra all having similar performance, I have spent more time ripping my hair out trying to understand why I can't achieve over 60fps without framegen, then I have spent actually playing the game.

Because of this and its current state, I cannot recommend it to most people. Will I continue to play this after review? Probably, but the performance needs a step up before I make a positive review.

POST REVIEW TIPS FOR PERFORMANCE

with both linux and windows, use the following mods:

-REFramework

-TU4.2 All in one (DirectStorage 1.3.2 DLSS 310.4 SL 2.9 FSR 3.1.5 n' 4.0.3 FG 3.1.6 XeSS 2.0.2)

-MHWs Tex Decompressor

While this does NOT fully fix the issues with the games performance, I was able to achieve between 80-120fps at HIGH settings with FRAMEGEN enabled, and not only eliminated the constant fps drops and stutters, but also lowered my CPU temps by about 10C.

Since MH Wilds launch (and also before that with the open beta's), most of the reviews I've seen of the game have been negative to an overwhelming degree, which let me be clear - I'm not saying the game is perfect and doesn't deserve criticism, but I feel like some people have been treating this game like the developers made an asset flipped Unity game and then bailed without a word... which obviously, isn't true.

I've played previous monster hunter games, specifically World and Rise, and I loved both of them. I'll be the first to admit that I absolutely had a bias when the game came out because of the fact that over the years I've become a bit of a die hard fan of monster hunter, and I scoffed at people complaining about how poorly the game ran because "graphics didn't matter" and "It runs fine for me", but as I kept playing over the past few months and the devs made extremely slow changes to performance (that seemingly didn't really do anything) I slowly realised that I was wrong, and the game absolutely had a problem. 

However, calling the game a cash-grab or something along those lines I think is a bit too much, even with the poor performance the game still clearly shows that Capcom put a lot into making this a true Monster Hunter™ experience, especially for long time fans of the series.

Now for an actual review. I love this game, despite the fact that the recent "performance update" caused the game to crash when I dared to set my graphics settings to high.

The new open area style of the game makes the world feel alive and lived in, especially when environmental factors are used during hunts. Literally just today, I'm pretty sure the Doshaguma I was hunting picked up a ceratonoth and threw it at me as it was nearby, I didn't even know they could do that. And while only 1 monster does this as of typing this review, the new mechanic of a monster becoming a sub-species mid fight because of damage you've done to them is a really cool idea and I'm hoping more monsters do it in the master rank expansion.

The weapons this time around feel powerful, most likely because of the new offset and power clash mechanics that some weapons have. Admittedly, I use a Switch Axe and a Gunlance, which are already weapons that have big "explosive" power even before the changes made in wilds, and I have heard that some weapons don't feel as up to standard compared to others. I still think each weapon is more then capable of holding its own though.

One thing I will say though, I do not know how they screwed up the multiplayer so badly when they got it right in Rise?

Overall, the games performance really brings it down, but the game itself is a lot of fun. Do I recommend this game? Yes*

*If you are willing to deal with the performance issues you'll inevitably have to deal with then the game is great, especially if you're already a fan of monster hunter. It shouldn't have to be that way, but if you can get past it the game really is good. If you've never played monster hunter but you do want to play it, but can't get past the performance issues, then I recommend playing World (or Rise, but world is closer to how Wilds plays), which at this point you could probably get for like 90% off in a sale.

1. Engine-level inefficiencies

The game relies heavily on stock engine systems (RE Engine), with minimal custom engine work.

Default engine pipelines for shaders, PSOs, and particles were left mostly unoptimized.

Result: the engine does far more work than necessary, causing GPU spikes and high system load even in low-action areas like towns.

2. Shader compilation and cache problems

Shaders are recalculated frequently, even for static elements like world fog or environmental materials.

Shader and PSO caches are fragile; they corrupt easily due to constant runtime recompilation.

Rebuilding caches can take 15+ minutes on high-end hardware.

Frequent cache flushes are required as a workaround to prevent crashes and performance spikes.

3. Particle system overwork

Many particle effects are treated as “live” even when they don’t need to be.

Effects that could be static or distance-cullable are recalculated continuously, adding unnecessary GPU/CPU load.

Distance-based LOD and static/frozen tagging is mostly ignored except for very specific effects like hair.

4. GPU load and thermal issues

Even top-tier GPUs (e.g., RTX 4070) hit ~80 °C on max settings due to constant shader and particle recalculation.

FPS capping or lowering settings doesn’t fully reduce thermal load because the engine’s workload is time-based, not frame-based.

The engine keeps the GPU saturated unnecessarily, generating heat and power draw spikes.

5. Inconsistent performance across similar systems

Players with almost identical hardware report wildly different experiences:

Some run the game flawlessly for hundreds of hours.

Others crash frequently or experience stuttering.

Cause: fragile shader/particle pipeline, cache state sensitivity, driver differences, async compilation timing.

6. Poor communication and deflection

Developers have blamed crashes on user drivers, not the engine.

Recommended GPU drivers only partially address the problem and don’t solve shader cache corruption or runtime overwork.

Messaging effectively shifts responsibility to players instead of acknowledging engine inefficiency and fragility.

7. Broader design philosophy issues

Focus was placed on world-building and content, with little attention to engine hardening.

Stock engine features were misused as “full game solutions” rather than scaffolding.

Minimal optimization for distance culling, static vs dynamic tagging, and resource throttling.

System requirements are inflated due to inefficient engine behavior rather than actual visual fidelity.

-Bottom line-

Monster Hunter Wilds suffers from deep engine-level inefficiencies that affect shader compilation, particle updates, cache management, GPU load, and thermal behavior.

Hardware upgrades alone do not solve these problems.

Core issues require engine-side fixes (shader/particle tagging, caching, distance-based LODs), which may never happen.

Player-observed workarounds (cache flushes, lower settings) can mitigate symptoms but don’t fix the underlying fragility.

Okay, I have played lots of monster hunters since monster hunter freedom 2 on psp. Imma give my honest opinion. There´s lot of pros and cons, so buckle up.

Keep in mind, I bought this in december on sale so it was already out for some time. The reason this matters is because now I can´t really find multiplayer games with real people. I always get those lately introduced ai companions. They rarely change to real players.

Monster hunter wilds differs a bit from predecessors and while many people criticize this, I find it quite good. 
For example, you don´t need to go everywhere for items, run around the specific mining locations, u just ride by with your bird horse, and grab it with a long distance grappler. I hated running to each location, I think this is good. 

Then you don´t get this main village stuff anymore where you go to quest boards and choose what you want to hunt. You progress a story line that always takes you somewhere else and meanwhile you in fact can talk to some npc and get a quest or support people. But this might be a main reason you can´t find people. I also forgot to add that when going back to the game from hibernate u are kicked out of any online session, so this might contribute to my perception of not finding any people. In mh world as far as I remember you could change back to online in the moment you made a sos.

Now to the story.

The story is so ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ boring. Some abstract s*** is pulled out of the a***, why nature is so cruel hunters have to fight back and now go hunt monsters. The stupid dialogue and childish portrayal of heroism makes my brain hurt. Then this "We´re all in it together and we´re all friends, let´s fight those bad bad monsters". This is around since world and I hated it already there. I get it, u need some sort of storytelling, but it was made in the worst way possible. At least now, the character has some dialogue in world, u would just nod the whole time to everything that it is said to you and make wild gestures.
U have the standard roster of characters. A wild girl with big b****, an innocent clumsy girl with big b****, an annoying child and cats, lots of cats.

In freedom 2 it made sense when u talked to the village leader, she said there was some dangerous monster that threatened the village and now "go hunt it". It was plain, simple and not a big deal, straight to the point. What it´s all about. Getting new cool monsters to see and fighting them.

In wilds, you have the big big tragedy of a child losing its village by a monster and it is so upset about it that it wants to run fight it alone at some place. I mean, wtf is this nonsense. 
As you can see, I hate the storytelling, but this is a problem that was around since world. Maybe it could have been made more interesting than just putting as many big b***** girls in there as you can. They even missed out on the standard roided up old man leader of the village with white hair.

The weapon system has greatly improved. You can now open wounds and hurt the monster with special attacks after a certain amount. You can mount it, this was already there but still. Then you have more attacks, a better synergy. Very positive, very very positive. Plus, you don´t run around for 20 consecutive games getting rare material that has a drop rate like 1 percent like the nergigante s*** they pulled off in world, you want it now, u get it now. After u hunt the monster. Great.
Also there is lots of new monsters, I have never seen before, really really nice. Except the congalala. Because it´s "so funny" to have an ape thats main attack is to poop you with great might. Of course it needed to be in here too.

The graphic´s is chef´s kiss. Very very good on my rtx 5080. Getting average 70 fps with raytracing, even 120 without. I love the graphics. It´s good on monitor, it´s good on tv. 

Another great thing is that you can download custom characters. So in the beginning you can change your appearance to mister proper, sylvester stallone, or again big b***** females. Nice.
Also the felyne has looks like beerus from dbz. nice nice.

Overall, many things people criticize I see as improvements. Nobody liked that grind for a 1 percent drop rate material or running to far away mining spots. It is good, it´s polished, it´s great, at least on pc. But the storytelling hurts. Typical brainrot nonsense story. 

But still, love this game. Much much love. Love the franchise. If I had listened to all this negative reviews, I would have missed out on this.

Its still Monster Hunter alright, but I don't know how this game really evolves much from World.  Yes, I understand its more open world, but is it really?  The quests are so disjointed that there's never really any point to making the world interconnected at all.  I'm sorry, but I cannot recommend this game because Capcom needs fans to finally standup and ask for this series to evolve properly.  I'm not going to complain about the lack of monsters, gameplay, art, story, and PC optimization.  I simply want what we've been asking for decades now which is a true open world MH, and this isn't it.  

Capcom needs to truly do away with this hand holding story nonsense and start taking a western approach to these games.  An actual "go anywhere" game where our friends can join us for the entirely of it and the lore is for the player to find and absorb if we want.  I don't need NPCs to literally WALK me to the objective which is the primary issue.  The map can remain exactly the same, but how can I experience it when I'm talking to an NPC, following them to the monster, killing monster, then teleporting back to camp.

In addition MH Wilds is just a bloated mess of options and GUI issues.  This game has the most convoluted coop I have ever seen, especially in a MH game.  Party link, environment link, waiting until they are done with cut scenes to join?  What is this?  There are 6+ tabs of options, then 4+ pages of options within those options.  That's way too many options for a game that's literally about grabbing a weapon and killing giant monsters with your friends.

Capcom, just give us the open world game we wanted.

Ever since Monster Hunter Tri, I've been a die hard fan of this series. I've played every game since and gone back to the ones before just to experience everything they had to offer since I just couldn't get enough of the gameplay or the world. Although every series on the planet have their up and downs when it came to quality, Monster Hunter was special, as I couldn't find a single game in the franchise I would call bad, at worst slightly above average. Perhaps I found World slightly lacking in the elements I truly enjoyed, but it was by no means a bad game, I enjoyed my time with it and would recommend it to any fan or interested player as a quality game.

Monster Hunter Wilds has no redeeming qualities, this is the first game in the series I will say with confidence is simply a bad game. It's not a bad Monster Hunter game, which would imply some sort of inherit quality from games past, it's a bad game period, and comparing it to what came before makes it look even worse. It baffles me that a team which provided me thousands of hours of enjoyment over nearly a dozen games spanning numerous console generations would create something so soulless, uninspired and sloppy.

To put it simply; This game sucks major ass, wastes your time and tests your patience at every corner. It runs like quadriplegic grandma who died in 1992. When it does work, its incredibly braindead easy, it's story is one of the worst things I've ever been subjected to, and the gameplay loop is a mindless slog that begins to feel like a chore barely a few hours in.

If I received this game for free, I would still ask for my money back and immediately assume the person who gave it to me hates my guts on the deepest and most primordial of levels.

Monster Hunter Wilds is a live service game. This game has more in common with Kill the Justice League than the games that came before. It's all there, the half baked plot, the terrible optimization, the obsession with huge RNG filled grinds to keep retention, FOMO limited time events, overpriced DLC packs, and repetitive coma inducing endgame loops. Simple engaging action RPGs like the old games don't make as much money as live services do. Why make a solid, simple replayable RPG when we can make a full priced skinner box designed to waste your time so you'll buy shiny skinpacks to oggle some blacksmith boobah to feel something in your atrophied, slop-filled, gooner brain.

Don't buy this, even on sale, go play literally any other game in the franchise.

It's an excellent Monster Hunting game, and got better with every title update.

The performance issues are real, but also overstated, and successive updates have improved performance in low-end GPUs. The recent TU4 patch in particular has improved performance by a significant margin.

Currently, the game is fully playable on 1080p at an average of 45 fps on a 4-year-old laptop GPU with just 6Gb of VRAM, with High texture quality, Medium quality shadows, and upscaling turned OFF. In my experience, there are few to no stutters and the only areas where the framerate drops significantly are those with a high number of NPCs in memory.

Mind you, that's still very bad performance by any measure and I can't pretend otherwise. However, it is entirely playable and the game looks extremely good. Better hardware and 8Gb of VRAM will get you better results, naturally, and you should manage to hit a stable 60fps outside of highly demanding areas.

The developers have promised another two patches aiming to improve PC performance, and I'm cautiously optimistic that those patches will have a significant impact, if they're anywhere as impactful as the TU4 Patch.

Most remaining performance issues can be fixed by clearing the Shader cache and spending a good 10 minutes or so in-game to let it be rebuilt. As with all REengine games, I highly recommend the REframework mod to further improve performance.

tl;dr: Give the game a chance. Performance is much better nowadays and the game is perfectly playable even on low-end hardware.

As of writing this review, I have played Monster Hunter Wilds for 200 hours and collected all awards. I love Monster Hunter, but this is a design and development disaster of a Monster Hunter game. The general design changes detract from the game I love, and it was only my love of Monster Hunter and determination to succeed that kept me playing through the overall poor performance and numerous crashes. Monster Hunter Wilds was released too early, and it's not a satisfactory product despite multiple updates. I can't recommend the game because of the combination of these issues, and anyone purchasing the game should know that it is not reliable software.

From the design standpoint, core Monster Hunter gameplay is significantly affected by the Seikret and introduction of Focus Mode and Wounds. The Seikret is an evolution of the Raider Rides from Monster Hunter: World Iceborne and Palamute from Monster Hunter Rise. Monster Hunter: World also introduced Scoutflies that showed the player a monster's location after investigation and multiple hunts and their memory faded when the player changed quarries, but the Seikret has no such limitation despite the presence of Scoutflies in Monster Hunter Wilds.  While the Seikret has less combat utility than the Palamute, the Seikret still completely removes the investigation and tracking phase of the game by delivering the player to the monster wherever it is, which detracts from the hunting element of Monster Hunter and makes it closer to a brawler or character action game like Devil May Cry.

Focus Mode allows the player to aim their attacks in any direction, and Wounds reward the player for targeting an area or limb on a monster and have weapon-specific interactions. Focus Mode significantly changes the gameplay of Monster Hunter by allowing the player to turn their character and attacks mid-swing, and almost completely removes the aiming element of Monster Hunter gameplay. In most Monster Hunter games, the player must commit to attacks in a particular direction and miss if the monster moves unexpectedly, but Focus Mode removes that skill and failure point almost entirely. To Capcom's credit, they attempted to balance Focus Mode by making the targeting reticule move slowly, but the ability itself radically alters Monster Hunter gameplay. On the other hand, Wounds are an evolution of part and limb breaking and tenderizing monster parts from Iceborne without the complications of the Clutch Claw. Wounds also replace sparkly item drops from monsters and reward the player with flinches and topples for consistently striking the same area or limb on a monster. While the item and gameplay rewards from Wounds might be too much, the core gameplay is enhanced by rewarding the player for playing well. Capcom should remove Focus Mode and keep Wounds in the next Monster Hunter game.

From the development standpoint, Monster Hunter Wilds doesn't look much better than Monster Hunter: World, but requires significantly more powerful hardware to reach the same performance target of 1080p at 60fps without frame generation. On default graphics settings, I also experienced numerous crashes from the game locking the graphics driver during my playtime, particularly in the forest and ruins regions. Player, NPC, food, and monster models often fail to load in quickly enough, resulting in the infamous origami models. Player, NPC, food, and monster textures also fail to load in quickly enough, resulting in an extremely ugly game until the textures load. The game also uses a pointillism effect for transparency or a player outline when the player's view of their character would be blocked, but both of them are unpleasant to see and look like hacks around larger, underlying graphical issues. Finally, the game's lighting process struggles to cast shadows in many areas, but particularly the forest and ruins regions. Overall, my crashes and general performance issues indicate inadequate testing and quality assurance time before release. Capcom should be ashamed to have released such poorly performing and unreliable software.
